#14 — Highly Innovative (79)

A Foundation Model for Instruction-Conditioned In-Context Time Series Tasks

Anish Saha, Konstantin Shmakov · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This paper scores 79 for introducing a genuinely novel instruction-conditioned in-context learning paradigm to time series, enabling a single model to adapt to forecasting, classification, and anomaly detection purely through structured demonstrations—a capability no prior time series foundation model offered. The hierarchical attention architecture and semantic tokenization scheme are creative and well-validated through rigorous ablations. Strong zero-shot results on fev-bench (81.3% win rate) and GIFT-Eval substantiate the approach. However, the lack of released weights, undisclosed training costs, and the fact that several claimed multi-task capabilities (imputation, source de-mixing) are never evaluated at inference time temper the impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing time-series foundation models (Chronos-2, TimesFM-2.5, TiRex, Moirai) incorporate context (long history windows, retrieved examples, cross-series pooling) to improve a single fixed task—usually forecasting. None treat context as explicit input–output demonstrations that define the task mapping at inference time, as in NLP in-context learning. This gap limits their adaptability: deploying a forecasting model for anomaly detection or classification still requires labeled data and fine-tuning. The paper asks whether a single time-series model can adapt to multiple tasks—forecasting, imputation, anomaly detection, classification, source de-mixing—purely by conditioning on structured demonstrations at inference time, without parameter updates. Methodology: The proposed model (iAmTime) is built on a T5 encoder–decoder backbone with three key components. First, a structured prompt-like tokenization scheme uses specialized semantic tokens ([START], [TARGET SERIES], [EXOG], [MID], [FUTURE EXOG], [END]) to explicitly encode the roles of targets, covariates, historical context, and task-specific future information within each example–query pair. This prevents representation leakage that would occur with raw numeric concatenation. Second, a three-stage hierarchical attention architecture processes inputs at: (1) individual series level (intra-series T5 encoder), (2) per-example fusion level (cross-target/covariate T5 encoder), and (3) cross-example fusion via mean pooling of example representations into a global ICL rule vector, which is concatenated with the query representation and processed by a final T5 encoder. Third, a mixture-of-experts non-autoregressive decoder with learned expert queries predicts 9 quantile levels jointly over the full forecast horizon in a single forward pass using pinball loss. Training follows an amortized meta-learning paradigm—sampling demonstration–query episodes from a distribution over five task types (forecasting, imputation, anomaly detection, classification, source de-mixing) with a curriculum that gradually increases context length, example count, and task complexity. The training corpus comprises ~66M time series: ~30M real univariate series from the Chronos corpus, ~2.5M from GIFT-Eval pretraining, ~30M TSMixup augmented series, ~10M KernelSynth GP-generated series, and ~50M synthetic multivariate series with controlled causal relationships (lagged influence, cointegration, Granger-style effects, nonlinear modulations). Key Results: On fev-bench (100 tasks, fully zero-shot), iAmTime achieves the highest aggregate CRPS (0.48) and MASE (0.63), a win rate of 81.3% and skill score of 51.9%—outperforming Chronos-2 (79.7%/51.5%), TimesFM-2.5 (69.0%/50.8%), TiRex (66.2%/46.7%), and all statistical baselines. On GIFT-Eval (97 settings, zero-shot), iAmTime achieves the lowest aggregate CRPS (0.47) and MASE (0.68), with average model ranks of 3.54 (CRPS) and 3.63 (MASE)—beating Chronos-2 (4.66/5.34) and TiRex (6.51/5.42). iAmTime ranks first across short-, medium-, and long-horizon settings and on both univariate and multivariate subsets. Ablations confirm all three components are necessary: removing in-context examples drops win rate from 86.1% to 70.9%; removing semantic tokens causes the largest skill score drop (from 37.2% to 20.5%); removing multi-task meta-learning (forecasting-only training) drops win rate to 59.1%. Distribution shift experiments show performance remains strong even when ICL examples are drawn from training-domain classes not seen in the inference domain. Strengths: - The explicit instruction-conditioned ICL formulation is genuinely novel for time-series: it moves beyond using context to improve a fixed task and instead lets demonstrations define the task mapping, enabling zero-shot adaptation to forecasting, classification, and anomaly detection from the same model. - The hierarchical encoder design (series → example → cross-example) is well-motivated and empirically validated; mean pooling for ICL rule extraction is permutation-invariant and handles variable numbers of examples without overfitting to position. - Rigorous ablations cleanly isolate contributions of examples, semantic tokens, and meta-training, and the distribution-shift experiment provides non-trivial evidence that the model uses demonstrations functionally rather than as memorized domain features. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper reports results only from Walmart researchers; model weights and training code are not released, making reproducibility and independent verification impossible. The training setup (hardware, parameter count, training time) is not disclosed, preventing fair cost comparisons with baselines. - Several self-supervised tasks (imputation, reconstruction, source de-mixing) are used only during training and are not evaluated at inference time—the stated reason being difficulty of specifying them through demonstrations in practice. This substantially narrows the scope of the multi-task claim and leaves the quality of these auxiliary capabilities unverified. - The ICL rule extractor uses simple mean pooling across examples, which discards ordering and relative importance of demonstrations. No analysis of how many examples are needed, or how sensitive performance is to example quality and selection strategy, is provided beyond a brief distributional robustness experiment.

#15 — Highly Innovative (79)

LongCat-Next: Lexicalizing Modalities as Discrete Tokens (74 ▲ )

Meituan LongCat Team, Bin Xiao, Chao Wang, Chengjiang Li, Chi Zhang, Chong Peng, Hang Yu, Hao Yang, Haonan Yan, Haoze Sun, Haozhe Zhao, Hong Liu, Hui Su, Jiaqi Zhang, Jiawei Wang, Jing Li, Kefeng Zhang, Manyuan Zhang, Minhao Jing, Peng Pei, Quan Chen, Taofeng Xue, Tongxin Pan, Xiaotong Li, Xiaoyang Li, Xiaoyu Zhao, Xing Hu, Xinyang Lin, Xunliang Cai, Yan Bai, Yan Feng, Yanjie Li, Yao Qiu, Yerui Sun, Yifan Lu, Ying Luo, Yipeng Mei, Yitian Chen, Yuchen Xie, Yufang Liu, Yufei Chen, Yulei Qian, Yuqi Peng, Zhihang Yu, Zhixiong Han, Changran Wang, Chen Chen, Dian Zheng, Fengjiao Chen, Ge Yang, Haowei Guo, Haozhe Wang, Hongyu Li, Huicheng Jiang, Jiale Hong, Jialv Zou, Jiamu Li, Jianping Lin, Jiaxing Liu, Jie Yang, Jing Jin, Jun Kuang, Juncheng She, Kunming Luo, Kuofeng Gao, Lin Qiu, Linsen Guo, Mianqiu Huang, Qi Li, Qian Wang, Rumei Li, Siyu Ren, Wei Wang, Wenlong He, Xi Chen, Xiao Liu, Xiaoyu Li, Xu Huang, Xuanyu Zhu, Xuezhi Cao, Yaoming Zhu, Yifei Cao, Yimeng Jia, Yizhen Jiang, Yufei Gao, Zeyang Hu, Zhenlong Yuan, Zijian Zhang, Ziwen Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 1 Apr 2026
Why this rank: LongCat-Next scores 79 for operationalizing the semantic completeness principle — demonstrating that language-aligned encoders preserve sufficient low-level information through residual connections to enable reconstruction, directly challenging the prevailing assumption that discrete vision modeling has an inherent performance ceiling. The architectural purity of using zero modality-specific components (no 3D RoPE, no modality-aware routing, no bidirectional attention) while achieving competitive results across text, vision, and audio is a strong statement about representation convergence. The unified parallel/serial audio generation via stochastic delay training is an elegant framework contribution. However, the reliance on frozen SAE encoders limits high-frequency detail, and some baseline comparisons use potentially inconsistent public report numbers.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Contemporary multimodal systems remain language-centric, treating vision and audio as external attachments projected into LLM embedding spaces. This fragmented architecture prevents a principled unified training objective and forces separate optimization paths for understanding versus generation — a fundamental conflict. The field lacks an industrial-strength recipe for a genuinely native multimodal model where all modalities are first-class citizens sharing a single discrete token space and a single autoregressive objective. The core technical barrier is that compressing rich visual signals into a finite codebook inevitably loses information, creating a perceived performance ceiling for discrete vision modeling relative to continuous alternatives. Methodology: LongCat-Next instantiates a framework called Discrete Native Autoregression (DiNA) on a LongCat-Flash Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) backbone (68.5B total, ~3B activated parameters). The key innovation is the Discrete Native Any-resolution Vision Transformer (dNaViT), which addresses the dual bottleneck of representational capacity and discretization loss. dNaViT uses a Semantic-and-Aligned Encoder (SAE) — concretely Qwen2.5-ViT with 28× spatial compression — as the pre-quantization space. SAE features, which are already language-aligned through large-scale vision-language pretraining, are then hierarchically discretized via 8-level Residual Vector Quantization (RVQ) with EMA-updated codebooks. A key theoretical insight is that residual connections in modern ViT encoders inherently preserve a latent pathway for low-level pixel information, enabling reconstruction even from encoders trained purely for semantics. De-tokenization uses a 400M-parameter ViT pixel decoder followed by a flow-matching refiner. For audio, a Whisper-large-v3 encoder feeds an 8-level RVQ, with a symmetric decoder and flow-matching refinement producing 24kHz audio. The backbone is fully modality-agnostic with no 3D RoPE, modality-aware MoE routing, or bidirectional attention. A lightweight Pre-Buffer FFN re-encodes multi-level summed embeddings to close the initial alignment gap between discrete and continuous representations. For audio generation, a unified training paradigm with random delays over segment-level text-audio pairs enables both parallel (low-latency) and serial (high-quality) speech generation as extremes of the same framework. Post-training uses GRPO-based RL with multi-dimensional reward models for both image generation and understanding, with novel entropy-based and training-inference mismatch filters to prevent entropy explosion. Key Results: On visual understanding, LongCat-Next-A3B achieves 64.7 on MathVision (vs. 60.2 for Qwen3-VL-A3B-Instruct), 83.1 on MathVista (vs. 80.1), 60.3 on MMMU-Pro, and 0.152 on OmniDocBench-EN (best among listed models). On CharXivRQ it scores 60.1, outperforming all baselines including GPT5-minimal (57.8) and Qwen3-VL (48.9). Against unified multimodal models on generation, LongCat-Next scores 84.44 on GenEval, 93.15 on LongText-EN, 84.38 on TIFF, and 76.36 on CVTG — substantially outperforming BAGEL, Janus-Pro, Show-o2, and other unified systems across generation benchmarks while maintaining comparable understanding. On audio, it achieves WER of 1.63/3.42 on LibriSpeech clean/other, MMAU score of 76.40, and ReasoningQA of 87.52 — competitive with or exceeding specialized omni models. On text-only agentic benchmarks (Tau2-Telecom: 62.06 vs. 4.39 for Qwen3-Omni), the model avoids the multimodal tax. Ablation studies show that with the Pre-Buffer module and sufficient data scale (300B tokens), discrete performance closes to within ~1% of continuous counterparts on OCR, DocVQA, and ChartQA. RL post-training yields ~5% improvements on general VQA/OCR and +7.5% counting accuracy on GenEval. Strengths: - The semantic completeness principle is operationalized concretely: using a language-aligned SAE as the pre-quantization space is both theoretically motivated and empirically shown to close the discrete-continuous performance gap, challenging the prevailing assumption that discrete vision modeling has an inherent ceiling. - Architectural purity — no modality-specific attention, routing, or positional encoding — enables modality-agnostic MoE to emergently specialize experts for vision and audio, and produces interlaced (rather than clustered) cross-modal embeddings consistent with the Platonic representation hypothesis. - The unified parallel/serial audio generation framework through stochastic delay training is elegant and practically valuable, enabling the same model to support both streaming and high-quality TTS without separate training. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The current de-tokenizer explicitly prioritizes semantic consistency over pixel fidelity; the paper acknowledges it is not optimized for reconstruction quality, which limits use in pixel-accurate generation tasks. The SAE encoder's frozen state constrains high-frequency detail recovery. - Despite strong aggregate results, the model trails specialized models on several individual benchmarks (e.g., OCRBenchV2, CountBench, ScreenSpot-V2, DocVQA vs. Qwen3-VL-A3B) and lags behind purely text-optimized models like Qwen3-Next-80B on knowledge benchmarks — the multimodal tax is reduced but not eliminated. - The paper compares to baselines (GPT5-minimal, Gemini2.5-Flash-Lite) sourced from public reports with potential version inconsistencies, and the RL ablations use a smaller Qwen-7B backbone rather than the full production model, making it difficult to assess the magnitude of RL gains at scale.

#16 — Highly Innovative (79)

PackForcing: Short Video Training Suffices for Long Video Sampling and Long Context Inference (38 ▲ )

Xiaofeng Mao, Shaohao Rui, Kaining Ying, Bo Zheng, Chuanhao Li, Mingmin Chi, Kaipeng Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 30 Mar 2026
Why this rank: PackForcing introduces a genuinely novel three-partition KV cache architecture that achieves O(1) memory complexity for autoregressive video diffusion, enabling 24× temporal extrapolation (5s training → 2-minute inference) on a single GPU. The dual-branch compression module combining 3D CNN and pixel-space re-encoding, the incremental RoPE correction exploiting rotary embedding multiplicativity, and the dynamic context selection with query subsampling are each technically elegant contributions. The bounded 4.2 GB KV cache versus 138 GB for full cache represents a qualitative capability shift, not just an incremental improvement, and the attention analysis rigorously motivating compression over eviction strengthens the theoretical foundation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Autoregressive video diffusion models generate videos block-by-block using KV caching, but face two compounding problems at minute-scale durations. First, KV cache grows linearly with video length—a 2-minute 832×480 video at 16 FPS requires ~749K tokens and ~138 GB of KV storage, exceeding single-GPU capacity. Second, small prediction errors compound iteratively, causing semantic drift and quality degradation (Self-Forcing's CLIP score drops 6.77 points over 60s). Existing workarounds (sliding windows, FIFO truncation) permanently discard historical context, breaking long-range coherence. No prior method simultaneously achieves bounded memory, KV compression, and stable long-horizon generation. Methodology: PackForcing introduces a three-partition KV cache design that decouples video history into functionally distinct regions: (1) Sink tokens — the first 8 latent frames kept at full resolution as semantic anchors, occupying <2% of the token budget; (2) Mid tokens — intermediate history compressed ~32× (from 6,240 to 182 tokens per block) via a dual-branch module combining a 4-stage progressive 3D CNN (HR branch, operating in latent space with 2×8×8 volume reduction) and a pixel-space re-encoding branch (LR branch: decode → pool 2×/4× → re-encode via frozen VAE), fused by element-wise addition; (3) Recent/current tokens — the last few blocks at full resolution for local coherence. A dual-resolution shifting pipeline concurrently computes compressed mid-token backups during generation of recent blocks, hiding compression latency. Dynamic Context Selection scores all compressed mid-blocks by query-key affinity and routes only the top-K (K=16) for each attention step, using query subsampling and half-head evaluation to keep overhead <1%. When mid-buffer capacity is exceeded and blocks are evicted, positional discontinuities from 3D RoPE are corrected via an incremental temporal-only rotation (cost <0.1% of inference FLOPs). The total attention context is bounded at ~27,872 tokens regardless of video length, making training on 5-second clips distribution-compatible with 2-minute inference. Key Results: On VBench-Long with 128 MovieGen prompts, PackForcing achieves state-of-the-art Dynamic Degree of 56.25 (60s) and 54.12 (120s), outperforming the next-best baseline DeepForcing by +2.58 and +1.28 respectively. It achieves the highest Overall Consistency (26.07 at 60s, 26.05 at 120s) among all methods. CLIP score stability over 60s is best-in-class: PackForcing declines only 1.14 points (34.04→32.90) versus Self-Forcing's 6.77-point drop. Performance degradation from 60s to 120s is marginal: Subject Consistency drops only 1.01 points (vs. 6.01 for Self-Forcing). Memory footprint is bounded at ~4.2 GB KV cache for any video length (vs. ~138 GB for full cache, which causes OOM). Generation runs at 15-16 FPS on a single A100/H200. The 24× temporal extrapolation (5s training → 120s inference) succeeds both zero-shot and with fine-tuning. Ablations confirm each component contributes: removing sink tokens causes CLIP to drop from 35.09 to 31.24; disabling RoPE adjustment widens early-to-late CLIP gap from 0.95 to 2.53; dynamic selection over FIFO improves Subject Consistency by +0.8; the dual-branch compression outperforms either branch alone on all metrics. Strengths: - The three-partition design is empirically motivated by attention pattern analysis showing that attention demand is near-uniform across all historical positions (mean importance = 0.499), which rigorously rules out FIFO and validates the need for compression rather than eviction. - The incremental RoPE adjustment is technically elegant: exploiting the multiplicative property of rotary embeddings to correct position gaps with a single complex multiply per eviction event, adding negligible overhead while eliminating a significant failure mode. - True O(1) attention complexity with bounded KV cache memory enables generation of arbitrarily long videos on commodity hardware — a qualitative capability improvement over all compared baselines, which OOM beyond ~80s. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The fixed 128× volume / ~32× token compression ratio is not adaptive to scene complexity — static scenes waste compression budget while complex scenes may suffer more quality loss, and this is acknowledged but unaddressed. - Subject Consistency (90.49) trails LongLive (92.00) at 60s, revealing a residual tension between dynamic motion richness and strict identity preservation; the paper does not offer a mechanism to control this tradeoff beyond sink size tuning. - Evaluation is limited to a single backbone (Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B at 832×480) and a single benchmark (VBench); generalization to higher resolutions (e.g., 1080p) or larger DiT backbones is unvalidated, and the LR branch's VAE decode-encode cycle may not scale efficiently at higher spatial dimensions.
Recommended 2603.25730 PDF
#17 — Highly Innovative (79)

Steerable Visual Representations (28 ▲ )

Jona Ruthardt, Manu Gaur, Deva Ramanan, Makarand Tapaswi, Yuki M. Asano · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 3 Apr 2026
Why this rank: SteerViT introduces a genuinely novel paradigm for conditional visual representations by inverting gated cross-attention to route vision through language context within frozen ViT layers. The continuous inference-time control knob enabling Pareto-optimal interpolation between steerability and representation quality is an elegant design contribution. The breadth of evaluation across retrieval, segmentation, classification, anomaly detection, and personalized discrimination demonstrates real generality rather than benchmark chasing. The 96% acc@1 on CORE versus 44% for both DINOv2 and CLIP represents a striking capability gap that this work closes.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Pretrained Vision Transformers (ViTs) like DINOv2 and MAE suffer from a fundamental limitation: they encode images by focusing on the most salient or dominant object, with no mechanism to redirect attention toward less prominent concepts of interest. This is a direct consequence of photographer bias in training datasets. Meanwhile, Multimodal LLMs can be guided with text prompts but produce language-dominant representations that degrade on generic vision tasks (segmentation, retrieval, classification). No prior work satisfies the three desiderata simultaneously: steerability, representation quality, and early vision-language fusion. The gap is significant—tasks like fine-grained localization, anomaly detection, and personalized object discrimination all require representations that can be redirected by task-specific priors. Methodology: SteerViT introduces "Steerable Visual Representations" by injecting textual conditioning directly into the internal layers of a frozen ViT backbone (DINOv2 ViT-B/14, but also shown to generalize to SigLIP and MAE). The key architectural innovation is gated cross-attention (CA) layers inserted into every other Transformer encoder block, totaling ~21M trainable parameters. The visual patch tokens attend to adapted text embeddings as keys and values—inverting the formulation used in Flamingo (which routes language→vision) to instead route vision→language context. The gate uses a tanh-gated scalar αℓ initialized to zero, ensuring the model starts identical to the frozen ViT and gradually opens the conditioning pathway during training. The text encoder is a frozen RoBERTa-Large producing token-level embeddings, projected to visual-aligned space via a trainable two-layer MLP. Training uses a patch-level referential segmentation proxy task (soft cross-entropy) on 162k images and 2.28M text-image pairs from RefCOCO/+/g, Visual Genome, LVIS, and Mapillary Vistas. A continuous control knob at inference (scaling αℓ by ω ∈ [0,1]) interpolates between the unmodified ViT and fully steered SteerViT, enabling a Pareto-optimal operating point at ω=0.6. Key Results: On the proposed CORE (COnditional REtrieval) benchmark—a controlled retrieval task that requires locating non-salient inpainted objects across scene-matched images—SteerViT achieves 96% acc@1, far ahead of DINOv2 (44%), CLIP (44%), and even SAM3 (88.5%), while MLLMs (InternVL3-2B: 76%, Qwen3-VL: 70%) fall well short. On GeneCIS (real-world zero-shot conditional retrieval), SteerViT achieves 25.4% R@1 vs. 9.6% for DINOv2 and 18.7% for the benchmark's specialized baseline. On the MOSAIC localization benchmark (attention maps over 4-image composites), SteerViT reaches PR-AUC of 50.2% vs. 14.3% for DINOv2. Critically, SteerViT preserves representation quality: linear probing on classification benchmarks (ImageWoof, Waterbirds, StanfordCars) and ADE20k segmentation match or exceed the underlying DINOv2 baseline. On PODS (personalized instance discrimination), SteerViT with detailed MLLM-generated descriptions achieves 58.1% PR-AUC vs. 29.6% for DINOv2 and 48.0% for fine-tuned DINOv2. On ADE20k anomaly detection (zero-shot), SteerViT matches or outperforms prior specialized methods. Strengths: - Elegant architectural design: inverting gated cross-attention to condition vision on language (early fusion) with only 21M trainable parameters on a frozen backbone is parameter-efficient and principled. - The gating mechanism provides a continuous inference-time control knob, enabling a Pareto frontier between steerability and representation quality without retraining. - Compelling empirical breadth: evaluated across retrieval, attention localization, classification, segmentation, anomaly detection, and personalized discrimination—demonstrating genuine generality rather than task-specific overfitting. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method is validated primarily on DINOv2 ViT-B/14; scaling to larger ViT variants or evaluating on ViTs with different pretraining objectives (e.g., supervised or contrastive) is limited in the main paper. - The CORE benchmark relies on inpainted synthetic objects (via FLUX.2), which may not fully capture the complexity and distribution shift of real-world non-salient object detection scenarios, despite the supplementary GeneCIS validation. - Text specificity sensitivity is a double-edged sword: coarse prompts can underperform vanilla DINOv2 (27.9% vs. 29.6% PR-AUC on PODS), meaning users must provide appropriately detailed prompts—a usability constraint not addressed by the framework itself.
Recommended 2604.02327 PDF
#18 — Highly Innovative (79)

Expert Streaming: Accelerating Low-Batch MoE Inference via Multi-chiplet Architecture and Dynamic Expert Trajectory Scheduling

Songchen Ma, Hongyi Li, Weihao Zhang, Yonghao Tan, Pingcheng Dong, Yu Liu, Lan Liu, Yuzhong Jiao, Xuejiao Liu, Luhong Liang, Kwang-Ting Cheng · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scores 79 for introducing a genuinely novel parallelism paradigm (FSE-DP) that treats a multi-chiplet array as a single pooled buffer with dynamically scheduled expert trajectories, rather than replicating weights or tokens. The virtualization abstraction via five lightweight rules elegantly decouples logical expert flow from physical placement without cycle-accurate global coordination — a meaningful architectural insight. End-to-end validation on a taped-out 5nm MCM chip elevates this well above simulation-only proposals, and the 1.22–2.00× speedup at one-fifth the on-chip memory is practically significant for edge MoE deployment.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs deployed on edge devices (AI PCs, robotics, autonomous driving) face three tightly coupled challenges on multi-chiplet accelerators: (1) severe on-chip SRAM scarcity relative to model size, (2) off-chip DDR bandwidth bottlenecks from frequent expert weight fetches exacerbated by low weight reuse in low-batch settings, and (3) dynamic workload imbalance caused by the long-tail token-per-expert distribution, which persists even with expert-balancing training losses. Existing parallel strategies—expert parallelism (EP), tensor parallelism (TP), data parallelism (DP)—all replicate either weights or tokens across chiplets, wasting on-chip memory without exploiting the high-bandwidth die-to-die (D2D) UCIe interconnects now available in modern chiplet packages. Prior multi-chiplet MoE work (e.g., Hydra) focuses on reducing all-to-all token routing but does not address the long-tail imbalance or off-chip memory bottleneck simultaneously. Methodology: The paper proposes Fully Sharded Expert Data-Parallelism (FSE-DP), a parallelization paradigm that treats the entire chiplet array as a single pooled buffer: only one physical copy of any expert slice is maintained across the package at any time. Expert weight slices are streamed as micro-slices along dynamically scheduled trajectories over D2D links, rather than replicated. The core technical components are: (1) Micro-slice flow: each expert slice is partitioned into micro-slices (the fundamental compute/transfer unit); an eager forwarding rule ensures each micro-slice is immediately forwarded after computation, minimizing on-chip residency and enabling overlap between D2D transfer and computation with only a ring-buffer's worth of extra storage. (2) Flow fusion: DDR loading is fused with the D2D micro-slice flow via five lightweight virtualization rules that make the system self-adapting without cycle-accurate global orchestration—chiplets compute whatever micro-slice arrived last and forward it immediately, decoupling logical trajectory from physical buffer placement. (3) Paired-load policy: experts are sorted by token count and paired hot-cold, interleaving compute-bound and memory-bound phases to keep both DDR and compute units utilized. (4) Token buffering: requests routed to extremely cold experts are deferred across iterations, aggregating tokens to improve weight reuse, controlled by a QoS timer to bound per-request latency penalty. A lightweight hardware scheduler (Expert Information Table + Idle Chiplet Vector + bitonic sorter, synthesized in UMC 28nm, 0.43 mm²) implements trajectory assignment with sub-microsecond latency. The system is validated on a taped-out 2×2 5nm MCM test chip with UCIe-compliant D2D links (288 GB/s aggregate) and DDR3-1600, evaluated via RTL cycle-accurate simulation. Key Results: FSE-DP is benchmarked against EP (standard expert parallelism) and Hydra (state-of-the-art chiplet-specialized MoE system) on four MoE models (Phi-3.5-MoE 41.9B, Yuan2.0-M32 40B, DeepSeek-MoE 16.4B, Qwen3-30B-A3B) at token counts of 16–1024 per iteration on Wikitext-2 and C4. Key findings: (a) Latency: FSE-DP achieves 1.22–2.00× speedup over baselines across configurations; gains are largest at low token counts where paired-load dominates, and remain meaningful at high token counts via better D2D utilization. (b) Memory: On-chip memory is compressed to under 32 MB—approximately one-fifth of EP/Hydra's requirement—by eliminating weight replication. (c) Utilization: FSE-DP reaches 66–68% utilization vs. EP's 36–42%, with much lower fluctuation. Hydra shows no consistent advantage over EP in low-batch D2D-rich settings. (d) Scalability: FSE-DP's utilization degrades significantly less than EP from 2×2 to 4×4 arrays due to trajectory-aware point-to-point communication replacing all-to-all collectives. (e) Ablation: paired-load and token buffering (20% slack) each contribute substantially; the optional DDR-priority Rule 5 yields only marginal gains. DSE reveals that ≥16 MB on-chip buffer and ≥48 GB/s DDR per die are needed for >60% utilization under area and power constraints (≤30 mm², ≤60 W). Strengths: - The virtualization abstraction (5 rules) elegantly decouples logical expert trajectories from physical buffer placement and link scheduling, making the fine-grained dataflow manageable in hardware without cycle-accurate global coordination—this is a genuinely novel contribution. - End-to-end silicon validation on a taped-out 5nm MCM chip with cycle-accurate RTL simulation gives the performance claims substantially more credibility than simulation-only work. - The paired-load and flow-fusion mechanisms together address both inter-expert imbalance (hot/cold pairing) and intra-expert computation-communication overlap (DDR+D2D fusion) in a unified framework rather than as disconnected heuristics. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is limited to a 2×2 chiplet array for the tape-out; larger-scale results (3×3, 4×4) rely on behavioral simulation calibrated to RTL, and the accuracy of that calibration is not rigorously validated. - Token buffering increases per-request latency, and the QoS timer mechanism is evaluated only at coarse slackness levels (10/20/30%); the interaction with real latency-sensitive workloads (e.g., interactive chat with strict SLOs) is not analyzed, and the threshold parameters (θ_min, N_threshold) appear to require manual tuning. - The approach requires sufficient D2D bandwidth and fine-grained computation/communication capabilities, limiting applicability to platforms with UCIe-class interconnects; the paper does not quantify how performance degrades on lower-bandwidth D2D links that are more common today.
Search Results 2603.27624 PDF
#19 — Highly Innovative (78)

Self-Improvement of Large Language Models: A Technical Overview and Future Outlook

Haoyan Yang, Mario Xerri, Solha Park, Huajian Zhang, Yiyang Feng, Sai Akhil Kogilathota, Jiawei Zhou · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This survey earns 78 for its genuinely system-level perspective that integrates all pipeline stages — from data acquisition through autonomous evaluation — into a coherent lifecycle framework, revealing inter-stage dependencies that prior surveys missed. The GRO (Generation–Reward–Optimization) unifying framework provides a practical vocabulary that cleanly separates generation strategy, reward type, and optimization algorithm. The honest, substantive treatment of failure modes (data autophagy, reward hacking, alignment faking) with concrete citations elevates this above typical survey work. Coverage of 300+ papers through early 2026 with detailed taxonomies makes it a valuable reference.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: As LLMs approach human-level capabilities, improving them solely through human supervision becomes increasingly costly and fundamentally limited: expert-annotated data is scarce and expensive, and human feedback cannot provide informative gradients once models surpass human competence in a domain. This motivates a paradigm shift toward self-improvement, where models autonomously generate data, evaluate outputs, and iteratively refine their own capabilities without continuous human-in-the-loop supervision. Existing surveys address localized mechanisms (training-time or inference-time) in isolation; this paper instead adopts a system-level perspective, unifying all stages of model development into a coherent closed-loop lifecycle framework. Methodology: The paper introduces a five-component lifecycle self-improvement system: (1) Data Acquisition, where the model autonomously collects or generates training data via static curation (model-guided web crawling), environment interaction (code execution, web browsing, game environments), and synthetic generation (prompt-based, transformation-based, interaction-based self-play); (2) Data Selection, progressing from heuristic static filters to metric-guided scoring (perplexity, loss, gradient, difficulty, uncertainty) to adaptive/learnable selection policies using RL, bandits, or bilevel optimization; (3) Model Optimization, unified under a Generation–Reward–Optimization (GRO) framework encompassing iterative rejection sampling, self-verification and refinement, and self-play paradigms with SFT/RL/hybrid training; (4) Inference Refinement, covering decoding strategies (sampling, tree search, logit adjustment, speculative decoding), reasoning-based improvement (feedback loops, planning, collaborative multi-agent), agentic system-based improvement (prompt optimization, memory, tooling, workflow evolution), and test-time training; and (5) Autonomous Evaluation via dynamic benchmarking and interactive environment evaluation. The paper systematically surveys ~300+ representative papers across all five components and organizes them into detailed taxonomies (Figures 3, 9), with extended discussion of challenges and failure modes including data autophagy, flawed feedback signals, reward hacking, emergent misalignment, and supervision bottlenecks. Key Results: As a survey, the paper synthesizes quantitative findings from constituent works rather than presenting original experiments. Notable results cited include: FineWeb-Edu's model-based quality filtering improving MMLU by 12% and ARC by 24% over unfiltered baselines; DCLM enabling a 7B parameter model to reach 64% 5-shot MMLU accuracy with 40% less compute; Phi-1 achieving 50.6% HumanEval accuracy with only 1.3B parameters via synthetic textbook data; RLEF reducing required coding samples by an order of magnitude on competitive programming; front-loading reasoning-intensive data in pretraining yielding 19% average downstream gains; STaR's iterative rationale bootstrapping; and TTRL surpassing majority-vote accuracy through test-time RL. The paper identifies the GRO (Generation–Reward–Optimization) framework as the unifying lens for model optimization, and notes that self-play methods (AbsoluteZero, R-Zero, SPICE) can achieve improvement without any external data. Across the field, the trend is from model-level optimization toward end-to-end autonomous agentic systems. Strengths: - Genuinely system-level perspective: unlike prior surveys focused on training or inference in isolation, this paper integrates all pipeline stages (data acquisition through autonomous evaluation) into a single coherent lifecycle framework, revealing inter-stage dependencies and emergent failure modes. - Comprehensive and up-to-date: covers 300+ papers through early 2026, with detailed taxonomies and summary tables (Tables 1–14) enabling rapid navigation of a fast-moving field. - Honest treatment of limitations: the challenges section (§7) provides substantive, specific analysis of failure modes—data autophagy, reward hacking, illusory generation-verification gaps, benchmark contamination, alignment faking—with concrete citations rather than generic disclaimers. - The GRO framework provides a practical unifying vocabulary for model optimization that cleanly separates generation strategy, reward type, and optimization algorithm. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is a survey without original empirical contributions; the framework itself is taxonomic rather than falsifiable, making it difficult to assess whether the five-component decomposition is the most useful or complete partition. - The coverage is heavily weighted toward English-language NLP and reasoning/coding tasks; self-improvement in multimodal, multilingual, or embodied settings receives comparatively limited treatment despite being noted as future directions. - The autonomous evaluation section (§6) is notably thinner than the other components, consisting mainly of an enumeration of dynamic benchmarks and interactive environments without deep analysis of what constitutes valid and exploitation-resistant evaluation for self-improving systems—the precise area where the framework is most fragile.
Citations 2603.25681 PDF
#20 — Highly Innovative (78)

Universal YOCO for Efficient Depth Scaling (11 ▲ )

Yutao Sun, Li Dong, Tianzhu Ye, Shaohan Huang, Jianyong Wang, Furu Wei · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 2 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Universal YOCO scores 78 for its elegant architectural insight of confining recursive depth scaling exclusively to efficient sliding-window attention layers, thereby decoupling depth enhancement from KV cache blowup. This is a non-obvious design choice that yields dramatic practical benefits: 38x less KV cache than comparable recursive approaches, 2x token efficiency improvement, and a striking +24.4% absolute gain on math benchmarks after thinking SFT. The thorough evaluation across scaling laws, loop counts, and inference profiling on production hardware strengthens confidence in the contribution's generality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard Transformer LLMs face a fundamental tension between depth scaling and inference efficiency. Applying recursive/looping computation to standard Transformers to increase effective depth at inference time bloats the KV cache linearly with loop count (O(LTND)) and reintroduces quadratic attention cost at every iteration, making test-time depth scaling prohibitively expensive. The prior YOCO architecture ("You Only Cache Once") addressed KV cache memory via a decoder-decoder split, but its self-decoder remained static and non-recursive. This paper asks whether recursive depth enhancement can be combined with YOCO's efficient-attention design to get the benefits of both without the cost of either. Methodology: YOCO-U (Universal YOCO) replaces YOCO's static Self-Decoder with a Universal Self-Decoder that iterates the same L/2-layer block T times via parameter sharing (analogous to Universal Transformers), but critically confines this recursion to the efficient-attention (sliding-window attention) layers only. The Cross-Decoder, which uses full global attention, remains non-recursive and receives a single shared KV cache produced by the final iteration of the Universal Self-Decoder. This architectural choice is the key insight: because the looped layers use O(W) local window attention rather than O(N) global attention, the KV cache overhead from recursion is negligible (O(WTLD) vs. O(ND) for the global cache), and no additional full-attention computation is introduced per loop. The model uses SwiGLU FFNs, RMSNorm, RoPE in the self-decoder and NoPE in the cross-decoder, and MoE (8/64 experts) in the large-scale experiments. Default setting is T=3 loops, yielding 2× FLOPs over the non-recursive baseline. Key Results: In large-scale experiments (10B total / 1.3B activated parameters, MoE, trained 300B tokens on AMD MI300X), YOCO-U outperforms non-recursive YOCO by +4.45 average accuracy across 8 LM-Eval tasks at equal FLOPs (46.23 vs. 41.78), and +5.30 at equal training steps (47.08 vs. 41.78). Token efficiency is dramatically improved: YOCO-U trained on 80B tokens matches YOCO trained on 210B tokens (62% fewer tokens). On 11 math benchmarks after Thinking SFT, YOCO-U achieves 67.1% vs. YOCO's 42.7% average accuracy (+24.4% absolute). In architecture comparisons at 1.3B dense scale, YOCO-U matches RINS (early-layer recursive Transformer) at 48.3 average accuracy, while consuming 38× less KV cache memory than RINS at 256K context (542 MB vs. 20480 MB). Prefilling throughput is 10× higher than standard Transformer and 20× higher than RINS at long contexts. Decoding throughput sacrifices only ~5% vs. non-recursive YOCO, versus RINS which drops to 40% of standard Transformer throughput. Parameter scaling shows YOCO-U achieves comparable loss with 50% fewer parameters than YOCO. Loop scaling from 1 to 5 shows consistent monotonic improvement. Strengths: - The core design insight—confine recursion to efficient-attention shallow blocks so that the global KV cache is generated once and reused—is elegant and directly solves the memory-scaling problem of naive Universal Transformers, yielding near-identical KV footprint to non-recursive YOCO. - Empirical evaluation is thorough: scaling laws across model sizes (300M–10.8B), token counts (up to 300B), loop counts (1–5), ablations on loop position (shallow vs. deep, shared vs. unshared KV), and explicit inference profiling on H100s with production-grade optimizations (Flash-Decoding, Paged Attention, kernel fusion). - The result that recursive computation and explicit test-time scaling (chain-of-thought) are orthogonal and additive is practically significant for deployment. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The claimed FLOP comparison is imprecise: the paper counts training FLOPs as tokens × layers, which ignores that global attention in the Cross-Decoder is quadratic in sequence length—the fair-FLOPs regime matters less at short sequences where most training occurs, and the advantage may be overstated at typical training lengths. - All experiments use variants of YOCO as the baseline; there is no direct comparison against strong recent models (e.g., Llama 3, Gemma, Mistral) trained at comparable compute budgets, making it difficult to assess where YOCO-U sits in the broader landscape rather than just relative to its own ablations. - The angular distance analysis (Figure 8) shows diminishing marginal returns as loop count increases (representations converge toward a fixed point), but the paper does not investigate whether adaptive or learned stopping criteria could improve efficiency further, leaving the fixed T=3 choice somewhat arbitrary.
Recommended 2604.01220 PDF
#21 — Highly Innovative (78)

Heddle: A Distributed Orchestration System for Agentic RL Rollout

Zili Zhang, Yinmin Zhong, Chengxu Yang, Chao Jin, Bingyang Wu, Xinming Wei, Yuliang Liu, Xin Jin · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scores 78 for cleanly decomposing the agentic RL straggler problem — which dominates 80%+ of training time — into three independently tractable sub-problems (scheduling, placement, resource allocation), each solved with principled algorithms including a proven-optimal contiguous DP partition. The trajectory-centric paradigm is a meaningful departure from step-centric frameworks, and the opportunistic overlap of prediction and KV cache migration with tool-call idle time is an elegant zero-overhead engineering insight. The 1.2×–2.5× throughput gains on 64 Hopper GPUs across three task types with clean ablations demonstrate real impact. The progressive predictor's reliance on domain-specific fine-tuning and the restriction to synchronous rollout are reasonable limitations for a first contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Agentic Reinforcement Learning (RL) trains LLMs via iterative cycles of multi-step rollout (trajectory generation with tool calls) and policy optimization. The rollout phase dominates training time — over 80% — due to a severe straggler effect: a long-tailed distribution of trajectory lengths causes most GPU resources to sit idle waiting for a small fraction of complex, multi-step interactions to finish. Existing frameworks (Verl, Slime) are step-centric: they treat each LLM generation call as a stateless, isolated request, discarding trajectory-level context. This leads to three compounding problems for long-tail trajectories: excessive queueing delay from round-robin scheduling, interference overhead from poor placement co-locating long and short trajectories, and inflated per-token time from homogeneous GPU configurations that cannot provide low-latency service to stragglers. Methodology: Heddle introduces a trajectory-centric design organized around a control plane / data plane split. The control plane contains three synergistic modules. (1) Trajectory-level Scheduler: A lightweight Qwen-0.6B regression model is fine-tuned on (context, remaining_length) tuples harvested from historical rollouts. At each agentic step, it updates trajectory length predictions asynchronously during tool-call intervals (zero critical-path overhead). These progressive predictions drive Progressive Priority Scheduling (PPS), an approximation of Longest-Processing-Time-First (LPT): longer-predicted trajectories get higher priority and can preempt lower-priority active requests, with KV cache persisted on eviction. (2) Trajectory-aware Placement: The paper proves (Lemma 5.1) that an optimal partition of sorted trajectories into workers must be contiguous, reducing the search from Stirling numbers S(n,m) to C(n-1,m-1), then solves it with O(n²m) dynamic programming. An interference factor F(g) is derived from a profiler-simulator that characterizes per-token time vs. batch size. At runtime, a Rust-based trajectory router executes opportunistic KV cache migration via GPU-Direct RDMA during tool-call idle windows to correct initial placement errors as prediction accuracy improves. (3) Trajectory-adaptive Resource Manager: Rather than uniform model parallelism (MP), Heddle assigns higher MP degrees to long-tail trajectories (lower per-token time) and lower MP to short trajectories (higher throughput). It uses sort-initialized simulated annealing to efficiently search the combinatorial GPU allocation space, with each candidate evaluated via the presorted DP algorithm (~42ms for n=6400, m=16). Key Results: Evaluated on a 64-GPU cluster (8×8 NVIDIA Hopper) with Qwen3 8B/14B/32B across coding (CodeForces), search (HotpotQA), and math (DAPO-Math) agentic RL workloads. Heddle achieves 1.2×–2.5× higher end-to-end rollout throughput vs. Slime, 1.4×–2.3× vs. Verl, and 1.1×–2.4× vs. Verl*. Gains scale with model size, as larger models suffer more from memory/compute contention. Ablation results show each component contributes independently: scheduling reduces rollout time by 1.1×–1.26×, placement improves throughput 1.2×–1.5×, and resource management adds 1.1×–1.3× speedup. The progressive predictor substantially outperforms static model-based and history-based baselines on tail-20% and tail-10% recall. System overheads (prediction ~0.1–0.28s, migration ~0.12–0.35s) are effectively hidden behind tool execution times (0.05–1.43s), and control-plane DP runs in ~37ms. Strengths: - Clean theoretical decomposition of the makespan problem into three independent, tractable sub-problems (scheduling, placement, resource), each attacked with a principled algorithm (LPT approximation, DP with proven optimality under contiguity, simulated annealing) - The opportunistic overlap of prediction and migration with tool-call idle time is an elegant engineering insight that eliminates overhead on the critical path without requiring architectural changes to the inference engine - Comprehensive evaluation across three task types, three model scales, and multiple ablation axes on real hardware (64 Hopper GPUs), with clear quantitative separation of each component's contribution Weaknesses / Limitations: - The placement DP assumes a monotonically increasing interference function determined solely by group size — a simplification that may not hold for workloads with heterogeneous prompt lengths, context sizes, or attention patterns, potentially degrading placement quality - The trajectory length predictor requires domain-specific fine-tuning data from historical rollouts, adding a bootstrapping cost for new task types and potentially performing poorly early in training when trajectory distributions are still shifting with the policy - The system is evaluated only in the synchronous rollout setting; the interaction with asynchronous RL (e.g., partial rollout with staleness bounds) and PD-disaggregated inference backends is discussed qualitatively but not empirically validated
Search Results 2603.28101 PDF
#22 — Highly Innovative (78)

Understanding and Mitigating Hallucinations in Multimodal Chain-of-Thought Models

Ji Ma, Wei Suo, Peng Wang, Yanning Zhang · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: This paper scores 78 for identifying a genuinely novel failure mode unique to multimodal chain-of-thought models — that divergent thinking steps cause ~5× more hallucinations than grounded steps — backed by multiple converging analyses. The visual entropy metric is mechanistically elegant, training-free, and computationally negligible, yet uniquely resolves the hallucination-reasoning tradeoff that plagues prior methods. The finding that a single set of hyperparameters transfers across three architecturally distinct models without tuning is surprising and practically significant.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Chain-of-Thought (MCoT) models—which generate explicit reasoning chains before answering visual questions—suffer from severe hallucinations where textual outputs contradict visual evidence. Prior work attributed this to visual attention decay during long reasoning chains, a phenomenon already well-studied in traditional LVLMs. This paper asks whether MCoT models have a distinct, previously unidentified cause of hallucinations unique to their explicit reasoning paradigm. Answering this is important because MCoT models (e.g., R1-Onevision, PixelReasoner) are increasingly deployed for complex visual reasoning, and existing hallucination mitigations designed for standard LVLMs may be poorly matched to the MCoT failure mode. Methodology: The paper conducts a systematic empirical investigation of hallucination patterns across the thinking and answering stages of MCoT models. Two key findings emerge: (1) hallucinations in answers are strongly correlated (ρ > 0.96) with hallucinations in the reasoning chain, and answer tokens attend almost exclusively to thinking tokens (<4% attention weight on image tokens), establishing that the reasoning chain propagates fabricated content to answers via self-attention. (2) Fine-grained analysis—using GPT-5 to label reasoning segments as "divergent" (associative, inference-driven) vs. "normal" (grounded, observational)—reveals that divergent thinking steps produce ~5× more hallucinations than normal steps. To exploit this finding without requiring labeled divergent steps at inference time, the paper introduces visual entropy: for each generated token y_t, it computes the entropy of the probability distribution over visual tokens' contributions to generating y_t (using the language head applied to visual hidden states). High visual entropy indicates the model is uncertain about visual content—the signature of divergent thinking. Visual entropy reliably classifies divergent vs. normal steps (McFadden's pseudo-R² > 0.9). The mitigation applies an exponential penalty to the standard decoding distribution during high-entropy steps: p̂_t = p_t · exp(−α · E(·,v)), where α ∈ [0,1] is the intervention degree and a threshold γ gates activation. Visual token probabilities are pre-computed in the prefill stage, so runtime overhead is negligible. Key Results: Evaluated on three MCoT backbones (GRIT-3B, PixelReasoner-7B, R1-Onevision-7B) against hallucination benchmarks (Object HalBench, POPE, MMhalBench) and general benchmarks (MathVista, MMStar, VSR). On Object HalBench, the method reduces CHAIR_S by >3 points over prior SOTA (FlexAC) for GRIT-3B (16.0 vs. 19.2) and achieves a 47.6% relative improvement in CHAIR_I over the backbone for R1-Onevision-7B. On PixelReasoner-7B, CHAIR_S drops from 22.0 (baseline) to 15.4 (ours) vs. 18.8 for FlexAC. On POPE adversarial, accuracy improves by 2.0 points over the backbone. Unlike DoLa and VCD which hurt math reasoning (VCD degrades MathVista by 0.7 points), this method improves reasoning: +1.4 MathVista and +1.9 VSR for PixelReasoner-7B. The method is compatible with all tested decoding strategies (greedy, nucleus, beam search) and composable with DoLa/VCD, yielding further additive gains. Hyperparameters γ=0.5 and α=0.75 transfer across all three model architectures without tuning. The method is 1.2× faster than FlexAC at inference time. Strengths: - The core diagnostic finding—that divergent thinking causes ~5× more hallucinations and is the unique MCoT failure mode—is backed by multiple converging analyses (co-occurrence statistics, attention visualization, fine-grained segment labeling) and is genuinely novel compared to prior visual-attention-decay explanations. - The proposed visual entropy metric is mechanistically motivated, training-free, and computationally cheap (pre-computed at prefill), making it practical to deploy on top of any MCoT model. - The method uniquely improves both hallucination and reasoning simultaneously, addressing a well-known tension in existing mitigation methods and making it broadly applicable. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The divergent/normal segment labeling used to validate visual entropy relies on GPT-5 annotations with human verification—a semi-automated process that may not generalize reliably across domains or reasoning styles, and whose label quality is not formally audited. - Experiments are restricted to 7B-scale models. Whether visual entropy remains a reliable indicator of divergent thinking in larger models (e.g., 70B+ or proprietary MCoT systems) is untested. - The threshold γ and intervention degree α are shown to be model-agnostic empirically across three models, but the underlying reason for this transferability is not theoretically explained, leaving open whether it holds for architecturally dissimilar future models.
Search Results 2603.27201 PDF
#23 — Highly Innovative (78)

VGGRPO: Towards World-Consistent Video Generation with 4D Latent Reward (44 ▲ )

Zhaochong An, Orest Kupyn, Théo Uscidda, Andrea Colaco, Karan Ahuja, Serge Belongie, Mar Gonzalez-Franco, Marta Tintore Gazulla · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 1 Apr 2026
Why this rank: VGGRPO scores 78 for the elegant insight of stitching a geometry foundation model directly to diffusion latents via a learned connector, bypassing the expensive and fragile VAE decoding that bottlenecked all prior geometry-aware alignment methods. It is the first such method to handle both static and dynamic scenes through 4D reconstruction, meaningfully extending scope beyond prior work. The finding that geometric alignment actually improves general perceptual quality rather than trading off against it is surprising and suggests geometry rewards act as beneficial regularizers. Strong results across two model scales and multiple benchmarks reinforce the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large-scale video diffusion models trained on internet-scale data achieve impressive visual quality but routinely fail to maintain 3D geometric consistency — exhibiting unstable camera trajectories, geometric drift, and cross-frame structural incoherence. This undermines downstream applications such as embodied AI and physics-aware simulation. Prior approaches fall into two camps: (1) architecture-level modifications (e.g., point cloud conditioning, auxiliary geometry decoders) that add complexity and compromise pretrained generalization, and (2) post-training alignment methods (e.g., Epipolar-DPO, VideoGPA) that compute geometry rewards in pixel/RGB space, requiring repeated expensive VAE decoding, and are restricted to static scenes because their epipolar or correspondence-based formulations do not extend to dynamic content. Methodology: VGGRPO (Visual Geometry GRPO) is a latent-space, geometry-guided post-training framework built on two coupled components. The first is a Latent Geometry Model (LGM): a pretrained geometry foundation model (specifically Any4D, which supports dynamic 4D reconstruction) is stitched to a video VAE encoder via a lightweight 3D convolutional connector trained to minimize feature alignment error between VAE latents and the intermediate representations of the geometry model. This allows the LGM to predict camera poses, depth maps, 3D point maps, and scene flow directly from diffusion latents without RGB decoding. The connector is found by optimizing a layer-selection objective over a calibration set, then fine-tuned end-to-end with a multi-modal alignment loss. The second component is GRPO-based policy optimization operating entirely in latent space. Two complementary rewards are defined from LGM outputs: (i) a camera motion smoothness reward measuring scale-normalized translational and rotational acceleration (penalizing jittery trajectories), and (ii) a geometry reprojection consistency reward that builds a scene point cloud, reprojects it into each view, and compares rendered depths to predicted depths — scoring the worst 3 views to focus on local failure cases. For each text prompt, K=64 video latents are sampled, scored, normalized within the group to form advantages, and used to update the video diffusion model (Wan2.1-1B or Wan2.2-5B) via LoRA with the standard GRPO clipped surrogate objective and KL regularization toward the reference policy. The framework further supports training-free test-time reward guidance by backpropagating geometry reward gradients through the differentiable LGM to adjust denoising velocity fields. Key Results: On a held-out benchmark of 190 static-scene captions (DL3DV + RealEstate10K) and 200 dynamic-scene captions (MiraData), VGGRPO substantially outperforms all baselines. On Wan2.2-5B, VGGRPO achieves VideoReward win rates of 62.63%/68.42% (VQ/MQ) on static and 56.50%/66.00% on dynamic scenes, compared to the best prior method VideoGPA at 54.74%/60.53% static and 40.00%/54.00% dynamic. Sampson epipolar error drops from 0.142 (base) to 0.093 (VGGRPO), versus 0.098 for VideoGPA. The dynamic scene gains are especially pronounced — prior methods trained only for static scenes degrade significantly on complex non-rigid motion while VGGRPO maintains robustness. On general VBench captions, VGGRPO improves subject consistency (0.9644 vs 0.9542 baseline), background consistency, aesthetic quality, imaging quality (0.6861 vs 0.6733), and motion smoothness, while all prior post-training methods actually degrade imaging quality below baseline. Latent-space reward computation reduces peak GPU memory from 76.80 GB to 68.57 GB and wall-clock reward time from 54.73s to 41.33s (24.5% faster). Ablations confirm both reward terms are necessary and complementary: motion reward alone stabilizes trajectory but leaves geometric artifacts; adding the reprojection reward resolves these. The LGM also shows substantially greater robustness to latent-space perturbations than the RGB-based geometry model, demonstrating better domain adaptation to generated content. Strengths: - Elegant architectural insight: replacing VAE decoding with a learned stitching connector that bridges diffusion latents directly to geometry foundation models, eliminating the main computational and robustness bottleneck of prior RGB-based reward methods. - First geometry-aware alignment method that handles both static and dynamic scenes via 4D reconstruction capability (Any4D), meaningfully extending the applicable scope beyond all prior work. - Strong empirical results across both geometry-specific and general video quality metrics, with the unusual property that geometric alignment actually improves (rather than degrades) general perceptual quality — suggesting the geometry rewards function as beneficial regularizers. - Model-agnostic design: the LGM can be instantiated with any geometry foundation model, and the framework is validated on two different video diffusion backbones at different scales. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The LGM requires a separate training phase (20 epochs, calibration set, alignment loss) before VGGRPO training can begin, and the full training pipeline requires ~1536 GPU hours — significant infrastructure overhead not discussed in terms of accessibility. - The geometry reprojection reward uses the LGM's own predicted depth for comparison, creating a circular dependency: the reward signal is only as reliable as the LGM's geometry estimates, and errors in LGM predictions on highly dynamic or out-of-distribution scenes could corrupt the reward signal rather than improve it. - Dynamic Degree on VBench slightly decreases (0.3962 vs 0.4237 baseline), and while the authors attribute this to jitter suppression, it reflects a genuine trade-off between geometric stability and perceptual dynamism that is not fully resolved. - Evaluation benchmarks are derived from the same data sources (DL3DV, RealEstate10K, MiraData) used for training prompts, raising potential concerns about distribution overlap and whether generalization to truly out-of-distribution scenes and camera motion types is as strong as claimed.

#24 — Highly Innovative (78)

Lingshu-Cell: A generative cellular world model for transcriptome modeling toward virtual cells (71 ▲ )

Han Zhang, Guo-Hua Yuan, Chaohao Yuan, Tingyang Xu, Tian Bian, Hong Cheng, Wenbing Huang, Deli Zhao, Yu Rong · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 1 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Lingshu-Cell introduces a principled paradigm shift by applying masked discrete diffusion directly to quantized UMI count tokens, correctly matching the generative framework to the sparse, discrete nature of single-cell transcriptomic data — a mismatch that prior continuous diffusion and autoregressive models ignored. The unified architecture handles both unconditional generation and mechanistically distinct perturbation types (CRISPR knockouts, cytokine stimulations) through lightweight conditioning, achieving first place on the competitive Virtual Cell Challenge leaderboard. Cross-species and cross-tissue generalization across 9 tissues and 5 species strengthens the case for genuine biological generality rather than dataset-specific overfitting.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) has produced large-scale atlases of cellular states, but most computational tools built on these atlases are descriptive—they classify and annotate cells rather than simulate them. Existing foundation models (scGPT, Geneformer, CellFM) learn static representations but cannot generatively sample new cellular states or predict how cells respond to unseen perturbations. Generative methods such as scDiffusion and scVI assume continuous data distributions that are poorly matched to the sparse, discrete, zero-inflated nature of raw UMI count data. Perturbation-focused models (STATE, CellFlow, AlphaCell) learn direct control-to-perturbed mappings but do not model the underlying state distribution. The field therefore lacks a unified cellular world model—one that both accurately captures the distribution of transcriptomic states and supports conditional simulation under arbitrary perturbations. Methodology: Lingshu-Cell is a masked discrete diffusion model (MDDM) that operates directly on quantized UMI count tokens. Each gene's expression level is mapped to a discrete vocabulary of ~281 tokens via a custom quantization scheme that preserves approximately two significant digits across the dynamic range of counts. The full ~18,000-gene profile of each cell is treated as an orderless discrete sequence—no gene selection or high-variability filtering is required. In the forward process each token is independently masked with probability t; a Transformer-based mask predictor trained with cross-entropy loss iteratively recovers all masked positions simultaneously in the reverse process, avoiding the left-to-right generation order bias of autoregressive models and the continuous-noise mismatch of DDPMs. Three key enhancements support conditional perturbation prediction: (1) Classifier-free guidance (CFG) steers sampling toward the perturbed manifold by jointly training conditional and unconditional models; (2) Embedding-space sequence compression groups adjacent gene embeddings and projects them through a learned linear map before the Transformer, reducing sequence length by a configurable patch size (PS=32 optimal) and improving capture of global expression patterns; (3) Biological prior injection identifies perturbation-responsive genes from external cell lines and preferentially initializes masked positions with these prior gene tokens at generation time. Conditioning information (cell type or donor identity plus perturbation target) is prepended as additional tokens, enabling zero-shot generalization to unseen identity-perturbation combinations. Key Results: For unconditional generation, Lingshu-Cell was benchmarked on PBMC data and across 8 human tissues and 4 non-human species (mouse, rhesus macaque, zebrafish, fly). It achieved Pearson correlations ≥0.9966 and Spearman ≥0.9929 on all tissues/species. On distributional metrics it outperformed scDiffusion and scVI: MMD of 0.0088 vs. 0.0178 (scDiffusion) and 0.0343 (scVI) on PBMCs, with consistently superior iLISI and 1-Wasserstein distance. For genetic perturbation prediction on the Virtual Cell Challenge (VCC) H1 benchmark (150 training / 100 test targets, 323K training cells from external lines), Lingshu-Cell ranked first by average rank (8.7) across 7 metrics among 25 teams, achieving the lowest MAE (0.052) and highest Pearson-Δ (0.306) despite using only ~600K training cells. For cytokine perturbation in PBMC donors (90 cytokine conditions; 4 held-out donors, 70% of conditions held out per donor), Lingshu-Cell ranked first on average score and on PDS, Pearson-Δ, Spearman #DEG, DES, Spearman LFC, and AUPRC metrics, outperforming STATE, scGPT, scVI, and PerturbMean. Strengths: - Principled alignment of the generative paradigm with the biological data modality: discrete, orderless masked diffusion is a better match for sparse UMI count data than continuous diffusion or autoregressive models, eliminating the need for prior gene selection. - Unified architecture for both unconditional simulation and multiple conditional perturbation modalities (genetic CRISPR knockouts and cytokine stimulations) via lightweight conditioning token prepending, demonstrating genuine generalization across mechanistically distinct perturbation types. - Strong empirical validation at scale across 9 tissues and 5 species in the unconditional setting, plus leading results on a competitive public benchmark (VCC leaderboard) and an independent cytokine dataset, providing multi-faceted evidence of generalizability. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation relies on population-level distributional metrics (MMD, iLISI, pseudobulk correlations) that cannot assess single-cell biological plausibility or preservation of extremely rare cell states; high statistical fidelity does not imply causal biological validity, and predictions require wet-lab validation. - The model is trained and evaluated on transcriptomic data only; it does not integrate epigenomic, proteomic, metabolomic, or spatial modalities, and lacks temporal dynamics, which are necessary for a truly comprehensive virtual cell capable of simulating differentiation trajectories or disease progression.
