#25 — Highly Innovative (77)

Incremental GNN Embedding Computation on Streaming Graphs

Qiange Wang, Haoran Lv, Yanfeng Zhang, Weng-Fai Wong, Bingsheng He · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The operator decomposition into ms_local/nbr_ctx/msg_cbn/aggregate is a genuinely novel theoretical contribution that extends provably correct incremental computation to GCN, GAT, and other models previously beyond reach of incremental frameworks. The formal equivalence theorem with four sufficient conditions provides a principled foundation rather than ad hoc engineering. Achieving 37×–146× speedups on billion-scale graphs while maintaining MSE < 10⁻⁴ versus full recomputation validates the approach at scale, though the 2.3×–8.2× memory overhead and catastrophic degradation under cache eviction reveal a hard tradeoff that limits practical deployment flexibility.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Graph Neural Networks deployed on streaming graphs must continuously update node embeddings as the graph evolves, which is critical for applications like real-time recommendation and fraud detection. The dominant approach—Runtime Embedding Computation (RTEC)—recomputes embeddings for affected vertices using their full L-hop neighborhoods. This triggers the neighbor explosion problem: even 0.1% edge updates can require up to 80% of full-graph recomputation cost, with 65%–95% of that work spent on unaffected subgraphs whose intermediate results remain valid. Existing non-incremental alternatives either sacrifice accuracy (neighborhood sampling) or still process far more edges than necessary (unaffected embedding reuse). Prior incremental approaches are limited to simple GNN models with constant edge messages and associative aggregation, failing on widely used models like GCN and GAT. Methodology: The paper proposes a unified incremental RTEC framework built on a fine-grained operator decomposition of GNN computation into four components: (1) ms_local()—edge-wise message computation independent of neighborhood context; (2) nbr_ctx()—aggregation of a neighborhood-level scalar context (e.g., degree normalization in GCN, attention sum in GAT); (3) msg_cbn()—combination of context with local messages; and (4) standard aggregate/update operations. By decoupling nbr_ctx from message computation, the aggregation can be made associative, enabling partial incremental updates. The reordered workflow (Algorithm 1) reuses cached aggregation state from unaffected neighbors, applies delta updates only over the affected subgraph, and uses the inverse msg_cbn⁻¹ to strip outdated context before reapplying the new one. Theorem 1 formally establishes four sufficient conditions (associativity of nbr_ctx and aggregate, distributivity of msg_cbn over aggregate, and monotonicity for the inverse) under which incremental results are provably equivalent to full recomputation. Models like GAT that involve destination embeddings in edge messages require a constrained mode with full-neighborhood recomputation for destination-affected vertices only. The framework is implemented as NeutronRT, a CPU–GPU co-processing system built on DGL. It uses a PMA-based dynamic CSR for graph storage, GPU-directed zero-copy memory access for sparse embedding I/O, chunked task scheduling with inter-chunk embedding reuse, and caches only aggregate embeddings (recomputing h^l on the fly) to reduce CPU memory by 19–30%. Key Results: On small graphs (ogbn-arxiv, ogbn-products, Reddit) with 6 GNN models, NeutronRT (NrtInc) achieves 1.7×–19.8× speedup over RTEC-Full and 1.7×–9.2× over RTEC-UER while matching full-RTEC accuracy (MSE < 10⁻⁴). On large billion-scale graphs (Twitter 1.5B edges, ogbn-papers 1.6B, Friendster 2.5B), NrtInc achieves 37.8×–145.8× speedup over RTEC-Full and 7.3×–26.1× over RTEC-NS10 for GCN, reducing per-batch processing from 107–324 seconds to 2.2–2.9 seconds. Computation graph construction overhead drops 90%–98% and overall computation by 95%–99%. Vertex accesses are reduced 3.6×–10.8× and edge accesses 4.9×–168.5× on billion-scale graphs. Accuracy: NrtInc improves 1.18% over periodic recomputation (MTEC-Period) and stays within 0.15% of the theoretically optimal solution, while NS5 can actually underperform periodic recomputation. NeutronRT outperforms the prior CPU–GPU incremental system InkStream by 5.3×–7.7× on large graphs, and both outperform CPU-based Ripple by 7.8×–12.3×. The speedup peaks at 285× when |ΔE| = 1K edges and degrades gracefully to 3.8× at |ΔE| = 100M. Strengths: - The operator decomposition is genuinely novel and principled: it extends incremental processing to GNN models (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE-mean, RGAT) that prior frameworks could not handle correctly, with a formal equivalence proof. - Experimental coverage is thorough—6 GNN models, 6 datasets spanning 3 orders of magnitude in scale (0.17M to 111M vertices, up to 2.5B edges), multiple baselines, and sensitivity analyses across batch sizes, latency targets, layer counts, and query sizes. - The CPU–GPU co-design is pragmatic: zero-copy PCIe access, chunked scheduling with shard reuse, and recomputation-based memory optimization together make billion-scale graphs tractable on a single A6000 GPU. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The memory overhead for incremental processing is substantial—2.3×–8.2× more CPU memory than RTEC-Full for large graphs—and the system degrades catastrophically (order-of-magnitude slowdown) when even 10% of cached embeddings must be evicted, revealing a hard space-time tradeoff with no graceful fallback. - The framework is restricted to edge insertion/deletion workloads; vertex feature updates trigger full destination-vertex recomputation for constrained models (GAT, AGNN), and the paper does not analyze scenarios with heavy feature churn common in real streaming applications. - Comparison with InkStream and Ripple is limited to GraphSAGE because those systems do not support GCN/GAT; this cherry-picks the simplest model for the head-to-head comparison and understates NeutronRT's advantage on complex models.
Search Results 2603.20622 PDF
#26 — Highly Innovative (77)

Measuring the Unmeasurable: Unveiling Latent Cognitive Capabilities of LLM

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 77 for its principled grounding in ACT-R cognitive architecture, which provides a theoretically motivated decomposition of LLM reasoning into 16 measurable micro-operations — a meaningful advance over ad hoc benchmark taxonomies. The creative adaptation of established cognitive psychology paradigms (Wason 2-4-6 task, semantic network graph metrics) as LLM evaluation tools is genuinely novel. The multilingual coverage and rigorous human validation further strengthen the contribution, though the narrow model pool limits generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks evaluate models at coarse granularity using static, single-task datasets that measure only final outcomes, ignoring intermediate reasoning steps. This leads to redundant overlap across benchmarks, poor generalization to real-world multi-step tasks, and an inability to diagnose where models actually fail. As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes domains (healthcare, law, education), there is a pressing need for fine-grained cognitive profiling that identifies specific reasoning strengths and weaknesses rather than aggregate accuracy scores. Methodology: The authors propose CogProbe, a hierarchical cognitive evaluation framework grounded in ACT-R (Adaptive Control of Thought-Rational) cognitive architecture from psychology. The taxonomy comprises 5 macro cognitive capabilities (Goal Setting, Declarative Knowledge Retrieval, Procedural Rule Execution, Association and Analogy, Metacognitive Monitoring) decomposed into 16 micro cognitive operations (e.g., Task Identification, Factual Recall, Logical Deduction, Inductive Reasoning, Self-checking). The accompanying CogEval dataset contains 30,330 instances across English, Chinese, and Spanish (10,110 per language), constructed via a three-tier strategy: Reuse (existing datasets directly mapping to target operations), Reform (prompt redesign to activate specific cognitive operations), and Re-create (novel datasets synthesized from cognitive psychology paradigms, e.g., adapting the Wason 2-4-6 rule-discovery task for inductive reasoning, and semantic network methods for associative thinking). Metacognitive operations are measured by prompting models to reflect after initial responses and computing calibration, accuracy change, and error correction rate. Evaluation uses operation-specific metrics: accuracy/F1 for discrete tasks, graph-theoretic metrics (efficiency, clustering coefficient, small-world index, modularity) for associative thinking, and average query count for inductive reasoning efficiency. Four PhD-level cognitive science experts annotated samples for cognitive alignment, achieving Fleiss' κ = 0.79. Key Results: Experiments on Llama3-8B, Qwen3-32B, Qwen3-Plus, and GPT-4.1 reveal five key findings. (1) All models excel at memory tasks (Factual Recall ≥ 0.65, Episodic Memory ≥ 0.83) but fail severely on higher-order operations — Goal Decomposition accuracy is 0 across all models, Rule Execution ranges 0.003–0.40, and Inductive Reasoning accuracy is 0 for Llama3-8B and GPT-4.1. (2) LLM difficulty mirrors Bloom's Taxonomy hierarchy, with performance degrading monotonically from knowledge/comprehension tasks to synthesis and metacognition. (3) Enabling "think mode" (explicit chain-of-thought) in Qwen3 models yields large but unstable gains — Rule Execution improves from 0.16 to 0.40 for Qwen3-32B, but post-reflection accuracy drops by −0.27 on the same task, suggesting the model shifts from genuine reasoning to superficial pattern matching. (4) Metacognitive capability differentiates genuine understanding from surface-level accuracy; GPT-4.1 is the only model that consistently maintains or improves performance after reflection. (5) Cross-linguistic performance differences primarily reflect training-data exposure rather than linguistic structure, except for associative thinking where Chinese logographic characters produce a "small-world" semantic network structure (E≈0.39, C≈0.52, Q=0.34) closely matching human high-creativity patterns, unlike English (fast but shallow) or Spanish (locally rich but globally fragmented). Strengths: - Strong theoretical grounding in ACT-R cognitive architecture provides principled, non-arbitrary decomposition of reasoning into orthogonal, measurable components — a significant advance over empirically-driven benchmark taxonomies like CogBench. - Novel evaluation paradigms for difficult-to-measure operations: the Wason rule-discovery adaptation for inductive reasoning and semantic network graph metrics for associative thinking are creative and well-validated extensions of established cognitive psychology methodology. - Multilingual coverage (English, Chinese, Spanish) with rigorous human validation (Adequacy=4.83, Fluency=4.79, α=0.81) and controlled isolation of cognitive operations via expert annotation (κ=0.76–0.79) demonstrates methodological care. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The model pool is narrow (4 models, only one non-Chinese vendor) and lacks frontier models like Claude, Gemini, or GPT-4o, limiting generalizability of the capability profiles and making it unclear whether findings about metacognitive deficits are universal. - Goal Decomposition accuracy is 0 for all evaluated models, which raises the question of whether the task design or evaluation criteria are appropriate — a ceiling-floor measurement artifact cannot be ruled out without ablation studies on prompt formulations. - The multilingual translation pipeline relies on Qwen-MT-Max rather than native speakers for generation, and while human validation was applied, culturally-embedded tasks (especially metacognitive and associative) may have non-trivial translation artifacts that the adequacy/fluency scores do not fully capture.

#27 — Highly Innovative (76)

Towards a Medical AI Scientist (64 ▲ )

Hongtao Wu, Boyun Zheng, Dingjie Song, Yu Jiang, Jianfeng Gao, Lei Xing, Lichao Sun, Yixuan Yuan · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 31 Mar 2026
Why this rank: This paper makes a meaningful leap by purpose-building an autonomous AI research framework for clinical medicine, addressing domain-specific constraints (clinical evidence grounding, heterogeneous medical data, ethical compliance) that generic AI Scientist systems ignore entirely. The clinician-engineer co-reasoning mechanism is a genuine architectural contribution that produces traceable hypothesis generation grounded in both medical literature and engineering codebases. The 91-93% execution success rate substantially outperforms frontier models, and having a generated manuscript accepted at a peer-reviewed venue is a concrete validation. However, it remains fundamentally a domain adaptation of the AI Scientist paradigm rather than a wholly new concept, and the gap to SOTA performance on actual tasks tempers the impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing "AI Scientist" frameworks (e.g., AI Scientist, AI Scientist-v2, AI-Researcher) automate scientific discovery but are domain-agnostic, making them poorly suited for clinical medicine. Medical AI research imposes three distinct challenges absent in general ML: (1) hypotheses must be grounded in clinical evidence and disease-specific priors, not just abstract optimization; (2) heterogeneous, high-dimensional clinical data (3D volumes, endoscopic video, EHR, ECG, multimodal) requires specialized preprocessing and evaluation; (3) medical publications must comply with explicit ethical standards around data provenance and patient privacy. No prior framework simultaneously addresses all three. The paper fills this gap with Medical AI Scientist, the first autonomous research framework purpose-built for clinical AI. Methodology: The system is a multi-agent pipeline comprising three core components. The Idea Proposer transforms loosely specified clinical tasks into executable research hypotheses via a clinician–engineer co-reasoning mechanism: an Analyzer formalizes the clinical challenge using structured literature retrieval (via OpenAlex), an Explorer identifies candidate computational paradigms by searching recent papers and open-source codebases, a Preparer/Surveyor builds an evidence base linking claims to executable implementations, a Generator produces hypotheses by bidirectional reasoning between clinical priors and engineering design, and an Assessor enforces scientific and ethical quality gates. The Experimental Executor runs inside a Dockerized sandbox, using domain-specific medical toolboxes (MONAI, SimpleITK) alongside general-purpose execution infrastructure, with an iterative Judger–Executor loop for self-correcting code debugging. The Manuscript Composer generates LaTeX manuscripts with a Scientific Narrative Enhancer to counter procedural over-description, an Ethics Reviewer to document dataset provenance, a Cross-Reference Resolver, and a self-healing LaTeX compilation engine. The system operates in three modes: Paper-based Reproduction, Literature-inspired Innovation, and Task-driven Exploration. Evaluation is conducted on Med-AI Bench, a new benchmark of 171 cases spanning 19 clinical tasks and 6 data modalities (medical images, video, EHR, physiological signals, text, multimodal), with difficulty stratified across three tiers. Key Results: On idea generation over 171 cases, Medical AI Scientist outperforms GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5-Pro across all six evaluation dimensions under both LLM-as-judge and blinded human assessment. In human expert evaluation, it achieves novelty scores of 4.40±0.49 (Innovation) and 4.32±0.47 (Exploration), versus GPT-5 below 3.50 and Gemini below 3.20. On experimental execution over 57 instances, success rates reach 0.91 (Reproduction), 0.93 (Literature Innovation), and 0.86 (Exploration), compared to GPT-5 at 0.72/0.60/0.75 and Gemini-2.5-Pro at 0.40/0.49/0.53. Implementation completeness (algorithm fidelity + pipeline integrity) peaks at 3.84±0.49 and 4.30±0.62 in Reproduction mode. On manuscript quality, a double-blind study with 10 domain experts comparing AI-generated papers to MICCAI/ISBI/BIBM papers gives a Stanford Agentic Reviewer score of 4.60±0.56, falling between ISBI (3.74±1.02) and MICCAI (4.86±0.47). Human experts rate the generated manuscripts highest in novelty and among the best in writing quality, with a modest gap only in coverage (3.44 vs. 3.68 for MICCAI). One system-generated manuscript was accepted at ICAIS 2025 (36.8% acceptance rate). Strengths: - The clinician–engineer co-reasoning mechanism is a genuine technical contribution: it produces traceable evidence links (medical literature + engineering codebases) that reduce hallucination and produce hypotheses grounded in verifiable disease priors, something absent in all prior AI Scientist systems. - The Med-AI Bench benchmark is a substantial community contribution — 171 stratified cases across 19 tasks and 6 modalities, with multi-dimensional difficulty scoring — providing a sorely needed evaluation standard for automated medical research systems. - Execution reliability is notably high (91–93% success) relative to GPT-5 (60–75%) and Gemini (40–53%), directly attributable to the Dockerized sandbox, domain-specific toolboxes, and iterative self-correction loop — a practically important result for real deployment. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The manuscript quality evaluation is restricted to a single task (diabetic retinopathy classification on APTOS 2019), making it unclear whether the quality claims generalize across the 19 benchmark tasks; the breadth of Med-AI Bench is not matched by breadth in the end-to-end manuscript evaluation. - The generated methods do not reach state-of-the-art performance on any task — the system acknowledges this directly, noting that overly intricate generated designs often degrade upon implementation, producing functional but sub-optimal pipelines. The gap to SOTA is unquantified across tasks. - Evaluation relies heavily on LLM-as-judge metrics (using GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5-Pro as both competitors and evaluators), which introduces potential circular bias; the human expert pool of 10 reviewers for a single task limits the statistical power and generalizability of the manuscript quality findings.
Recommended 2603.28589 PDF
#28 — Highly Innovative (76)

SKILL0: In-Context Agentic Reinforcement Learning for Skill Internalization (72 ▲ )

Zhengxi Lu, Zhiyuan Yao, Jinyang Wu, Chengcheng Han, Qi Gu, Xunliang Cai, Weiming Lu, Jun Xiao, Yueting Zhuang, Yongliang Shen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 3 Apr 2026
Why this rank: SKILL0 introduces a well-motivated and cleanly executed paradigm for transferring competence from context to parameters via progressive skill withdrawal during training. The helpfulness-driven adaptive curriculum is genuinely novel — retiring skills based on measured policy benefit rather than a fixed schedule is a principled design that avoids both premature removal and persistent over-reliance. The 5x+ token efficiency reduction at comparable or better accuracy has clear practical significance for multi-turn agentic deployment. The empirical rise-then-fall helpfulness curve provides strong evidence for the transient scaffolding hypothesis, making this a convincing proof of concept for skill internalization as an alternative to inference-time augmentation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM agents increasingly rely on inference-time skill augmentation — retrieving structured behavioral knowledge from a skill bank and injecting it into the context at each step. While effective, this paradigm has three fundamental limits: retrieval noise corrupts the agent's context with irrelevant guidance; injected skill content imposes compounding token overhead across multi-turn interactions; and, most critically, the model never actually acquires the skills it merely reads — competence resides in the context, not in the parameters. The paper asks whether skills can instead be internalized into model weights, enabling zero-shot autonomous behavior without any runtime retrieval. Methodology: SKILL0 introduces In-Context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL), a training regime that begins with full skill context and progressively withdraws it via a Dynamic Curriculum, until the agent operates in a completely skill-free setting at inference. Skills are organized offline into a hierarchical SkillBank structured as Markdown files per task/category. During training, interaction history and retrieved skill files are rendered together into a compact RGB image (visual context), which is encoded by a vision encoder into embeddings — avoiding raw token injection. The compression ratio is itself a learned output: the policy jointly predicts the next action and the compression factor at each step, with a composite reward that incentivizes both task success and efficient compression (log-scale compression bonus only on successful trajectories). The Dynamic Curriculum operates in NS progressive stages. At each stage, every skill file's on-policy helpfulness Δk is evaluated by comparing agent performance with and without that file on a matched validation sub-task. Skills with Δk ≤ 0 are dropped; the top-M(s) remaining skills (where M(s) linearly decays to zero) are retained. This ensures distribution shifts between curriculum stages remain bounded, stabilizing PPO importance ratios. The final stage enforces S = ∅, completing the transfer of competence from context to parameters. The base model is Qwen2.5-VL (3B and 7B), trained for up to 180 steps on 4 H800 GPUs using a GRPO-style objective with KL regularization. Key Results: On ALFWorld (embodied household tasks, 3,827 instances across 6 categories), SKILL0-3B achieves 87.9% average success rate vs. 78.2% for AgentOCR (+9.7%) and 79.9% for GRPO. SKILL0-7B reaches 89.8%, outperforming SkillRL-7B (89.9%) — a skill-augmented method that requires skill injection at inference — while consuming only 0.41k tokens/step vs. SkillRL's unreported but substantially higher cost. On Search-QA (7 single/multi-hop QA benchmarks), SKILL0-3B achieves 40.8% average vs. 34.2% for AgentOCR (+6.6%), and SKILL0-7B reaches 44.4% vs. 43.1% for EvolveR. Particularly strong out-of-domain generalization is observed on Bamboogle (63.7% / 66.9%), a multi-hop benchmark not used in training. Token efficiency is a standout result: SKILL0 uses ≤0.38k tokens/step on ALFWorld and ≤0.18k on Search-QA — more than 5× fewer than SkillRL (2.21k / 0.87k). Ablations confirm the necessity of the full three-step curriculum (Filter & Rank & Select): removing Rank alone causes a 13.7-point collapse when skills are absent at inference, while the full SKILL0 achieves a +1.6 gain in the skill-free vs. skill-augmented comparison, demonstrating genuine internalization rather than prompt dependency. Strengths: - The core idea of separating training-time scaffolding from inference-time autonomy is well-motivated and cleanly executed; the rise-then-fall helpfulness curve (Figure 6) provides strong empirical evidence that skills function as transient scaffolding rather than persistent crutches. - Token efficiency improvements are substantial and practically meaningful — over 5× reduction vs. skill-augmented baselines at comparable or better accuracy, which matters for multi-turn agentic deployment. - The helpfulness-driven curriculum is genuinely adaptive rather than schedule-based: skills are retired when the policy no longer benefits from them, avoiding both premature removal (instability) and persistent retention (over-reliance). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method inherits its SkillBank directly from SkillRL, so quality of skill internalization is fundamentally bounded by quality of the initial skill library; no method for constructing or improving SkillBank from scratch is proposed. - Evaluation is limited to two domains (embodied navigation and search QA) with only 3 curriculum stages and small-scale training (180 steps); it is unclear how the curriculum schedule generalizes to domains with larger, more heterogeneous skill libraries or longer-horizon tasks. - The visual context rendering approach (converting text to RGB images) introduces an indirect and potentially fragile encoding pathway; the compression ratio is self-generated by the policy, creating a nested optimization whose stability properties are only loosely bounded theoretically.
Recommended 2604.02268 PDF
#29 — Highly Innovative (75)

On-the-fly Repulsion in the Contextual Space for Rich Diversity in Diffusion Transformers (16 ▲ )

Omer Dahary, Benaya Koren, Daniel Garibi, Daniel Cohen-Or · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 31 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Scores 75 for identifying the Contextual Space — enriched text tokens within DiT MM-Attention blocks — as a theoretically well-motivated intervention point that resolves the long-standing upstream/downstream trade-off in diversity enhancement. The insight that these representations are structurally informed but not yet spatially committed is non-trivial and validated by strong Pareto dominance over all baselines with only 20-30% runtime overhead. Generalization to image editing without modification further demonstrates the robustness of the finding.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models optimized for quality and human preference suffer from "typicality bias": for any given prompt they consistently converge on a narrow set of visually similar outputs. Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG), the workhorse of high-quality generation, exacerbates this by sharpening the probability distribution around a single mode. Existing diversity-enhancing methods face a structural dilemma: upstream interventions (modifying initial noise or prompt embeddings) lack structural feedback from the forming image and require costly iterative optimization; downstream interventions (repelling samples in the image latent space during denoising) act on spatially committed representations, pushing samples off the learned data manifold and producing artifacts. This trade-off is especially acute in fast distilled "Turbo" models, where the generative path is decided in very few steps. Methodology: The paper identifies the "Contextual Space" — the internal manifold formed by text tokens within the Multimodal Attention (MM-Attention) blocks of Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architectures such as Flux and SD3.5. Unlike the static cross-attention conditioning in UNet-based models, DiT MM-Attention blocks facilitate a bidirectional exchange between text features and image features at every transformer block, producing enriched text tokens that are simultaneously prompt-aware and structurally informed by the emerging image. Critically, these token positions maintain a stable semantic ordering across the batch, unlike image latents whose semantic content shifts spatially from sample to sample. The method applies on-the-fly repulsion to these contextual text tokens between MM-Attention blocks during the forward pass. Adopting the particle guidance framework, repulsive forces are computed as gradients of a diversity loss over the batch and used to update token positions iteratively within each block (M inner iterations). Because the intervention acts directly on internal activations, no backpropagation through the network is required, and since each timestep reinitializes from the same unmodified prompt encoding, there is no risk of permanent semantic drift. The repulsion is applied across all MM-blocks with emphasis on early-to-mid denoising stages, steering the model's generative "intent" before composition is spatially locked in. Key Results: Evaluated on 1,000 MS-COCO 2017 prompts with 4 images per prompt (4,000 images per configuration) using Vendi Inception Score (semantic diversity), ImageReward (human preference), VQAScore (prompt adherence), and KID (distributional fidelity). The method achieves a strictly superior Pareto frontier over all baselines — CADS, SGI, Particle Guidance (PG), and SPARKE — across all three tested architectures: Flux-dev, SD3.5-Large, and SD3.5-Turbo. Runtime overhead is only 20–30% above the base model (e.g., 12.80s vs. 10.34s for Flux-dev), compared to SGI's 5–11× overhead. A user study with 45 participants and 450 responses found the method preferred over all competing approaches in overall quality, with only a near-tie against SGI (which is far more expensive). Contextual Space repulsion also generalizes out of the box to image editing with Flux-Kontext, producing diverse yet semantically consistent edits without any modification. Strengths: - Identifies a theoretically well-motivated intervention point (the Contextual Space) that genuinely resolves the upstream/downstream trade-off: representations are already structurally informed but not yet spatially committed, enabling meaningful semantic steering without manifold violation. - Computationally efficient by design: no backpropagation through the network, no optimization loop, only 20–30% runtime overhead, and fully compatible with fast distilled models where trajectory-based methods fail entirely. - Strong generalization across architectures, sampling regimes (standard vs. distilled), and even to image-editing models, demonstrating that the method is not overfit to a specific model configuration. Weaknesses / Limitations: - No user control over the axis of variation: repulsion is unconstrained in the Contextual Space and may produce diversity along coarse structural or compositional dimensions rather than user-specified fine-grained attributes (e.g., color, lighting). - The method is inherently specific to DiT architectures with MM-Attention blocks and does not transfer to UNet-based models. - Evaluation is limited to batches of four images per prompt; performance characteristics at larger batch sizes, where particle repulsion dynamics change significantly, are not studied.
Recommended 2603.28762 PDF
#30 — Innovative (74)

LoopRPT: Reinforcement Pre-Training for Looped Language Models

Guo Tang, Shixin Jiang, Heng Chang, Nuo Chen, Yuhan Li, Huiming Fan, Jia Li, Ming Liu, Bing Qin · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: LoopRPT identifies a genuine and underexplored architectural mismatch — standard RL's sparse output rewards are structurally incompatible with latent-recurrent models — and proposes a well-designed solution with step-wise dense rewards, difficulty-aware exit penalties, and noisy latent rollouts. The Pareto dominance in accuracy-vs-compute tradeoffs is convincing, and 35% compute reduction with improved accuracy is practically significant.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard RL alignment methods (RLVR) for LLMs operate on output tokens, creating a fundamental mismatch with Looped Language Models (LoopLMs) whose reasoning unfolds implicitly across iterative latent steps rather than explicit token sequences. LoopLMs like Ouro use parameter-shared recurrent backbones with adaptive early-exit gates, enabling multi-step latent computation without emitting intermediate tokens—a computationally efficient alternative to chain-of-thought. However, sparse reward signals tied to final output tokens fail to provide useful credit assignment signals for the intermediate latent iterations, and the dynamic early-exit mechanism introduces additional training instability under standard RLVR. The paper addresses how to apply RL meaningfully within the pre-training phase for this architecture class. Methodology: LoopRPT adapts the Reinforcement Pre-Training (RPT) paradigm—which reframes next-token prediction as a reasoning task with verifiable self-supervised rewards—specifically for LoopLMs. The framework has three interlocking components: (1) Entropy-based hard-token selection: only the top-ρ% of tokens (by teacher uncertainty) receive RL updates, concentrating learning signal on positions where latent computation is most valuable; (2) Step-wise rewards via EMA teacher: an exponential moving average teacher model provides stable baselines; the reward at each latent step k combines an accuracy gain (student log-probability minus teacher reference log-probability at the teacher's exit step tref) minus a difficulty-aware time penalty that penalizes computation beyond tref, scaled inversely with token difficulty so harder tokens incur smaller exit penalties; (3) Noisy latent rollouts for joint optimization: Gaussian noise is injected into recurrent hidden states to generate on-policy trajectory diversity (since latent reasoning has no discrete action space), enabling GRPO-style policy gradient updates over the exit distribution. Simultaneously, a step-weighted next-token objective strengthens backbone representations at early latent steps. The total loss combines a policy-gradient term (LPG), step-weighted representation loss (Lrep), exit entropy regularization (Lent), and a K3-style KL surrogate anchoring the student to the EMA teacher (LKL). The framework is instantiated on the Ouro architecture at 1.4B and 2.6B parameter scales, trained on OMNI-MATH competition mathematics problems for 3 epochs on 8×A100 GPUs. Key Results: On next-token reasoning tasks stratified by difficulty, LoopRPT-2.6B improves Hard-split Peak accuracy by +3.58 points and reduces average inference steps from 3.51 to 2.28 under adaptive exit (a 35% reduction in compute), achieving Pareto dominance in accuracy-vs-computation tradeoff curves across all difficulty levels. Explicit CoT prompting on comparable Qwen3-1.7B degrades performance severely (Hard accuracy drops from 19.19 to 7.44), while LoopRPT improves it. On downstream benchmarks, LoopRPT-2.6B achieves GSM8K 85.36 vs. 81.76 baseline (+3.60), HumanEval+ 71.95 vs. 70.13 (+1.82), and MBPP+ +2.91 at 1.4B scale—competitive with models 2-3× larger (Qwen2.5-4B, Qwen3-4B) on several tasks. Per-step accuracy analysis confirms the gains are due to improved early-step representation quality rather than merely encouraging premature exits: LoopRPT substantially improves step-1 and step-2 accuracy especially on hard tokens. Ablations confirm all components are necessary; removing Lrep+Lent causes the largest joint degradation in both accuracy and efficiency. Strengths: - Cleanly identifies and addresses a genuine architectural mismatch: standard sparse-reward RL is structurally ill-suited for latent-recurrent models, and the proposed step-wise dense reward formulation is principled and theoretically grounded (with proofs connecting entropy selection to gradient energy, EMA stability to slow reference drift, and noisy rollouts to a smoothed on-policy objective). - The difficulty-aware time penalty design is technically elegant—harder tokens receive smaller exit penalties, preventing premature forced exits on tokens that genuinely require deep computation, which is a non-obvious design choice supported by ablation. - Pareto dominance across multiple difficulty levels and model scales (1.4B and 2.6B) strengthens the claim of generality, and the downstream transfer to math and code benchmarks demonstrates practical utility beyond the next-token reasoning proxy task. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is confined to two model scales (1.4B and 2.6B) on a single looped architecture (Ouro with K=4 loops), making it unclear whether findings generalize to deeper loop counts, other LoopLM architectures (e.g., Universal Transformers), or significantly larger scales. - Training exclusively on competition mathematics (OMNI-MATH) limits the evidence for generality; while downstream improvements appear on coding benchmarks, the mechanism by which math-focused RPT training transfers to code is not explained, and gains on general language benchmarks (MMLU, BBH) are marginal (≤0.77 points), raising questions about whether the approach improves general reasoning or primarily exploits domain-specific patterns in the training data.
Recommended 2603.19714 PDF
#31 — Innovative (74)

EgoSim: Egocentric World Simulator for Embodied Interaction Generation (30 ▲ )

Jinkun Hao, Mingda Jia, Ruiyan Wang, Xihui Liu, Ran Yi, Lizhuang Ma, Jiangmiao Pang, Xudong Xu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 3 Apr 2026
Why this rank: EgoSim introduces a genuinely novel closed-loop egocentric simulation paradigm with an updatable 3D world state that directly addresses scene drift and interaction propagation — two fundamental unsolved problems in prior work. The scalable data pipeline mining 400K+ monocular web videos without expensive multi-camera rigs is a meaningful departure from existing data collection strategies, and the cross-embodiment transfer from human hands to robotic grippers via sparse keypoints is a creative representational choice. However, the reliance on a long chain of external models and limited long-horizon evaluation temper the impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing egocentric world simulators suffer from two critical deficiencies: (1) they lack explicit 3D grounding, causing structural drift and camera-motion/hand-motion entanglement under viewpoint changes; and (2) they treat the scene as a static backdrop, failing to propagate interaction effects (e.g., a moved object) across multi-stage simulation. Compounding this, state-of-the-art methods rely on scarce, expensive paired data from synchronized multi-camera rigs or synthetic environments, limiting their generalization to real-world dexterous manipulation. EgoSim addresses all three gaps simultaneously. Methodology: EgoSim formulates closed-loop egocentric simulation as a cycle of two modules. The Geometry-action-aware Observation Simulation model is a DiT-based video generator (initialized from Wan-2.1-Fun-14B-InP, 14B parameters) that conditions on three signals: (i) a rendered scene video produced by projecting the current 3D point cloud along the estimated camera trajectory, providing an explicit spatial anchor; (ii) a 2D action keypoint video obtained by projecting 3D hand/end-effector keypoints through camera intrinsics, using sparse keypoints rather than dense meshes for cross-embodiment generality; and (iii) a binary visibility mask marking unobserved regions, enabling the model—initialized with inpainting weights—to hallucinate coherent backgrounds. The Interaction-aware State Updating module maintains a persistent 3D world state through three steps: state reconstruction via modified VIPE (DROID-SLAM with multi-view depth alignment using DepthAnything3); interaction-aware object tracking via a VLM + SAM3 pipeline that identifies, segments, and composites the latest-frame geometry of interactive objects; and incremental TSDF fusion aligned via the Sim3 Umeyama algorithm. A scalable data pipeline automates extraction of aligned quadruplets (static 3D scene, camera trajectory, action sequence, interaction video) from ~400K web-scale monocular egocentric clips (EgoDex + EgoVid) plus 50K robotic manipulation clips, with an optional low-cost EgoCap capture device using an uncalibrated head-mounted smartphone. Key Results: On EgoDex (tabletop manipulation), EgoSim achieves PSNR 25.056 / SSIM 0.896 / LPIPS 0.170 / Depth-ERR 8.888 / Cam-ERR 0.0013, versus the best baseline InterDyn at PSNR 22.250 / Depth-ERR 44.345 / Cam-ERR 0.0226—a nearly 5× reduction in depth error and over 17× reduction in camera error. Under continuous generation (121-frame, two-clip sequences), EgoSim (Continuous) degrades modestly to PSNR 19.165 / SSIM 0.835 / LPIPS 0.220 / Depth-ERR 10.943. On the in-the-wild EgoVid benchmark, EgoSim reaches PSNR 16.684 versus InterDyn's 14.612. For cross-embodiment transfer to AgiBot bimanual robotics, pretraining on human egocentric data boosts PSNR from 15.180 to 18.670 and LPIPS from 0.350 to 0.282 after only 100 fine-tuning steps. Ablations confirm all three architectural choices (trajectory rendering, visibility mask, TSDF fusion) are necessary. Strengths: - Closed-loop updatable 3D world state directly solves the scene-drift problem that plagued all prior egocentric simulators, with explicit ablation evidence that each component (interaction-aware object update, TSDF fusion, spatial mask filtering) contributes meaningfully. - Scalable data pipeline mines web-scale monocular video without expensive multi-camera hardware, unlocking 400K+ training clips—orders of magnitude more than prior work. - Embodiment-agnostic keypoint representation enables zero-shot-style transfer from human hand data to robotic gripper manipulation, validated quantitatively on AgiBot. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The pipeline chains many brittle external models (DepthAnything3, DROID-SLAM, HaMeR, SAM3, Grounding DINO, VLM, Qwen-Image-Editing), each introducing failure modes; the authors acknowledge monocular depth/pose estimation fails in heavily occluded or highly dynamic scenes. - Continuous generation is only evaluated to 121 frames (two clips), leaving long-horizon drift behavior—the central claimed capability—inadequately characterized; the modest degradation reported may compound severely over many more interaction stages. - The evaluation protocol is limited to two datasets (EgoDex, EgoVid) and four baselines, none of which represent the closest architectural competitor (DWM with updatable states), making it unclear how much of the gain comes from the updatable state versus the stronger Wan-2.1 backbone.
Recommended 2604.01001 PDF
#32 — Innovative (74)

Is Your LLM-as-a-Recommender Agent Trustable? LLMs' Recommendation is Easily Hacked by Biases (Preferences)

Zichen Tang, Zirui Zhang, Qian Wang, Zhenheng Tang, Bo Li, Xiaowen Chu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The ε-Bound Protocol is a genuinely clever methodological contribution that addresses a real confound in prior LLM bias evaluations — by calibrating quality margins to the ambiguous boundary, the benchmark produces interpretable results that naïve setups cannot. The cross-domain, cross-model coverage is thorough and the findings on cognitive vs. structural bias susceptibility are practically relevant for the growing LLM-as-agent paradigm. However, the small candidate pool size (5 options) and shallow mitigation experiments limit the work's completeness.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: As LLMs are increasingly deployed in autonomous agentic workflows (Deep Research, e-commerce assistants, automated hiring), they are frequently tasked with selecting optimal options from large candidate pools — a paradigm the authors term LLM-as-a-recommender. Unlike the well-studied LLM-as-a-Judge setting, agentic recommendation is a strict discrimination task where a single wrong selection can cascade into workflow failure. The robustness of LLMs in this setting is underexplored. Prior bias studies focus on pairwise comparisons in abstract QA settings, failing to capture realistic selection complexity. A further gap is that when quality gaps between candidates are large, LLMs' strong reasoning masks latent bias, making naïve evaluations uninformative. Methodology: The paper introduces BiasRecBench, built via a Bias Synthesis Pipeline with Calibrated Quality Margins. The core insight is the ε-Bound Protocol: candidate pools are constructed so the optimal option o* strictly outperforms distractors by a small, controlled margin ε (e.g., satisfying one additional attribute), placing the decision at the ambiguous boundary where bias can manifest without being overridden by reasoning. Three domains are instantiated: academic paper review (reviewer scores as quality proxy), e-commerce (attribute satisfaction count), and job recruitment (JD alignment criteria). Biases are injected into sub-optimal options via two mechanisms: (1) fixed phrase concatenation (e.g., appending "Amazon's Choice", "Affiliation: Google DeepMind") and (2) generative rewriting via Claude-3.7-Sonnet. Bias types span context-agnostic (Position, Verbosity, Instruction, Distraction) and domain-specific categories (Authority, Bandwagon, SOTA, Marketing, Urgency, Brand). Evaluation uses three metrics: Original Accuracy (Acc_ori), Biased Accuracy (Acc_inj), and Bias Success Rate (BSR). Mitigation is explored via prompt-based defense and SFT on a 50-instance debiased preference dataset. Key Results: Across Gemini-2.5-Pro, Gemini-3-Pro, GPT-4o, and DeepSeek-R1, context-relevant biases cause severe degradation. In paper review, Authority bias drops Gemini-3-Pro accuracy by 38.0% (95%→57%) and Bandwagon bias drops Gemini-2.5-Pro by 25.5% (92.5%→67%). In e-commerce, Bandwagon bias reduces all models by 18.5–24.5%, and Instruction bias collapses Gemini-2.5-Pro by 46.0% (86.5%→40.5%). In recruitment, Authority bias drops Gemini-3-Pro by 42.0% (83.5%→41.5%). The ε-ablation confirms the core hypothesis: at ε=1, GPT-4o suffers −23.5% from Instruction bias and −13.5% from Distraction bias; at ε=2 (wider gap), both effects vanish (Acc remains >98%). Reasoning-enhanced models (DeepSeek-R1-Distill-14B vs. Qwen-14B) show modest improvement on structural biases but remain highly vulnerable to cognitive biases — Authority in paper review still causes 21pp degradation. Prompt-based mitigation recovers up to 21% Acc in the worst cases (Instruction bias, recruitment); SFT boosts both Acc_ori and Acc_inj but opens an adversarial alignment attack surface. Strengths: - The ε-Bound quality calibration is technically sound and addresses a genuine confound that plagues prior bias evaluations — it is the key differentiator that makes results interpretable. - The bias taxonomy is practically grounded, distinguishing domain-specific injections (fake CEO endorsement, flash-sale urgency, GitHub stars) from generic structural biases, and showing meaningfully different susceptibility profiles. - Cross-domain and cross-model coverage is thorough: 4 frontier models, 3 small-scale models, 3 domains, 200 samples each, 3 runs averaged — results are consistent and reproducible. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Candidate pool size is limited to ~5 options per query, which is far smaller than real agentic retrieval settings (e.g., hundreds of search results in Deep Research); the reported vulnerabilities may be conservative or may change non-monotonically at scale. - The ε-Bound is defined via discrete attribute counts, which works cleanly for structured domains (resume criteria, product specs) but the paper review quality operationalization (borderline reject vs. strong accept via numeric reviewer scores) relies on human consensus scores that embed their own biases, potentially making ground truth less clean than claimed. - Mitigation experiments are shallow: prompt defense is a simple system-prompt instruction and SFT uses only 50 training examples with no held-out domain generalization test; neither approach is evaluated on whether it degrades performance on unbiased inputs or causes over-refusal.
Recommended 2603.17417 PDF
#33 — Innovative (74)

FEAT: A Linear-Complexity Foundation Model for Extremely Large Structured Data

Zhenghang Song, Tang Qian, Lu Chen, Yushuai Li, Zhengke Hu, Bingbing Fang, Yumeng Song, Junbo Zhao, Sheng Zhang, Tianyi Li · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: FEAT introduces a theoretically grounded dual-axis architecture combining bidirectional Mamba-2 and convolutional gated linear attention to achieve O(N) complexity for tabular foundation models, backed by formal proofs of bounded variance and symmetric receptive fields. The 40× inference speedup at 500K samples while maintaining competitive accuracy is a genuinely enabling result for industrial-scale deployment. However, performance parity rather than superiority on clean benchmarks, and the lack of component-level ablations, temper the contribution from groundbreaking to solidly innovative.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large structured-data foundation models (LDMs) extend the in-context learning (ICL) paradigm to tabular/structured data, enabling zero-shot inference across classification, regression, and imputation tasks without dataset-specific retraining. However, existing LDMs (TabPFN, LimiX, TabICL) rely on full self-attention for cross-sample modeling, whose O(N²) complexity causes OOM failures beyond ~50,000 samples on a 140GB GPU, completely prohibiting training on datasets with millions of records. Naive replacement with linear sequence models (SSMs like Mamba, linear attention) causes representation collapse due to two structural mismatches: (1) fixed hidden states compress information as N grows, diluting signal-to-noise ratio (the "linear trap"), and (2) causal/unidirectional ordering conflicts with the permutation-invariant nature of tabular rows (the "causal mask deficit"). A third challenge is pre-training stability: real-world structured data is heavy-tailed and heteroscedastic, causing gradient explosions under standard MSE loss and synthetic-only SCM pre-training. Methodology: FEAT introduces a multi-layer dual-axis encoding architecture operating on a 3D tensor X ∈ ℝ^{N×D×d}. Cell-level embedding uses an MLP for value projection combined with Subspace Orthogonal Discriminative Feature Encoding (S-DFE), which assigns each column a randomly sampled pairwise-orthogonal identity vector — guaranteeing permutation invariance and zero-shot adaptability to unseen schemas. Each FEAT block applies: (1) Feature-axis modeling: standard multi-head self-attention over the D features of each sample independently (O(N·D²·d)), capturing intra-sample correlations; (2) Sample-axis modeling: a fixed topology of three Adaptive-Fusion Bi-Mamba-2 (AFBM) layers followed by one Convolutional Gated Linear Attention (Conv-GLA) layer. AFBM runs Mamba-2 in both forward and backward directions and fuses them via a learnable projection, eliminating the causal bias and providing a symmetric receptive field equivalent to full self-attention (proven via Jacobian analysis) at O(N) cost. Conv-GLA applies a 1D depthwise convolution as a low-pass filter to suppress noise, then accumulates a covariance memory matrix S_{i,j} ∈ ℝ^{d×d} via data-dependent gating — mathematically bounding the accumulated variance at O(1) w.r.t. N, resolving the linear trap. Pre-training uses a hybrid SCM pipeline featuring: scale-free topology via preferential attachment (mimicking hub confounders), prototype-based root initialization (breaking i.i.d.), heteroscedastic noise (variance scales with signal magnitude), Kumaraswamy warping for heavy tails, and native nominal target synthesis. The loss is a dynamically balanced combination of Huber-based reconstruction (CCMM objective), cross-entropy for classification, and Huber regression loss, with per-task normalization by batch cardinality to prevent gradient domination. Key Results: On scalability (RQ1), at 500,000 samples FEAT achieves 564ms latency vs. 22,499ms for TabICL v2 (~40× speedup); LimiX and TabPFN fail with kernel errors above 100,000 samples. FEAT latency grows from 149ms to 565ms as N scales from 5K to 500K — near-constant, confirming O(N) behavior. On predictive performance (RQ2) across 11 real-world benchmarks, FEAT achieves AUC 0.9251 on Tabzilla-CLS (best among all methods), 0.8638 AUC on Tabarena-CLS (best), and 0.8991 AUC on GI-CLS (best). In regression, FEAT achieves best RMSE/R² on CTR23-REG (0.4053/0.7509), GI-REG (R²=0.6732, best), PFN-REG (0.5257/0.6246, best), and Talent-REG (R²=0.6985, best). On dense structured benchmarks like Tabarena-REG and BCCO-REG, TabICL/TabPFN narrowly outperform FEAT (R²=0.8106 vs. 0.7924 on Tabarena-REG). Across all 11 datasets, FEAT consistently matches or exceeds Transformer-based LDMs, and outperforms all gradient boosting baselines (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost) in zero-shot mode. Strengths: - The combination of AFBM (bidirectional Mamba-2) and Conv-GLA is theoretically well-grounded: the paper provides formal proofs of O(N) complexity, bounded variance (Theorem 3.4), and zero-phase global contextualization (Theorem 3.5), making the architectural choices principled rather than ad hoc. - The hybrid SCM pre-training pipeline is substantially more realistic than prior synthetic-only approaches, incorporating scale-free topology, multi-modal row clusters, heteroscedastic noise, and Kumaraswamy warping — each targeting a specific statistical property of real-world data. - The 40× inference speedup at 500K samples while maintaining competitive accuracy is a genuine, practically important result: it directly enables deployment on industrial-scale datasets where prior models simply OOM. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Predictive performance parity, not superiority, is the primary claim — on highly structured or low-noise datasets (Tabarena-REG, BCCO-REG), full-attention models (TabICL v2, TabPFN 2.5) maintain a measurable edge (e.g., R²=0.8106 vs. 0.7924), suggesting bounded hidden states still impose some representational cost. - The variance boundedness proof (Theorem 3.4) rests on the assumption that M ≪ N informative tokens exist and that gates converge to Bernoulli-like behavior — assumptions that may not hold in dense, low-noise tabular settings, which may explain the regression gap on cleaner benchmarks. - The paper lacks ablations isolating the individual contributions of AFBM vs. Conv-GLA, S-DFE vs. standard positional encoding, and each component of the hybrid SCM pipeline, making it difficult to assess which innovations are load-bearing vs. incremental.
Citations 2603.16513 PDF
#34 — Innovative (74)

Reasoning Shift: How Context Silently Shortens LLM Reasoning (22 ▲ )

Gleb Rodionov · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 2 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 74 for identifying a genuinely surprising and practically important phenomenon: irrelevant context silently compresses reasoning traces by up to 53%, with the compression coming entirely from reduced post-answer self-verification rather than faster problem-solving. The resampling experiment — holding reasoning prefixes fixed while varying context — is a methodologically clever isolation technique that elevates this beyond a simple benchmarking study. The consistency across four diverse frontier models strengthens the finding's generality. However, the paper is purely diagnostic with no mitigation strategy, all experiments are limited to math benchmarks, and the mechanistic analysis relies on a single model, capping its contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Reasoning LLMs trained with RL-based test-time scaling produce long chain-of-thought traces with self-verification and uncertainty management behaviors that are key to their strong performance on hard tasks. These models are increasingly deployed in agentic and multi-turn settings where the surrounding context contains irrelevant information. The paper asks a pointed question: does the presence of irrelevant context silently degrade the quality of reasoning, not just retrieval accuracy? Prior work showed long contexts hurt retrieval and multi-turn coherence; this paper investigates the orthogonal effect on reasoning depth and self-checking behavior. Methodology: The paper conducts a systematic empirical study across four frontier reasoning models (Qwen3.5-27B, GPT-OSS-120B, Gemini 3 Flash Preview, Kimi K2 Thinking) and three distractor conditions compared to an isolated baseline: (1) Long input — 64k tokens of Shakespeare prepended to a math problem; (2) Subtask — two independent problems bundled in the same query; (3) Multi-turn — the problem appears as a second turn after an unrelated first task. Benchmarks used are IMOAnswerBench (hard competition math) and MATH500 (for ablations). The key metric is reasoning token count alongside accuracy, judged by Gemini 3 Pro Preview. For mechanistic analysis, the authors adopt a sentence-level classification framework (Venhoff et al. 2025) categorizing CoT sentences into eight functional roles (problem setup, plan generation, fact retrieval, active computation, result consolidation, uncertainty management, self-check, final answer) and study transition probability matrices between these roles. A resampling experiment tests whether the same reasoning prefix, continued under different context conditions, produces different completion tendencies. Key Results: Across all models and distractor conditions, reasoning trace lengths drop substantially: up to 50% fewer tokens versus baseline for the same problem. Accuracy drops are consistent but moderate on IMOAnswerBench: −12% for Qwen3.5-27B, −9% for GPT-OSS-120B, −15% for Gemini 3 Flash Preview, and −9% for Kimi K2 Thinking under Subtask/Long input. Even inserting only 128 irrelevant tokens reduces average reasoning length by 18%; 64k tokens reduces it by 53%. The phenomenon is markedly more pronounced in thinking mode (53% reduction) than non-thinking mode (19%). Crucially, the time-to-first-answer-candidate is nearly identical across conditions (925 vs. 939 tokens on average), revealing that the compression comes entirely from reduced post-answer verification, not faster problem-solving. Transition matrix analysis confirms the dominant signal: the probability of transitioning from "final answer" directly to end-of-thinking is 57% at baseline vs. 68% under long-input, i.e., the model skips self-checks more often. Resampling from the same partial traces under different context conditions shows the ratio of traces that immediately terminate jumps from 21% (baseline) to 46% (16k long input), accompanied by sharp drops in uncertainty tokens like "wait" (11%→5%), "alternatively" (17%→5%), and "but" (46%→20%). The same phenomenon is observed at every post-training checkpoint of OLMo-3 (instruct, SFT, DPO, full think), ruling out a training-stage artifact. Strengths: - The resampling experiment is the paper's strongest methodological contribution: by holding the reasoning prefix fixed and varying only context, it isolates that the behavioral shift is a context-conditioned generation effect, not just a difference in problem representation. - The finding is consistent across four diverse frontier models and two benchmarks, lending it generality beyond a single architecture quirk. - Sentence-level CoT analysis provides a mechanistic account (reduced post-answer self-verification) rather than just reporting token counts, which is a meaningful step toward understanding why the shift occurs. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All experiments use math benchmarks; whether the reasoning shift generalizes to coding, science, or open-ended reasoning tasks is entirely untested and explicitly acknowledged as future work. - The mechanistic analysis (transition matrices, resampling) is conducted only on Qwen3-32B on MATH500 — a single model on a different benchmark than the main results table — limiting claims about generality of the mechanism. - The paper offers no mitigation strategy (no prompt engineering fix, no training recipe, no context management technique), making it purely diagnostic; the practical takeaways for practitioners are limited to awareness of the problem. - The distractor conditions are synthetic and relatively simple (Shakespeare text labeled as "old data"); realistic agentic contexts with interspersed relevant and irrelevant content may exhibit different or stronger effects that remain unexplored.
Recommended 2604.01161 PDF
#35 — Innovative (73)

Sparser, Faster , Lighter Transformer Language Models

Edoardo Cetin, Stefano Peluchetti, Emilio Castillo, Akira Naruse, Mana Murakami, Llion Jones · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: This paper earns 73 for the TwELL sparse format, a genuine kernel-engineering contribution whose tile-alignment insight eliminates the costly format-conversion kernel that has blocked practical sparse inference for years. The co-design of data formats and CUDA kernels delivers real measured speedups (17-21% inference, 11-22% training) on H100s with benefits that increase with model scale — a rare and practically significant property.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Scaling large language models (LLMs) delivers continually improving capabilities but at staggering computational cost. Feedforward layers account for over two-thirds of model parameters and more than 80% of total FLOPs in large models, yet prior work has documented that only a small fraction of hidden neurons activate for any given token — a natural sparsity that, in principle, should translate to significant compute savings. The frustrating paradox is that existing sparse kernels for unstructured sparsity often run slower than dense operations on modern GPUs, whose hardware and software stacks are heavily optimized for dense computation. Prior attempts to realize practical speedups have required major deviations from standard training recipes (e.g., top-K masking, straight-through estimators) and have seen little adoption. This paper closes the gap between theoretical and realized savings by co-designing sparse data formats and CUDA kernels tailored to the execution model of modern NVIDIA GPUs. Methodology: The paper introduces two novel sparse data formats and a suite of CUDA kernels for both inference and training. For inference, the key contribution is Tile-wise ELLPACK (TwELL), which divides the gate activation matrix into horizontal 1D tiles matching the tile dimensions of standard tiled matmul kernels. This alignment allows TwELL to be materialized in the epilogue of the gate projection kernel itself — with no additional kernel launch, global memory round-trip, or CTA synchronization — unlike prior ELL-based formats that require a costly separate conversion pass. A second fused kernel then uses TwELL indices to execute the up and down projections jointly, loading only non-zero activations and skipping zero columns, reducing DRAM traffic substantially. For training, a hybrid sparse format dynamically partitions rows by non-zero count: sparse rows go into a compact ELL matrix while outlier rows with atypically high activation counts fall back to a dense backup. This avoids ELL's requirement that the maximum non-zeros be known in advance and small — a condition violated in practice since per-token sparsity variance is enormous (maximum non-zeros can exceed the mean by over an order of magnitude). Custom kernels combine ELL sparse matmuls on CUDA cores with Tensor Core matmuls for the dense tail, and a transposition kernel handles backward-pass gradient computation without expensive format conversions. Sparsity itself is induced with minimal recipe modification: ReLU activations on the gate projection and an L1 regularization term on hidden activations with a tunable coefficient. No architectural surgery, top-K thresholding, or straight-through estimators are required. Key Results: On a 1.5B parameter Transformer++ model trained chinchilla-optimally (30B tokens on FineWeb), an L1 coefficient of 2×10⁻⁵ reduces average non-zero activations from ~911 (already 20% sparse without regularization) down to ~29, well below 1% of the hidden dimension of 5632, with cross-entropy increasing by less than 2% and no statistically meaningful drop on seven downstream reasoning benchmarks. Across model scales from 0.5B to 2B parameters, sparse models maintain essentially identical task accuracy while achieving: 17–20.5% throughput speedup during forward inference, 11–21.9% speedup during the training step, 15–17% energy reduction per token, and 19–28% peak memory reduction — all on H100 PCIe GPUs. Critically, benefits scale with model size: the 2B model yields larger relative speedups than the 0.5B model, and average non-zeros decrease from 39 to 24 as scale increases. Analysis of sparsity structure reveals a -0.996 Pearson correlation between per-layer mean non-zeros and inference speedup contribution, and shows that token position and semantic content predictably modulate activation density, with high-information tokens and early sequence positions consuming disproportionately more compute. On RTX PRO 6000 GPUs, training speedups are substantially higher than on H100s, indicating that the approach degrades more gracefully on less capable hardware. Dead neuron reinitialization (randomly perturbing permanently inactive gate projection columns) recovers further performance, achieving 46.6% mean task accuracy (vs. 46.4% dense baseline) with 19.1% faster inference. Strengths: - The TwELL format is a genuine technical contribution: its tile-alignment insight eliminates the costly ELL conversion kernel that has blocked prior work, enabling seamless fusion into existing matmul pipelines with minimal engineering overhead. - The hybrid training format cleanly handles the non-uniform sparsity distributions that made pure ELL-based training kernels brittle, with practical hyperparameter choices (ELL width 128, overflow buffer 1/8 of batch) that are robust across sparsity levels. - The sparsity induction method is minimal and compatible with standard training recipes — no architectural changes beyond swapping SiLU for ReLU, no alterations to optimizer, sequence length, or batch size, enabling straightforward adoption. - Benefits increase with model scale, aligning with prevailing scaling trends and making the approach more rather than less relevant as models grow. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are limited to models up to 2B parameters trained on ~40B tokens; it is not demonstrated whether the same sparsity levels and efficiency gains hold at 7B, 13B, or 70B+ parameter scales or at chinchilla-optimal token counts for those sizes, which is where practical impact is greatest. - The switch from SiLU to ReLU introduces a small but consistent performance gap (~0.7% mean task accuracy at 1.5B scale in this paper's own ablation), which may compound at larger scales or longer training horizons — the claim that this is negligible is not definitively established at production scale. - The hybrid training format introduces hyperparameters (ELL width, dense overflow buffer size) that must be tuned per sparsity level and can require adaptive resizing during training when sparsity evolves abruptly, adding operational complexity not present in dense training.
Search Results 2603.23198 PDF
#36 — Innovative (73)

Omni-SimpleMem: Autoresearch-Guided Discovery of Lifelong Multimodal Agent Memory (16 ▲ )

Jiaqi Liu, Zipeng Ling, Shi Qiu, Yanqing Liu, Siwei Han, Peng Xia, Haoqin Tu, Zeyu Zheng, Cihang Xie, Charles Fleming, Mingyu Ding, Huaxiu Yao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 3 Apr 2026
Why this rank: The core empirical finding that bug fixes and architectural changes individually outweigh all hyperparameter tuning combined is a sharp, falsifiable insight that meaningfully distinguishes autoresearch from AutoML. The set-union hybrid retrieval strategy and progressive pyramid expansion are concrete, transferable design contributions with strong ablation support. However, reliance on an unpublished black-box pipeline (AUTORESEARCHCLAW) and serious metric-gaming concerns — where the largest gains may reflect token-overlap F1 optimization rather than genuine memory quality improvement — prevent a higher score.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: AI agents operating over extended time horizons must retain, organize, and recall heterogeneous multimodal experiences (text, images, audio, video), but existing memory systems fall short in two ways. Raw-retrieval approaches (embedding similarity over stored inputs) suffer from storage bloat and retrieval noise at scale; structured memory systems (MemGPT, Generative Agents) are text-only and discard visual/auditory signals. Both categories are products of slow manual research cycles that miss tightly coupled cross-component interactions. Traditional AutoML can tune numerical hyperparameters but cannot perform code comprehension, bug diagnosis, or architectural redesign — precisely the operations that yield the largest gains in complex pipelines. The paper asks whether autonomous research pipelines can fill this gap for multi-component AI system design. Methodology: The authors deploy AUTORESEARCHCLAW, a 23-stage autonomous research pipeline, to discover OMNI-SIMPLEMEM starting from SimpleMem, a unimodal text-only baseline (F1 = 0.117 on LoCoMo). The pipeline iterates: analyze prior results → generate hypothesis → implement code change → evaluate → decide PROCEED (≥0.5% gain) / ITERATE (ambiguous) / PIVOT (two consecutive regressions, revert and try new direction). Approximately 50 experiments run across two benchmarks in ~72 hours. The resulting OMNI-SIMPLEMEM architecture has three core principles. (1) Selective ingestion: modality-specific novelty filters (CLIP cosine similarity for video frames, VAD for audio, Jaccard overlap for text) discard redundant inputs before storage. Retained signals are encapsulated as Multimodal Atomic Units (MAUs), M = ⟨summary, embedding, pointer-to-raw, timestamp, modality, links⟩, separating hot storage (compact metadata for fast retrieval) from cold storage (raw assets loaded lazily). (2) Progressive retrieval with hybrid search: dense FAISS retrieval and sparse BM25 are merged via set-union (dense results first, BM25-only appended) — a key pipeline discovery, as score-based re-ranking was found to degrade semantic ordering. A pyramid mechanism then expands retrieved candidates through three levels (summaries → full text → raw content) under explicit token budget B, gated by cosine similarity thresholds rather than additional LLM calls. (3) Knowledge graph augmentation: entities and relations extracted per MAU build a typed graph (7 entity types, 7 relation types); entity resolution merges surface-form variants using hybrid cosine + Jaro-Winkler similarity; at query time, h-hop neighborhood expansion with distance-decayed relevance scoring surfaces relational evidence for multi-hop queries. Key Results: On LoCoMo (1,986 QA pairs, 5 question categories), OMNI-SIMPLEMEM achieves F1 = 0.492–0.613 across five LLM backbones (GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4.1-nano, GPT-5.1, GPT-5-nano), versus SimpleMem SOTA at 0.342–0.432 (+38–43% relative improvement). On Mem-Gallery (1,711 QA pairs, 240 multimodal dialogues, 1,003 images), it achieves F1 = 0.749–0.810, versus previous SOTA (MuRAG) at 0.697 and SimpleMem at 0.498–0.538. Ablation confirms pyramid expansion (−17% F1 without it) and BM25 hybrid search (−14%) are the two most critical components. Throughput reaches 5.81 queries/sec with 8 parallel workers (3.5× faster than the best baseline), enabled by concurrent read-only FAISS and BM25 indices. Critically, the taxonomy of discovery types reveals that the highest-impact improvements were a missing response_format parameter (one-line bug, +175% on LoCoMo), returning full dialogue text instead of LLM-generated summaries (+53% on Mem-Gallery), and prompt constraint positioning (+188% on the KR category) — none of which are reachable by traditional hyperparameter search. All hyperparameter changes combined contributed less than any single one of these. Strengths: - The core empirical finding — that bug fixes, architectural changes, and prompt engineering each individually outweigh all hyperparameter tuning combined — is a sharp, falsifiable claim backed by detailed per-iteration logs, and meaningfully distinguishes autoresearch from AutoML. - The set-union merging strategy for hybrid dense-sparse retrieval is a concrete, transferable finding: the paper provides both the motivation (score-based re-ranking disrupts semantic ordering) and ablation evidence (−14% without BM25 hybrid). - Operational transparency is unusually high: full optimization trajectories (Tables 6–7), per-category F1 across iterations (Table 8), latency breakdowns, and complete prompt catalogs are provided, enabling reproducibility and reuse. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation metric alignment concern is non-trivial: several of the largest gains (e.g., +175% from adding response_format, +53% from returning raw text on Mem-Gallery) are specific to token-overlap F1 as the metric rather than genuine improvements in memory quality or agent utility. It is unclear how much of the improvement reflects better memory versus better metric gaming. - The paper relies on AUTORESEARCHCLAW (a separate, not-yet-published system) as a black box for the most interesting part of the contribution — the discovery process itself. The 23-stage pipeline is described only in outline; readers cannot assess how much human judgment went into scoping, prompt design for the pipeline, or benchmark selection, making it hard to evaluate the true degree of autonomy.
Recommended 2604.01007 PDF
#37 — Innovative (73)

Extend3D: Town-Scale 3D Generation (14 ▲ )

Seungwoo Yoon, Jinmo Kim, Jaesik Park · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 1 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Extend3D's under-noising technique is a creative and well-motivated contribution: by noising less than the denoising start point, occluded regions are treated as generative noise rather than preserved structure, enabling clean scene completion without retraining. The coupled overlapping patch-wise denoising with 3D-aware optimization losses yields dramatic quantitative improvements (halving LPIPS, +7dB PSNR over competitors). However, the restriction to near-overhead viewpoints and high memory requirements temper the practical impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Generating large-scale 3D scenes from a single image is a critical unmet need for game development, filmmaking, and simulation. Existing 3D generative models (Trellis, Hunyuan3D) are trained on object-centric datasets and use fixed-size latent spaces, which limits resolution and prevents them from faithfully representing wide scenes — outputs become blurry as scene size grows. Prior training-free pipelines that stitch object-centric models sequentially (3DTown, SynCity, EvoScene) suffer from visible seams between tiles, broken consistency, vanishing floors, and inability to handle occluded regions faithfully. There is thus a gap for a scalable, training-free image-to-3D scene pipeline that achieves spatial coherence and high fidelity across arbitrarily large extents. Methodology: Extend3D is a training-free pipeline built on Trellis (a two-stage latent flow model) that extends the 3D latent space in x and y by scale factors a and b. The pipeline has three core components. (1) Overlapping patch-wise flow: the extended latent is divided into overlapping patches via a sliding window (division factor d), each patch processed by the pretrained model conditioned on a spatially aligned image crop; patch vector fields are merged by averaging in overlap regions, coupling patches without retraining. Dilated sampling is also applied during sparse structure generation to enforce global structural coherence, following DemoFusion. (2) Initialization with prior: a monocular depth estimator (MoGe-2) provides an initial point cloud, voxelized into an occupancy grid. Crucially, the authors introduce "under-noising" — adding noise at level t_noise but denoising from t_start > t_noise — so that occluded/missing regions are treated as additional noise and filled by the generative model. Iterative SDEdit (n_iter rounds) progressively completes occluded areas. (3) Optimization with prior: at each denoising step, the vector field is optimized with Adam using two losses: a binary-cross-entropy-inspired sparse structure loss that prevents initialized voxels from vanishing, and a rendering loss (LPIPS − SSIM) computed by differentiably rendering the predicted 3D Gaussians into the input camera viewpoint. This prevents object-centric drift (vanishing floors, rotated objects) and eliminates seams. The full pipeline runs in O(abd²n_iter) complexity and outputs 3D Gaussians or mesh. Key Results: On appearance metrics across 100 diverse images, Extend3D achieves LPIPS 0.240 / SSIM 0.611 / PSNR 20.4 dB, versus best competitor EvoScene at 0.482 / 0.310 / 13.2 — roughly halving LPIPS and adding 7 dB PSNR. On geometry (45 UrbanScene3D image-mesh pairs), Chamfer Distance drops to 0.0086 versus Trellis 0.0315 and EvoScene 0.0188, with F-score at 0.05 reaching 0.694 versus 0.498 for EvoScene. Compared to SynCity on text-prompted scenes, Extend3D improves CLIP score from 0.251 to 0.276, HPSv3 from 3.254 to 3.519, and Intra-LPIPS (patch consistency) from 0.631 to 0.571. Human preference studies (10 annotators, 14 scenes) show win rates of 87.1% over EvoScene and 73.6% over Hunyuan3D on geometry; only ties against Trellis (50.0%) on geometry, but wins 66.4% on faithfulness. The ablation confirms each component is load-bearing: patch-wise flow alone gives LPIPS 0.606; adding initialization drops it to 0.425; SS optimization to 0.400; SLAT optimization to 0.240. Computationally, at a=b=2, d=4 the method needs 28 GB VRAM and 14.1 minutes, substantially less than SynCity (49 GB, 52 min) and EvoScene (68 GB, 35 min). Strengths: - The under-noising concept is technically novel and well-motivated: by noising less than the denoising start point, the model treats structural holes as noise to fill rather than structure to preserve, enabling clean completion of occluded regions without any task-specific training. - The 3D-aware optimization objectives (sparse structure BCE loss + rendering LPIPS/SSIM loss) are principled and directly address the core failure modes of object-centric models in scene settings, maintaining denoising trajectories aligned with scene rather than object dynamics. - Simultaneous generation of all patches with coupled denoising paths (rather than sequential outpainting) is the right architectural choice and leads to measurably better inter-patch consistency. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The SLAT optimization requires substantial GPU memory (28 GB at a=b=2; 61 GB at a=b=6), making it impractical at high scale factors without disabling the texture refinement — a real deployment limitation. - Performance degrades significantly on street-level (non-aerial) images due to vanishing-point-induced scale mismatch between x and y coordinates; the method is effectively restricted to overhead or near-overhead viewpoints, limiting applicability to common camera perspectives. - The ablation reveals a geometry-texture trade-off from SLAT optimization (F-score slightly degrades from 0.708 to 0.694 when adding SLAT optimization), and iterative SDEdit shows diminishing returns or degradation beyond n_iter=2, indicating brittle hyperparameter sensitivity in the completion stage.
Recommended 2603.29387 PDF
#38 — Innovative (73)

Vocabulary shapes cross-lingual variation of word-order learnability in language models

Jonas Mayer Martins, Jaap Jumelet, Viola Priesemann, Lisa Beinborn · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: This paper introduces a methodologically clean perturbation framework using the Mallows permutation model with a single continuous parameter, directly resolving confounds in all prior word-order perturbation work. The key finding — that vocabulary structure (R²=0.97) rather than the traditional free/fixed word-order typology predicts cross-lingual variation in word-order learnability — is genuinely surprising and challenges established typological assumptions. Limited to ten European languages from a single corpus, which tempers the impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Why do some languages tolerate free word order (e.g., Czech, Finnish) while others do not (e.g., English, French)?
