Prior work on word-order perturbation experiments suffered from three limitations: (1) shuffling at the subword level inadvertently disrupts morphology simultaneously with syntax, conflating two typological dimensions; (2) disparate shuffling methods with discrete parameters make cross-study comparisons difficult; and (3) most studies are English-only or cover only a narrow language sample. The paper asks whether learnability as measured by language model surprisal is sensitive to word-order flexibility, and what drives cross-lingual differences in that sensitivity. Methodology: The authors train lightweight autoregressive transformer language models (50.5M parameters, PICOLM framework) from scratch on synthetic word-order variants of ten European languages drawn from the Europarl corpus. The key technical contribution is a continuous, unified perturbation scheme based on the Mallows permutation model parameterized by a single continuous order parameter θ. For each sentence length n, one permutation is sampled from the Mallows distribution and applied deterministically to all sentences of that length, preserving global (model-independent) text entropy, morphological integrity, and vocabulary while varying only word-order regularity across a full spectrum from sentence reversal (θ → −∞) through full scrambling (θ = 0) to the original order (θ → +∞). Ten languages spanning fixed-word-order (English, French, Portuguese, Danish, Swedish) and free-word-order (Czech, Latvian, Hungarian, Estonian, Finnish) types were studied. Model surprisal S(θ) is the evaluation metric, and surprisal change ΔS(θ) = S(θ) − S_orig isolates the effect of perturbation. To explain cross-lingual variation, the authors compute vocabulary statistics (Zipf-based word and subword coverage metrics, sentence length, morphological complexity proxies) and fit a multivariate partial-least-squares (PLS) regression with leave-one-language-out cross-validation against the full surprisal curve S(θ). Key Results: Three main findings emerge. First, model surprisal increases monotonically with word-order irregularity across all languages, confirming that autoregressive transformers have a locality bias. Second, sentence reversal causes only a minor asymmetry (median ΔS⁺/⁻ ≈ 0.096, ~6% of the irregular-order surprisal change), indicating models are largely insensitive to the typological correlations violated by reversal. Third, and most importantly, the binary free/fixed word-order typology does not explain cross-lingual variation in robustness to shuffling (Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney p = 0.55 for ΔS at θ = 0). Instead, vocabulary structure — Zipf-based coverage, sentence length, and morphological complexity proxies — predicts per-language surprisal curves with R² = 0.97 overall and per-language R² ranging from 0.93 (Hungarian) to 0.99 (Portuguese, Latvian) in leave-one-out cross-validation. The vocabulary component alone explains R̄² = 0.65 of variance per θ-slice; adding a morphological-complexity component reaches R̄² = 0.79. The word- vs. subword-level shuffling comparison shows that subword-level shuffling inflates surprisal differences disproportionately for morphologically rich Balto-Slavic and Uralic languages, demonstrating that prior subword-level experiments confounded syntax and morphology. Vocabulary size experiments reveal that free/fixed-language separation in original surprisal emerges only above |V| = 8,000 tokens and is driven by differences in subword coverage curves. Strengths: - Methodologically clean design: word-level shuffling with the Mallows model's single continuous parameter θ preserves global entropy and morphological integrity, directly addressing a key confound in all prior perturbation work. - Broad, principled multilingual scope: ten typologically diverse European languages with controlled parallel corpus (Europarl), enabling genuine cross-lingual comparison. - The PLS regression with leave-one-language-out cross-validation is well-suited to the small n=10 sample with highly collinear predictors, and the R²=0.97 predictive accuracy is strong evidence for the vocabulary-structure hypothesis. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The study is limited to ten European languages from a single parliamentary corpus, which restricts typological diversity (all ten are SVO, all Indo-European or Uralic) and may not generalize to tone languages, morphologically isolating languages outside Europe, or non-SVO dominant orders. - The use of small (50.5M parameter) models trained for only 1,000 steps may not reflect the behavior of large-scale language models; the vocabulary size interaction effects hint that scaling dynamics could alter conclusions, especially for the irregular-order regime. - The analysis is purely associative: PLS identifies vocabulary structure as predictive of surprisal but cannot establish whether vocabulary statistics causally drive word-order robustness or merely co-vary with the true causal factors (e.g., morphological case marking richness). The paper acknowledges this limitation but does not attempt causal identification.
Recommended 2603.19427 PDF
#39 — Innovative (73)

NearID: Identity Representation Learning via Near-identity Distractors (19 ▲ )

Aleksandar Cvejic, Rameen Abdal, Abdelrahman Eldesokey, Bernard Ghanem, Peter Wonka · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 3 Apr 2026
Why this rank: NearID identifies a sharp, previously underappreciated failure mode in dominant identity metrics (CLIP-I, DINO scores) where background context systematically trumps object identity. The matched-context distractor construction is methodologically principled and the lightweight training recipe (3.6% parameters, 6.5 A100-hours) with strong out-of-distribution transfer is impressive. However, the scope remains bounded to rigid synthetic objects and concept-preservation evaluation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard vision encoders (CLIP, DINOv2, SigLIP2) and even large vision-language models systematically conflate object identity with background context. When a visually similar but distinct object instance (a "near-identity" impostor) is placed on the same background as a reference image, these encoders score the impostor higher than the true identity viewed against a different backdrop. This directly undermines CLIP-I and DINO scores — the dominant automated metrics for personalized image generation and editing — producing inflated evaluations when a model generates the wrong object on the correct background. No prior work had formally characterized or addressed this failure mode with a controlled evaluation protocol. Methodology: NearID introduces three tightly coupled components. First, it defines near-identity (NearID) distractors: semantically similar but distinct object instances inpainted into the exact same background as a reference image, removing all contextual shortcuts so identity becomes the sole discriminative signal. Second, the paper builds the NearID dataset: 19,386 identities with up to three multi-view positives each (sourced from the SynCD pipeline using Objaverse 3D assets) and 316,505 matched-context distractors synthesized via four generative models (SDXL, FLUX.1, Qwen-Image, PowerPaint), yielding 7 inpainting configurations. Third, the method trains only a lightweight Multi-head Attention Pooling (MAP) projection head (~3.6% of total parameters, ~15M out of 428M) on top of a frozen SigLIP2-SO400M backbone using the NearID loss: a two-tier contrastive objective combining (a) a per-index symmetric InfoNCE discrimination term that places NearID distractors explicitly in the softmax denominator alongside batch negatives, and (b) a softplus ranking regularizer that enforces distractors rank above batch negatives without hard margins or oracle supervision. This enforces the hierarchy: same identity > NearID distractor > random batch negative. The dataset is jointly trained with the MTG part-level edit dataset (upsampled 4x) to provide fine-grained calibration. Training requires ~6.5 A100-hours. Key Results: On the NearID benchmark, frozen SigLIP2 achieves 30.74% Sample Success Rate (SSR); NearID raises this to 99.17% SSR and 99.71% Pairwise Accuracy (+68.4 and +50.9 points respectively). Every standard frozen encoder (CLIP, DINOv2, SigLIP2) scores 0.0% SSR on the part-level Mind-the-Glitch (MTG) benchmark; even the dedicated VSM metric reaches only 7.0%; NearID achieves 35.0% SSR (+35 points). MTG Pearson correlation to oracle scores improves from 0.180 to 0.465 (full evaluation) and from 0.366 to 0.486 (paired context). On DreamBench++ human alignment, NearID improves metric-to-human correlation from 0.516 (SigLIP2) to 0.545, with gains on out-of-distribution categories: animals (+0.105) and humans (+0.065) despite training exclusively on rigid objects. The expected drop on Style (−0.092) confirms genuine identity learning rather than score inflation. Qwen3-VL 30B, despite explicit anti-background prompting, achieves only 49.7% SSR — far below NearID — at ~324x the inference cost. Ablations show that naive placement of distractors in the InfoNCE denominator without the ranking regularizer achieves near-perfect discrimination (99.6% SSR) but collapses alignment (M-H drops to 0.251); the softplus ranking term is essential for preserving graded semantic structure. Strengths: - The problem identification is sharp and the evaluation reveals a previously underappreciated but practically significant failure mode of widely-used metrics (CLIP-I, DINO scores) in personalization benchmarks. - The matched-context distractor construction is principled: by controlling the background exactly while varying identity, confounds are isolated in a way that random negatives or synthetic augmentations cannot achieve. - The training recipe is parameter-efficient (only 3.6% of parameters trained, 6.5 A100-hours), generalizes across unseen distractor generators (≥97.8% SSR across all 9 configurations), and transfers to out-of-distribution domains (animals, humans) without those in training. - The ablation suite is thorough: loss decomposition, ranking weight (α), positive cohesion (β), foreground masking, VLM scaling, per-source discrimination breakdowns, and data component ablations all clearly motivate the design choices. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The dataset and positives are restricted to rigid synthetic objects rendered via diffusion from Objaverse 3D assets; coverage of deformable objects, fine-grained natural textures (fur, fabric), and real-world photographs with messy backgrounds is limited by design. - The benchmark is scoped to concept-preservation evaluation (same instance regardless of scene); text-guided editing scenarios — where identity preservation and edit fidelity are in tension — are explicitly out of scope and represent a gap the authors acknowledge. - Part-level discrimination on MTG (35% SSR) remains substantially below object-level performance (99.2% SSR), and the gain over VSM's 7% is notable but not definitively solving fine-grained part sensitivity.
Recommended 2604.01973 PDF
#40 — Innovative (73)

Description of a collaborative sperm whale birth and shifts in coda vocal styles during key events

nature.com · Michael Bronstein - new articles
Why this rank: This paper presents the most comprehensive multimodal dataset ever collected for a wild cetacean birth — an event observed only once before in 60 years for sperm whales. The synchronized audio-video-photo methodology enabling VLMC-based vocal style analysis linked to precise behavioral events is methodologically creative. The phylogenetic reconstruction dating cooperative lifting to >36 Mya adds genuine comparative biology insight. The n=1 limitation is inherent to the rarity of the event rather than a design flaw. While observational rather than experimental, the findings on vocal style shifts during birth and interspecific encounters open new research directions in cetacean bioacoustics.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Wild cetacean births are among the rarest events in marine biology — observed in fewer than 10% of cetacean species, with only one prior scientific observation of a sperm whale birth in the last 60 years. This gap leaves fundamental questions unanswered: How do these deep-diving, negatively buoyant neonates survive the first minutes of life? What role does the social group play? How do acoustic signals change during high-stakes events? This paper addresses these gaps with the most comprehensive multimodal dataset ever collected for any wild cetacean birth. Methodology: On July 8, 2023, the Project CETI team observed a birth within the well-documented sperm whale Unit A (11 individuals from a social unit monitored since 1996) off Dominica. Data were collected simultaneously via: underwater audio (omnidirectional hydrophone at 96 kHz), aerial drone video (two synchronized DJI Mavic-3 quadcopters at ≥25 m altitude), shipboard DSLR photography, and behavioral observation logs in a custom iPad app. Acoustic analysis extracted 5,731 codas (31,364 clicks) from 3h32min of audio using an automated coda annotator. Vocal style changes were quantified using Variable Length Markov Chain (VLMC) models fit to sliding windows of 150 codas (90% overlap), with distances between consecutive windows measured via modified KL divergence — compared against a baseline from legacy recordings of Unit A under normal social conditions. Kullback-Leibler divergence was also used to compare coda feature distributions before vs. after each key event. Phylogenetic mapping of birth-assistance behaviors was performed via equally-weighted parsimony on a time-calibrated cetacean phylogeny spanning 93 species. Key Results: Active labor lasted approximately 34 minutes (flukes-first emergence to delivery). The newborn surfaced for its first breath within 2 minutes and attempted swimming within 3 minutes. All 11 unit members participated, with adults physically lifting the newborn — sometimes nearly completely out of water — beginning within one minute post-birth and continuing for three hours. The dominant coda type was 1+1+3 (>78% tempo type 3; 4,394 of 5,731 total codas), consistent with Eastern Caribbean Clan social identity markers; the second most common was 4R, a proposed Unit A identifier. VLMC vocal style distances showed statistically significant departures from the group's canonical baseline at three key moments: just before/during birth onset, immediately after birth completion, and before/during interactions with an approaching school of 50–150 short-finned pilot whales. Vowel-like spectral features (a- and i-type) were detected in birth-event codas and were audible hundreds of meters away. Phylogenetic analysis places the origin of newborn-lifting behavior at >36 million years ago (predating the toothed/baleen whale split), while collaborative group lifting appears restricted to four odontocete species across three families (Delphinidae, Monodontidae, Physeteridae), with a last common ancestor >34 million years ago. Strengths: - Unprecedented multimodal dataset (synchronized audio, aerial video, photo-ID, behavioral logs) for a single wild cetacean birth, enabling cross-validation across modalities and precise event timestamping to within ~1 second. - Rigorous acoustic analysis using VLMC-based vocal style modeling with a proper baseline from legacy Unit A recordings, yielding interpretable, event-linked deviations rather than merely descriptive statistics. - Integration of behavioral observation, bioacoustics, and phylogenetic reconstruction into a unified narrative — the evolutionary analysis grounding cooperative lifting in deep cetacean history adds genuine comparative biology value. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Single-event study (n=1 birth) from a single social unit; all behavioral and acoustic claims are inherently anecdotal in the statistical sense — no replication across births, units, or clans is possible, and generalizability to sperm whales broadly is unknown. - Individual whale attribution of vocalizations is impossible with a single omnidirectional hydrophone — the VLMC analysis captures group-level vocal style, not individual contributions, leaving open who is vocalizing and why during each event transition. - The pilot whale interaction is described and discussed but its nature (aggression, harassment, curiosity, or interspecific altruism) remains unresolved, and the behavioral coding (open jaws, erratic movement) is qualitative and observer-dependent.
Recommended
#41 — Innovative (73)

Language Models Don't Know What You Want: Evaluating Personalization in Deep Research Needs Real Users

Nishant Balepur, Malachi Hamada, Varsha Kishore, Sergey Feldman, Amanpreet Singh, Pao Siangliulue, Joseph Chee Chang, Eunsol Choi, Jordan Lee Boyd-Graber, Aakanksha Naik · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: This paper earns a high score for its rigorous empirical demonstration that LLM judges systematically fail to predict real user satisfaction in personalized deep research — a finding with broad implications for the field's over-reliance on automated evaluation. The taxonomy of nine failure modes grounded in 1,044 human judgments provides actionable, concrete targets. However, the contribution is primarily evaluative rather than methodological, the user study is limited to 21 CS researchers, and the personalization system itself uses standard prompt-based techniques without exploring fine-tuned or retrieval-augmented alternatives.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deep Research (DR) systems synthesize scientific literature into multi-section reports to help researchers cope with explosive publication growth, yet virtually none adapt to the individual researcher asking the query. Personalized DR could tailor content focus, framing, and depth to a user's background — but the field lacks both systems that do this and evaluation methodology that reveals whether personalization actually helps. A survey of 31 ACL'25 personalization papers finds that all evaluate offline (synthetic data and/or LLM judges) and only two run studies with real users, raising the question of whether offline evaluations adequately capture what users value. Methodology: The paper introduces MYSCHOLARQA (MYSQA), a three-stage personalized DR tool built on top of SCHOLARQA. Stage 1 constructs a persistent researcher profile (25 sentence-level inferences across five aspects: knowledge, research style, writing style, positions, audience) from researcher-selected papers using a reasoning LLM. Stage 2, given a user query, generates 16 personalized and generic actions across four categories (content, style, specificity, research ideas) that the user can toggle and edit before submission. Stage 3 runs a modified SCHOLARQA pipeline that injects the approved actions into all execution prompts (retrieval, section planning, generation) and highlights which text spans correspond to each action. The paper then conducts both offline evaluation (synthetic dataset of 281/291 dev/test query-user pairs from ScholarQA-CS2 and CS-PaperSum, 16 LLM-judged metrics, comparisons against OPENSCHOLAR, STORM, Perplexity Sonar, and OpenAI o3 DR) and online evaluation (90-minute interviews with 21 active CS researchers on Upwork, think-aloud protocol, open thematic analysis of 1,044 satisfaction judgments). An additional experiment tests whether four off-the-shelf LLM judges (Gemini-2.5 Flash, GPT-4.1, Claude-4 Sonnet, DeepSeek-V3) can predict the nine discovered failure modes as binary classifiers. Key Results: On offline benchmarks MYSQA performs well: profiles achieve 97% inference accuracy (Gemini-Pro), 99.4% category accuracy, and specificity scores of ~4.0/5; personalized actions win over generic actions at 91–95% win rate; reports beat all baselines on 3/5 quality metrics (answer coverage 91.4, citation precision 91.8, citation recall 81.4) and beat the base SQA system across the board. In the user study, 73% of profiles, actions, and reports are satisfactory. However, 27% reveal nine failure modes entirely invisible to offline metrics: four profile errors (DOMAIN 27.6% — terminology mismatches; OVERCLAIM 17.9% — overgeneralization; CONVENTION 12.8% — field-generic inferences; CONTRAST 12.2% — misrepresented contrasts), two action errors (NARROW 43.8% — overly constraining actions; OFFTOPIC 23.6% — query drift), and three report errors (UNINFORM 38.0% — insufficient depth; PRESENT 25.3% — wrong format/style; IGNORE 22.8% — dropped requirements). Critically, none of the four LLM judges predict user satisfaction better than a majority-class baseline on any of the nine aspects, regardless of the number of few-shot examples or whether metric definitions are provided. Strengths: - The core empirical finding — that LLM judges systematically fail to predict user satisfaction even with full context, few-shot examples, and well-designed prompts — is demonstrated rigorously with statistical significance testing (Bonferroni-corrected binomial tests) across multiple models and prompt variants. - The qualitative taxonomy of nine failure modes is grounded in 1,044 human judgments with 90% inter-annotator agreement, giving the community concrete, actionable targets for future work. - The system itself makes non-trivial design choices (profile attribution with paper citations, action-level highlights, persistent profiles vs. per-query clarification) that are validated against user preferences in both formative and usability studies. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The online study is limited to 21 CS researchers recruited via Upwork, all using MYSQA specifically, limiting generalizability to other user populations, domains, and DR systems; the nine failure modes may not be exhaustive or representative of non-CS users. - The paper evaluates only prompt-based personalization (profile → action → report via Claude-4 Sonnet) and does not compare against fine-tuned personalization approaches or retrieval-augmented user modeling methods, leaving open whether the identified failure modes are fundamental or artifact of the prompt-only design. - Latency is a practical barrier (profile generation ~3 min, report ~5 min), and the paper notes that MYSQA's positive reception may partly reflect novelty effects rather than sustained utility; longitudinal studies are acknowledged but not conducted.
Recommended 2603.16120 PDF
#42 — Innovative (72)

Therefore I am. I Think (15 ▲ )

Esakkivel Esakkiraja, Sai Rajeswar, Denis Akhiyarov, Rajagopal Venkatesaramani · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 3 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 72 for asking a genuinely important safety question — whether chain-of-thought is causal or post-hoc rationalization — and answering it with a well-designed triangulation of probing, causal steering, and behavioral analysis. The finding that tool-calling decisions are encoded at >90% AUROC before any reasoning token is produced has direct implications for CoT-based oversight. The narrow scope (two small models) and highly variable flip rates (7-79%) prevent stronger conclusions about frontier-scale generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large language reasoning models (e.g., o1, DeepSeek-R1) produce chain-of-thought (CoT) traces before arriving at decisions, creating an implicit assumption that visible deliberation drives action selection. This paper challenges that assumption for tool-augmented reasoning models: does a model arrive at an action choice through its chain-of-thought, or does it encode the decision before visible reasoning begins, with CoT serving merely as post-hoc rationalization? The question matters for AI safety and reliability—if CoT is performative rather than causal, it cannot be trusted as an explanatory or oversight mechanism. Methodology: The authors study tool-calling decisions (call vs. no-call) as a binary, interpretable proxy for action choice. They evaluate two open-weight reasoning models (Qwen3-4B and GLM-Z1-9B) on two benchmarks: NVIDIA When2Call (3,652 examples covering tool_call, direct, request_for_info, and cannot_answer categories) and BFCL (combining Irrelevance and Simple subsets). Three methodological pillars are combined: (1) Linear probing — logistic regression probes trained on residual-stream activations at the `pre_gen` token (before any reasoning token is produced) and at multiple percentile positions through the thinking trace, evaluated via 5-fold stratified cross-validation with AUROC; (2) Activation steering — a mean-difference steering vector (v = μ+ − μ−) computed from `pre_gen` activations and injected/suppressed at inference by adding αv to the residual stream (α ∈ {4, 8, 12}), measuring flip rates on 100 held-out examples; (3) Behavioral analysis — blinded pairwise classification of baseline vs. steered CoT traces by GPT-5.4 and Claude Sonnet 4.6 as judges, assigning each steered response to one of six categories (seamless divergence, confabulated support, constraint override, inflated deliberation, decision instability, no meaningful difference). Key Results: Pre-generation probes achieve AUROC >90% in all four model-benchmark combinations, and >95% in three of four, demonstrating that tool-calling decisions are encoded before a single reasoning token is produced. Accuracy dips mid-reasoning (~5–10% of the thinking trace) but recovers to near-perfect by `think_end`. Pre-generation probe decisions agree with `think_end` decisions >80% of the time, which in turn match actual model outputs with near-perfect accuracy. Activation steering produces behavior flips in 7–79% of examples depending on model, benchmark, and α. For Qwen3-4B at α=12 on When2Call, suppression flips 49% of tool-call examples to no-tool, and injection flips 62% of no-tool examples to tool-call; thinking mode is consistently more steerable than no-thinking mode. Steering inflates CoT token count substantially in most conditions (average ratio 1.38–2.18×), with resistant examples also showing inflation (up to 2.30×) even when the final decision does not change. Behavioral analysis shows that when injection succeeds, Qwen3 primarily exhibits confabulated support (57% of flipped cases on When2Call), while GLM shows more resistance with no meaningful difference as the dominant class. A control experiment using an unrelated steering direction (True/False from ProntoQA) produces 0% flip rates, confirming specificity of the effect. Strengths: - Combines three complementary methods (probing, causal steering, behavioral analysis) to build a coherent mechanistic picture rather than relying on a single line of evidence. - The causal intervention (activation steering) with a carefully chosen control direction (ProntoQA True/False) substantially strengthens the inferential validity beyond mere correlation from probing. - Behavioral taxonomy with dual LLM judges and reversed presentation order adds qualitative texture and inter-judge agreement measurement to what would otherwise be purely quantitative flip-rate results. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Scope is narrow: only two relatively small open-weight models (4B and 9B parameters) are subjected to causal analysis; GPT-OSS-20B is excluded from steering due to MoE architecture incompatibilities, limiting generalizability to frontier-scale models. - Flip rates are modest and highly variable (7–79%), making it difficult to characterize how reliably decisions are pre-encoded versus decided during reasoning; the paper does not deeply analyze what differentiates the resistant examples from the flipped ones. - The behavioral taxonomy relies on LLM judges, whose reliability for fine-grained distinctions (e.g., confabulated support vs. constraint override) is uncertain—judge disagreement rates reach 38% in some BFCL conditions, which the paper acknowledges but does not resolve.
Recommended 2604.01202 PDF
#43 — Innovative (72)

Navigating the Mirage: A Dual-Path Agentic Framework for Robust Misleading Chart Question Answering

Yanjie Zhang, Yafei Li, Rui Sheng, Zixin Chen, Yanna Lin, Huamin Qu, Lei Chen, Yushi Sun · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Scores 72 for a well-motivated decomposition of the misleading chart problem into spatial misalignment and cognitive sycophancy, with targeted mechanisms for each. The asymmetric GRPO reward penalizing trap answers at 2× is a creative training signal, and the result that a trained 8B model surpasses Gemini-3.1-Pro on both deceptive and standard charts is notable. However, evaluation is limited to small multiple-choice benchmarks and depends heavily on external components.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are surprisingly vulnerable to misleading charts—visualizations that exploit cognitive biases through axis manipulation, cherry-picking, or disproportionate encoding. On targeted benchmarks like Misleading ChartQA, state-of-the-art VLMs score below 50% accuracy, performing no better than untrained humans. The root cause is "cognitive sycophancy": models process charts holistically and latch onto macro visual trends (e.g., a downward slope) while ignoring contradicting numerical ground truth. Prior OCR-enhanced pipelines address part of the problem but introduce catastrophic entity misalignment—extracted numbers lack spatial context to distinguish axis ticks from data labels. Methodology: The paper proposes ChartCynics, an agentic dual-path framework that decouples visual perception from numerical verification. The Diagnostic Vision Path uses a graphic element detector (nemotron-graphic-elements-v1) to extract high-resolution ROI crops of chart components (title, legend, axes). A Diagnostic Agent performs a "blind test"—inspecting only crops without seeing the question—to produce a structured report containing a factual Diagnosis and an Action Directive (e.g., "axis is inverted; ignore visual slope"). A separate Reasoning Agent then anchors its chain-of-thought to this directive under an unconditional trust policy. The OCR-Driven Data Path uses LlamaParse (backed by GPT-4o) to serialize chart content into structured Markdown, with a dynamic dual-tier trust system distinguishing high-confidence explicit data labels from less reliable axis ticks. An Agentic Summarizer fuses both paths via a five-step Detective Chain-of-Thought (D-CoT): perception audit, numerical anchoring, deception mapping, sufficiency check, and adversarial trap rejection. The Summarizer is optimized in two stages: (1) Oracle-Informed SFT distills reasoning traces from a Qwen3-VL-32B teacher using 5,238 examples, constraining the teacher to reference only visible chart elements despite having access to ground-truth CSV data; (2) Deception-Aware GRPO applies asymmetric reward shaping—correct answers yield +1.0, but selecting the specific visual trap answer incurs −2.0—alongside rewards for numerical grounding (Spearman rank correlation), semantic contradiction identification, and structural format compliance. Key Results: On the Misleading ChartQA (MC) test set (305 samples), ChartCynics achieves 74.43% accuracy with the Qwen3-VL-8B backbone, a +28.86 absolute point gain over the 45.57% baseline and surpassing Gemini-3.1-Pro (70.49%). On the out-of-domain CDCC benchmark (110 samples), it reaches 64.55% versus the baseline's 35.45%. The training-free framework alone yields substantial gains: +15.09% on Qwen3-VL-8B and +16.72% on GPT-o4-mini. The WM (wrong due to misleader) rate for the fully optimized model drops from 40.00% to 11.15% on MC. Critically, on the Mixed Standard and Misleading Benchmark (MSMB), ChartCynics (train-free) achieves 94.26% on standard charts versus ChartMoE's 88.52%, demonstrating that the skeptical paradigm does not cause over-skepticism on benign data. Ablations confirm that neither OCR-only nor crop-augmented VLM alone matches the full dual-path system, and that GRPO provides a meaningful 5.9-point accuracy lift beyond SFT alone while compressing the trap error rate further. Strengths: - The decoupled architecture cleanly separates two failure modes (spatial misalignment and visual sycophancy) and addresses each with a targeted mechanism, yielding a coherent and well-motivated design. - The asymmetric GRPO reward that penalizes trap selection twice as heavily as generic errors is a principled training signal that directly operationalizes the desired behavior rather than relying on generic accuracy rewards. - The result that a trained 8B open-source model outperforms much larger proprietary systems (Gemini-3.1-Pro) on both deceptive and standard benchmarks is striking and practically significant. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The system relies on two external, non-trivial components (LlamaParse with GPT-4o, and a separate graphic element detector) that add latency, cost, and failure modes outside the training loop; robustness to OCR or detection errors under distribution shift is not systematically characterized. - Evaluation uses only multiple-choice questions on two relatively small benchmarks (305 and 110 samples), which may not reflect the diversity of real-world misleading chart types or open-ended reasoning demands; generalization to free-form answers is untested. - The SFT training data is derived from a single teacher model (Qwen3-VL-32B) on the same distribution as the primary test benchmark (Misleading ChartQA), raising questions about whether performance gains reflect genuine reasoning acquisition or benchmark-specific pattern learning.
Search Results 2603.28583 PDF
#44 — Innovative (71)

Attention-aware Inference Optimizations for Large Vision-Language Models with Memory-efficient Decoding

Fatih Ilhan, Gaowen Liu, Ramana Rao Kompella, Selim Furkan Tekin, Tiansheng Huang, Zachary Yahn, Yichang Xu, Ling Liu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: AttentionPack introduces a genuinely novel insight: applying randomized SVD across merged attention heads rather than per-head yields dramatically better compression ratios (9.18× vs. 4.18× explained variance). The attention-aware partial decompression mechanism elegantly reuses already-computed attention statistics to dynamically allocate rank, and the demonstrated orthogonality with quantization and eviction methods shows practical composability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) incur severe memory overhead during autoregressive decoding due to the KV cache, which grows with sequence length, batch size, and hidden dimension. Visual tokens — numbering 256–576 per image — dominate cache size, and for a 13B-parameter VLM processing 16 images at batch size 64, the cache alone can reach ~214 GB. Existing mitigations fall into two categories: token eviction (H2O, ScissorHands, FastV), which leaves the hidden-dimension footprint unchanged; and quantization (KVQuant, GEAR), which struggles with outliers and hardware compatibility. Neither approach exploits the structure of visual token representations across attention heads. Methodology: AttentionPack introduces two complementary mechanisms. First, multi-head KV compression: after the prefill phase, the key and value matrices for visual and textual tokens are compressed separately using randomized SVD applied to the cache merged across all heads (rather than per-head). This exploits the empirically observed low-rank structure of visual token vectors — combining heads before SVD achieves 9.18× explained-variance compression vs. 4.18× per-head for keys. The result is two low-rank factors per token type: a compressed cache matrix and a decompression matrix. Compression ratio is tunable via rank hyperparameters Rkv and Rvv. Second, attention-aware partial decompression: rather than fully decompressing all tokens at every decoding step, the method tracks per-token importance scores using an exponential moving average of accumulated attention weights (Eq. 1). Tokens are partitioned into groups; high-importance tokens are decompressed at full rank while low-importance tokens use a reduced rank (e.g., 1/4 of the compression rank), cutting decompression FLOPs by up to 67.5% with negligible quality loss. A fused CUDA kernel integrates decompression directly into the FlashAttention SRAM tiling loop for further latency reduction. Key Results: On LLaVA1.5-7B with rank-64 compression, AttentionPack achieves 5.09× cache reduction (328 MB → 64.5 MB per instance) with accuracy changes of +0.24/−0.24/+0.32 percentage points across A-OKVQA, MMMU, and OCR-VQA versus full KV caching — matching or beating all baselines (ScissorHands 2×, H2O 2×, Minicache 4.51×) at higher compression. Throughput in batch inference increases by +54% at rank 64. On VideoLLaVA-7B, compression reaches 8.11× (1114.6 MB → 137.5 MB) with +60% batch throughput and only 0.39%/0.05% accuracy degradation versus FastV. When combined with 4-bit quantization, cache drops to 15.5 MB (+115% throughput) with ~0.5% quality loss versus standalone KVQuant. The fused kernel implementation halves decoding latency in both single and batch settings compared to the standard implementation. On LongBench text QA (LLaMA3.1-8B), AttentionPack achieves 40% cache reduction under the same context length with +0.6% F1 improvement over H2O under matched memory budgets. Strengths: - The insight to apply SVD across combined heads rather than per-head is empirically well-motivated and yields significantly higher compression for the same quality budget (9.18× vs. 4.18× explained-variance ratio for keys). - The attention-aware partial decompression is elegant: it reuses already-computed attention statistics to dynamically allocate decompression rank, reducing FLOPs without discarding tokens. - Strong orthogonality with existing methods (eviction, quantization, GQA, FlashAttention kernel fusion) is demonstrated concretely, making the approach practically composable. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Compression is applied separately to visual and textual tokens but not to newly generated tokens — the uncompressed buffer grows between compression events (period Tp), so peak memory savings are lower than the reported averages. - Single-instance latency can increase by up to 30% from decompression overhead, and the improvement claimed ("up to 8×") is peak batch throughput, not single-query latency — this distinction is underemphasized. - Experiments are limited to LLaVA-family and QwenVL architectures on relatively short-context benchmarks; applicability to models with millions of context tokens or architectures with very different visual token counts (e.g., high-resolution tiling models producing 2000+ tokens per image) is not demonstrated.
Search Results 2603.23914 PDF
#45 — Innovative (71)

PUPPET: Neural - Symbolic Standardized Patients for Mental Health

openreview.net · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: PUPPET's neuro-symbolic Observe-Think-Behave architecture is a creative solution to the well-documented compliance and stochasticity failure modes of pure LLM patient simulations, and the exposed causal chain for pedagogical feedback is a meaningful contribution beyond prior work. However, the core technical components (probabilistic logic programming, LLM-based evaluation, state machines) are individually well-established, and the text-only limitation significantly constrains ecological validity for clinical training where paralinguistic cues are essential.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mental health systems face a critical therapist shortage, creating demand for scalable training solutions. The gold standard for clinical training is Standardized Patients (SPs) — trained actors who simulate psychological conditions for realistic, safe practice. Human SPs are scarce and costly. While LLM-based patient simulations have achieved conversational realism, they fail as Virtual Standardized Patients on two fronts: (1) they do not produce faithful, clinically appropriate reactions to trainee errors (either ignoring the error or exhibiting unrealistic compliance), and (2) their black-box nature prevents explainable pedagogical feedback — trainees cannot discern whether a patient reaction was caused by their specific intervention or model stochasticity. This gap prevents genuine skill acquisition. Methodology: The paper proposes PUPPET, the first neural-symbolic Virtual Standardized Patient, governed by an Observe-Think-Behave (OTB) architecture. The Observe module uses a Chain-of-Thought LLM valuator to assign continuous truth values ∈ [0,1] to atomic propositions representing trainee and patient behaviors (e.g., ShowEmpathy, ExpressEmotion). The Think module implements a Probabilistic Logic Programming (PLP) inference engine: trainer-defined IF-THEN rules are composed from atomic propositions using probabilistic logic operators (¬, ∧, ∨, →), yielding rule truth values that map to a Probabilistic State Machine (PSM) governing mental state transitions (e.g., Open → Defensive → Hostile in CBT; Non-Suicidal → Suicidal in crisis scenarios). TopK filtering and renormalization inject controlled stochasticity, preserving natural variability while enforcing clinical logic. The Behave module generates responses conditioned on the updated mental state via an LLM prompted with the patient profile and current state description. Built on top of PUPPET, the PUPPET-TRAINER framework exposes the complete causal chain (Intervention → Rule → State → Response) to trainees as structured, logic-grounded feedback. Three clinical scenarios are supported: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), Motivational Interviewing (MI), and crisis hotline (12356). Key Results: Against three baselines (Base, Profile-Based, AnnaAgent) across CBT and MI scenarios with two backbone LLMs (DeepSeek-V3.2-chat, GPT-4o-mini), PUPPET achieves 74.00% mental state accuracy in MI with DeepSeek (+24 percentage points over the best baseline), along with the highest Persona Fidelity (4.10/5) and Behavioral Authenticity (4.05/5). Ablation shows removing the symbolic component collapses accuracy from 74% to 54%, and replacing probabilistic logic with hard deterministic rules drops Behavioral Authenticity to 3.59 — validating that both components are necessary. On pedagogical quality, five licensed counselors rated PUPPET-TRAINER significantly above LLM-as-a-Supervisor on all five metrics (Granularity, Scenario Faithfulness, Causal Consistency, Structural Insight, Usefulness; all p < 0.05). A longitudinal self-efficacy study (N=30, 5 weeks, CASES-R scale) showed both PUPPET-TRAINER variants outperformed the baseline (p < 0.05 from week 2 onward), with the Progressive Rule variant sustaining growth through week 5 while the Fixed Rule group plateaued. Discriminant validity experiments showed PUPPET correctly generates distinct state trajectories for Expert, Novice, and Toxic interaction profiles, and novice trajectories after 5 sessions converge toward expert patterns. Strengths: - The neuro-symbolic design elegantly solves the compliance/randomness failure modes of pure LLM baselines: probabilistic logic preserves naturalistic variability while trainer-defined rules guarantee clinical fidelity, a combination not previously achieved in patient simulation. - The PUPPET-TRAINER feedback mechanism is genuinely novel — exposing a full symbolic causal chain (rule ID, confidence, triggered state transition) rather than post-hoc LLM commentary, enabling Situated Learning and Schema Acquisition grounded in established pedagogical theory. - Evaluation is unusually thorough for an NLP paper: automatic metrics (BERTScore, mental state accuracy), expert human ratings on theory-grounded rubrics, a longitudinal RCT-style self-efficacy study, and discriminant validity testing together form a compelling multidimensional case. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The symbolic rule base requires substantial expert authorship per scenario — the paper shows detailed rule tables for three scenarios but does not address the scalability or cost of constructing these for new therapeutic modalities or populations, nor the risk that imperfect rules produce systematically incorrect patient behavior. - The system is text-only, omitting the paralinguistic signals (prosody, facial expression, body language) that are critical in real clinical practice and that human SPs are specifically trained to use, limiting ecological validity. - The self-efficacy study (N=30, n=10 per group) is small, involves novice counselors only, and lacks long-term follow-up or real-patient outcome measures, leaving open whether PUPPET-TRAINER training transfers to clinical competence beyond self-reported confidence.
Search Results
#46 — Innovative (71)

Improving Inference Efficiency of Hybrid State Space Models through Speculative Decoding

escholarship.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scores 71 for solving the non-trivial state backtracking problem that blocked speculative decoding for SSMs, with two complementary algorithms (Activation Replay and Joint Backtracking) offering a clean memory/compute tradeoff. The STree formulation reducing tree-structured SSM computation to a single matrix product via the topology mask is a genuine structural insight. Hardware-aware Triton kernels demonstrate practical realizability. Limited by weak n-gram drafting in Chapter 2 and modest absolute speedups compared to Transformer speculative decoding baselines.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Autoregressive LLM inference is bottlenecked by memory bandwidth: each forward pass loads the entire model weight to generate a single token, leaving compute cores severely underutilized. Speculative decoding addresses this for Transformers by drafting multiple candidate tokens with a cheap model and verifying them in parallel with the target model. State Space Models (SSMs) like Mamba have even lower per-token compute than Transformers (linear vs. quadratic in context length), making their memory-bandwidth bottleneck more severe and speculative decoding potentially more impactful. However, SSMs discard intermediate states after each forward pass and maintain only a compact Markov state—unlike the KV-cache in Transformers—making state backtracking after token rejection a non-trivial challenge. No prior work had solved this, and existing speculative decoding methods were inapplicable to SSMs. This thesis fills that gap with two complementary contributions: (1) efficient single-sequence and multi-sequence speculative decoding for SSMs, and (2) the first tree-based speculative decoding framework for SSMs and hybrid architectures. Methodology: Chapter 2 addresses the core backtracking problem via two algorithms. Activation Replay (ARP) caches the inputs to the SSM block (pre-state activations) during verification; after the number of accepted tokens is known, a cheap replay pass re-runs only the state-update kernel on those activations to recover the correct state. Joint Backtracking and Verification avoids a separate replay pass by appending a padded "backtracking sequence" to the next verification batch; "no-op" tokens (which set transition matrix A to identity and input matrices B, C to zero) allow this sequence to advance the SSM state in parallel with ongoing verification, at the cost of one extra state copy in memory. Both methods are paired with a custom fused multi-step Selective-Scan-Update (SSU) Triton kernel that keeps the large SSM state in GPU shared memory across multiple steps, avoiding repeated HBM round-trips. Just-In-Time (JIT) state copying within CUDA graphs further reduces overhead. The framework is extended to multi-sequence speculation: SSMs' fixed-state dimension (unlike the variable-length KV-cache) allows batching N sequences with M drafts each as an N×M batch, modulating speculation depth dynamically based on incoming traffic modeled as a Poisson process. Chapter 3 introduces STree (State-Space Speculative Tree), the first tree-based speculative decoding method for SSMs. Transformers support packed tree verification via a topology-aware attention mask; SSMs lack this because their scan assumes strictly causal linear sequences. The key insight is that under diagonal A matrices, the accumulated state transition for any tree node i can be written as Atree = L × Alog, where L is the tree mask and Alog stacks the log-diagonal of each token's A matrix. This allows all tree outputs to be computed from a single packed sequence with one shared initial state—no repeated tokens, no extra per-branch states. A hardware-aware Triton kernel implements this tree scan keeping all intermediate results in shared memory. The 1D convolution is similarly adapted via index-gathering. Activation Replay then recovers the correct state for the accepted prefix before the next iteration. Key Results: For single-sequence speculative decoding (Chapter 2), using Mamba-2-2.7B with n-gram (PNG) drafting on MT-Bench, GSM8K, and HumanEval: Joint Backtracking achieves an average 1.82× wall-clock speedup (1.60×, 1.95×, 1.90× respectively); ARP achieves 1.64× on average. Across model sizes (1.3B–13B), relative overhead of the verification framework decreases with model size: for 6 speculated tokens, ARP overhead drops from 1.98× to 1.22× and Joint from 1.32× to 1.16×, indicating favorable scaling. The fused SSU kernel improves per-step runtime by 17.8% over chunk scan, and JIT state copy adds another 20.0%, for a combined 39.4% reduction. For multi-sequence speculation, throughput gains range from 3.19× (batch=1) to 1.29× (batch=16), with peak gain at small batch sizes as expected. For STree (Chapter 3), against the unrolled FSS baseline on full binary trees, STree's forward pass scales from 27.6ms to 34.0ms as trees grow from 4 to 6 layers, while FSS jumps from 27.3ms to 59.8ms; FSS also runs out of memory at large tree sizes (M=4/5, N=16 on 24GB GPU) while STree remains feasible. In end-to-end generation on MambaInLlama-8B (50% SSM/Transformer hybrid) with MT-Bench/HumanEval/GSM8K: STree achieves 1.74×/1.95×/1.98× speedup at temperature=0 vs. autoregressive baseline (vs. vanilla SD's 1.69×/1.93×/1.93×), and a more pronounced advantage at temperature=1 (1.36×/1.50×/1.45× vs. 1.23×/1.32×/1.28×). With a more trained distilled draft model (264K steps), STree reaches 64.30 tokens/sec (1.62×) vs. vanilla SD's 56.49 (1.42×). The relative overhead of STree vs. vanilla SD decreases as model size grows (gap shrinks from 48.9% at 2.3B to 18.9% at 23B). Strengths: - Identifies and rigorously addresses the core algorithmic obstacle (state backtracking) with two complementary methods that expose a memory/compute tradeoff, giving practitioners a meaningful choice. - The STree formulation is mathematically clean: reducing tree-structured SSM computation to a single matrix product via the topology mask and diagonal-A assumption is a genuine structural insight, not just an engineering trick. - Hardware-aware implementation throughout (custom Triton/CUDA kernels, CUDA Graph compliance, JIT state copies) demonstrates that the theoretical gains are realizable in practice with competitive absolute numbers. - The analysis in Section 3.2 deriving closed-form acceptance-length thresholds for when tree decoding helps (different for pure-SSM vs. hybrid models) is practically useful and absent from prior speculative decoding work. Weaknesses / Limitations: - End-to-end results in Chapter 2 use only n-gram (PNG) drafting—a weak, model-free heuristic. No results with a learned or distilled smaller SSM draft model, which significantly limits the demonstrated speedup ceiling and comparability to state-of-the-art Transformer speculative decoding results (e.g., EAGLE achieves 3×+). - STree's advantage over vanilla SD on H100 largely disappears at temperature=0 (greedy), where it matches but does not exceed vanilla SD. The improvement is sensitive to hardware bandwidth: faster GPUs reduce the memory-bandwidth bottleneck that speculative decoding exploits, limiting generality of the approach across hardware generations. - Experiments are limited to models up to 8B parameters with real pretrained weights; larger models (7B, 13B, 23B) are tested only with random weights for latency profiling, making it unclear whether quality-adjusted speedups hold at scale. - The draft model for STree experiments is distilled from the target model using a 2-layer SSM trained for 264K steps—a non-standard, potentially suboptimal setup that makes fair comparison with speculative decoding on Transformer-based models difficult.

#47 — Innovative (71)

Out of Sight but Not Out of Mind: Hybrid Memory for Dynamic Video World Models (133 ▲ )

Kaijin Chen, Dingkang Liang, Xin Zhou, Yikang Ding, Xiaoqiang Liu, Pengfei Wan, Xiang Bai · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 30 Mar 2026
Why this rank: HyDRA identifies a clear and previously unaddressed failure mode of video world models — dynamic subject exit-and-reentry — and formalizes the Hybrid Memory paradigm as a clean conceptual separation of static archival and dynamic tracking. The HM-World benchmark with deliberately engineered exit-entry events fills a genuine evaluation gap. The Dynamic Retrieval Attention mechanism is technically sound, efficiently replacing global self-attention with top-K retrieval over compact spatiotemporal tokens. The limitation to synthetic-only quantitative evaluation and degradation with multiple subjects tempers the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing video world models treat environments as static canvases—they excel at memorizing and reconstructing static backgrounds under camera viewpoint changes, but fail when dynamic subjects (pedestrians, animals) exit and later re-enter the camera frame. When subjects go out of view, current methods either freeze them, distort them, or lose them entirely upon re-entry. This is a fundamental gap because the physical world contains independently-moving agents whose trajectories must be extrapolated even when they are off-screen. The core challenge is threefold: (1) spatiotemporal decoupling of camera ego-motion from subject motion, (2) out-of-view trajectory extrapolation with no direct visual evidence, and (3) feature entanglement between static background and dynamic subject latents in standard diffusion representations. Methodology: The paper introduces three contributions. First, it formalizes the Hybrid Memory paradigm: models must simultaneously act as archivists for static backgrounds and motion trackers for dynamic subjects during out-of-view intervals. Second, it constructs HM-World, a 59K-clip synthetic dataset rendered in Unreal Engine 5 with decoupled camera and subject trajectories, 17 scenes, 49 subjects, 10 subject motion paths, and 28 camera trajectory types—uniquely featuring deliberate exit-entry events (back-and-forth camera motions) absent from prior datasets. Third, it proposes HyDRA (Hybrid Dynamic Retrieval Attention), built on Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B (a 1.3B-parameter video diffusion transformer). HyDRA has two key modules: (a) a 3D-convolution Memory Tokenizer that compresses context latents into spatiotemporally-aware tokens (kernel size 2×4×4, capturing cross-frame motion in the temporal dimension); and (b) Dynamic Retrieval Attention, which replaces standard 3D self-attention. For each denoising target latent, it computes spatiotemporal affinity between the query (spatially pooled) and each memory token key slice via element-wise inner products, selects the top-K most relevant tokens (K=10), concatenates them with a local temporal window, and performs standard attention. This avoids flooding the model with irrelevant context and enables selective recall of the hidden subject's appearance and motion. Key Results: On the 1,000-sample HM-World test set (held-out scenes and subjects), HyDRA achieves PSNR 20.357, SSIM 0.606, LPIPS 0.289, DSCctx 0.827, and DSCGT 0.849—outperforming the camera-conditioned Wan2.1 baseline (PSNR 18.696, SSIM 0.517), DFoT (PSNR 17.693), and Context-as-Memory (PSNR 18.921) across all metrics. Against the commercial WorldPlay model evaluated zero-shot, HyDRA leads by 5.5 PSNR points. Ablations confirm that temporal interaction in the Memory Tokenizer is critical (removing it drops PSNR by 1.28), that 10 retrieved tokens is sufficient (5 causes artifact hallucinations), and that dynamic affinity retrieval outperforms FOV-based geometric retrieval (Subject Consistency +1.8 points) by selecting feature-relevant tokens rather than geometrically-closest camera poses. Strengths: - Identifies a clear, previously unaddressed failure mode of video world models and provides a clean formalization of the Hybrid Memory paradigm that separates static and dynamic consistency requirements. - HM-World is a well-designed benchmark: synthetic rendering provides ground-truth trajectories and subject poses, and the deliberately engineered exit-entry events create rigorous test conditions not found in existing datasets. - The Dynamic Retrieval Attention is technically sound and practically efficient—replacing global self-attention with top-K retrieval over compact tokens reduces computational cost while improving subject consistency, with ablations validating each design choice. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The dataset is entirely synthetic (Unreal Engine 5), and while open-domain qualitative results are shown, there is no quantitative evaluation on real-world video, leaving generalization to unconstrained scenes unvalidated. - Performance degrades with three or more subjects or severe occlusions, yet the paper provides only qualitative acknowledgment of this limitation without quantitative analysis of the failure regime. - The method is evaluated only on a single base model (Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B at 1.3B parameters), leaving open whether the architectural choices and gains transfer to larger or architecturally different video generation backbones.
