#48 — Innovative (71)

MiroEval: Benchmarking Multimodal Deep Research Agents in Process and Outcome (52 ▲ )

Fangda Ye, Yuxin Hu, Pengxiang Zhu, Yibo Li, Ziqi Jin, Yao Xiao, Yibo Wang, Lei Wang, Zhen Zhang, Lu Wang, Yue Deng, Bin Wang, Yifan Zhang, Liangcai Su, Xinyu Wang, He Zhao, Chen Wei, Qiang Ren, Bryan Hooi, An Bo, Shuicheng Yan, Lidong Bing · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 2 Apr 2026
Why this rank: MiroEval's process-centric evaluation layer — auditing research trajectories and measuring bidirectional process-report alignment — is a genuine methodological contribution that reveals failure modes invisible to output-only benchmarks. However, the benchmark's small size (100 tasks) and construction from internal MiroMind testing data create a significant conflict-of-interest concern that tempers the innovation score.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deep research systems — LLM agents that autonomously plan multi-step web investigations, gather evidence, and synthesize long-form reports — are proliferating rapidly, but evaluation methodology has not kept pace. Existing benchmarks suffer from four compounding limitations: they assess only the final report using fixed rubrics (ignoring the underlying research process); they provide limited multimodal coverage beyond short-form QA; they rely on synthetic or academic queries that fail to capture real-world complexity; and they are static, becoming stale as the information landscape evolves. These gaps prevent practitioners from understanding whether a system genuinely conducts rigorous, traceable investigation or merely produces a plausible-looking document. Methodology: MiroEval introduces a benchmark of 100 tasks (70 text-only, 30 multimodal) and a three-layer evaluation suite. Benchmark construction follows a dual path: 65 queries are derived by privacy-preserving rewriting of real user patterns observed during internal testing of the MiroMind system, stratified by difficulty and routed to six rewriting strategies (basic retrieval through contradiction/error-premise detection); 35 queries are generated automatically via trend-grounded LLM generation seeded with Serper API headlines and filtered through three stages (search validation, deep-research necessity at confidence ≥ 0.7, and inverse quality assessment retaining only queries where parametric-only baselines score ≤ 0.75). Both paths support periodic refresh. The evaluation suite has three layers: (1) Adaptive Synthesis Quality — dynamically generates task-specific rubric dimensions (fixed: Coverage, Insight, Instruction-following, Clarity; dynamic: 1–3 domain-specific expertise or grounding dimensions) with inferred importance weights, enabling concrete attachment-specific checkpoints rather than generic criteria; (2) Agentic Factuality — decomposes reports into atomic claims, retrieves evidence from live web and uploaded attachments via native multimodal or retrieval-augmented processing, and labels each claim RIGHT / WRONG / CONFLICT / UNKNOWN; (3) Process-Centric Evaluation — parses raw research trajectories into structured atomic units, scores five intrinsic dimensions (Search Breadth, Analytical Depth, Progressive Refinement, Critical Thinking, Efficiency), and measures bidirectional process–report alignment (P→R: key findings reaching the report; R→P: report conclusions traceable to the process) plus contradiction detection. The overall process score is a weighted combination of intrinsic quality and alignment (Equation 4). Thirteen commercial deep research systems are evaluated, using GPT-5.1/5.2 and GPT-5-mini as judge models. Key Results: Three principal findings emerge. First, the three evaluation dimensions are non-redundant: system rankings shift substantially across dimensions. Kimi-K2.5 leads non-MiroThinker systems on Synthesis (75.7) but ranks near the bottom on Factuality (65.4, trailing OpenAI by 18 points), while Manus has the lowest Synthesis (55.4) yet a competitive Factuality Ratio (72.6). Second, process quality is a reliable predictor of overall outcome (Pearson r = 0.88 between Process score and combined outcome), but exposes weaknesses invisible to output-level metrics. Analytical Depth (range 41.6–67.3 across systems) is the single most discriminative intrinsic metric, and Efficiency is a universal weakness (even the best system scores only 68.1). Critically, Report→Process (R→P) scores are dramatically lower than Process→Report (P→R) across all systems — the gap exceeds 23 points for MiroThinker-H1 and approaches 30 points for Doubao — indicating a systematic traceability deficit: reports routinely contain claims that cannot be traced back to the documented research trajectory. Third, multimodal tasks are substantially harder: overall scores drop 3–10 points, with Synthesis suffering the largest average decline (~6 points) but Factuality Ratios remaining nearly stable (−0.2 points on average), confirming the bottleneck lies in visual report generation and analytical depth, not factual precision. The MiroThinker series demonstrates the most balanced performance: MiroThinker-H1 achieves 77.5 (text-only) and 74.5 (multimodal) overall, ranking first or second on every individual dimension. Human verification confirms 92.0% benchmark precision (Fleiss' κ > 0.76 for both validity and non-triviality), and a human ranking study with 5 expert annotators yields Kendall's τ = 0.91 between automated and human rankings. Strengths: - The process-centric evaluation layer is a genuine contribution: auditing research trajectories and measuring bidirectional process–report alignment reveals a class of system failures (traceability gaps, wasted retrieval, shallow follow-up) that are completely invisible to output-only benchmarks. - The adaptive synthesis evaluation avoids the brittleness of fixed rubrics by extracting key facts from attachments and generating precise, instance-specific checkpoints (e.g., detecting fabricated growth rates from an image containing only names and ranks), as demonstrated convincingly in the case studies. - The dual-path refreshable construction pipeline, grounded in real user queries and live web trends with three-stage filtering, is a principled approach to benchmark longevity and temporal relevance that most prior work lacks. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is small (100 tasks total, 30 multimodal) and was constructed using internal testing data from the MiroMind system itself, raising a potential conflict of interest: the benchmark's complexity profile and modality distribution directly reflect the MiroThinker system's usage patterns, which may systematically favor MiroThinker variants in evaluation. - Process evaluation requires access to intermediate reasoning traces, limiting applicability to closed-source systems and making the process scores non-comparable across systems that expose different granularities of internal state. - The factuality layer identifies cross-source CONFLICT cases but does not resolve them — the CONFLICT label flags disagreements without determining which source is authoritative, leaving a key open problem for high-stakes use cases where knowing which evidence is correct is essential.
Recommended 2603.28407 PDF
#49 — Innovative (71)

TAMI-MPC: Trusted Acceleration of Minimal-Interaction MPC for Efficient Nonlinear Inference

Zhuoran Li, Hanieh Totonchi Asl, Yifei Cai, Ebrahim Nouri, Danella Zhao · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: TAMI-MPC presents a well-integrated hardware/software co-design that meaningfully advances privacy-preserving inference on resource-constrained devices. The protocol insight — exploiting input-independence of one OT operand for offline TEE derivation — is technically sound, and the correlated-randomness reuse scheme yielding 584× speedup is a non-trivial contribution. The conservative security model (TEE confined to offline work) is a genuine improvement over prior TEE-based PPML. However, the 2-party limitation and still-large absolute latencies (108s for ResNet-50) constrain the practical novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Privacy-preserving machine learning as a service (PPMLaaS) relies on secure multi-party computation (MPC) to protect sensitive data during inference. The dominant bottleneck is nonlinear evaluation—functions like ReLU, GELU, Softmax, and Max-pooling all reduce to secure comparison via the Millionaires' protocol. This protocol depends on two expensive primitives: OT-based leaf comparison (requiring random oblivious transfer, ROT) and tree merge via Beaver triple–based multiplication (requiring log₂n interactive rounds). On resource-constrained clients (IoT sensors, wearables with ~500 MHz CPUs and 256 MB RAM) connected over constrained networks (WAN/mobile), nonlinear evaluation causes a ~9× slowdown versus moderate hardware settings. Existing MPC frameworks (CryptFlow2, Cheetah, Bumblebee) were designed for LAN/server-grade hardware and do not address this deployment gap. Methodology: TAMI-MPC combines cryptographic protocol redesign with a specialized FPGA accelerator, targeting both communication and computation bottlenecks. On the protocol side, the key insight is that in standard PPMLaaS pipelines, one OT input to the leaf comparison is always input-independent (a mask derived after a homomorphic linear layer), enabling it to be derived offline from synchronized seeds inside Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs, using Intel SGX). This eliminates the first online round of leaf comparison, reducing it from 2 rounds to 1 and cutting communication from n(k + 2^k) to nk bits. For tree merge, the authors replace the sequential Beaver-triple multiplication tree (log₂n rounds, 4(n−1) ROTs) with a one-round polynomial multiplication: all n leaf comparison outputs are merged in a single round by exchanging masked differences lt_j ⊕ r_j, with pre-shared subset-product correlated randomness derived non-interactively from TEE seeds. Two optimizations further reduce overhead: (1) interleaving leaf comparison and tree merge so that one of the two online messages in FPolyMult is already available from the TEE, halving communication; (2) a correlated-randomness reuse scheme using inclusion-exclusion over active index sets, reducing randomness generation by up to 584× for 64-bit inputs. Critically, the TEE is confined to offline, input-independent tasks; online inference security derives entirely from MPC, so TEE compromise during inference does not expose client or server secrets. On the hardware side, an FPGA accelerator on a Zynq-7000 SoC addresses the two remaining compute bottlenecks: (a) pipeline-aware interleaving of AES key expansion and encryption across 4 parallel units eliminates the loop-carried dependency in the correlation-robust hash (CRH), reducing memory transfers from 58N to 6.25N blocks; (b) a memory-efficient packed polynomial execution model for tree merge uses a precomputed LUT for deterministic randomness access and encodes 512/n comparisons per memory transaction, balancing the pipeline and achieving massive parallelism. Key Results: At the primitive level, TAMI-MPC reduces offline communication to zero and online rounds to 1 for both leaf comparison and tree merge, with 8× less online communication volume (vs. Cheetah/Bumblebee). Correlated randomness generation for tree merge improves by two orders of magnitude—up to 584× speedup at 64-bit input length. The FPGA accelerator achieves: 3.97× speedup on the CRH module, 2.72× on leaf comparison (FComp), and 80.15× on tree merge (FPolyMult), for an overall 3.38× speedup on the full Millionaires' protocol vs. CPU baseline. For activation microbenchmarks over a WAN, speedups reach 7× (ReLU vs. Cheetah), 8.8× (Softmax), and 10× (GeLU vs. Bumblebee), rising to 17× under LAN for GeLU. End-to-end on a mobile network (100 Mbps, 80 ms latency), TAMI-MPC completes ResNet-50 inference in 108s (4.86× over Cheetah, 4.42× over CryptFlow2) and BERT-base in 380s (7.44× over Bumblebee). Hardware utilization is modest: ~30% BRAM and ~1% DSP on a Z-7030, confirming feasibility on embedded platforms. Strengths: - The security model is clearly delineated and conservative: TEE is restricted to offline, input-independent work, so online inference security is entirely MPC-backed and TEE compromise does not break the system—a meaningful improvement over prior TEE-based PPML works. - The correlated-randomness reuse analysis (Opt.#2) is technically non-trivial and yields a dramatic, quantified reduction (up to 584×) that makes the otherwise exponential-appearing one-round polynomial merge tractable. - The hardware/software co-design is tightly integrated: the packed polynomial execution and pipeline-aware AES interleaving directly address the specific memory and compute bottlenecks exposed by the protocol, rather than applying generic optimizations. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper targets 2-party computation only; extension to the general n-party setting is not discussed, limiting applicability to multi-server or federated scenarios. - End-to-end latency numbers (108s for ResNet-50 over mobile) remain large for real-time inference use cases, and the paper does not compare against model-compression or approximation-based alternatives that trade accuracy for lower MPC cost. - The evaluation uses Intel SGX as the sole TEE, which has known side-channel vulnerabilities (Spectre, SGX-specific attacks); the threat model assumes SGX integrity during the offline phase but does not analyze risks from SGX-level side channels leaking the input-independent randomness.
Search Results 2603.24861 PDF
#50 — Innovative (71)

Justitia: Fair and Efficient Scheduling of Task-parallel LLM Agents with Selective Pampering

chenc10.github.io · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Justitia makes a meaningful contribution by adapting classical fair-queuing theory to the emerging problem of task-parallel LLM agent scheduling, introducing the well-motivated memory-centric KV token-time cost model and proving a constant delay bound. The reframing from instantaneous fairness to finish-time fairness is a genuine conceptual advance for GPU serving systems. However, the core scheduling algorithm is a direct adaptation of GPS/virtual-time fair queuing from networking, and the per-agent-type MLP predictor, while pragmatic, limits applicability to known agent types.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern LLM applications increasingly rely on task-parallel agents—groups of concurrent inference requests that collectively produce a single output (e.g., MapReduce summarization, self-consistency reasoning, fact verification). When multiple such agents share a GPU server, existing schedulers fail on at least one of two dimensions: FCFS-based systems (vLLM, Parrot) suffer head-of-line blocking; SJF-based systems improve throughput but starve large agents; fairness-centric schedulers like VTC enforce instantaneous equal resource sharing, which delays every agent compared to monopolized execution. No prior system simultaneously achieves low average completion time and bounded worst-case delay relative to fair sharing. Methodology: Justitia introduces three coordinated mechanisms. First, memory-centric cost modeling defines agent service cost as KV token-time—the cumulative KV cache occupancy across all decode iterations of an inference: c = pd + d²/2, where p is prefill length and d is decode length. This captures the quadratic relationship between generation length and true resource consumption, unlike VTC's linear token-count model. Second, MLP-based demand prediction maintains a separate lightweight 4-layer MLP per agent type, trained on 100 samples using TF-IDF-vectorized prompts, to predict KV token-time at agent arrival time. This exploits the empirically observed stability of per-agent-type resource demand distributions. Third, virtual-time fair queuing adapts the classical GPS/fair-queuing algorithm from network packet scheduling. Each arriving agent is assigned a virtual finish time F_j = V(a_j) + C_j, where V(t) is virtual time advancing at rate M/N_t (total KV blocks divided by active agent count). Agents are served in ascending F_j order using a priority queue (O(log N) selection), giving all inferences of the highest-priority agent exclusive GPU access—the "selective pampering" principle. The paper proves a constant delay bound: f_j − f̄_j ≤ 2c_max + C_max/M, guaranteeing that no agent is delayed more than a constant beyond its GPS completion time regardless of competing arrivals. Key Results: Evaluated on vLLM with LLaMA-7B (A100), LLaMA-13B (4×V100), and Qwen2.5-32B (H800) using 300 mixed agents across 9 agent types at 1×–3× load intensity. Justitia reduces average JCT by 57.5% vs. VTC and 61.1% vs. Parrot. 92% of agents complete no later than under VTC (finish-time fair ratio ≤ 1.0), with a worst-case delay of only 26% above VTC baseline. Ablation shows compute-centric cost modeling (VTC's p+2d formula) degrades JCT by up to 42.3%. MLP prediction achieves 53% average relative error vs. 452% for Distillbert, with 2.16 ms inference overhead vs. 55.7 ms, and trains in ~1 minute vs. ~2 hours. Performance degrades only 9.5% even with 3× cost prediction noise. Scheduling overhead stays under 10 ms across all tested arrival rates. The starvation experiment confirms that large-agent delay under SRJF grows unboundedly with mice-job count, while Justitia's delay remains bounded. Strengths: - The reframing of fairness from instantaneous resource sharing to finish-time fairness is conceptually well-motivated and unlocks a practical efficiency improvement without sacrificing theoretical guarantees; the constant delay bound (Theorem B.1) is a genuine formal contribution. - Memory-centric cost modeling is empirically validated to be substantially more accurate than token-count modeling, and the quadratic KV token-time metric is a natural fit for the KV-cache-bounded serving regime of vLLM. - The per-agent-type MLP predictor is a pragmatic engineering choice: it exploits agent-specific input stability observed empirically, achieves orders-of-magnitude lower overhead than Distillbert-based alternatives, and requires only 100 training samples. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The per-agent-type MLP model requires prior profiling for each new agent type; the system has no mechanism to handle truly novel or one-off agents, and the claim of "demand stability" may not generalize to open-ended agents with highly variable outputs. - The constant delay bound 2c_max + C_max/M, while formally correct, is not quantitatively tight—C_max can be large for long-running agents, potentially making the guarantee vacuous in practice; the paper does not discuss empirical values of this bound relative to observed delays. - The evaluation uses only a fixed set of 9 agent types with a workload distribution tuned to match prior literature; there is no evaluation of cold-start behavior (new agent types arriving without trained MLPs) or heterogeneous multi-tenant scenarios where tenants submit different agent mixes.
Search Results
#51 — Innovative (71)

Reflect to Inform: Boosting Multimodal Reasoning via Information-Gain-Driven Verification

Shuai Lv, Chang Liu, Feng Tang, Yujie Yuan, Aojun Zhou, Kui Zhang, Xi Yang, Yangqiu Song · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scores 71 for a technically clean self-iterative framework that addresses the well-identified visual drift problem without architectural changes or external teachers. The information gain criterion for filtering redundant reflections is a principled solution to the core reward-hacking failure mode, and the homologous reconstruction principle (validated via KL divergence) is a meaningful design insight. Comprehensive ablations provide strong mechanistic evidence. However, the overall approach builds substantially on existing RLVR and reflection paradigms, gains on hard reasoning benchmarks remain modest, and the three-stage pipeline's computational cost limits practical impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) suffer from "visual drift" during long-form generation: as the output token sequence grows, attention to visual tokens monotonically decays and models fall back on textual priors, producing hallucinations. Prior fixes either inject new visual signals explicitly (high-resolution patches, visual markers, interleaved tokens) — disrupting the autoregressive pipeline with high computational cost — or attempt naive implicit re-focusing without guidance on what to retrieve, degenerating into redundant self-paraphrasing. The paper also identifies a more fundamental instability: the same model can be "visually clear" on one rollout and "visually blind" on another for the identical question. The key observation is that the latent capacity for visual re-attention exists but is unreliably triggered. Methodology: The paper proposes Visual Re-Examination (VRE), a self-iterative training framework that teaches a 7B MLLM (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) to autonomously insert a structured `<reflection>` block during reasoning, re-attending to original image tokens without any architectural changes or additional visual inputs. The pipeline has three stages. First, difficulty-aware cold-start SFT: N=8 rollouts are sampled per question and partitioned by pass rate into stable (always correct), intractable (always wrong), and unstable (mixed) sets. Only unstable samples drive reflection synthesis. For each, the model's own partial reasoning trace is concatenated with the original image+question and re-fed into the same model to generate a reflection. Three reflection types are synthesized: Elaborative (for stable samples, to broaden grounding), Corrective (for wrong unstable rollouts, to fix misperceptions), and Recheck (for correct unstable rollouts, to reduce accidental correctness). A strict Information Gain criterion filters out redundant self-paraphrasing, keeping only reflections that introduce novel visual evidence. Correct-answer trajectories form the cold-start SFT dataset (~80k samples). Second, GRPO-based RLVR: the cold-start model generates reflection-formatted trajectories; a three-component reward is computed — format reward (penalizing structural violations), accuracy reward (LLM judge on final answer), and reflection reward (LLM scorer checking whether the reflection block introduces genuine image-derived evidence). The policy is optimized for 100 steps; beyond 150 steps, reward hacking emerges and performance collapses. Third, post-RL self-distillation: the RL model revisits the discarded "intractable" hard prompts via iterative generation; newly solvable cases are rejection-sampled and used to fine-tune the RL model into a final policy, stabilizing the gains. A homologous reconstruction principle — using the same visual backbone for both initial reasoning and reflection synthesis — is central, preventing perceptual mismatch that arises when distilling from a stronger external model (validated via KL divergence comparison against GPT-5-distilled baselines). Key Results: VRE (7B, no tool use) achieves 83.8% on V*-Bench (+7.4% over Qwen2.5-VL-7B), outperforming tool-augmented models Thyme (82.2%), DeepEyesV2 (81.8%), and DyFo (81.2%). On ChartQA it gains +5.1% (88.8) and on OCRBench_v2 up to +7.5% (62.6/64.7 en/zh). On mathematical reasoning: MathVista 71.2 (+3.0%), MathVerse 53.1 (+3.9%), WeMath 68.7 (+6.6%), MathVision 26.5 (+2.8%), LogicVista 48.7 (+2.8%). VRE matches or exceeds GPT-4o on several benchmarks (MathVerse 53.1 vs. 50.8; WeMath 68.7 vs. 69.0) and narrows the gap with Claude 3.7. Ablation confirms that cold-start SFT provides large perceptual gains but negligible reasoning gains (+0.4% MathVerse); RLVR is the primary driver of reasoning improvement (+2.9% MathVerse); post-RL SFT adds consistent small gains across all domains. Attention visualizations show a sharp spike in visual token attention within the `<reflection>` block, confirming genuine re-grounding rather than textual hallucination. Information entropy of VRE reflections (mean 1.08) substantially exceeds that of the Semantic-back baseline (0.66), quantifying the novelty of retrieved visual evidence. Among previously unsolvable hard queries, the Post-RL SFT model generates at least one correct trajectory on 68.1%, demonstrating genuine capability expansion rather than format memorization. Strengths: - The self-iterative, teacher-free design is principled: by restricting training to the model's own capability boundary (unstable samples only) and using homologous reconstruction, VRE avoids distribution shift from external teacher models, validated empirically via KL divergence analysis. - The Information Gain criterion for both data curation and the reflection reward is a technically clean solution to the core failure mode of reward hacking (verbose self-paraphrasing), and its effect is directly measurable via conditional entropy, providing interpretable validation. - Comprehensive ablation (component breakdown, SFT data scaling, RL step scaling, trajectory distribution, KL/entropy analysis, attention visualization) provides strong mechanistic evidence that each design choice contributes as claimed and that the improvements stem from genuine visual re-grounding. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The reflection reward depends on an LLM-based scorer (Qwen2.5-72B) to judge whether a reflection provides genuine visual information gain; this is itself an approximation, and the scorer cannot actually see the image, so it evaluates visual grounding claims purely from text — a potential source of false positives in reward signal. - The gains on complex mathematical reasoning (MathVision: 26.5 vs. 23.7 base) are modest, and VRE still lags behind Claude 3.7 (41.3) and tool-augmented models on some benchmarks; the approach has not closed the gap with larger proprietary models, and the paper does not explore scaling VRE to larger base models. - The three-stage pipeline with GRPO on 64× Ascend 910B NPUs is computationally expensive; wall-clock training cost and inference overhead from the mandatory reflection block (adding ~600 tokens per query) are not systematically discussed, limiting practical deployment assessment.
Citations 2603.26348 PDF
#52 — Innovative (71)

PRBench: End-to-end Paper Reproduction in Physics Research (23 ▲ )

Shi Qiu, Junyi Deng, Yiwei Deng, Haoran Dong, Jieyu Fu, Mao Li, Zeyu Li, Zhaolong Zhang, Huiwen Zheng, Leidong Bao, Anqi Lv, Zihan Mo, Yadi Niu, Yiyang Peng, Yu Tian, Yili Wang, Ziyu Wang, Zi-Yu Wang, Jiashen Wei, Liuheng Wu, Aoran Xue, Leyi Yang, Guanglu Yuan, Xiarui Zhan, Jingjun Zhang, Zifan Zheng, Pengfei Liu, Linrui Zhen, Kaiyang Li, Qichang Li, Ziheng Zhou, Guo-En Nian, Yunwei Xiao, Qing-Hong Cao, Linjie Dai, Xu Feng, Peng Gao, Ying Gu, Chang Liu, Jia Liu, Ming-xing Luo, Yan-Qing Ma, Liang-You Peng, Huichao Song, Shufeng Wang, Chenxu Wang, Tao Wang, Yi-Nan Wang, Chengyin Wu, Pengwei Zhao, Hua Xing Zhu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 31 Mar 2026
Why this rank: PRBench defines a genuinely novel evaluation paradigm—end-to-end research reproduction—that no prior benchmark has attempted, and the striking 0% callback rate across all frontier agents is a surprising and informative result that clearly quantifies the gap between LLM comprehension and scientific execution. The five mechanistically distinct failure modes provide actionable diagnostic value for the field. However, the benchmark's small scale (30 tasks) and single-domain scope limit generalizability, and the weighted scoring choices are not empirically justified.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing benchmarks for scientific AI evaluate isolated capabilities — code generation, bug fixing, scientific reasoning — but none assesses whether LLM-powered agents can perform end-to-end reproduction of published research: reading a paper, implementing its algorithms from scratch, executing the computation, and obtaining quantitatively correct results. This gap is important because AI assistance for science is increasingly claimed, yet the full pipeline demands coordinated integration of long-context comprehension, scientific reasoning, correct formula implementation, and iterative debugging. PRBench directly addresses this open question by providing a rigorous benchmark grounded in real published physics papers. Methodology: PRBench comprises 30 expert-curated tasks spanning 11 physics subfields (QCD, quantum optics, nuclear physics, plasma, condensed matter, etc.), all sourced from over 20 research groups at Peking University. Each task requires an agent to read a full published paper, implement the described algorithms, execute the computation in a sandboxed Docker environment, and produce numerical outputs matching the original publication. Task curation follows a four-stage pipeline: paper selection (requiring self-contained methodology and computational feasibility within a few hours), expert reference reproduction (domain experts produce ground-truth code and outputs), task specification (standardized CSV ground-truth files, detailed scoring rubrics), and independent expert verification. Evaluation follows the Agentified Agent Assessment (AAA) paradigm using a two-agent A2A protocol: a white agent executes code inside Docker; a green agent orchestrates and grades. Scoring is multi-dimensional: methodology understanding (5%), code correctness (30%), data reproduction accuracy (60%), and task completeness (5%). A strict End-to-End Callback Rate measures whether all dimensions simultaneously exceed 0.9. Agents evaluated include OpenAI Codex (GPT-5.3-Codex), OpenCode variants with GPT-5.3-Codex, Kimi K2.5, DeepSeek V3.2, Minimax 2.7, and GLM-5; each task is run three times per agent and scores averaged. Key Results: The best-performing agent, OpenAI Codex (GPT-5.3-Codex), achieves only 34% overall. All OpenCode-based agents score below 29%, with data accuracy scores mostly below 20% across all agents. Methodology understanding and instruction-following scores are comparatively strong (50–78%), but code correctness (16–43%) and data reproduction accuracy (9–21%) reveal a fundamental execution gap. Most strikingly, the End-to-End Callback Rate is 0% for every evaluated agent — no agent successfully reproduces any task end-to-end with all dimensions above 0.9. This holds despite agents often appearing to produce complete, structurally plausible code and correctly identifying relevant equations. Strengths: - The benchmark is genuinely hard and ecologically valid: tasks are drawn from real frontier research, validated by domain experts who perform full reproductions themselves, with verified ground-truth numerical outputs — unlike synthetic or toy benchmarks. - The evaluation framework is rigorous and anti-gaming: Docker isolation prevents ground-truth leakage, outputs are compared against CSV numerical data rather than figure images (avoiding multimodal evaluation artifacts), and an explicit anti-fabrication check is included. - The failure analysis is unusually specific and instructive, identifying five mechanistically distinct failure modes (formula errors, algorithmic substitution, methodological convention mismatch, inability to debug silent failures, resource/execution constraint violations) with concrete case studies (DMRG, lattice QCD, strong-field ionization). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is small (30 tasks) and domain-restricted (physics only, single institution), which limits statistical power and generalizability; subfield counts as low as 1–2 tasks make subfield-level conclusions unreliable. - The weighted scoring formula heavily penalizes data accuracy (60% weight), which may cause the overall score to underrepresent genuine partial progress in methodology understanding or code structure — the weighting choices are not empirically justified. - The benchmark includes only a snapshot of frontier models evaluated at a single point in time; with rapidly improving agents, the benchmark may saturate quickly, and the current 0% callback rate makes it hard to differentiate agent quality at the low end of performance.

#53 — Innovative (69)

REAL: Regression-Aware Reinforcement Learning for LLM-as-a-Judge

Yasi Zhang, Tianyu Chen, Mingyuan Zhou, Oscar Leong, Ying Nian Wu, Michal Lukasik · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: REAL closes a real theoretical gap by proving MSE minimization implies optimal Pearson correlation and cleanly resolves the policy-dependent reward problem with the generalized policy gradient decomposition. The out-of-domain generalization gains being larger than in-domain gains is a strong signal of genuine capability improvement. However, the restriction to pointwise scoring and a single training dataset limits scope.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-as-a-Judge systems assign numeric scores to model outputs, but this is fundamentally a regression task. Standard RL post-training (e.g., GRPO, REINFORCE) uses binary 0/1 accuracy rewards, treating a prediction of 1 and a prediction of 4 equivalently when the ground truth is 5 — ignoring ordinal structure entirely. Regression-aware SFT methods like RAFT and TRACT address the ordinal issue but remain confined to static, ground-truth trajectory supervision without active exploration of reasoning pathways. No prior work combined regression-aware objectives with RL exploration. Empirically, standard RL applied to LLM-as-a-Judge causes correlation metric collapse during training. Methodology: REAL (REgression-Aware Reinforcement Learning) introduces a policy-dependent regression reward: r_REAL(θ, x, c) = -(ŷ_θ(x,c) - y)² + λ log π_θ(y|c,x), where ŷ_θ is the RAIL expected-value predictor (weighted sum over digit token probabilities). Because this reward explicitly depends on policy parameters θ, the standard policy gradient — which assumes ∇_θr = 0 — is invalid. The authors invoke the generalized policy gradient (Schulman et al., 2015), deriving a gradient that decomposes into two terms: (1) a CoT policy-gradient term that explores reasoning trajectories weighted by the regression reward, and (2) a prediction refinement term that backpropagates directly through the differentiable reward. Training uses RLOO variance reduction with K sampled CoT trajectories per prompt and a dynamic sampling strategy that filters to only partially-correct prompts (0 < acc < 1). The authors also prove (Lemma 3.1) that minimizing squared error loss is a sufficient condition for maximizing Pearson correlation — closing a theoretical gap in prior regression-aware LLM work. Experiments use Mistral2-7B, Qwen3-8B, and Qwen3-32B on the Feedback Collection training set, evaluated on Feedback Bench (in-domain), FLASK, Vicuna Bench, and MT Bench (out-of-domain). Key Results: REAL consistently outperforms zero-shot, RAFT, TRACT, standard binary-reward RL, and JEPO across all three model scales and all four benchmarks. On Qwen3-32B, REAL achieves average Pearson/Spearman of 71.0/70.0 versus RAFT's 62.6/62.8 — gains of +8.4/+7.2 over the SFT baseline and +18.3/+11.2 over the base model. On Mistral2-7B, REAL reaches average Pearson 67.9 vs. TRACT's 63.2 and standard RL's 64.1. The out-of-domain improvement is particularly pronounced: on MT Bench (Mistral2-7B), REAL achieves Pearson 59.3 vs. TRACT 52.1 and standard RL 52.9. Performance at N=1 generation is nearly identical to averaging N=10 generations, indicating a robust learned policy. Ablations confirm: larger group size K improves performance (K=16 > K=8 > K=4); regression-only reward (λ=0) already substantially beats TRACT; and initializing from a strong SFT checkpoint further boosts REAL's gains. Strengths: - The theoretical grounding is non-trivial and closes a real gap: the proof that MSE minimization implies optimal Pearson correlation provides principled justification for the regression reward, not just empirical motivation. - The generalized policy gradient derivation cleanly resolves the policy-dependent reward problem, producing an interpretable two-term decomposition that distinguishes reasoning exploration (CoT update) from score prediction (backprop update) — a conceptually meaningful separation. - Out-of-domain generalization gains are substantially larger than in-domain gains, which is the harder and more practically important regime for a judge model; this validates that REAL learns transferable evaluation skills rather than dataset-specific patterns. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method is restricted to pointwise scalar scoring and does not address pairwise preference learning, which is a dominant paradigm in RLHF alignment and LLM evaluation. - The theoretical optimality result (Lemma 3.1) relies on the conditional independence assumption c ⊥ y | x — i.e., that the chain of thought carries no additional information about the label beyond the input. This is questionable: a good CoT that correctly analyzes the response should be informative about y, potentially undermining the clean theoretical story. - Evaluation is limited to a single training dataset (Feedback Collection) and a narrow benchmark suite focused on instruction-following quality scoring; generalization to other judge tasks (e.g., mathematical correctness, safety) is undemonstrated.

#54 — Innovative (69)

Subspace-Aware Graph Construction and Contrastive Alignment for Multimodal Recommendation with Large Language Models

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Applying Orthogonal Matching Pursuit for subspace-aware graph construction is a technically principled and novel contribution to multimodal recommendation, capturing combinatorial multi-entity relationships that standard kNN graphs structurally miss. The disentangled collaborative-semantic architecture with contrastive alignment is well-motivated, and results show substantial gains (up to 28% on Baby). Weakened by missing scalability analysis and degraded performance when textual metadata is sparse.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal recommender systems (MRS) must integrate heterogeneous signals—text, images, collaborative interactions—into coherent user and item representations. Prior work constructs item-item semantic graphs via fixed-k nearest neighbors based on feature similarity, which captures only shallow pairwise relationships within a single entity type. Cross-entity semantic relations (user-item) and complex combinatorial user-interest patterns (e.g., a user interested in both "Business" and "Streetwear" styles whose joint interest defines a distinct subspace) are ignored. A second problem is that collaborative signals tend to dominate and suppress semantic knowledge during representation learning, causing semantically unrelated items to overlap in embedding space. The paper addresses both: how to extract richer semantic structure across all entity pairs, and how to keep semantic and collaborative signals from collapsing into each other. Methodology: The proposed framework, SCALE (Subspace-aware graph Construction and contrastive ALignment for multimodal rEcommendation), has three main components. First, a Profile Generator uses Llama-3.2-3B to produce natural-language profiles for both items (from title/description metadata) and users (from interaction histories), which are then encoded via a contrastive text encoder into dense representations. Second, a Semantic Graph Construction stage builds two complementary graph types: (a) Subspace-Aware Graphs (SAG) using the Orthogonal Matching Pursuit (OMP) algorithm—each entity is sparsely reconstructed as a linear combination of its neighbors under the subspace clustering assumption, yielding graphs for user-user (C^U), item-item (C^I), and cross-entity user-item (C^UI) spaces that capture combinatorial multi-entity relationships beyond pairwise similarity; and (b) Feature Similarity Graphs (FSG) using kNN cosine similarity for visual and textual modalities. The two graph types are combined via element-wise logical intersection into a unified semantic graph. Third, graph convolutions are run independently on the interaction graph (LightGCN-style on the user-item bipartite) and the semantic graph, producing separate interaction-based and semantic-based representations. An Interaction and Semantic Alignment module then applies two contrastive losses: a semantic alignment loss (InfoNCE-style) that pulls interaction and semantic representations of the same entity closer, and a relation alignment loss that uses the OMP-derived graphs as positive/negative pair supervision to explicitly pull semantically related entities closer and push unrelated ones apart in the final embedding space. The final representation is the sum of interaction and semantic embeddings, optimized jointly with BPR ranking loss. Key Results: On three Amazon product datasets (Baby, Sports, Clothing) with multimodal features, SCALE outperforms the strongest baseline (MENTOR) by 7.68%–27.96% on Recall@20. Specific numbers: Baby Recall@20 improves from 0.1048 (MENTOR) to 0.1341 (+27.96%); Sports from 0.1139 to 0.1257 (+10.36%); Clothing from 0.0989 to 0.1065 (+7.68%). On text-only datasets (Book, Yelp) against LLM-enhanced baselines (KAR, RLMRec), SCALE with LightGCN backbone achieves Recall@20 of 0.1624 on Book (vs. RLMRec+SGL at 0.1537, +5.7%) and 0.1280 on Yelp (vs. RLMRec+SGL at 0.1263, +1.3%). Ablation confirms that all components contribute: removing OMP-based SAG, FSG, the LLM profile generator, semantic alignment loss, or relation alignment loss each degrades Recall@20 on Baby. Performance on Clothing is relatively lower due to 93.83% missing item descriptions and 94.10% textual redundancy, which degrades LLM profile quality. Sensitivity analysis shows best results at OMP sparsity k_o=10, edge pruning ratio 0.6, one semantic GCN layer (more layers amplify noise), and two interaction GCN layers. Strengths: - The use of OMP for subspace-aware graph construction is technically principled and genuinely novel in the MRS context—it captures combinatorial multi-entity relationships that kNN-based methods structurally cannot. - Including cross-entity (user-item) semantic edges in the graph is an underexplored design choice that provides complementary signal to the interaction bipartite, and its ablation confirms positive contribution. - The disentangled treatment of collaborative and semantic streams—running separate graph convolutions and aligning via contrastive loss rather than fusing features early—is a sound architectural choice that demonstrably prevents collaborative signals from suppressing semantic information. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The scalability of the OMP-based graph construction is a concern: building dictionaries of size k_s=800 and running OMP for every user and item at each training cycle could be prohibitively expensive for large-scale industrial deployments; the paper does not report wall-clock training times or complexity analysis. - The framework relies heavily on high-quality LLM-generated profiles; on Clothing (93.83% missing descriptions), performance gains shrink substantially, and the method's advantage over purely collaborative baselines may vanish in domains with sparse or low-quality textual metadata. - The relation alignment loss uses randomly sampled negatives with no guarantee of semantic or interaction dissimilarity, which the paper itself acknowledges reduces the effectiveness of negative sampling—a principled hard-negative mining strategy is absent.
Recommended
#55 — Innovative (68)

On Token's Dilemma: Dynamic MoE with Drift-Aware Token Assignment for Continual Learning of Large Vision Language Models (27 ▲ )

Chongyang Zhao, Mingsong Li, Haodong Lu, Dong Gong · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 31 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Scores 68 for the clean empirical identification of routing-drift as the root cause of forgetting in MoE continual learning, and the well-motivated token taxonomy (new/old/ambiguous) that directly informs each design choice. The TAG mechanism is interpretable, parameter-free, and orthogonal to existing replay strategies, yielding a 12% reduction in backward transfer on the CoIN benchmark.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning (MCIT) requires Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) to incrementally learn new tasks without catastrophic forgetting of prior ones. Mixture of Experts (MoE) architectures with LoRA experts are a natural fit: each new task adds new experts while freezing old ones, providing parameter isolation. However, even with frozen old experts, MoE-based continual learners still forget. The paper identifies the root cause as routing-drift: training the newly added router on new-task data distorts how old-task tokens are assigned at inference, routing them to new experts that were never trained on old-task patterns. Prior work sidesteps this via task-specific routing, sacrificing token-level routing flexibility and requiring reliable task identification. Methodology: The paper conducts a controlled two-task experiment to categorize tokens in new-task data into three types based on routing score distributions: new tokens (high affinity to new expert group), old tokens (high affinity to old group), and ambiguous tokens (small affinity difference between groups). Controlled masking experiments confirm that new tokens drive new-task learning with minimal forgetting, old tokens contribute little to new-task learning but corrupt the new router when given non-negligible new-expert weight, and ambiguous tokens are the worst offenders—they provide almost no learning benefit yet directly cause forgetting when routed to new experts. This is the token's dilemma. Motivated by this analysis, the proposed LLaVA-DyMoE introduces two complementary regularizations. Token Assignment Guidance (TAG) computes per-token group confidence scores (max logit within each expert group) and a relative ambiguity score D_rel. A binary mask routes a token to new experts only if it is unambiguous (D_rel > τ) AND new-dominant (c_new > c_old); all other tokens are forced to the frozen old expert group via score masking (−∞). Routing Score Regularization (RSR) adds two soft losses: an exclusivity loss L_exc = g_old × g_new that penalizes simultaneous activation of both groups, and a specialization loss L_spe (BCE form) that encourages routing weight toward new experts for genuinely new tokens. The total objective combines the standard next-token prediction loss, an auxiliary load-balancing loss on new experts, and the two RSR terms. The regularization applies only during training and imposes no constraints at inference. Key Results: On the CoIN benchmark (8 sequential VQA tasks, 569k training / 261k test samples, LLaVA-v1.5-7B backbone), LLaVA-DyMoE achieves a Mean Final Accuracy (MFN) of 57.03% vs. 49.68% for the strongest non-regularized MoE baseline (IncMoELoRA), a 7.35% gain. Backward Transfer (BWT, measuring forgetting) improves from −16.67% to −4.67%, a 12% reduction. Compared to all baselines including O-LoRA (MFN 49.53%, BWT −17.54%) and SEFE (MFN 58.57%, BWT −10.45%), LLaVA-DyMoE has by far the lowest forgetting. When combined with replay (buffer size 200), BWT drops further to −1.55% vs. ProgLoRA's −6.59% at the same budget. Ablations confirm that TAG alone accounts for the largest single gain (+3.68% MFN, BWT from −15.44 to −7.04), with L_exc and L_spe providing incremental improvements. The method scales to 13B (MFN 60.39%, BWT −4.64%), is robust across task orderings and instruction templates, and adds only 4.4% training overhead with zero inference cost. Strengths: - The token-type taxonomy (new / old / ambiguous) is empirically grounded via clean controlled experiments and directly motivates each design choice, making the method principled rather than heuristic. - TAG is a hard, interpretable routing correction with no learnable parameters of its own, avoiding the risk of overfitting the regularizer to training dynamics. - The method is orthogonal to data-based (replay, augmentation) and task-level routing strategies, and combining them yields consistent additive gains—demonstrating genuine complementarity rather than overlapping mechanisms. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is confined to a single benchmark (CoIN) with a single backbone family (LLaVA-v1.5); generalization to other LVLM architectures (e.g., encoder-free models, larger MoE-native models) is unverified, and the authors themselves flag scalability as future work. - The ambiguity threshold τ and regularization weight α are hyperparameters that require tuning; while sensitivity is modest within a narrow range (τ = 10–20% works best), the threshold implicitly defines what counts as 'ambiguous' and may need re-tuning as task distributions shift. - The REF (visual grounding) task remains very weak for all methods (best result: 9.25%), suggesting the MoE-LoRA design has fundamental limits for spatially precise tasks that neither TAG nor RSR addresses.

#56 — Innovative (68)

Parallel In-context Learning for Large Vision Language Models

Shin'ya Yamaguchi, Daiki Chijiwa, Tamao Sakao, Taku Hasegawa · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Parallel-ICL offers a theoretically grounded approach connecting Fano's inequality to practical chunking and ensemble design choices, which elevates it above a pure engineering contribution. The surprising finding that chunked parallel inference consistently outperforms full-context MM-ICL — likely by mitigating the 'lost in the middle' phenomenon — is a meaningful secondary insight. However, the core technique (k-means chunking + weighted Product-of-Experts) combines well-established components.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-modal in-context learning (MM-ICL) in large vision-language models (LVLMs) improves with more demonstrations, but Transformer attention scales quadratically with context length. Since LVLMs represent images as thousands of visual tokens, adding demonstrations dramatically inflates prefill cost. For example, going from 8-shot to 32-shot with LLaVA-OV-7B increases latency from 1.0s to 3.5s while accuracy gains plateau (56.9% → 58.9%). Prior efficiency approaches (task vectors) require hundreds of demonstrations and per-task optimization, defeating MM-ICL's goal of effortless dynamic adaptation. The paper asks: can we approximate long-context MM-ICL at inference time with no extra overhead? Methodology: Parallel-ICL is a plug-and-play inference algorithm with two stages. First, context chunking: demonstrations are partitioned into K subsets using k-means clustering on CLIP multi-modal features (concatenated image and text embeddings), ensuring each chunk contains diverse yet internally coherent demonstrations. Second, context compilation: each chunk is processed independently by the LVLM in parallel, and the resulting per-chunk logits are combined via a weighted Product-of-Experts (PoE) ensemble — a weighted sum at the logit level before softmax. Weights are computed as softmax over cosine similarities between the query and each chunk's demonstrations in CLIP feature space, prioritizing task-relevant chunks. The design is grounded in an ensemble learning bound (Fano's inequality) showing that low error requires high inter-chunk diversity and high query relevance — motivating the k-means chunking and similarity-based weighting respectively. Key Results: On 32-shot MM-ICL across MI-Bench-ICL, GQA, TextVQA, and COCO Caption with LLaVA-OV-7B, Qwen2.5-VL-7B, and InternVL3.5 (8B/14B/38B), Parallel-ICL (2-chunk) achieves 1.15–1.45× speedup with approximation ratios of 99–103% relative to full-context MM-ICL. At 4-chunk, speedup reaches up to 1.78× at 96–102% accuracy. Strikingly, at N=32 with K=2 or K=4, Parallel-ICL consistently outperforms full-context MM-ICL across all tested LVLMs (e.g., 63.4% vs. 58.9% on MI-Bench-ICL Demo with LLaVA-OV-7B), attributed to mitigation of the "lost in the middle" phenomenon. Combining with DivPrune (demonstration reduction) achieves up to 3.0× speedup at 102% approximation ratio on Qwen2.5-VL. Ablation confirms that both k-means chunking (vs. random: 63.4% → 59.3%) and similarity-based compilation (vs. uniform: 63.4% → 60.0%) are individually necessary, and multi-modal features outperform text-only or image-only features. Scalability holds across 8B, 14B, and 38B InternVL3.5 models. Strengths: - Theoretically grounded design: the chunking and compilation strategies are directly motivated by a Fano's inequality bound on ensemble error, making the connection between diversity/relevance and practical algorithm choices principled rather than heuristic. - The unexpected accuracy gains over full-context MM-ICL (approx. ratio > 100%) at high shot counts provide a compelling secondary contribution — evidence that chunked parallel inference can mitigate the "lost in the middle" problem in long demonstration contexts. - Orthogonality to complementary methods (e.g., DivPrune token pruning) is cleanly demonstrated, showing Parallel-ICL is an independent dimension of efficiency improvement that composes multiplicatively with existing approaches. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Speedup is modest: 1.15–1.78× at 32-shot is useful but not transformative. Since prefill dominates latency and chunks are processed sequentially in the paper's batch-wise setup, true parallel execution across multiple GPUs or with better batching could change the picture — but this is not fully explored. - K is a sensitive hyperparameter that must be chosen via a validation set, and its optimal value depends jointly on N and the task. At small N (e.g., 8-shot with K=8), the per-chunk query overhead can eliminate all latency gains, and the paper offers only the heuristic guidance to match K to N rather than a principled selection rule.
Search Results 2603.16092 PDF
#57 — Innovative (68)

Iterative Multi-Granular RAG with Contextual Hierarchical Graph

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: MGranRAG's key innovation is achieving global semantic coherence through a lightweight hierarchical graph (spaCy-only, zero LLM indexing cost) while retaining iterative retrieval adaptivity — directly resolving the cost-quality tradeoff that plagues GraphRAG and HippoRAG. The dual local-global loop with PPR propagation is principled and each component is cleanly ablated. However, the framework builds squarely on established RAG paradigms, and the high retrieval-time token costs and modest gains on single-hop tasks temper the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard RAG systems suffer from evidence fragmentation: they retrieve topically relevant but logically isolated passages, which breaks down for multi-hop questions requiring coherent reasoning chains across multiple documents. Iterative RAG methods improve flexibility but remain constrained by local information horizons, missing global associative structures. Structure-augmented approaches (GraphRAG, HippoRAG) capture global relationships but carry prohibitive construction costs from LLM-based entity/relation extraction. The gap is a method that provides global semantic coherence at low construction cost while retaining iterative adaptivity. Methodology: MGranRAG combines a lightweight Contextual Hierarchical Graph (CHG) with a dual local-global iterative retrieval loop. The CHG is built offline using spaCy (no LLM calls): documents are chunked into passages, sentences, and noun phrases/named entities, connected via hierarchical containment edges (passage→sentence→phrase). This creates a three-tier graph G=(V,E) at negligible cost compared to semantic KG construction. Online retrieval iterates T rounds. In each round, Local Evidence Refinement prompts an LLM (Qwen2.5-7B or Qwen3-8B in few-shot CoT) to extract sentence-level evidence units with associated key phrases from the current top-M candidate passages, and labels each unit as fully supported, partially supported, or irrelevant with respect to the query and its CoT-decomposed sub-queries. A confidence score ψ(ε) aggregates these labels. The evidence-augmented query then re-ranks passages via dense retrieval. Global Structure-Aware Calibration maps these evidence units onto CHG nodes — passage weights combine cosine similarity with momentum from prior iterations; sentence weights use the confidence score; phrase weights incorporate confidence, phrase-type features, and inverse node degree. Personalized PageRank (PPR) propagates these weights through the graph to produce a globally coherent re-ranking. When the LLM discovers phrases absent from the CHG, they are incrementally added at negligible overhead. The final passage set after T iterations feeds a separate reader LLM (Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct) for answer generation. Key Results: Evaluated on six benchmarks (HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHopQA, MuSiQue, NQ, PopQA, NarrativeQA) with 1,000 questions each. MGranRAG (Qwen3-8B, T=3) achieves average Recall@5 of 84.2% and average F1 of 56.2%, both best overall. Compared to the strong NV-Embed-v2 dense retriever baseline (73.4% R@5, 52.6% F1), gains are especially large on multi-hop datasets: PopQA +22.7% R@5, 2Wiki +19.4% R@5, HotpotQA +3.7% R@5. It outperforms HippoRAG 2 (78.2% R@5, 55.0% F1) on average and reaches 98.2% R@5 on HotpotQA. Even at T=1 it beats all baselines on average. QA performance is comparable to RL-trained iterative methods like R1-Searcher (54.7% F1) on multi-hop benchmarks despite using only few-shot prompting. Ablation confirms CHG alone yields +18.6%/+21.7% R@5 on 2Wiki/HotpotQA; query decomposition adds further gains; full EAR completes the picture. Token usage at retrieval time (4.5M input tokens for T=1, 12M for T=3 on 2Wiki) is higher than baselines but requires zero indexing-time LLM tokens, unlike HippoRAG 2 (5.8M index input tokens). Strengths: - The CHG construction is genuinely lightweight (spaCy only, no LLM at index time), directly addressing the cost bottleneck of GraphRAG/HippoRAG without sacrificing global structure. - The dual local-global loop is principled: local evidence extraction sharpens query signals, and PPR over the hierarchical graph propagates them globally — these two mechanisms are complementary and each is ablated to confirm independent contribution. - Plug-and-play design is validated across multiple retrievers (Contriever, NV-Embed-v2) and multiple LLM backbones (1.5B to 8B), showing the framework is not brittle to component choices. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Retrieval-time token costs are substantially higher than baselines (12M input tokens for T=3 vs. 3-4M for Search-R1/R1-Searcher), driven by fixed ~3k-token evidence extraction prompts per iteration, making real-time or large-scale deployment expensive. - The CHG lacks semantic edges between passages (no cross-document coreference or entity linking), so it can miss connections that require understanding that two passages discuss the same entity under different surface forms — a limitation the authors acknowledge but defer to future work. - Gains on simpler single-hop datasets (NQ, NarrativeQA) are modest or inconsistent, suggesting the framework's complexity is not always warranted and the overhead may not be justified for non-multi-hop use cases.
Recommended
#58 — Innovative (68)

An Agentic Outlook on Neuro- Symbolic AI: Towards Modular, Adaptive, and Explainable AI Systems

scitepress.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: ANSA introduces a formally grounded intra-system agency model for neuro-symbolic AI, reframing component coordination as autonomous agent communication with explicit arbitration semantics. The six-agent architecture is validated across three structurally diverse domains with measurable accuracy gains over neural-only and symbolic-only baselines. While evaluation relies on synthetic data and the formal model lacks theoretical guarantees, the conceptual contribution — treating NeSy subsystems as goal-directed agents with feedback loops — is a genuinely creative framing that could influence how modular AI systems are designed.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing neuro-symbolic (NeSy) AI systems combine neural learning with symbolic reasoning but rely on rigid, pipeline-based architectures where components interact in fixed, predetermined ways. These static designs struggle with ambiguity, conflicting predictions, evolving domain knowledge, and long-term adaptation. More fundamentally, the literature lacks a principled account of how internal components should coordinate, arbitrate disagreements, exchange feedback, and evolve over time — leading to ad hoc designs that are difficult to generalize or extend. This paper addresses that conceptual gap by asking: can NeSy systems be redesigned as collections of autonomous, communicating agents with explicit coordination semantics? Methodology: The paper introduces ANSA (Agentic Neuro-Symbolic Architecture), grounded in a formal model of "intra-system agency." Each intra-system agent is defined as a tuple ⟨S, I, O, G, π⟩ (state, inputs, outputs, goal, policy), and the full system is formalized as M = ⟨A, G, μ, F⟩ where A is the agent set, G a directed communication graph, μ an arbitration operator, and F a feedback mechanism. ANSA instantiates this with six specialized agents: (1) Neural Agent for data-driven perception, (2) Symbolic Agent for rule/ontology-based reasoning, (3) Orchestrator Agent that realizes μ via confidence-weighted arbitration, (4) Verifier Agent that enforces hard safety/compliance constraints, (5) Explainer Agent that derives natural-language rationales from symbolic traces, and (6) Feedback Agent that monitors drift and triggers retraining or rule revision. The framework is instantiated across three domains: medical diagnosis (GRU + Prolog/SNOMED-CT rules + BioGPT explainer + CLIPS verifier), financial advising (feedforward NN + OWL/SWRL ontology + HermiT reasoner), and chess commentary (transformer encoder + SWI-Prolog tactical motifs + Stockfish verifier). Key Results: Medical diagnosis: the hybrid ANSA configuration (50% engineered features + 50% symbolic knowledge) achieved 88.08% accuracy and F1=88.4%, surpassing the neural-only baseline of 84.22%. Symbolic augmentation of treatment recommendation lifted Logistic Regression from 53.4% to 79.3% and Gradient Boosting from 86.0% to 88.3%; a symbolic-enhanced Random Forest reached 93.1%. Financial advising: ANSA achieved 91.2% agreement with expert-validated strategies vs. 87.3% (neural-only) and 81.4% (symbolic-only); 89% of generated explanations were rated clear and actionable by domain experts. Chess: ANSA achieved 85.6% accuracy on 300 tactical puzzles and 91.2% agreement with expert game analyses; tactic detection improved from 76.3% (symbolic-only) and 82.5% (neural-only) to 89.4% (orchestrated); 92% of explanations rated clear by domain experts. BLEU scores were consistently low (0.03–0.08) across all domains, but dismissed by human evaluators in favor of qualitative grounding and contextual relevance. Strengths: - Clean formal foundation: the intra-system agency model (Definitions 1–5) elevates ANSA above an engineering heuristic, enabling principled analysis of coordination, arbitration, and adaptation independent of specific implementations. - Demonstrated cross-domain generality: the same six-agent template is instantiated across three structurally diverse domains (high-stakes clinical, regulatory-constrained financial, adversarial game), each with domain-appropriate neural and symbolic backends. - Confidence-aware arbitration with symbolic override (θ=0.85 threshold) is a pragmatically useful design that prevents the neural agent from overriding safety-critical symbolic constraints. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation relies heavily on synthetic data (25,000 synthetic financial profiles, Kaggle symptom dataset) and small expert panels; real-world clinical or financial deployment would require much more rigorous validation, and the expert evaluation methodology is not described in detail (number of raters, inter-rater agreement, blinding). - The formal model provides definitions but no theoretical guarantees — convergence, soundness of arbitration under disagreement, or formal properties of the adaptation operator δ are left unaddressed, making the "formal characterization" largely descriptive rather than analytically useful. - The paper conflates agent numbering between the general ANSA architecture and domain instantiations (e.g., Verifier is a5 in medicine but listed as a4 in the financial section), suggesting the instantiations were not fully systematized despite the claimed architectural uniformity. - The low BLEU scores (0.03–0.08) are dismissed without a proper automatic evaluation alternative; it is unclear whether grounded explanation quality can be measured at all in the current evaluation setup, which limits reproducibility and comparison to other XAI methods.
Search Results
#59 — Innovative (68)

UniDriveVLA: Unifying Understanding, Perception, and Action Planning for Autonomous Driving (12 ▲ )

Yongkang Li, Lijun Zhou, Sixu Yan, Bencheng Liao, Tianyi Yan, Kaixin Xiong, Long Chen, Hongwei Xie, Bing Wang, Guang Chen, Hangjun Ye, Wenyu Liu, Haiyang Sun, Xinggang Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 3 Apr 2026
Why this rank: UniDriveVLA provides a well-grounded empirical diagnosis of the perception-reasoning interference problem in driving VLAs (the cosine similarity convergence analysis is compelling) and addresses it with a principled Mixture-of-Transformers architecture that yields consistent gains across all task types. The sparse perception query design avoiding dense BEV is a clean architectural contribution. The work is innovative but operates squarely within the established VLA-for-driving paradigm, and the substantial degradation in general VQA capability and lagging motion prediction accuracy limit its practical advantage over specialized approaches.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for autonomous driving face a fundamental tension between spatial perception and semantic reasoning. Vanilla 2D VLMs preserve strong language reasoning but lack spatial grounding; injecting 3D representations into shared decoder parameters improves spatial perception but causes representation interference that degrades the native reasoning capability of the pretrained VLM. The root cause identified by the authors is not the 3D representations themselves, but joint optimization of heterogeneous spatial and semantic objectives within shared parameters — leading to feature collapse where perception and language tokens converge to nearly identical representations (cosine similarity approaching 1 across layers). Methodology: UniDriveVLA adopts a Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture with three specialized experts — Understanding, Perception, and Action — each with independent QKV projections, FFN, and normalization layers. Cross-expert coordination is achieved through Masked Joint Attention: understanding tokens use causal masking and cannot attend to downstream perception/action tokens (preserving VLM semantics); perception tokens attend to understanding tokens for semantic context; action tokens attend to both. The perception expert uses a sparse paradigm: task-specific sparse queries (detection, mapping, ego-status, occupancy, motion) initialized via K-Means instance banks are updated through deformable feature aggregation and inter-task communication, extracting spatial priors from multi-scale 2D visual features without dense BEV construction. Action tokens are generated via flow matching from noised velocity sequences. Training proceeds in three stages: (1) large-scale multimodal pretraining on a 3:7 driving-to-general data mixture to anchor semantic reasoning; (2) joint optimization of all tasks with LoRA on the VLM backbone and a 0.5× learning-rate multiplier to prevent catastrophic forgetting; (3) freezing the VLM and fine-tuning perception and action experts with an additional motion forecasting objective. The backbone is Qwen3-VL (2B and 8B variants with SigLIP-2 encoder). Key Results: On Bench2Drive closed-loop evaluation (CARLA simulator), UniDriveVLA achieves a Driving Score of 78.37 and Efficiency of 198.86 — best among methods trained without PDM-Lite expert demonstrations — with a Success Rate of 51.82%, competitive with Orion (54.62%). On fine-grained driving abilities, it leads on Merging (38.75%) and Overtaking (80.00%). On nuScenes open-loop planning without ego-state, UniDriveVLA-Large achieves 0.51 avg. L2 (ST-P3 protocol) and 0.90 avg. L2 (UniAD protocol), best in the ego-free setting. Perception on nuScenes: detection mAP 0.407, NDS 0.460, map mAP 0.535 (competitive with specialized methods). The MoT ablation (Table 7) shows clear gains over a shared-weight decoder: General VQA 45.5 vs. 31.1%, DriveBench 54.9 vs. 50.8%, L2 0.533 vs. 0.641, collision rate 14.0% vs. 17.5%, confirming the decoupling hypothesis. General VQA capability is partially retained (43.3 MMStar, 76.3 AI2D) though substantially below pure VLMs like Qwen3-VL-8B (63.0 MMStar, 83.2 AI2D). Strengths: - The perception–reasoning interference diagnosis is empirically grounded (cosine similarity analysis, Fig. 2) and the MoT fix addresses the root cause rather than symptoms, yielding consistent gains across all task types simultaneously. - Sparse perception integrated with a unified query decoder (detection + mapping + ego + motion + occupancy) avoids the computational redundancy of dense BEV while still providing rich spatial priors that flow into the action expert via masked attention. - The three-stage training strategy with LoRA and reduced VLM learning rates is a principled solution to catastrophic forgetting, and the ablation on planning components (Table 5) provides clear evidence for each perception module's contribution. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Motion prediction accuracy lags specialized baselines (minADE 1.264–1.396 vs. SparseDrive's 0.600), indicating the unified framework still sacrifices performance on individual tasks relative to task-specialized models. - General multimodal capability degrades substantially after driving adaptation (MMStar drops from ~63 to 43.3, VLMsAreBlind from 61.9 to 26.6), suggesting the three-stage strategy does not fully prevent forgetting of general visual reasoning despite its design intent. - Closed-loop Success Rate (51.82%) remains below PDM-Lite-trained methods (AutoVLA 57.73%, SimLingo 66.82%, R2SE 69.54%), and the efficiency/comfortness trade-off (lowest comfortness 11.78 among compared methods) raises practical deployment concerns.
Recommended 2604.02190 PDF
#60 — Innovative (68)

GaussianGPT: Towards Autoregressive 3D Gaussian Scene Generation (9 ▲ )

Nicolas von Lützow, Barbara Rössle, Katharina Schmid, Matthias Nießner · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 2 Apr 2026
Why this rank: GaussianGPT brings the autoregressive generation paradigm to explicit 3D Gaussian representations with a clean formulation where completion, outpainting, and unconditional generation emerge from the same model. The 3D RoPE design that decouples spatial locality from serialization order is a genuine technical insight validated by ablation. However, the extreme inference latency (~6,000s for a 12m×12m scene) and purely qualitative scene-level evaluation limit the demonstrated impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Most 3D scene generation methods use diffusion or flow-matching over holistic scene representations, making incremental editing, completion, and unbounded scene extension unnatural. In practice, 3D environments are built progressively — rooms are extended, objects are added, partial observations are completed. There is no strong autoregressive paradigm for full 3D scene synthesis that operates directly on explicit, renderable representations. The gap is precisely the lack of a principled tokenization of 3D Gaussians that would let a GPT-style model reason about spatial structure step-by-step. Methodology: GaussianGPT frames 3D scene generation as sequential next-token prediction over vector-quantized 3D Gaussian primitives. The pipeline has two stages. First, a sparse 3D convolutional autoencoder (following the L3DG architecture) maps a 3D Gaussian scene into a sparse latent grid, which is then discretized using Lookup-Free Quantization (LFQ) into a codebook of size 4,096. The autoencoder is trained with L1 color loss, VGG19 perceptual loss, occupancy BCE loss, and an entropy-maximizing codebook loss. Second, the latent grid is serialized into a 1D token sequence via a fixed xyz column-major traversal order. Crucially, geometry and appearance are decoupled by interleaving separate position tokens (predicting the next occupied voxel index) and feature tokens (predicting the Gaussian appearance at that voxel), with separate prediction heads and vocabularies for each type. A GPT-2 medium causal transformer with a 16,384-token context window models this token sequence. Rather than 1D positional encoding, the model injects 3D Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) keyed to actual voxel (x,y,z) coordinates, with a 4th token-type dimension to further separate geometry from appearance. At inference, completion and outpainting emerge naturally: a partial scene is serialized as a prefix, and the model continues generation from there. Large scenes (e.g., 12m×12m) are synthesized by sliding a context window column by column without any architectural change. Training datasets include Aria Synthetic Environments (25,000 scenes), 3D-FRONT (4,472 scenes), and PhotoShape (15,576 chairs). Key Results: On unconditional chair generation (PhotoShape), GaussianGPT achieves FID=5.68, KID=1.835×10⁻³, and COV=67.40%, beating all baselines including L3DG (FID=8.49, KID=3.147×10⁻³, COV=63.80%), DiffRF, EG3D, and π-GAN, while remaining competitive on geometric MMD (4.278 vs. L3DG best of 4.241). For scene generation, qualitative comparisons to L3DG show plausible indoor layouts with full-scale spatial chunks. Scene completion conditioned on 1/4 or 1/2 of a chunk produces diverse, semantically consistent completions across 3D-FRONT validation scenes. Large-scale 12m×12m scenes are synthesized via autoregressive outpainting with stable floor alignment and consistent room structure across chunk boundaries, though generation is slow (~6,000 seconds per 12m×12m scene on a GH200 GPU). The serialization ablation shows that simple xyz ordering matches or outperforms locality-preserving space-filling curves (Z-order, Hilbert), because 3D RoPE already injects spatial locality into attention directly — making the serialization order largely irrelevant to quality. Strengths: - The 3D RoPE design is technically sound and principled: decoupling spatial locality from serialization order means the model attends based on true voxel proximity, not sequence proximity, which is a non-trivial insight validated by the ablation showing Hilbert curves offer no benefit. - Completion, outpainting, and unconditional generation all emerge from the same single model via prefix conditioning, with no task-specific architecture changes — a clean and generalizable formulation. - The separate position/feature vocabulary design explicitly decouples geometry from appearance prediction, preventing index space collisions and enabling independent control of chunk size and codebook size. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Scene generation is extremely slow: ~6,000 seconds for a 12m×12m scene on a high-end GH200 GPU, and ~90 seconds for a single 4m×4m chunk. This is a fundamental bottleneck of autoregressive token-by-token sampling over dense 3D grids and severely limits practical utility compared to diffusion methods. - Scene-level evaluation is purely qualitative — there are no quantitative metrics (FID, COV, MMD) on scene generation, only on object-level chairs. This makes it difficult to rigorously assess how well the model captures the distribution of indoor scenes or how it compares numerically to L3DG or DiffuScene on scene synthesis benchmarks. - The autoencoder becomes a fidelity bottleneck on real-world data (ScanNet++ v2): high-frequency geometric details are not faithfully reconstructed, producing noisy Gaussians, and the real-world training set (895 scenes) is too small for reliable generalization.
Recommended 2603.26661 PDF
#61 — Innovative (68)

Theory-Based Interpretability in Deep Knowledge Tracing via Grounded Transformers

mdpi.com · karypis - new results
Why this rank: gTransformer introduces a principled representational grounding mechanism that anchors Transformer latent spaces to BKT constructs via additive logit-space composition, which is a genuinely creative bridge between interpretable and deep KT paradigms. The triple-validation protocol (structural, semantic, functional) sets a new rigor standard for interpretability claims in educational AI. The framework's extensibility to other reference theories gives it broader potential impact, though weak semantic alignment for initial mastery and restriction to BKT/math domains temper the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Knowledge Tracing (KT) — estimating how student mastery evolves during interactions with educational content — is caught between two paradigms: interpretable but rigid probabilistic models (e.g., Bayesian Knowledge Tracing, BKT) and high-accuracy but opaque Deep Knowledge Tracing (DKT) architectures. Existing ante hoc interpretability attempts that incorporate side information (e.g., IRT parameters) do not ground model outputs in the full causal logic of pedagogically validated constructs, nor do they enable student-level individualisation without high computational cost. The paper targets this gap: achieving DKT-level predictive accuracy while producing outputs that are intrinsically interpretable in terms of BKT parameters (initial mastery P(L0) and learn rate P(T)) that educators already understand and trust. Methodology: The paper introduces gTransformer, a grounded Transformer that implements "representational grounding" — anchoring the model's latent space to BKT concepts via three coordinated mechanisms. (1) Input augmentation: student interaction sequences (question id, skill, binary correctness) are enriched with population-level BKT parameter priors from a pre-fit reference model. (2) Difficulty-aware embeddings: question embeddings decompose into a concept base vector plus a scaled concept-specific difficulty axis, allowing multiple questions on the same skill to share semantic identity while differing in difficulty. (3) A stacked encoder–decoder Transformer (4 encoder + 8 decoder layers) produces a context vector z_t encoding the student's full interaction history. Grounded parameters are estimated via additive composition in logit space: a trainable scalar base (initialized from BKT priors) plus a context projection (dot product of z_t with a learnable semantic axis), followed by sigmoid activation. This ensures BKT priors anchor the estimate while the Transformer adjusts for individual history. The model has three output heads: (a) an unconstrained MLP head for supervised accuracy, (b) an interpretable BKT logic head that feeds grounded (pL0, pT) through BKT update equations to produce theory-constrained predictions, and (c) linear probe heads for validation.
