Training uses a three-term multi-objective loss balancing supervised cross-entropy (λ=1.0), reference model cross-entropy (λ=0.5), and an MSE probing loss that constrains latent representations to encode BKT constructs linearly (λ=1.0). Interpretability is verified through a triple-validation protocol: structural alignment via diagnostic probing (ΔR² selectivity), semantic alignment via Spearman rank correlation between grounded parameters and BKT priors, and functional alignment via a composite confidence metric. Key Results: On five benchmark datasets (ASSISTments 2009/2015, Algebra 2005, Bridge to Algebra 2006, NeurIPS 2020), the ablated gTransformer achieves AUC of 0.7831 on AS2009 (best among all evaluated models), and ranks second on AL2005, BDG2006, and NIPS34 (behind only AKT). The performance–interpretability trade-off is characterized by: (a) interpretability drop of only 0.2% AUC when grounding constraints are added; (b) interpretability cost of 3.9% AUC when switching to the fully BKT-constrained prediction head; (c) interpretability gain of +19.9% AUC over population-level BKT. For grounded interpretability, structural probing on AS2009 shows selectivity ΔR² = 0.619 for P(L0) and ΔR² = 0.570 for P(T) (both exceeding the 0.5 threshold), confirming that BKT constructs are the dominant organising principle in the latent space, not incidental correlates. Semantic alignment shows moderate Spearman ρ = 0.544 for learn rate and weaker ρ = 0.360 for initial mastery (the latter indicating strong individualisation away from population priors). Functional confidence achieves medium or high confidence on 89.2% of student–skill pairs. Context-aware personalisation is demonstrated qualitatively: for students with identical response sequences to the same questions, BKT produces identical mastery trajectories while gTransformer produces diverging predictions based on full interaction history. Strengths: - The representational grounding mechanism is principled and elegant: additive logit-space composition ensures outputs remain structurally anchored to BKT while allowing data-driven individualisation, avoiding the degeneracy issues of unconstrained parameter estimation. - The triple-validation protocol (structural, semantic, functional) is substantially more rigorous than prior interpretability claims in KT, using selectivity-corrected probing to rule out spurious latent correlations. - The approach is explicitly extensible: grounding is not tied to BKT and could be applied to any interpretable reference model (AFM, PFA) or even non-educational scientific domains, giving the framework broad generalization potential. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The semantic alignment for initial mastery (Spearman ρ = 0.360) is weak, meaning the grounded pL0 values have substantially departed from BKT semantics toward black-box optimisation — partially undermining the claim of faithful pedagogical grounding for this parameter. - Guess and slip parameters are fixed to population-level BKT estimates rather than individualised, limiting the model's ability to capture item-level variation in performance errors; the authors acknowledge this as a simplification to avoid degeneracy but it leaves a gap in full individualisation. - Evaluation is restricted to a single theoretical reference framework (BKT) on math tutoring datasets; whether the +19.9% interpretability gain and the grounding fidelity results hold for other domains, richer theoretical models, or more diverse student populations remains unvalidated.
Search Results
#62 — Innovative (68)

Data-efficient pre-training by scaling synthetic megadocs

Konwoo Kim, Suhas Kotha, Yejin Choi, Tatsunori Hashimoto, Nick Haber, Percy Liang · arxiv.org · Percy Liang - new articles
Why this rank: Scores 68 for the creative insight that organizing synthetic data into megadocuments enables longer training without overfitting, yielding 1.8× data efficiency. The latent-thoughts interleaving mechanism and the clean experimental framework isolating compute-vs-data tradeoffs are meaningful contributions, but the work operates at small scale (300M params, 200M tokens) and relies on a much stronger external generator, leaving open whether the findings transfer to frontier training regimes.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Training compute for large language models is growing 4-5× per year while the supply of unique web text is relatively fixed, creating a data-constrained pre-training regime. Prior work on synthetic data augmentation (e.g., rephrasing) focuses on transforming data into higher-quality or task-proximal formats, leaving open the question of whether synthetic data can improve i.i.d. modeling of the original web distribution. This paper directly addresses that gap: given a fixed corpus of 200M web tokens and unlimited compute, how should synthetic data be generated and organized to maximally reduce validation loss on the real distribution? Methodology: The paper operates in the "asymptotically infinite compute" regime introduced by Kim et al. (2025), training over-parameterized 300M-parameter autoregressive transformers on 164K DCLM documents with carefully tuned epoching and weight decay. Synthetic data is generated using Llama 3.1 8B Instruct as an external generator. Three synthetic data algorithms are evaluated by scaling G (generations per document): (1) Simple rephrasing — each document is independently rephrased into Wikipedia-style text and shuffled as independent training examples; (2) Stitched rephrasing — all G rephrases from the same source document are concatenated (with the real document appended last) into a single long megadocument, inspired by In-context Pre-training (ICPT); (3) Latent thoughts — the source document is split into G+1 chunks and the generator inserts rationale text connecting each prefix to its suffix, stretching the document into a megadoc with interleaved `<think></think>` spans. Megadocs routinely exceed the model's 4096-token context window, requiring cross-document attention to be enabled. Training mixes a real-data stream with a synthetic stream at a tuned mixing fraction, and both streams are epoched under hyperparameter search (learning rate, weight decay, epoch count, mixing fraction). Data efficiency is quantified by inverting the standard recipe data scaling law. Key Results: Simple rephrasing with tuned mixing reduces i.i.d. validation loss from 3.55 (real data only) to 3.41 at G=32, corresponding to 1.48× data efficiency, with a 5% average accuracy gain on PIQA/SciQ/ARC Easy. Stitched rephrasing at G=32 reaches loss 3.37 (1.64× data efficiency, +6% benchmarks); latent thoughts reach 3.34 (1.80× data efficiency, +9% benchmarks). Critically, the gap between megadoc methods and simple rephrasing widens as G increases — megadocs show less saturation. On long-context validation (arXiv CS papers), the improvements are larger: stitched rephrasing and latent thoughts achieve 0.14 and 0.19 loss reduction at G=32 relative to simple rephrasing. Megadoc benefits hold on short documents (≤621 tokens) as well, evidencing general transfer rather than a narrow long-context effect. The favorable scaling of megadocs is mechanistically attributed to their enabling 5× more training steps before overfitting (optimal epoch count rises from 8 to 32, mixing fraction from 0.75 to 0.90). All three augmentation methods compose additively with model ensembling, each improving the infinite-ensemble loss asymptote by ≥0.12 over real-data-only ensembles, whereas self-distillation does not compose with ensembling. At 1.5B parameters, loss wins are larger (e.g., +0.068 for simple rephrasing vs. 300M), suggesting benefits grow with student capability. Strengths: - The framing around asymptotically infinite compute with careful mixing and epoching provides a clean, principled way to characterize synthetic data scaling, and monotonicity of loss in G is rigorously enforced by the parameterization. - Megadoc construction is computationally cheap (no corpus-level embedding retrieval unlike ICPT), and the mechanistic explanation (enabling longer training without overfitting) is validated by controlled hyperparameter ablations. - Results are thorough: long-context, short-context, multiple held-out domains (GitHub Python, Wikipedia), downstream benchmarks, ensemble composition, and student scaling ablations all consistently support the main claims. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All experiments use an external generator (Llama 3.1 8B Instruct) that is far stronger than the student, and the paper cannot empirically demonstrate self-improvement in the actual data-constrained pre-training setting studied (200M token students trained from scratch). The argument for generalization to self-improvement rests on indirect evidence from separate works. - The experimental scale is small (300M parameters, 200M tokens), and it is unclear whether the observed data efficiency ratios and saturation behavior at G=32 hold at the model and data scales relevant to frontier training — the paper does not fit parametric scaling laws across both model size and generation count jointly.
Recommended 2603.18534 PDF
#63 — Innovative (68)

Natural-Language Agent Harnesses (8 ▲ )

Linyue Pan, Lexiao Zou, Shuo Guo, Jingchen Ni, Hai-Tao Zheng · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 30 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The idea of externalizing agent harness control logic as portable natural-language artifacts is a genuinely fresh framing that enables controlled ablation of individual harness modules — something previously impossible with code-coupled harnesses. The RQ3 finding that NLAH migration outperforms the original code harness by 16.8 points through principled relocation of reliability mechanisms is surprising and insightful. However, the RQ1 results showing the full system slightly underperforming simpler ablations on resolution rate, combined with small sample sizes and single-seed evaluation, substantially temper the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern AI agent performance increasingly depends on the surrounding "harness" — the control stack managing multi-step reasoning, tool use, memory, delegation, and stopping logic. Yet harness design is typically buried in controller code, framework-specific defaults, and runtime conventions, making it difficult to transfer across systems, compare fairly, or ablate cleanly. Two systems that nominally differ by one design choice often differ simultaneously in prompts, tool mediation, artifact conventions, and verification gates, collapsing evaluation into opaque bundle comparisons. This paper asks whether the high-level control logic of a harness can instead be externalized as a portable, executable natural-language artifact. Methodology: The paper proposes two artifacts: (1) Natural-Language Agent Harnesses (NLAHs), structured natural-language representations of harness control bound to explicit contracts, roles, stage structure, adapters, state semantics, and a failure taxonomy; and (2) the Intelligent Harness Runtime (IHR), which places an LLM in-loop to interpret NLAH text directly, executing harness logic under a shared runtime charter that separates generic runtime policy from task-family harness logic. The IHR decomposes into an in-loop LLM interpreter, a backend providing terminal tools and a multi-agent interface (spawn/wait child agents), and a runtime charter defining state semantics and contract enforcement. An optional file-backed state module externalizes durable state into path-addressable, compaction-stable artifacts. Experiments use Codex CLI with GPT-5.4 at reasoning effort "xhigh" on Docker-isolated Ubuntu 24.04 servers. Three research questions are evaluated: RQ1 (behavioral effect of runtime charter and harness logic), RQ2 (module composability and ablation), and RQ3 (code-to-text migration fidelity), on subsets of SWE-bench Verified (125 samples) and OSWorld (36 samples). Key Results: For RQ1, Full IHR does not meaningfully change issue resolution rate on SWE-bench (74.4% TRAE, 72.8% Live-SWE) relative to ablations removing the runtime skill (both 76.0%) or harness skill (75.2%), but dramatically changes process metrics: Full IHR TRAE uses 16.3M prompt tokens vs. 1.2M without the harness skill, with ~90% of all tokens and tool calls occurring in delegated child agents. Most instances (>110/125) do not flip between conditions; meaningful differences concentrate on a small frontier of component-sensitive cases. For RQ2, modules show heterogeneous effects: self-evolution provides the best accuracy-to-cost ratio (+4.8 on SWE, +2.7 on OSWorld) by tightening the attempt loop, while file-backed state (+1.6/+5.5) and evidence-backed answering (+1.6/0) improve process structure and auditability with mild score gains. Verifier (-0.8/-8.4) and multi-candidate search (-2.4/-5.6) are cost-dominated and can hurt, because local acceptance can diverge from benchmark acceptance. For RQ3, the NLAH realization of OS-Symphony reaches 47.2% vs. 30.4% for the native code harness, primarily because the migrated harness shifts from brittle GUI repair loops to file-backed state and artifact-backed verification, providing stronger completion certificates. Strengths: - The separation of shared runtime charter from task-family harness logic is a clean architectural contribution that enables controlled ablation of individual harness pattern modules — something not previously possible with code-coupled harnesses. - The RQ3 result is the paper's most surprising finding: the NLAH migration of OS-Symphony outperforms the original code harness (+16.8 points) not through faithful replication but through a principled relocation of reliability mechanisms from GUI repair to durable artifact closure, revealing a concrete benefit of the representation shift. - The paper is appropriately self-critical: it explicitly documents cases where more structure hurts (sympy__sympy-23950 resolved by simpler conditions but not by five heavier modules), and frames RQ1/RQ2 as alignment studies rather than monotonic improvement claims. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark subsets are small (125 SWE, 36 OSWorld) and evaluated with a single random seed, making the numerical comparisons — where differences of 1-3 percentage points drive the argument — statistically unreliable; the authors acknowledge plans to rerun on full benchmarks with a cheaper model. - The key performance results for RQ1 show Full IHR slightly underperforming simpler ablations on raw resolution rate (74.4% vs. 76.0% without runtime skill for TRAE), which undermines the operational viability claim; the paper reframes this as a process-change story, but for practitioners the headline number still matters. - Runtime contamination is a real and acknowledged confound: the strong shared runtime charter may absorb behavioral effects that would otherwise be attributed to harness text, making it difficult to cleanly separate what the NLAH specification itself contributes from what the IHR interpreter injects.
Recommended 2603.25723 PDF
#64 — Innovative (67)

Are Language Models Any Good at Density Modeling?

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Elegant experimental design that creates a synthetic number language as a controlled testbed for directly measuring LLM distribution fidelity — a gap no prior work addressed. The syntactic-vs-semantic decomposition of learnability and the substring bias discovery are insightful contributions. However, the vast gap between a 6.3M-parameter model on a 37-word vocabulary and real LLMs significantly limits the actionability of findings.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large Language Models are increasingly used as proxies for the probability distribution of natural language — e.g., for corpus-level linguistic analyses, simulating human writers, or estimating sentence frequencies. This rests on the untested assumption that pre-trained LLMs are accurate density models. The problem is that no ground truth distribution exists for natural language, making it impossible to directly measure how well a model captures the true distribution. Prior work (Meister & Cotterell 2021; Ilia & Aziz 2024) has raised doubts using indirect corpus metrics or human comparisons, but none have directly measured distribution fidelity against a known target. Methodology: The authors create a controlled synthetic language consisting of all integers from 0 to 2,000,000 written out in English words (e.g., "forty six thousand seven hundred and seven"). This language has genuine syntactic and semantic complexity analogous to natural language, while being orderable on a single numeric axis, enabling well-defined probability mass functions. A GPT-2-style transformer (6.3M parameters, 8 decoder layers, 4 attention heads, embedding dim 256) is trained from scratch on 10% of this language (i.i.d. sampled) under various target distributions: uniform, linear, binomial, sinusoidal with period 3, and sinusoidal with period 10. The learned distribution P_model is compared to the true distribution P_true via KL divergence (using a low-variance unbiased estimator) and visual inspection of PMF/CMF curves. A secondary experiment tests "passage mode" where numbers are grouped into multi-element sequences, probing whether sentence-position context affects learned probabilities. Key Results: Models learn syntactically correct numbers with <1% error rate quickly, regardless of training data fraction. For distributions learnable from syntactic patterns (uniform, linear, binomial, sinusoidal-period-10), the model achieves near-perfect alignment: KL divergence drops from 0.23 to 0.004 for uniform (98.33% improvement), from 0.39 to 0.029 for linear (92.51% improvement), and from 0.43 to 0.007 for sinusoidal-period-10 (98.36% improvement). However, for sinusoidal-period-3 — whose phase cannot be read off any syntactic suffix — the model completely fails, with KL divergence actually increasing from 0.688 to 0.732 (−6.43%). The model collapses to approximately uniform rather than learning divisibility-by-3 semantics. A strong "substring bias" is identified: numbers like "one" and multiples of ten receive disproportionately high probabilities because they appear as substrings in large fractions of the corpus (e.g., "hundred" appears in >99.99% of all numbers), and the model fails to discount these co-occurrences from contextually isolated appearances. Passage-mode experiments confirm that first-position probabilities in a passage closely mirror the standalone trained distribution, supporting prior work's assumption that sentence-level probabilities can be treated as context-independent. Strengths: - Elegant experimental design: the synthetic number language is a principled controlled testbed that allows ground-truth distribution comparison — a gap no prior work filled directly. - The syntactic vs. semantic decomposition of distribution learnability is a clean and insightful conceptual contribution that explains why models succeed or fail. - The substring bias discovery is a concrete, well-evidenced failure mode with direct implications for downstream NLP tasks (e.g., answer-preference bias in QA models). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The synthetic language (2M numbers, 37-word vocabulary, 6.3M parameter model) is vastly simpler than real LLMs trained on natural language; the generalizability of findings — particularly the degree of substring bias — to billion-parameter models on diverse corpora is asserted but not demonstrated. - The paper only tests one-dimensional, numeric distributions; natural language has high-dimensional, compositional structure that may exhibit qualitatively different density modeling failure modes not captured here. - The "passage mode" experiment that validates prior work's assumption is done on the same synthetic language, so it provides only weak indirect evidence for the original natural-language claim.
Recommended
#65 — Innovative (67)

SAMSEM--A Generic and Scalable Approach for IC Metal Line Segmentation

Christian Gehrmann, Jonas Ricker, Simon Damm, Deruo Cheng, Julian Speith, Yiqiong Shi, Asja Fischer, Christof Paar · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: SAMSEM makes a meaningful contribution by combining multi-scale fusion, topology-aware training via Betti matching loss, and the largest-to-date diverse IC SEM dataset (14 ICs, 48 metal layers, 200–20 nm nodes) to achieve strong out-of-distribution generalization. The use of electrically significant defect metrics rather than pixel accuracy is well-motivated for the downstream netlist extraction task, and the 4.5× OOD improvement over baselines is substantial. The multi-scale decision algorithm elegantly addresses the downscaling-induced short circuit problem without requiring architectural changes to SAM2. While fine-tuning a foundation model is not itself novel, the domain-specific innovations in loss design and evaluation methodology represent a clear contribution to hardware assurance.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Post-manufacturing hardware assurance requires physically inspecting integrated circuits (ICs) layer by layer via scanning electron microscope (SEM) imaging to verify the absence of malicious circuitry (hardware Trojans). A critical step is accurately segmenting metal lines from SEM images to reconstruct gate-level netlists. Existing ML-based approaches fail to generalize across ICs: models trained on one IC's images require retraining or manual parameter tuning for each new IC, technology node, or SEM configuration. Prior work evaluated on at most four ICs and ignored out-of-distribution generalization entirely. The problem is compounded by varying metal line geometry, SEM imaging artifacts, sample preparation defects, and fine-grained structures that fuse under naive downscaling. Methodology: SAMSEM fine-tunes Meta's Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM2) for IC metal layer segmentation. Three core innovations distinguish it from prior work. First, a dual-model multi-scale segmentation pipeline is introduced: Model 1 processes the full original-size image (downscaled to 1024×1024), capturing global context; Model 2 processes 512×512 patches (upscaled to 1024×1024), preserving fine-grained structure. A patch-level decision algorithm selects between the two outputs based on speckle counts and inter-model agreement, using thresholds tuned via grid search. Second, topology-aware training via the Betti matching loss (based on persistent homology) supplements pixel-based BCE+Dice losses with the combined objective L_seg = L_pixel + λ·L_Betti. This penalizes topologically incorrect segmentations—open circuits and short circuits—which are the errors that matter for netlist extraction, even if pixel accuracy is otherwise comparable. Third, all three SAM2 components (image encoder, prompt encoder, mask decoder) are fine-tuned jointly, rather than just the decoder as in the predecessor SAMIC; ablations show the image encoder dominates accuracy gains. Hyperparameters are optimized via Optuna's hyperband pruner over a two-phase search (pixel losses first, then topology loss). The model is trained on an unprecedented dataset of 48 metal layers across 14 ICs spanning 200 nm to 20 nm process nodes, diverse SEM equipment, and varying sample preparation techniques. Key Results: On in-distribution evaluation (seven ICs used for training, tested on held-out images from those same ICs), SAMSEM achieves a 0.72% ESD error rate versus 4.44% for U-Net (the strongest baseline), a roughly 6× improvement. Pixel-level metrics (PA=0.972, Dice=0.960, IoU=0.925) are similar across all methods, confirming that the ESD gap is specifically due to topological correctness rather than raw pixel quality. On out-of-distribution evaluation (seven fully unseen ICs), SAMSEM achieves 5.53% ESD error versus 24.77% for DeepLabV3 (best baseline), representing roughly a 4.5× improvement; SAMIC degrades to 27.16% out-of-distribution. Ablations confirm that (a) multi-scale fusion is essential—single-model variants yield 5.25–9.52% OOD error; (b) Betti matching loss reduces shorts and false positives substantially; (c) fine-tuning all encoder/decoder components, especially the image encoder, is critical. Training on all 14 ICs yields a final ESD error rate of 0.62%. Generalization improves monotonically with the number of training ICs and has not yet saturated, suggesting further dataset growth will yield additional gains. Strengths: - The multi-scale fusion design directly addresses a concrete failure mode (downscaling-induced short circuits) with an elegant, lightweight decision rule rather than architectural changes to SAM2. - Use of the ESD metric—counting electrically significant errors (opens, shorts, FPs, FNs)—rather than pixel accuracy is the right evaluation choice for this downstream task, and the results show SAMSEM's advantage is specifically in this practically meaningful dimension. - The dataset scale (14 ICs, 48 metal layers, spanning 200–20 nm) is a genuine contribution to a field that has historically been starved of diverse training data; releasing the model and scripts as open source lowers barriers for follow-on work. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Ground truth is manually annotated without access to GDSII design files, introducing labeling noise that is acknowledged but unquantified; some reported ESD errors may be artifacts of imperfect annotation rather than model errors. - The topology loss (Betti matching) shows mixed OOD behavior: it reduces shorts and false positives but increases open circuits relative to pixel-only training, and its hyperparameter optimization was constrained by compute budget—the authors explicitly note they could not exhaustively explore the Betti matching parameter space. - Training a single H100 for 72 hours and hyperparameter search running for 7 days limits practical reproducibility and iteration speed; the approach as presented is not easily accessible to resource-constrained researchers despite being open-sourced.
Search Results 2603.16548 PDF
#66 — Innovative (66)

QuitoBench: A High-Quality Open Time Series Forecasting Benchmark (11 ▲ )

Siqiao Xue, Zhaoyang Zhu, Wei Zhang, Rongyao Cai, Rui Wang, Yixiang Mu, Fan Zhou, Jianguo Li, Peng Di, Hang Yu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 2 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 66 for its principled TSF regime taxonomy that replaces superficial domain labels with quantitative trend/seasonality/forecastability characterization—a methodological advance that reveals previously obscured findings like the context-length crossover and data-scaling dominance. The leakage-free single-provenance design and dense rolling-window evaluation are rigorous. However, as a benchmark paper from a single organization (Alipay), the contribution is inherently bounded in novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Time series forecasting lacks the canonical, large-scale benchmarks that anchor progress in NLP (GLUE), vision (ImageNet/COCO), and speech (LibriSpeech). Existing large-scale benchmarks such as GIFT-Eval and the Timer suite suffer from four structural flaws: (1) coarse domain-based categorization (electricity, traffic, weather) that conflates series with radically different predictability; (2) severe distributional skew—GIFT-Eval concentrates 50.7% of series in a single TSF regime, Timer 76.2%—causing aggregate metrics to reflect data prevalence rather than model capability; (3) information leakage from reuse of public datasets that overlap with foundation model pre-training corpora; and (4) short-series bias (50% of GIFT-Eval series have fewer than 200 time points), precluding long-context evaluation. The result is that the field cannot reliably answer whether a 200M-parameter foundation model is worth deploying over a 1M-parameter task-specific model, or under what conditions each excels. Methodology: The paper introduces QUITO, a 1.6-billion-token time series corpus of application traffic from Alipay's production platform, spanning nine business verticals (finance, commerce, advertising, infrastructure, risk/compliance, etc.) at two granularities (10-minute and 1-hour). The core methodological contribution is a TSF regime taxonomy: each series is characterized along three axes—trend strength (T), seasonality strength (S), and forecastability (F)—computed via STL decomposition (for T and S) and normalized spectral entropy via Welch's method (F = 1 − H). Binarizing each axis at threshold τ=0.4 yields 8 TSF regime cells. QUITOBENCH is then constructed by stratified sampling of approximately 162 series per regime cell (1,290 test series total), achieving near-uniform ~12% per-cell distribution versus the extreme skew of prior benchmarks. Leakage is eliminated by construction: all data originates from a single proprietary operational environment with no overlap with any public pre-training corpus, and a global temporal cutoff separates train/validation/test. Evaluation employs dense rolling windows (unit stride, up to 1,489 windows per series per horizon), generating ~1.6×10^7 predictions per model—one to two orders of magnitude more than sparse GIFT-Eval-style evaluation. Ten models are benchmarked: five deep learning architectures (CrossFormer, DLinear, iTransformer, PatchTST, TSMixer; 0.3–5M parameters), three foundation models (Chronos-2, TimesFM-2.5, TiRex; 30–200M parameters), and two statistical baselines, across 18 configurations (3 context lengths × 3 horizons × 2 forecasting modes). Key Results: Four findings emerge that prior benchmarks obscure. (1) Context-length crossover: at L=96, deep learning models outperform foundation models by 24.6% MAE; at L≥576 the ranking reverses, with foundation models leading by 15–22%. Foundation models improve 43–50% MAE when context grows from short to long; deep learning improves only 7–12%. (2) Forecastability dominates difficulty: across all models, high-forecastability series achieve 3.64× lower MAE than low-forecastability series. The pathological HIGH_LOW_LOW regime (high trend, low seasonality, low forecastability) averages MAE 0.749 versus 0.205 for HIGH_HIGH_HIGH; even the best model (CrossFormer, MAE 0.600) performs 2.91× worse there than on easy series, and statistical baselines fail catastrophically (MAE > 1.0). Foundation models degrade only 1.75–1.8× across forecastability levels versus 2.0–2.3× for deep learning, suggesting better noise tolerance from pre-training. (3) Parameter efficiency: CrossFormer (1M parameters, MAE 0.279) outperforms Chronos-2 (100M parameters, MAE 0.314); on average, deep learning models at 1.9M parameters match foundation models at 110M parameters (MAE 0.312 vs. 0.319; not statistically significant, Cohen's d=−0.067). (4) Data scaling dominates model scaling: CrossFormer's MAE drops 66% (0.725→0.248) as training tokens increase from 10K to 100M, versus 24% for TimesFM-2.5; both families plateau well before 100M parameters, confirming that more data is far more valuable than larger models. Cross-benchmark validation shows Spearman ρ=0.865 between QUITOBENCH and Timer rankings, and cross-metric consistency of ρ=0.847 between MAE and MSE rankings per configuration. Strengths: - The TSF regime framework is a genuinely principled alternative to domain-label categorization, empirically validated: forecastability explains 3.64× difficulty variation, while domain labels show no consistent relationship with error magnitude. - The leakage-free, single-provenance design with global temporal cutoffs is methodologically rigorous and eliminates both direct and indirect contamination channels that compromise most existing benchmarks. - Dense rolling-window evaluation (up to 1,489 windows per series) produces far more statistically stable per-series MAE estimates than sparse GIFT-Eval-style schemes, enabling reliable regime-level diagnosis. - The scaling analysis with CrossFormer and TimesFM-2.5 provides concrete, practically actionable guidance: data scaling yields 2–3× more improvement per log-unit than model scaling for both families. - Cross-benchmark (ρ=0.865) and cross-metric (ρ=0.847) consistency validates that findings are neither dataset-specific artifacts nor metric-dependent. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All data originates from a single organization (Alipay), and while the authors demonstrate that QUITO achieves 1.67× higher regime entropy than Timer, workload patterns specific to other cloud platforms (batch-heavy HPC, CDN edge caches, non-Chinese internet traffic) are necessarily absent; the claim of generalizability beyond Alipay-style operational traffic remains an assumption rather than an empirical fact. - The benchmark covers only point forecasting of application traffic; the authors explicitly acknowledge that results may not transfer to probabilistic forecasting, anomaly detection, or domains with qualitatively different data (e.g., financial returns, biomedical signals). - The data scaling experiment is limited to two models (CrossFormer and TimesFM-2.5), with TimesFM-2.5 chosen partly because Chronos and TiRex do not expose clean fine-tuning APIs; the conclusion that "data scaling dominates" may not generalize uniformly across all foundation model architectures, particularly tokenization-based models like Chronos. - The binary TSF threshold (τ=0.4) is analyst-chosen; while sensitivity sweeps over [0.3, 0.5] confirm robustness for τ≥0.4, the threshold itself is not derived from theory, and the regime taxonomy reduces continuous temporal dynamics to 8 discrete cells that may not capture all practically important distinctions.
Recommended 2603.26017 PDF
#67 — Innovative (65)

On the Foundations of Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence

TJ Dunham · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scores 65 for formalizing a real but underappreciated problem — floating-point non-associativity silently undermining AI verification — and delivering an impressive engineering solution (99K lines of Rust achieving bitwise cross-platform determinism for 7B models). The trust entropy framework and verification collapse theorem provide useful theoretical structure. However, the deeply confounded perplexity comparison (INT8 Chat vs FP16 base), narrow model coverage, and overstated impossibility claims about floating-point trust weaken the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern neural network inference is non-deterministic: the same model with the same input produces different outputs on different hardware due to IEEE 754 floating-point non-associativity. ARM NEON (128-bit) and x86 AVX2 (256-bit) use different SIMD reduction trees, producing different rounding at every dot product. These per-layer divergences compound exponentially through transformer depth, eventually flipping token selections. The paper argues this is not a minor nuisance but the single largest obstacle to trustworthy AI, because it silently invalidates verification (a re-executor on different hardware gets a different result), reproducibility, auditability (audit traces are hardware-specific), and cross-platform certification. Every proposed trust property — fairness, robustness, privacy, safety, alignment — is shown to presuppose platform-deterministic inference, usually without stating the assumption. Methodology: The paper formalizes the Determinism Thesis: an AI system can be verified, reproduced, audited, and certified if and only if its inference function is platform-deterministic. The theoretical core includes: (1) necessity/sufficiency proofs (Theorems 4–5) showing that trust iff determinism; (2) trust entropy — a Rényi collision entropy over the distribution of outputs across hardware platforms — with an exact closed-form for verification failure probability: P(reject | honest) = 1 − 2^{−H_T}; (3) a Compositional Determinism Decay theorem quantifying exponential divergence growth through residual layers; and (4) a Determinism-Verification Collapse theorem showing that verification collapses to O(1) hash comparison under determinism but requires solving an intractable set-membership problem over combinatorially many valid outputs without it. The engineering solution is the ARC engine: a pure integer arithmetic inference engine implemented in ~99,000 lines of Rust. Weights are stored as INT8 with per-row Q16 scale factors; activations are Q16 (16 fractional bits); dot products accumulate in 64-bit integers. Every transformer operation is re-implemented in integer arithmetic: RMSNorm uses Newton-Raphson inverse square root in Q16 converging in 3 iterations; RoPE precomputes cos/sin tables at load time in Q16; SiLU uses a 257-entry lookup table with linear interpolation. The only floating-point occurs at RoPE table precomputation, but FP64 ULP differences (≈2^{−52}) vanish under Q16 quantization (resolution 2^{−16}). KV cache is kept at full Q16 precision after INT8 KV experiments caused incorrect position selection at ~10 tokens. The trust stack layers integer arithmetic → determinism → DAG consensus → cryptographic attestation → verification → certification. Circle STARK proofs over the Mersenne-31 field provide succinct verification for light clients, with a 152-byte on-chain commitment receipt per layer. Key Results: In 82 cross-platform tests (ARM Apple M2 Ultra NEON vs. x86 Vultr AVX2), the ARC engine produces zero hash mismatches across Llama-2-7B (6.7B parameters, 8–512 tokens) and TinyLlama-1.1B (8–1,024 tokens). As a controlled comparison, the candle Q4_K_M floating-point backend produces identical hashes across same-architecture x86 nodes for sequences ≤64 tokens but diverges at 128+ tokens — precisely as predicted by the non-associativity theorem. Four geographically distributed nodes (US, Europe ×2, Asia) running Llama-2-7B via candle agreed on 30/30 prompts (≤64 tokens), with 356 on-chain attestation transactions finalized through DAG consensus. On performance, the ARC GPU engine achieves 76 ms/token on M2 Ultra versus 175 ms/token for candle float — 2.3× faster — because integer arithmetic eliminates FP denormal handling and rounding-mode management. On CPU it is 1.26× faster (139 vs. 175 ms/token). Perplexity on WikiText-2 is 144 (INT8 Chat model) vs. 5.47 (FP16 base model), a gap the paper attributes mainly to instruction-tuning distribution shift and INT8 bit-width rather than the deterministic approach per se. STARK proof generation for 1024×1024 layers takes 1,357 ms with a constant 152-byte on-chain commitment regardless of dimension. Strengths: - The central insight — that arithmetic associativity is the root cause of AI non-determinism and that integer arithmetic resolves it by algebraic necessity — is both correct and underappreciated; the formal framework (trust entropy, verification collapse) gives it precise quantitative content. - The implementation is practical and impressive in scale: 7B-parameter cross-architecture bitwise determinism is demonstrated, not just claimed, with a live testnet and reproducible artifacts. - The performance claim is counterintuitive and striking: the integer engine is faster than the floating-point baseline on every tested backend, turning determinism from a cost into a win. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The perplexity comparison (INT8 Chat PPL=144 vs. FP16 base PPL=5.47) is deeply confounded — different model variants, different bit widths — and the paper's own caveats reveal that no clean ablation isolates the quantization cost of the deterministic approach; the quality case remains unproven for instruction-following tasks at realistic sequence lengths. - The experimental scope is narrow: only Llama-2-7B and TinyLlama-1.1B are tested, only greedy decoding is implemented (temperature sampling requires a seeded PRNG, which is described but not validated), STARK proofs cover only dense layers (attention, RMSNorm, and activation proofs are listed as future work), and the testnet uses underpowered 2-vCPU cloud nodes that cannot test the integer engine's throughput at scale. - Several theoretical claims are overstated. The sufficiency proof (Theorem 5) constructs trust via re-execution but assumes re-execution is practical, which is expensive for 7B+ models; STARK proofs are offered as the lightweight alternative but the AIR covers only matrix multiply. The "proof" that floating-point inference is fundamentally incompatible (Theorem 9) is a valid counterexample but the broader claim that no trust is possible under floating-point ignores probabilistic trust frameworks and same-hardware deployments.
Search Results 2603.24904 PDF
#68 — Innovative (65)

Make Geometry Matter for Spatial Reasoning (21 ▲ )

Shihua Zhang, Qiuhong Shen, Shizun Wang, Tianbo Pan, Xinchao Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 31 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Scores 65 for rigorously documenting the geometry underutilization failure mode and providing a clean two-part solution. The query-guided masking for dynamic scenes is a thoughtful adaptation, and ablations convincingly isolate each component's contribution. However, the core techniques (token masking, learned gating) are well-established, and static scene gains are modest.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-language models (VLMs) struggle with spatial reasoning despite excelling at general visual understanding. A natural remedy is injecting geometry tokens from pretrained 3D foundation models into VLMs. However, the authors identify a reproducible failure mode: under naive token fusion with standard fine-tuning, injected geometry tokens are largely ignored by the VLM, which instead defaults to 2D appearance shortcuts. For static scenes, naive geometry injection yields only marginal gains (49.8 → 50.2 average); for dynamic scenes, it actually hurts performance relative to no geometry at all (64.0 → 62.8). This underutilization problem is the central motivation for GeoSR. Methodology: GeoSR introduces two complementary mechanisms on top of the standard geometry-aware VLM framework (Qwen2.5-VL-7B backbone with VGGT or π³ as the frozen geometry encoder). First, Geometry-Unleashing Masking applies a binary mask over 2D vision tokens during training (masking ratio γ=0.8, enabled with probability β=0.5), forcing the model to consult geometry tokens when visual shortcuts are suppressed. For static scenes, masking is random (MAE-style); for dynamic scenes, masking is query-guided — attention weights from a QFormer cross-attention over geometry tokens identify the most spatially relevant positions, and corresponding vision tokens are masked via TopK selection. Second, Geometry-Guided Fusion replaces naive additive or concatenation fusion with a learned per-token, per-channel gating mechanism: α = σ(W_g[LN(F̃_V) ‖ LN(F̃_G)] + b_g), producing fused tokens F = α⊙V + (1−α)⊙G. For dynamic settings, a cross-attention step first redistributes compact bottleneck geometry evidence back to full spatiotemporal resolution before gating. Masking is disabled at inference; only the learned gate controls geometry contribution. Training uses 4×H200 GPUs for 1 epoch (~14–20 hours). Key Results: On static spatial reasoning (VSI-Bench, 5k+ QA pairs), GeoSR achieves 51.9 average accuracy, surpassing VG-LLM (50.7) and Spatial-MLLM (48.4), and outperforming proprietary models GPT-4o (47.8) and Gemini-1.5-Pro (48.8). On dynamic spatial reasoning (DSR-Bench, 1484 QA pairs), GeoSR achieves 66.1 average accuracy, a substantial improvement over the prior best open-source method GSM (58.9) — a +7.2 point gain — and far exceeding GPT-5 (30.8) and Gemini-2.5-Pro (31.7). Ablations confirm both components are necessary: replacing Geometry-Guided Fusion with naive fusion drops dynamic average from 66.1 to 64.7; removing Geometry-Unleashing Masking drops it to 64.9; both removed together drops to 58.1. Computational overhead is negligible: GeoSR adds only 0.07B parameters (9.16B → 9.23B) and 0.01s per inference step over the geometry-aware baseline. Strengths: - Identifies and rigorously documents a previously overlooked failure mode (geometry underutilization under naive fusion), supported by reproducible ablations across both static and dynamic benchmarks - The masking-based training signal is simple and principled — analogous to MAE but adapted with query-guided TopK selection for dynamic settings — and adds negligible inference cost - The gated fusion mechanism provides fine-grained per-token, per-channel control, which is clearly more appropriate than uniform mixing; the design cleanly separates the "make geometry effective" and "make geometry reasonable" objectives - Strong empirical results across two distinct benchmark regimes with thorough ablation studies validating each design choice independently Weaknesses / Limitations: - The gains on static spatial reasoning over the best prior method (VG-LLM: 50.7 → 51.9) are modest, suggesting that static scenes may not benefit as dramatically from the approach and that the headroom from geometry is smaller there - Evaluation benchmarks (VSI-Bench, DSR-Bench) are acknowledged to have annotation quality issues — ambiguous question formulations and noisy ground-truth labels from semi-automatic pipelines — which limits the reliability of absolute performance numbers and may obscure true model capabilities - The method is evaluated only with Qwen2.5-VL-7B as backbone and specific geometry encoders (VGGT, π³); generalizability to other VLM architectures or geometry models is not demonstrated
Recommended 2603.26639 PDF
#69 — Innovative (65)

Know3D: Prompting 3D Generation with Knowledge from Vision-Language Models (9 ▲ )

Wenyue Chen, Wenjue Chen, Peng Li, Qinghe Wang, Xu Jia, Heliang Zheng, Rongfei Jia, Yuan Liu, Ronggang Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 30 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Know3D presents a technically sound approach to injecting VLM semantic knowledge into 3D generation via intermediate MMDiT hidden states rather than decoded pixels, which is a meaningful contribution over naive pipeline approaches. The language-controllable back-view synthesis capability is genuinely new. However, the improvements over baselines are modest in absolute terms, and the reliance on VLM quality introduces brittleness.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Generating 3D assets from a single image is fundamentally ill-posed: existing models can only hallucinate occluded regions (e.g., the back of an object) based on limited 3D training data, producing results that are either geometrically implausible or misaligned with user intent. Unlike images or text, 3D datasets are orders of magnitude smaller, so 3D generative models lack the world knowledge needed to reason about unseen structure. No prior work enabled users to semantically specify what the back-view of a generated 3D asset should look like via natural language. Methodology: Know3D introduces a two-stage framework that transfers semantic knowledge from a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) into a 3D generation model without directly injecting abstract VLM features. First, Qwen-Image-Edit (a VLM-diffusion hybrid using Qwen2.5-VL + Multimodal Diffusion Transformer / MMDiT) is fine-tuned on 5k high-quality 3D assets with LoRA (rank=64) to generate spatially accurate back-view images from a front-view image and a text description. A stochastic prompting strategy during fine-tuning trains the model for both unconditional and text-conditioned back-view generation using Conditional Flow Matching. Second, instead of decoding the generated back-view image and re-extracting features (which amplifies errors), Know3D extracts intermediate latent hidden states directly from MMDiT layers at a specific denoising timestep (t=0.25) and injects them into TRELLIS2, the 3D generation backbone, via a new parallel cross-attention branch with zero-initialized output projection for stable training. This avoids pixel-level noise amplification and preserves TRELLIS2's pretrained priors by keeping its self-attention and image-cross-attention layers frozen. Key Results: On HY3D-Bench, Know3D achieves the best ULIP (0.2174) and Uni3D (0.3518) scores on the test split, outperforming Hunyuan3D-2.1 (0.2140 / 0.3434), TRELLIS (0.2143 / 0.3421), and Hunyuan3D-2MV (0.2108 / 0.3413). Ablation studies on sparse voxel generation show MMDiT hidden states at t=0.25 achieve IoU=0.352 and CD=2.262, compared to VAE features (IoU=0.308, CD=2.803) and DINOv3 features (IoU=0.342, CD=2.385). The t=0.25 timestep outperforms earlier (t=0, IoU=0.343) and later (t=0.75, IoU=0.336) stages. Qualitatively, Know3D demonstrates flexible language-controlled back-view synthesis—users can vary text prompts to obtain structurally distinct back geometries—while Hunyuan3D-2MV generates distorted geometries when given similar view conditions. Strengths: - The hidden-state injection approach is technically well-motivated: MMDiT intermediate features capture both semantic and spatial structure, outperforming VAE and DINOv3 alternatives by significant margins. - The method achieves true language-controllable back-view synthesis, a capability absent in all prior single-image-to-3D methods, without requiring a full LLM-as-generator paradigm that degrades quality. - LoRA-based fine-tuning with zero-initialized cross-attention injection preserves pretrained priors and enables stable training, a sound engineering choice. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is limited to ULIP and Uni3D (image-mesh semantic similarity) and proxy geometry metrics (IoU, CD on sparse voxels); there is no user study or perceptual quality metric directly measuring back-view controllability fidelity. - The framework depends critically on Qwen-Image-Edit producing reasonable back-view images; the paper acknowledges that when Qwen-Image generates erroneous results, the MMDiT hidden-state injection partially compensates but does not fully recover, suggesting brittleness to VLM failure modes. - The dataset for fine-tuning (5k assets from TexVerse) is relatively small; generalization to diverse object categories not well-represented in this set is not rigorously evaluated.
Recommended 2603.22782 PDF
#70 — Innovative (65)

ShotStream: Streaming Multi-Shot Video Generation for Interactive Storytelling (84 ▲ )

Yawen Luo, Xiaoyu Shi, Junhao Zhuang, Yutian Chen, Quande Liu, Xintao Wang, Pengfei Wan, Tianfan Xue · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 30 Mar 2026
Why this rank: ShotStream makes a solid practical contribution by enabling real-time interactive multi-shot video generation at 16 FPS — a 25× speedup over bidirectional methods — through a well-executed teacher-student distillation pipeline. The dual-cache memory with RoPE discontinuity indicator is a clean positional encoding solution, and the two-stage progressive distillation effectively bridges the train-test gap. However, the individual components (DMD distillation, context caching, RoPE modifications) are relatively standard techniques recombined for a new setting rather than fundamental methodological advances.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-shot video generation — producing sequences of cinematically coherent shots that maintain character and scene consistency while advancing a narrative — is essential for long-form AI storytelling. Existing methods rely on bidirectional attention architectures that suffer from two critical limitations: (1) lack of interactivity, since all shot prompts must be provided upfront and changing any shot requires full re-generation; and (2) prohibitive latency, since bidirectional attention scales quadratically with sequence length (e.g., HoloCine takes ~25 minutes for a 240-frame video). Purely autoregressive long-video models exist but are limited to single-scene generation and struggle with multi-shot narrative coherence. ShotStream targets the intersection: real-time, interactive, multi-shot video generation. Methodology: ShotStream uses a teacher-student distillation pipeline built on top of Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B (a 1.3B DiT-based text-to-video model). Step 1 fine-tunes a bidirectional next-shot teacher: sparse context frames from previous shots (dynamically sampled within a 6-frame budget) are encoded via the 3D VAE and injected by temporal token concatenation with the noisy target latent, enabling the model to generate the next shot conditioned on historical context. Shot-specific captions are also provided via cross-attention to bind visual history to text. Only the 3D self-attention layers are trained (10K steps, lr=1e-5). Step 2 distills this ~50-step bidirectional teacher into an efficient 4-step causal generator via Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD), which minimizes reverse KL divergence between the data and generator distributions. Two innovations address the unique challenges of causal multi-shot generation: (a) A dual-cache memory mechanism — a global context cache (sparse historical frames for inter-shot consistency) and a local context cache (recently generated frames for intra-shot consistency) — coupled with a RoPE discontinuity indicator (Θ_t = φt + kθ per shot boundary) to explicitly distinguish the two caches, preventing temporal ambiguity. (b) A two-stage progressive distillation strategy: Stage 1 (intra-shot self-forcing) conditions on ground-truth history while rolling out the current shot chunk-by-chunk causally; Stage 2 (inter-shot self-forcing with LoRA) conditions on self-generated prior shots, closely mirroring inference and bridging the train-test gap. Key Results: On a 100-prompt evaluation set generated by Gemini 2.5 Pro, ShotStream achieves state-of-the-art across all key dimensions against 7 baselines (3 bidirectional: Mask2DiT, EchoShot, CineTrans; 4 causal: Self Forcing, LongLive, Rolling Forcing, Infinity-RoPE). Intra-shot subject consistency: 0.825 vs. best baseline 0.758 (LongLive). Inter-shot subject consistency: 0.654 vs. best 0.594 (LongLive). Background inter-shot consistency: 0.645 vs. best 0.565 (LongLive). Transition control (Shot Cut Accuracy): 0.978 vs. best 0.715 (Infinity-RoPE). Text alignment: 0.234 vs. best 0.223 (Rolling Forcing). Throughput: ~16 FPS on a single H200 GPU, representing >25× speedup over bidirectional models, with minimal degradation vs. single-scene causal models. In a user study (54 participants, 24 video comparisons), ShotStream was preferred by 87.69% on visual consistency, 76.15% on prompt following, and 83.08% on visual quality — decisively outperforming all baselines. Ablations confirm that each component (dynamic sampling, multi-caption injection, frame concat, RoPE discontinuity, two-stage distillation) contributes meaningfully; the inter-shot self-forcing stage is particularly critical for long-term color and style consistency. Strengths: - The dual-cache memory + RoPE discontinuity indicator is a clean, training-free positional encoding solution to the ambiguity problem in causal multi-shot generation, outperforming learned embeddings. - The two-stage distillation strategy effectively bridges the train-test gap in a principled way, with both stages shown by ablation to be necessary — Stage 1 builds next-shot capability, Stage 2 corrects inter-shot error accumulation. - The evaluation methodology is rigorous: automatic metrics across five dimensions (intra/inter-shot consistency, transition control, text alignment, aesthetic quality) plus a substantial user study (54 participants), with baselines drawn from both bidirectional and autoregressive families. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The model is trained on an internal, non-public 320K multi-shot video dataset with proprietary hierarchical annotations, making reproducibility and independent benchmarking difficult despite code release. - Backbone capacity is limited (1.3B parameters); the paper acknowledges visual artifacts and identity confusion under complex prompts and attributes this to model scale rather than proposing a solution. Scaling experiments are absent. - Evaluation uses only 100 LLM-generated prompts (via Gemini 2.5 Pro), which may not capture the full diversity and difficulty of real cinematic storytelling; no public multi-shot video benchmark is used for comparison.
Recommended 2603.25746 PDF
#71 — Innovative (65)

HippoCamp: Benchmarking Contextual Agents on Personal Computers (16 ▲ )

Zhe Yang, Shulin Tian, Kairui Hu, Shuai Liu, Hoang-Nhat Nguyen, Yichi Zhang, Zujin Guo, Mengying Yu, Zinan Zhang, Jingkang Yang, Chen Change Loy, Ziwei Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 2 Apr 2026
Why this rank: HippoCamp opens a genuinely underexplored evaluation space — device-scale personal file systems with multimodal, longitudinal reasoning — and the 46K structured trajectory annotations enable actionable failure diagnosis. The systematic finding that retrieval and answer quality decouple is valuable, though the benchmark's limitation to three archetypal profiles constrains generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Personal AI assistants must navigate users' local file systems—vast, heterogeneous, multimodal repositories of documents, images, audio, and video accumulated over years. Yet no standardized benchmark exists for evaluating agents on this capability. Existing evaluations target web automation, code generation, or document-level retrieval in generic domains, ignoring the long-term continuity, cross-modal evidence dependencies, idiosyncratic folder structures, and personalized reasoning required in realistic personal computing. HippoCamp fills this gap by benchmarking agents on realistic, device-scale personal file systems requiring grounded retrieval, cross-file pattern inference, and long-horizon reasoning. Methodology: HippoCamp constructs three archetypal user profiles—(a) Bei Weiwei (student/content creator), (b) Adam Turner (legal executive), and (c) Victoria Anne Clarke (senior financial analyst)—aggregated from interviews with 100+ real participants, totaling 42.4 GB across 2K+ heterogeneous files (documents, images, video, audio, text). From these profiles, 581 QA pairs spanning two task types are constructed: factual retention (retrieve and reason over verifiable, file-grounded facts) and profiling (synthesize cross-file, cross-modal, longitudinal cues into coherent user-level inferences about preferences, routines, and workflows). Each QA is paired with a densely annotated structured trajectory—step-wise rationale, localized file evidence with atomic pointers, and agent capability labels (search, perception, reasoning)—yielding 46.1K annotations for fine-grained failure diagnosis. Evaluation covers three method regimes: RAG pipelines (Standard RAG, Self-RAG), search-agent methods (ReAct with Gemini-2.5-Flash and Qwen3-30B, Search-R1), and autonomous agents (Docker terminal agents with GPT-5.2, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Qwen3-VL; ChatGPT Agent Mode). Assessment uses LLM-as-judge (GPT-4o) for answer accuracy and F1/recall against annotated evidence file sets for retrieval quality. Key Results: A large performance gap separates all evaluated systems from human capability. The best system overall, ChatGPT Agent Mode, achieves only 48.3% profiling accuracy and 62.8% factual retention accuracy. RAG methods fail badly on profiling (Standard RAG: 26.7% Acc; Self-RAG: 10.0% Acc). Search agents improve on factual retention (ReAct Gemini-2.5-Flash: 55.3% on Adam profile) but collapse on profiling (Search-R1: 5.0% overall profiling Acc, 0.0% on two of three profiles). Retrieval and answer quality systematically decouple: when retrieval F1 exceeds accuracy, methods locate relevant files but fail to convert them into correct answers (evidence grounding breakdown); when accuracy exceeds F1, models answer correctly via parametric knowledge without grounding in file evidence (hallucination mode). Profile difficulty tracks structural clarity, not domain complexity—Adam's formal legal environment (ChatGPT: 90.3% factual, 55.0% profiling) is far easier than Bei's informal, media-rich college environment (31.2% factual, 35.0% profiling). Capability-wise decomposition identifies multimodal perception as the dominant bottleneck: even the strongest system achieves only 28.5% profiling perception accuracy versus 56.5% search accuracy. Five recurring failure modes are identified: off-target retrieval, grounding avoidance, evidence hallucination, entity misbinding, and missing verification. Strengths: - Device-scale realism: 42.4 GB across 2K+ files spanning five modalities creates a genuine stress test, unlike toy or single-domain benchmarks. - Fine-grained failure diagnosis: 46.1K structured trajectory annotations with atomic evidence pointers enable localization of failure to specific pipeline stages (search, perception, reasoning), making the benchmark actionable for targeted improvement. - Systematic coverage of evaluation regimes: The benchmark evaluates three qualitatively different paradigms (RAG, search-agent, autonomous agent) under a unified, profile-local evaluation framework with retrieval and answer quality metrics decoupled. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Only three user profiles: Despite being constructed from 100+ participants, the benchmark ultimately collapses to three archetypal personas. Generalization beyond these archetypes—particularly to non-professional, non-student, or non-Western users—is not validated. - LLM-as-judge reliability: Binary correctness judgments from GPT-4o serve as the primary accuracy signal; profiling tasks require assessing nuanced, open-ended personal inferences where judge calibration is uncertain, and no systematic human audit of judge accuracy is reported for profiling specifically.
