#80 — Innovative (63)

Circuit Complexity of Hierarchical Knowledge Tracing and Implications for Log-Precision Transformers

Naiming Liu, Richard Baraniuk, Shashank Sonkar · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Applying circuit complexity theory to characterize transformer limitations on hierarchical knowledge tracing is a novel and intellectually sharp framing that brings formal computational theory to bear on an empirical field. The permutation diagnostic is an actionable methodological contribution. However, the central hardness claims remain conditional on major open problems in complexity theory, and the synthetic experimental setup limits practical relevance.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Knowledge tracing (KT) systems must model how mastery of prerequisite concepts propagates through deep concept hierarchies to enable mastery of advanced concepts. While transformer-based KT models have seen extensive empirical development, their computational capabilities and limitations on hierarchical prerequisite reasoning remain poorly characterized. The paper asks: what can transformers provably compute about deep prerequisite trees, and when do training dynamics cause them to miss the hierarchical structure entirely? Methodology: The paper takes a circuit-complexity lens, exploiting the known result that log-precision transformers (activations using O(log n) bits) lie in logspace-uniform TC0. Two prerequisite aggregation rules are formalized on balanced k-ary concept trees: (1) recursive majority (KTMAJ), where each internal node is mastered iff a strict majority of its k children are mastered; and (2) alternating ALL/ANY (KT∧/∨), where nodes alternate between requiring all vs. any prerequisites. For KTMAJ, the authors prove an NC1 upper bound via O(log n)-depth bounded-fanin circuits, and show that proving KTMAJ ∉ TC0 would imply a major open lower bound separating TC0 from NC1. For the monotone setting (KT∧/∨), they invoke classical results by Håstad and Goldmann to obtain an unconditional strict depth hierarchy: every depth-(t−1) monotone threshold circuit for an alternating ∧/∨ tree requires size exp(n^Ω(1/t)). A reduction via MAJ3(a,b,0)=a∧b and MAJ3(a,b,1)=a∨b transfers these lower bounds to majority-tree variants under monotone circuits. Empirically, a 4-layer transformer encoder (794K parameters) is trained on KTMAJ trees at depths 3–6 and compared against a sum-only MLP baseline and the exact oracle. A scaffolding intervention adds level-tagged separator tokens and auxiliary supervision on intermediate subtree majority values. Key Results: Without scaffolding, the transformer matches the sum-only MLP to within noise at every depth (79.7% vs. 80.0% at depth 3; 68.4% vs. 68.3% at depth 6), while the oracle achieves 100%. A permutation diagnostic confirms the transformer is effectively permutation-invariant: shuffling leaf positions causes ≤0.3% accuracy change for the transformer but drops the oracle from 65.8% to 56.4% at depth 4. Adding structural encoding alone (level-tagged separators) produces zero improvement over the flat baseline. Adding auxiliary supervision on intermediate subtrees (Struct+Aux) yields near-perfect accuracy at depth 3 (99.96%) and depth 4 (99.36%), with permutation drops of 27.6 and 32.7 points respectively confirming structure-dependent computation. At depth 5, root accuracy improves from 70.8% to 77.2%. At depth 6 (729 leaves, 364 separator positions), Struct+Aux fails entirely—separator accuracy falls to 53% (near chance) with no permutation sensitivity—under the fixed 4-layer, dmodel=128 architecture. Strengths: - Clean theoretical framing: the NC1 upper bound and the conditional transformer implication (Corollary 2) are tight and clearly scoped, with explicit acknowledgment of what remains open in general TC0. - The monotone barrier (Theorem 2 + Corollary 1) provides genuine unconditional evidence that layered prerequisite structure resists shallow parallelization under a principled and educationally motivated restriction. - The permutation diagnostic is a sharp empirical probe that cleanly separates permutation-invariant shortcut learning from genuine hierarchical computation, providing actionable diagnostic methodology for KT evaluation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The central theoretical claim—that hierarchical KT tasks are hard for log-precision transformers—remains entirely conditional on open circuit lower bounds (TC0 vs. NC1), limiting the paper's contribution to a theoretical landscape map rather than any new impossibility result. - The empirical setup is highly controlled (synthetic, balanced ternary trees, i.i.d. Bernoulli(0.5) leaves) and does not connect to any real student interaction datasets or real concept graphs, making it unclear how these findings translate to practical KT systems. - The failure at depth 6 is attributed ambiguously to optimization difficulty, insufficient capacity, or wrong inductive bias, but no experiments distinguish among these causes (e.g., no ablation over model size, training set size, or number of epochs beyond early stopping).
Search Results 2603.23823 PDF
#81 — Innovative (63)

Clue Matters: Leveraging Latent Visual Clues to Empower Video Reasoning

Kaixin zhang, Xiaohe Li, Jiahao Li, Haohua Wu, Xinyu Zhao, Zide Fan, Lei Wang · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: ClueNet's two-stage decoupled supervision approach for bridging the training-inference gap in video QA is a meaningful methodological contribution, and the ACF's dual-criterion gating on semantic relevance and visual faithfulness is principled. However, the overall paradigm of extracting intermediate reasoning steps for VideoQA is well-explored, and the dependence on scene-graph annotations limits scalability beyond annotated benchmarks.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Video Question Answering (VideoQA) remains difficult for multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) because prevailing end-to-end architectures generate answers in a one-shot, black-box manner without explicit intermediate reasoning grounded in visual evidence. This causes three failure modes the authors diagnose empirically: (1) visual perception bias — models misallocate attention to query-irrelevant regions; (2) clue cognition bias — models misinterpret the semantic meaning of perceived objects/events; (3) inductive reasoning bias — answers are decoupled from verifiable visual evidence, leading to hallucinations. Existing Chain-of-Thought and scene-graph approaches partially address these but lack faithful clue extraction, utility-aware filtering, and end-to-end clue-answer alignment simultaneously. Methodology: ClueNet introduces a Clue Cognizer that represents each extracted visual event as a temporal quintuplet ⟨t_start, t_end, subject, predicate, object⟩ (e.g., "<2-4> person take bottle"), tracked incrementally across video segments. Training proceeds in two stages. Stage 1 (Decoupled Supervision) uses alternating-batch optimization of two losses: an autoregressive clue generation loss supervised against scene-graph annotations, and an answer generation loss conditioned on ground-truth clues — enforcing clue-answer alignment without gradient conflict. Stage 2 (Inference Supervision) introduces an Adaptive Clue Filter (ACF): a lightweight two-branch gating network that scores each candidate clue on Semantic Relevance (cosine similarity between clue and question embeddings) and Visual Faithfulness (cosine similarity between clue text and the corresponding frame visual tokens), combining them via a sigmoid gate with L1 sparsity regularization. The model is then fine-tuned to answer from ACF-refined self-generated clues, closing the training-inference gap. At inference time, a training-free Keyframe Selection module first clusters frames via Temporal Scene Clustering then re-ranks by patch-level cosine similarity to question keywords, selecting 16 keyframes. A Visual Compression step subsequently drops frames whose clue-weighted faithfulness score falls below threshold τ, yielding ~21% FLOP reduction. The base model is VideoLLaMA3 (SigLIP-SO400M visual encoder + Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct LLM); the ACF and keyframe modules are the only architectural additions. The framework is theoretically grounded via the Information Bottleneck principle, where Stage 1 maximizes I(C;A) and ACF+VC minimize I(C;V,Q). Key Results: On STAR (situated reasoning), ClueNet achieves 77.6% overall accuracy, beating VideoLLaMA3 by +5.5% and ViLA by +8.4%; gains are largest on Sequence (+5.1% over VideoLLaMA3) and Interaction (+5.8% over ViLA) splits requiring temporal and causal reasoning. The sole underperformance is the Feasibility split (−1.3% vs. InternVideo2.5), attributed to commonsense knowledge not recoverable from visual clues. On NExT-QA (multiple-choice), ClueNet reaches 84.3%, outperforming Qwen2.5-VL by +2.8% and VideoLLaMA3 by +3.0%, with the Temporal sub-task showing +4.7% over Qwen2.5-VL. On the open-ended NExT-OE task, ClueNet achieves WUPS@0/0.9 of 35.5/29.2 overall, with a striking +10.3/+10.9 margin over Qwen2.5-VL on Descriptive questions. On MVBench (out-of-distribution generalization across 20 tasks), ClueNet scores 69.8%, edging InternVideo2.5 (68.7%) and VideoLLaMA3 (68.3%), and exceeding GPT-4o (60.8%). Ablations confirm that Stage 2 recovers −1.3% lost by Stage 1 alone and adds +3.0% total; ACF contributes +2.5% on STAR; Visual Compression saves ~21% GFLOPs (54K→40.6K) and cuts per-sample inference time from 6.5s to 3.9s at a cost of only −0.3% accuracy. Oracle clue experiments show all models gain ~20% accuracy with ground-truth clues, and ClueNet achieves 94.8% oracle accuracy — +4% above all baselines — confirming its superior clue exploitation capability beyond clue quality alone. Strengths: - The two-stage training design cleanly separates the training-inference gap problem: Stage 1 learns clue-conditioned reasoning with perfect clues; Stage 2 bridges to noisy self-generated clues via the differentiable ACF, and ablations quantitatively validate each component's contribution. - The ACF's dual-criterion gating (Semantic Relevance + Visual Faithfulness) is principled and lightweight — only linear layers added — yet delivers the single largest accuracy jump (+2.5% on STAR) of any component, directly addressing the core hallucination problem. - The oracle clue experiment is a particularly compelling diagnostic: it isolates the perception bottleneck from the reasoning bottleneck, shows the failure is systemic across architectures (~20% uniform gap), and cleanly motivates the approach without cherry-picking. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework depends on scene-graph clue annotations (from Action Genome / NExT-QA / STAR) for Stage 1 supervision; this annotation requirement limits scalability to benchmarks without such structured labels, and the paper does not address how to bootstrap clue supervision in their absence. - Performance on the Feasibility split and Static attribute tasks (e.g., Object Existence on MVBench) is at or below par, revealing that the clue-grounding paradigm provides little benefit — and may slightly hurt — when questions require unobservable commonsense or static perceptual judgments rather than temporal event reasoning. - The Keyframe Selection module (ResNet-18 TSC + spaCy keyword matching) is a heterogeneous pipeline component that adds engineering complexity and contributes only +0.3% standalone accuracy; its benefit appears almost entirely mediated by downstream ACF filtering rather than being an independently valuable contribution.
Search Results 2603.15008 PDF
#82 — Innovative (63)

Trace2Skill: Distill Trajectory-Local Lessons into Transferable Agent Skills (31 ▲ )

Jingwei Ni, Yihao Liu, Xinpeng Liu, Yutao Sun, Mengyu Zhou, Pengyu Cheng, Dexin Wang, Erchao Zhao, Xiaoxi Jiang, Guanjun Jiang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 30 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Scores 63 for the well-motivated parallel-then-consolidate design that principally addresses sequential overfitting in skill evolution, and for the surprising cross-model transfer result where 35B-authored skills improve a 122B agent. The asymmetric analyst design with verified causal explanations is clever engineering. However, the contribution is primarily a pipeline architecture rather than a conceptual advance, and domain transfer claims rest on structurally similar xlsx-based tasks.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-based agents increasingly rely on structured skill documents (SOPs encoded as markdown directories) to navigate complex tasks. Manual skill authoring is a severe scalability bottleneck, and automated skill generation via parametric LLM knowledge produces negligible gains — a baseline skill generated purely from parametric knowledge performs nearly identically to no skill at all. Concurrent work addresses this with online sequential skill evolution, where each incoming trajectory edits the skill one-at-a-time, but this leads to two pathologies: (1) skill fragmentation, where narrowly-tailored skills proliferate rather than consolidating into one comprehensive guide, and (2) sequential overfitting, where early trajectories disproportionately shape the skill before adequate domain coverage is achieved. The gap is important because even a high-quality human-written skill can actively harm a smaller model while helping a larger one, revealing that manually authored skills are strong priors but not portable ones. Methodology: Trace2Skill is a three-stage offline distillation framework that mirrors how human experts write documentation: accumulating broad domain experience first, then writing. Stage 1 (Trajectory Generation): a frozen agent runs in parallel on an evolving task set using an initial skill (either human-authored or an LLM draft), producing a labeled pool of success (T+) and failure (T−) trajectories via a ReAct harness. Stage 2 (Parallel Multi-Agent Patch Proposal): a fleet of specialized sub-agents processes each trajectory independently and simultaneously — Success Analysts (A+) use a single-pass workflow to identify generalizable behavior patterns; Error Analysts (A−) implement a full ReAct agentic loop that can inspect input/output files and iteratively validate a root-cause hypothesis before proposing a patch, with a quality gate that discards patches lacking verified causal explanations. Crucially, all analysts operate on a frozen copy of the initial skill with no visibility into other agents' patches, preserving full diversity. Stage 3 (Conflict-Free Patch Consolidation): all patches are merged via a hierarchical log-depth merge tree (batch size B=32), where each merge step deduplicates, resolves conflicts, and — critically — performs inductive reasoning by upweighting patterns that appear prevalently across many independent patches and discarding idiosyncratic one-off observations. Three programmatic guardrails enforce correctness: rejection of patches referencing non-existent files, conflict detection for overlapping line ranges, and a skill format validator. The evolved skill is consumed directly at inference with no retrieval index. The full pipeline is self-contained: the same LLM generates trajectories, proposes patches, and performs consolidation, enabling evolution with open-source models as small as 35B parameters and no proprietary API dependency. Key Results: Evaluated primarily on SpreadsheetBench-Verified (400 samples, split 200/200 for evolving/test) with OOD transfer to WikiTableQuestions. Skill Deepening from a strong human-written baseline: 122B-authored +Combined gains +21.5 pp on SpreadsheetBench-Vrf over the human-written baseline for the 122B agent and +21.2 pp for the 35B agent, with the 35B-authored +Combined reaching +20.0 pp for the 35B agent and +42.2 pp on WikiTQ. Skill Creation from a weak parametric draft: 35B-authored Creation +Error gains +57.65 pp on WikiTQ for the 122B agent (reaching 81.38%, surpassing the human-written skill by +6.7 pp), the best single result in the table. Across all conditions, the +Error analyst is the most reliable signal (always positive), +Combined the most consistently strong, and +Success the most volatile. For math reasoning (DAPO-Math/AIME 2026), Trace2Skill yields +3.0/+2.9 pp same-model and +5.0/+5.0 pp cross-model improvements. For DocVQA, the 122B-authored skill gains +0.1639 ANLS (+15.3 pp accuracy) same-model and transfers equally to the 35B agent, while the 35B-authored skill degrades same-model performance, suggesting skill authoring requires stronger inductive reasoning capacity than task execution. Parallel consolidation outperforms sequential online editing by +4.0 pp Vrf at 20× lower wall-clock time (3 min vs. 60 min). Trace2Skill +Combined outperforms a ReasoningBank retrieval baseline by +13.8 pp Vrf for the 122B agent. Agentic error analysis outperforms single-LLM-call error analysis in Avg across all four Author-Mode configurations, with gaps up to +13.3 pp, attributable to the LLM-only analyzer hallucinating failure causes and over-attributing parse errors (57% vs. 14% false attribution rate). Strengths: - The parallel-then-consolidate design is a principled solution to sequential overfitting: all patches are derived from the same frozen skill, preventing cascading drift, and the inductive merge correctly implements prevalence-weighted generalization rather than recency bias. - The asymmetric analyst design (single-pass success vs. agentic error loop with artifact access and fix validation) is well-motivated and empirically confirmed — the quality gate that requires a verified causal explanation substantially reduces false-positive patches and is the primary driver of cross-model transferability. - Strong evidence of genuine generalization: skills evolved by a 35B model improve a 122B model, and spreadsheet skills transfer to WikiTableQuestions without modification, directly challenging the assumption that trajectory experience is inherently model- and task-specific. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper's claim that skills are "domain-agnostic" rests on transfer from spreadsheet editing to Wikipedia table QA, which share the same xlsx toolchain — these are structurally similar enough that the SoPs (recalc.py, openpyxl vs. pandas) transfer verbatim. Transfer to genuinely dissimilar domains (e.g., code generation, agent web navigation) is only partially validated via the math and DocVQA experiments, which are skill creation from scratch and cannot probe whether the core consolidation mechanism generalizes. - There is no mechanism to attribute performance impact to individual sections of the evolved skill, nor to prune sections that are neutral or harmful. This is acknowledged as a limitation, but it means the evolved skill may carry dead weight or subtly conflicting guidance that the experiments cannot detect, particularly in the volatile +Success conditions. - The DocVQA results reveal a model-capability threshold for skill authoring: the 35B model actively degrades same-model performance on DocVQA despite outperforming the 122B model on the task itself, yet the paper's prescribed remedy ("use a stronger skill author") is not operationalized as a principled criterion for when self-evolution is safe versus harmful.
Recommended 2603.25158 PDF
#83 — Innovative (62)

MCW-KD: Multi-Cost Wasserstein Knowledge Distillation for Large Language Models

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: MCW-KD scores 62 for its principled unification of multiple cost perspectives within a single OT formulation, avoiding the pitfalls of manual weight tuning across cost matrices. The dual-form derivation is rigorous and the modular design improves multiple existing KD methods when plugged in. However, evaluation is limited to ROUGE-L on instruction-following tasks, and the computational overhead of six cost matrices per step is unquantified.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Knowledge distillation (KD) is a standard technique for compressing large language models into smaller student models, but conventional divergence-based methods (KL, JS, reverse KL) require point-wise correspondence between teacher and student outputs—i.e., shared tokenizers and vocabularies. This breaks down when teacher and student have heterogeneous architectures or tokenization schemes, producing misaligned representational spaces and suboptimal knowledge transfer. While Optimal Transport (OT)-based methods such as ULD and MultiLevelOT partially address this by working across mismatched spaces, they rely on a single cost function that captures only one facet of representational discrepancy (e.g., token-level alignment without sequence-level semantics), leaving a gap in comprehensive multi-perspective alignment. Methodology: MCW-KD introduces a Multi-Cost Wasserstein Distance framework that simultaneously optimizes multiple cost matrices within a single unified OT formulation. Rather than taking a weighted sum of individual Wasserstein distances (which requires manual weight tuning) or multi-objective optimization (which requires multiple backward passes), the method jointly optimizes over both the transport plan π and a weight vector α on the simplex, with a regularizer Ω(α) that prevents any single cost from dominating. Entropic regularization (Sinkhorn) is added for computational tractability, and a dual form is derived theoretically. The framework is applied to two representational spaces: (1) Last hidden states, using three cost matrices—Edit Distance (C_MED, Levenshtein-based token-level syntactic alignment), Contextual Magnitude (C_MC, window-averaged L2 distance in projected hidden space), and Cosine Direction (C_SC, 1 minus cosine similarity of normalized embeddings); (2) Output distributions, using Euclidean distance (C_ED), KL divergence (C_KL, emphasizing high-confidence tokens), and Salience Score difference (C_SS, token importance via learned projections). A time-dependent interpolated teacher distribution is introduced during output alignment to provide a smoother curriculum target. The final loss combines cross-entropy, the two MCW losses, and DSKD's dual-space distillation loss as a supplementary term. Key Results: Evaluated on five instruction-following benchmarks (Dolly, Super-Natural Instructions, SelfInstruct, DialogSum, VicunaEval) across five teacher–student pairs with mismatched tokenizers (e.g., Qwen1.5-1.8B→GPT2-120M, Qwen2.5-7B→GPT2-1.5B, Mistral-7B→TinyLLaMA-1.1B, Qwen2.5-7B→OPT-2.7B), MCW-KD consistently achieves the highest average ROUGE-L across all configurations. Notable margins over the best baseline (DSKD): +9.33% on S-NI with Qwen2.5→GPT2-1.5B, +13.98% on SelfInstruct with the same pair, +6.30% on Dialog with Mistral→TinyLLaMA. Average ROUGE-L improvements over DSKD range from ~1–2.4 points across pairings. Under shared vocabulary settings, MCW-KD outperforms all tokenizer-agnostic baselines and is competitive with divergence-based methods optimized for same-tokenizer scenarios (best overall average of 17.31 vs. AKL at 17.02). Ablation confirms that both alignment spaces are necessary, that dynamic weight learning is essential (fixed equal weights underperform), and that the method is robust across a wide range of γ and λ hyperparameter values. GPT-4o-mini pairwise evaluations corroborate ROUGE-L rankings. Strengths: - Principled unification: the multi-cost OT formulation avoids the pitfalls of both fixed-weight aggregation and multi-objective gradient methods, with a rigorous dual-form derivation that makes it trainable. - Comprehensive cost design: the six cost matrices span syntactic, semantic magnitude, semantic direction, distributional, confidence-weighted, and salience-based perspectives—covering complementary aspects that no prior single-cost method captures jointly. - Versatility demonstrated: MCW-KD is framed as a modular loss term and shown to improve ULD, MinED, and MultiLevelOT when plugged in, not just DSKD, supporting broad applicability. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Computational cost: computing six cost matrices and solving an OT problem per training step is substantially more expensive than simple divergence-based losses; the paper acknowledges OT plan computation can be demanding at scale but provides no wall-clock comparison. - Scope of evaluation: experiments use ROUGE-L as the primary metric on instruction-following datasets; there are no evaluations on reasoning benchmarks (e.g., MMLU, GSM8K) or tasks where ROUGE-L is a poor proxy for quality, limiting conclusions about generality. - Cost matrix prespecification: the authors acknowledge that hand-designed cost matrices may not capture all representational aspects, and there is no learned or adaptive mechanism for discovering better cost structures beyond the six proposed ones.
Recommended
#84 — Innovative (62)

Rocks, Pebbles and Sand: Modality-aware Scheduling for Multimodal Large Language Model Inference

Konstantinos Papaioannou, Thaleia Dimitra Doudali · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: RPS-Serve's rocks-pebbles-sand abstraction is clean and practically well-motivated, and the resource-aware classifier over naive modality-label routing is a meaningful contribution that correctly handles boundary cases. However, the underlying techniques — priority queues with aging, resource profiling, and tiered scheduling — are standard systems building blocks applied to a new but natural domain, limiting the conceptual novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) serving text, images, and video introduce additional inference stages (vision preprocessing and encoding) that existing LLM serving systems — designed for homogeneous text workloads — cannot handle efficiently. The core failure mode is head-of-line blocking: video and image requests occupy 1–3 orders of magnitude more KV-cache memory and prefill time than text requests, and under FCFS scheduling they monopolize GPU resources. Text requests, which are latency-critical for interactive applications, suffer TTFT delays exceeding 15 seconds and SLO violation rates above 60% under heavy multimodal mix. Chunked prefill, the standard mitigation for long text prompts, is insufficient because image/video prefills are orders of magnitude larger. Methodology: The paper presents RPS-Serve, a modality-aware scheduler built on top of vLLM v0.8.4. The key abstraction maps requests to three resource tiers: videos are "rocks" (dominant GPU footprint, 10^4–10^5 tokens), images are "pebbles" (moderate, ~10^2–10^3 tokens), and text is "sand" (lightweight, 10–10^4 tokens). The system has five components: (1) a Workload Profiler that runs offline, one-time per model-modality pair (~20 min/pair) to characterize prefill latency and memory; (2) an Impact Estimator that uses linear regression for text prefill and 90th-percentile quantile regression for image/video to predict per-request prefill latency and KV-cache token count; (3) a Request Classifier trained via lightweight clustering on these two features — critically, it is resource-aware rather than modality-label-based, correctly handling long text that overlaps image resource demands; (4) a Queue Manager with three independent FCFS queues; and (5) a Priority Regulator that assigns each request a dynamic priority as StaticPriority_c + (1 − e^(−k_c · waiting_time^p_c)), where the exponential growth rates differ by tier so sand escalates fastest, rocks slowest. This prevents starvation while maintaining sand-first responsiveness. The scheduler also eliminates preemptions entirely for sand requests. Key Results: Evaluated on 7 MLLMs (LLaVA, Gemma, Qwen, Pixtral, 500M–12B) on a single NVIDIA A100 40GB, against vLLM+chunked-prefill and Earliest Deadline First (EDF) baselines under heavy multimodal mix (MH workload, 2 req/s). RPS-Serve reduces average TTFT by 54% overall and 78.5% for latency-critical (sand) requests vs. vLLM. RPS-Serve delivers TTFT below 1 second for sand across all tested models; vLLM fails this target for all but Gemma-4B. SLO violations for sand drop below 15% under MH vs. >60% for vLLM. Under text-only workloads, RPS-Serve achieves 0.05s average TTFT and <0.5% SLO violations, confirming no regression on the original use case. Under reduced KV-cache (25% of original), sand TTFT stays below 1 second while vLLM sees SLO violations exceeding 90%. Under increasing request rates, RPS-Serve sustains low tail latency (P90 TTFT) where EDF degrades toward vLLM levels. The ablation confirms that both the smart (resource-aware) classifier and the aging priority regulator are necessary: naive modality-label classification harms rock latency by 50%+ and static priorities leave sand with significant SLO violations. Strengths: - The rocks-pebbles-sand abstraction is empirically grounded, clean, and practically motivated; the characterization (orders-of-magnitude differences in memory and latency across modalities) is well-documented across four model families. - The resource-aware classifier is a meaningful contribution over naive modality-label routing — it correctly handles boundary cases (long text, short video) and is extensible to new modalities without architectural changes. - The system is model-agnostic (no attention mechanism modifications), single-node efficient, and built as a plug-in to vLLM, making deployment realistic and the approach broadly applicable. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Single-node evaluation only (one A100 40GB); the paper acknowledges that multi-GPU or multi-node deployment introduces unaddressed challenges around model partitioning and inter-node networking. - The Priority Regulator has six hand-tuned hyperparameters (StaticPriority, p_c, k_c per tier); the paper does not provide guidance on sensitivity to these values or how to set them for a new deployment without empirical tuning. - Rocks (video requests) are explicitly penalized and can still experience high SLO violation rates under memory pressure — the paper frames this as an intentional trade-off but does not quantify the practical cost for video-heavy workloads or provide mechanisms to adapt when video SLOs are also business-critical.
Search Results 2603.26498 PDF
#85 — Innovative (62)

Gen-Searcher: Reinforcing Agentic Search for Image Generation (43 ▲ )

Kaituo Feng, Manyuan Zhang, Shuang Chen, Yunlong Lin, Kaixuan Fan, Yilei Jiang, Hongyu Li, Dian Zheng, Chenyang Wang, Xiangyu Yue · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 31 Mar 2026
Why this rank: Gen-Searcher introduces a well-framed approach to knowledge-grounded image generation through trained agentic search, with the dual-reward design addressing a real noise problem in RL training with stochastic generators. The zero-shot transferability across generators of different capabilities is a meaningful result. However, heavy reliance on proprietary models throughout the pipeline and the hard bottleneck of downstream generator quality constrain the practical contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern text-to-image generation models are fundamentally bottlenecked by frozen parametric knowledge acquired during pretraining. They fail on knowledge-intensive prompts requiring specific real-world facts (exact appearance of a historical artifact, stats from a specific game character, current events) or up-to-date information. Existing mitigations are inadequate: RAG-based retrieval is limited by static database coverage and single-round shallow retrieval; prompt-based search workflows lack trained search behavior and produce brittle, suboptimal results; even advanced proprietary models that do search (e.g., Nano Banana Pro) restrict themselves to text search without retrieving visual references, leaving fine-grained visual grounding unsolved. Methodology: Gen-Searcher frames knowledge-grounded image generation as a trained agentic search problem. The system is initialized from Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct and equipped with three tools: `search` (web text retrieval), `image_search` (visual reference retrieval via text query), and `browse` (deep webpage reading via a Qwen3-VL-30B summarizer). At inference, the agent iteratively reasons, issues tool calls, and aggregates textual and visual evidence before producing a final grounded prompt plus reference images, which are then fed into a downstream image generator. Training proceeds in two stages. First, supervised fine-tuning on Gen-Searcher-SFT-10k teaches basic multi-turn tool use from curated agentic trajectories generated by Gemini 3 Pro. Second, agentic RL via GRPO on Gen-Searcher-RL-6k optimizes long-horizon search strategy. The key training contribution is a dual reward: a text-based reward (R_text, scored by GPT-4.1 on a 5-level scale) evaluates whether the gathered grounded prompt contains sufficient information for synthesis; an image-based reward (R_image = K-Score) evaluates the actual generated image. The final reward is R = (1-α)R_image + αR_text with α=0.5. Text reward is needed because open-source generators (Qwen-Image) introduce high stochasticity—correct evidence does not reliably produce correct images—making pure image reward too noisy for stable GRPO training. The training data pipeline is entirely synthetic: Gemini 3 Pro generates ~20-category multi-hop search prompts, produces agentic search trajectories, and Nano Banana Pro synthesizes ground-truth images. Seed1.8 scores and filters ~30K raw samples down to ~17K; 630 human-verified samples become the held-out KnowGen benchmark, and the rest is split into SFT-10k and RL-6k. The evaluation metric K-Score weights visual correctness (0.4) and text accuracy (0.4) most heavily, with faithfulness (0.1) and aesthetics (0.1) as secondary components, scored on a 3-level discrete scale by GPT-4.1. Key Results: On KnowGen, open-source image generators without search score only 9–15 K-Score, while Gen-Searcher-8B + Qwen-Image reaches 31.52 (+16.54 points over Qwen-Image's 14.98). The gains are primarily in visual correctness and text accuracy—the two highest-weight dimensions—confirming the method addresses the right failure modes. Transferability is strong: trained only with Qwen-Image as rollout generator, Gen-Searcher transfers zero-shot to Seedream 4.5 (+16.28 points, 31.01→47.29) and to Nano Banana Pro (+2.92 points, 50.38→53.30, achieving best overall). On WISE, Gen-Searcher + Qwen-Image improves from 0.62 to 0.77, outperforming all open-source baselines including LongCat-Image (0.65), with particularly large gains in Chemistry (0.40→0.75). Ablation confirms each component contributes: prompt workflow alone gives 22.91, +SFT gives 28.15, +RL gives 31.52; removing either reward signal drops performance to ~29.4–29.6. The α hyperparameter is robust across 0.3–0.6. Strengths: - The dual-reward design is technically well-motivated and empirically validated: the noise analysis of pure image reward for open-source generators is a genuine insight, not post-hoc rationalization. - Strong zero-shot transferability across generators with very different capabilities (8B open-source to proprietary) suggests the agent learns a generator-agnostic grounding policy, not generator-specific prompt hacking. - The fully synthetic data pipeline is practically important—it demonstrates that no human-annotated search-generation trajectories are needed, making the approach scalable. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark and training data both use proprietary models (Gemini 3 Pro, Nano Banana Pro, Seed1.8, GPT-4.1) as oracles throughout the pipeline, creating a tight coupling to closed systems that undermines reproducibility and may inflate reported gains relative to a fully open baseline. - The image generator (Qwen-Image) remains a hard bottleneck: qualitative examples show that even with correct retrieved evidence, the generator fails on multi-subject consistency and text rendering—Gen-Searcher cannot compensate for fundamental generator limitations, limiting the ceiling of the approach on current open-source generators. - K-Score evaluation relies on GPT-4.1 as judge, whose own visual grounding capabilities and biases become confounded with the quality measurement, and the 3-level discrete scoring (0/0.5/1) compresses fine-grained performance differences that matter for comparing closely competing methods.
Recommended 2603.28767 PDF
#86 — Innovative (61)

TARo: Token-level Adaptive Routing for LLM Test-time Alignment

Arushi Rai, Qiang Zhang, Hanqing Zeng, Yunkai Zhang, Dipesh Tamboli, Xiangjun Fan, Zhuokai Zhao, Lizhu Zhang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: TARo's key insight — that optimal base/reward model interpolation varies dramatically per token — is well-supported empirically, and the weak-to-strong generalization property (training a router on small models that transfers to 70B) is practically valuable. The logit-space routing design enabling scale-agnostic transfer is a sound architectural choice. However, the approach is more of an engineering refinement of existing test-time alignment than a conceptual breakthrough.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Post-training methods like RLVR (GRPO, DAPO) have substantially improved LLM reasoning but are costly, domain-specific, and can degrade non-reasoning capabilities. Test-time alignment (TTA) offers a lighter alternative by steering a frozen base model with a reward model during decoding, but existing approaches (e.g., GenARM) rely on a fixed scalar interpolation weight α between base and reward model logits. This fixed α must be manually tuned per domain and model scale, and as shown empirically, the optimal α varies dramatically across tasks—a single fixed value can even cause the guided model to underperform the base model. The paper addresses the gap that TTA methods have been explored primarily for preference alignment, not reasoning. Methodology: TARO (Token-level Adaptive Routing) operates in two stages. First, a reasoning reward model is fine-tuned on Math-StepDPO-10K, a dataset of step-wise preference pairs where two continuations share a correct reasoning prefix but diverge at the next step. The reward model is trained with a standard DPO preference loss to assign higher token-level log-likelihoods to logically valid continuations. Second, a lightweight MLP router gθ is trained (on 1000 samples each from Math-StepDPO-10K and UltraFeedback) to predict a token-specific routing coefficient αt ∈ (0,1) at each decoding step. The router takes as input either (i) the full concatenated logits from base and reward models (R^{2V}) or (ii) top-k logits paired with learnable token-index embeddings (more compact). An optional Bernoulli entropy regularizer encourages decisive routing. The final guided distribution is (1−αt)·π_base + αt·π_reward, updated per token. Crucially, the router operates on logits rather than hidden states, making it scale-agnostic: the same router trained on a small backbone can steer much larger models without retraining. Key Results: On MATH500, TARO with Llama-3.1-8B+DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B reward achieves 54.4% vs. 32.0% base and 49.2% GenARM (+22.4% over base, +5.2% over GenARM). With Qwen-2.5-3B+Qwen-2.5-0.5B reward, TARO reaches 64.8% vs. 60.8% base and 56.4% GenARM. On out-of-distribution MedXpertQA (clinical reasoning), TARO improves over GenARM (13.2% vs. 11.2% for Llama; 9.8% tie for Qwen). On AlpacaEval instruction following, TARO achieves 20.8% LC win rate vs. 10.8% GenARM for Llama. Weak-to-strong generalization: the router trained on 8B/3B backbones transfers to Llama-3.1-70B and Qwen-2.5-14B without retraining, improving over both base and GenARM. TARO outperforms majority voting (N=8) on MATH500 (64.8% vs. 56.4%) at ~4× less compute. Ablations confirm token-level routing is essential: prompt-level routing collapses to 33.2% vs. 49.6% on MATH500, and using base+reward logits outperforms hidden-state-based routers while remaining scalable. Strengths: - Eliminates the fragile fixed-α hyperparameter by learning token-specific routing, which the token analysis (Table 2) shows correctly assigns high reward influence to reasoning scaffolds ("Step", "cases", operators) and low influence to fluency tokens. - Weak-to-strong generalization is a practically important property: one trained router works across model families and scales without retraining, enabling cheap deployment. - Logit-based routing is a principled design choice—logits span a shared vocabulary space regardless of model size, unlike hidden states which shift dimensionality and distribution across scales. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The full-logit router variant (Design i) for Llama reduces throughput significantly (80.7→32.5 TPS) due to large vocabulary concatenation (128K tokens), making it potentially impractical in latency-sensitive settings despite comparable QPS. - TARO fails on some simple problems where the base model succeeds via enumeration (Table 11), suggesting the reward model's systematic step-by-step bias can introduce errors when the optimal strategy is heuristic search—and the system lacks backtracking or self-correction mechanisms. - Evaluation is limited to two model families (Llama-3.1, Qwen-2.5) on three benchmarks; it is unclear how sensitive the results are to the choice of reward model architecture or training data, and whether the approach scales to frontier-scale models (>70B) where running two models simultaneously may be prohibitive.
Recommended 2603.18411 PDF
#87 — Innovative (61)

Why Do LLM-based Web Agents Fail? A Hierarchical Planning Perspective

Mohamed Aghzal, Gregory J. Stein, Ziyu Yao · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The hierarchical decomposition into planning, execution, and replanning layers provides a principled diagnostic lens that is more actionable than end-to-end success metrics. The concrete finding that low-level perceptual grounding — not high-level reasoning — is the dominant bottleneck is empirically well-supported across three frontier models and redirects research attention appropriately. The PDDL vs. NL comparison yielding measurably more compact and aligned plans adds practical value. Limited by small benchmark size (104 tasks), text-only evaluation, and the scaling challenge of requiring human-annotated reference plans.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-based web agents remain far below human reliability on realistic, long-horizon web navigation tasks, yet existing evaluations rely almost exclusively on end-to-end success metrics. These coarse metrics conflate fundamentally different failure modes — flawed high-level strategy, poor grounding of plans into UI actions, and inability to recover when the environment diverges from expectations — making it impossible to diagnose where agents break down or which capabilities to improve. The paper addresses this by proposing a structured, process-based evaluation framework grounded in hierarchical planning theory. Methodology: The authors decompose LLM web agent behavior into three layers: (1) high-level planning — decomposing a task instruction into abstract subgoals; (2) low-level execution — translating each subgoal into a sequence of concrete DOM interactions; and (3) replanning — detecting failures and revising the plan. They evaluate two high-level plan representations: natural language (NL) and PDDL (Planning Domain Definition Language), which enforces explicit preconditions and effects per action. The framework is applied to the Mind2Web-Live benchmark (104 live-website tasks), extended with human-aligned high-level plans derived from expert key-node annotations. Three frontier models are compared: gpt-5-nano, claude-haiku-4.5, and gemini-flash-2.5, across three action-space configurations (full action-object prediction, action-ID selection, and an expanded abstract action set). High-level plan quality is measured via six alignment metrics against human-authored plans (perfect match, partial, missing, decomposed, unmatched, matched). Low-level execution is measured via subgoal completion rate, plan completion rate, plan efficiency, and task success rate. An LLM-as-judge evaluates both postcondition satisfaction and overall task success, validated against manual annotation on 50 sampled instances (82–86% human agreement). Key Results: PDDL-based high-level plans consistently outperform NL plans across alignment metrics: perfect match improves from 60.6% to 67.7%, unmatched steps drop from 29.4% to 15.4%, and overall matched rate rises from 70.6% to 84.6%. PDDL plans are also more compact (4.39 vs. 5.51 high-level steps; 8.89 vs. 11.94 low-level actions on average). Despite this, low-level execution remains the dominant bottleneck: even when given human-authored gold plans, the best model (gpt-5-nano) achieves only a 38.5% plan completion rate and 36.4% task success rate, compared to 69.7% subgoal completion when grounding human plans. Failure mode analysis reveals severe perceptual grounding issues: 34% of actions are redundant (no DOM change), 32% of goto actions produce hallucinated/invalid links, and 16.7% of actions navigate outside the target domain. A single round of replanning substantially improves PDDL-based agents: plan completion jumps from 37.5% to 81.4% and task success from 36.4% to 45.1% for gpt-5-nano. gemini-flash-2.5 struggles most with execution (17.3% success with human plans) and gains minimally from replanning, while claude-haiku-4.5 avoids hallucinated links but gets stuck in repetitive loops (16.7% of failures). Strengths: - The hierarchical decomposition framework cleanly isolates three distinct failure modes and provides a principled basis for per-capability attribution that coarse end-to-end metrics cannot offer. - Empirical findings are consistent across three diverse frontier models and three action space configurations, lending robustness to the conclusions. - The paper's diagnosis that low-level perceptual grounding — not high-level reasoning — is the dominant bottleneck is a concrete, actionable finding that redirects research attention appropriately. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is limited to 104 tasks and text-only DOM observations; no visual/multimodal settings are considered, which is increasingly relevant as modern web agents use screenshots. - Replanning is capped at a single round and only two local/global strategies, leaving open whether iterated or more sophisticated replanning changes the landscape significantly. - The high-level alignment evaluation requires a pre-annotated set of reference human plans for every task, making the framework difficult to scale or apply to new benchmarks without substantial human annotation effort.
Search Results 2603.14248 PDF
#88 — Innovative (61)

From Data to Laws: Neural Discovery of Conservation Laws Without False Positives

Rahul D Ray · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: Scores 61 for the diversity filter (ρ > 10) that elegantly eliminates the false-positive pathology plaguing existing conservation law discovery methods, achieving perfect F1 across all tested systems. The decoupled neural-symbolic pipeline is well-designed. However, the benchmark is entirely synthetic with known ground truth, the symbolic extraction requires system-specific hand-crafted bases, and the method has not been validated on real experimental data where the scientific payoff would be highest.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Discovering conservation laws directly from trajectory data is a fundamental challenge in scientific machine learning. Existing methods suffer from four interconnected problems: (1) failure under parameter variation across trajectories, since most methods assume fixed system parameters; (2) inability to discover non-polynomial invariants such as the logarithmic invariant of the Lotka–Volterra predator–prey model; (3) susceptibility to local minima and false positives, where variance-minimizing networks converge to spurious near-constants on chaotic systems; and (4) evaluations limited to a handful of systems, making generalization claims weak. The false positive problem is particularly acute—methods like Hamiltonian Neural Networks (HNN) routinely claim conservation laws on systems that have none, such as the Lorenz attractor. Methodology: The paper introduces NGCG (Neural-Guided Conservation-law Generator), a four-stage decoupled neural-symbolic pipeline. Stage 1 trains a frozen MLP dynamics model (2 hidden layers, 256 units, Tanh) for trajectory prediction only—crucially, it plays no role in invariant discovery. Stage 2 trains a small scalar network ϕ (3 layers, 64 units) to minimize a normalized variance loss L = (mean intra-trajectory variance) / (inter-trajectory variance + ε), which simultaneously enforces constancy within trajectories and prevents the trivial constant solution. To escape local minima, 10 independent restarts are run and the best by validation constancy is selected. Stage 3 applies system-specific symbolic extraction in parallel: a polynomial Lasso that finds the minimum-variance eigenvector of the mean trajectory covariance matrix over degree-≤4 monomials; a log-basis Lasso for Lotka–Volterra using {x, y, log x, log y} features; explicit spatial moment candidates for PDEs; and a PySR genetic programming fallback applied to (state, ϕ-output) pairs. Stage 4 enforces a strict constancy gate (τ = 0.01) and a diversity filter (ρ > 10, the ratio of inter-trajectory variation to intra-trajectory variation) that rejects candidates which happen to be near-constant across all trajectories—the key mechanism for eliminating false positives on chaotic systems. Key Results: On a benchmark of nine dynamical systems (7 ODEs, 2 PDEs), NGCG achieves DR=1.0, FDR=0.0, F1=1.0 on all four systems with true invariants—harmonic oscillator, Lotka–Volterra, coupled springs, and Hénon–Heiles—with constancy values 2–3 orders of magnitude lower than the best baseline. On the harmonic oscillator, NGCG achieves constancy 1×10⁻⁴ vs. HNN's 2.3×10⁻³. For Hénon–Heiles, the polynomial Lasso recovers the exact quartic Hamiltonian at machine precision (constancy ≈ 0.0). NGCG is the only method succeeding on Lotka–Volterra (constancy 4×10⁻⁴); MLP+PySR, SINDy, and IRAS all fail with constancy >0.1 or outright non-discovery. On five systems without conservation laws (double pendulum, Lorenz, three-body, Burgers, KS), NGCG correctly outputs no law (FDR=0.0), while HNN produces false positives on double pendulum (F1=0.33) and SINDy/MLP produce spurious laws on the PDE systems (FDR up to 1.0). The method is robust to Gaussian noise up to σ=0.1, needs only 50–100 trajectories, is insensitive to hyperparameters for R≥5 restarts, and runs in 45–50 seconds per system on a single GPU. Strengths: - The diversity filter (ρ > 10) is a genuinely novel and effective mechanism: it catches the class of pathological false positives—expressions that pass the constancy gate but are trivially constant across all trajectories—demonstrated concretely on the Lorenz system where a candidate with constancy 4.6×10⁻⁴ is correctly rejected because ρ ≈ 0.16. - The decoupled architecture is principled: separating dynamics learning from invariant discovery avoids the catastrophic interference seen in end-to-end approaches, and the staged pipeline gracefully degrades—even a suboptimal ϕ network can guide PySR to the correct symbolic form. - System-specific symbolic extraction, particularly the log-basis Lasso, directly encodes inductive bias for non-polynomial invariants, enabling Lotka–Volterra success where all prior methods fail; the theoretical analysis in the appendix proves that this Lasso recovers the exact invariant under noiseless conditions. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark, while broader than most prior work, is entirely synthetic and small-scale (≤4 state dimensions for ODEs). All nine systems are well-understood with known governing equations; the method has not been validated on real experimental data where the ground truth invariant is unknown, which is the scientifically interesting regime. - The symbolic extraction strategy is system-specific in a way that requires prior knowledge: the log-basis Lasso is hand-crafted for Lotka–Volterra, the PDE reduction to three spatial moments is designed for integral-type invariants, and the polynomial degree cap of 4 presupposes bounded complexity. This limits generality—the pipeline would need manual extension for new system types with unfamiliar functional forms or high-dimensional PDEs. - Baselines were evaluated with only a single random seed (seed 0) for computational reasons, while NGCG was evaluated with three seeds, introducing an asymmetry in the comparison. Although the authors argue baselines are stable across seeds, the claim remains informally supported.
Search Results 2603.20474 PDF
#89 — Innovative (61)

TDMM-LM: Bridging Facial Understanding and Animation via Language Models

Luchuan Song, Pinxin Liu, Haiyang Liu, Zhenchao Jin, Yolo Yunlong Tang, Zichong Xu, Susan Liang, Jing Bi, Jason J Corso, Chenliang Xu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The geometry tokenization achieving 300–500× compression over image tokens while preserving subtle facial dynamics is a meaningful technical contribution, and Open3DFaceVid (187 balanced emotion labels with 3DMM ground truth) fills a genuine data gap. However, the core approach of discretizing motion parameters into tokens for LLM-based generation follows established patterns from body motion work (T2M-GPT), and evaluation primarily on synthetic data sharing the training distribution weakens generalization claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Text-guided facial animation lags significantly behind full-body motion generation due to two compounding problems: (1) existing video-language models process frames as hundreds of image tokens, causing temporal sparsity that destroys subtle facial dynamics (micro-expressions, lip twitches, brow raises) when frames are downsampled; and (2) real-world training corpora are overwhelmingly neutral-expression-dominated (YouTube, TikTok, VoxCeleb), leaving models with a narrow, low-variance facial prior. Existing labeled datasets like MEAD offer only 8 coarse one-hot emotion labels and studio-controlled subjects, lacking the open-form text annotations needed for language-driven modeling. This creates a fundamental gap: no large-scale, class-balanced, richly text-annotated corpus of 3D facial motion exists. Methodology: The paper addresses the data gap by introducing Open3DFaceVid, a synthetic corpus of ~80 hours (57.2K clips, 4–6s each) generated by prompting multiple T2V models (Wan2.1/2.2, HuMo, Open-Sora, Veo2/3) with a curated lexicon of ~200 emotion and facial-action descriptors (grin, pout, smirk, squint, etc.), ensuring class balance across 187 labels. Per-frame FLAME 3DMM parameters (expression codes, head yaw/roll/pitch) are extracted via monocular regression, and ~10K wild clips annotated by Gemini are appended to narrow the synthetic-to-real gap. Building on this data, the authors train a Geometry VQ-VAE that discretizes 3DMM sequences into a compact codebook by operating in mesh space (vertex positions) rather than raw coefficient space, avoiding many-to-one mapping ambiguities where similar expressions map to different coefficient patterns. Each video frame is encoded as a single geometry token—replacing 300–500 image tokens—drastically reducing context length while preserving temporal continuity. Two complementary tasks are then trained on the same LLM: (1) Motion2Language fine-tunes a Qwen3 backbone to consume geometry-token sequences and produce free-form natural-language descriptions of emotion, intensity, and head dynamics; (2) Language2Motion uses an autoregressive transformer conditioned on word-level T5 embeddings to predict geometry token sequences from text prompts, enabling single-keyword control over emotional style and intensity. Key Results: For Motion2Language on geometry inputs, the model outperforms HumanOmni and Gemini-2.5 VLM across all GPT-4-judged and human-rated metrics: emotion correctness 4.02 vs. 2.45 (Gemini), motion correctness 3.35 vs. 2.91, intensity 3.63 vs. 3.51, human emotion rating 4.29 vs. 2.88. On natural image inputs (a fairer comparison for VLMs), Gemini-2.5 leads on emotion (4.21 vs. 4.02), but the proposed method uses only 1 token per frame versus hundreds for Gemini, a major efficiency advantage. For Language2Motion, the model outperforms T2M-GPT on all metrics: L2 0.219 vs. 0.226, Fréchet Distance 31.75 vs. 37.04, token accuracy 0.920 vs. 0.895, expression correctness 4.13 vs. 3.57, and human rating 3.95 vs. 3.91. Dataset ablations confirm that Open3DFaceVid (187 labels, well-separated t-SNE clusters) significantly outperforms MEAD (8 labels) and Gemini-annotated YouTube (37 labels, heavy neutral bias) in both tasks. Scaling experiments show Motion2Language saturates at 4B–8B parameters, while Language2Motion continues benefiting from larger backbones. Strengths: - First unified framework to cast 3D facial parameter modeling as a language problem, enabling both semantic understanding and controllable generation with a single LLM backbone. - Geometry tokenization achieves ~300–500× token compression versus image-based VLMs while preserving subtle temporal facial dynamics, making it both efficient and effective for long sequences. - Open3DFaceVid provides by far the richest facial-motion dataset available (187 emotion labels, class-balanced, with both text and 3DMM ground truth), which is likely to have standalone value for the community. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method is evaluated primarily on synthetic test clips that share the same T2V generation distribution as training data; generalization to diverse real-world video (e.g., extreme occlusions, non-frontal poses, in-the-wild lighting) is not demonstrated. - On natural image inputs—the setting most relevant to real deployment—Gemini-2.5 outperforms the model on emotion understanding (4.21 vs. 4.02 CorE), and the paper uses a circular evaluation for Language2Motion correctness (the trained Motion2Language model scores its own sibling task). - Camera constraints during dataset synthesis ("frame remains steady, head and shoulders centered") limit head-motion diversity; the ablation acknowledges this results in narrower CorM scores compared to YouTube, potentially limiting utility for dynamic talking-head applications.
Recommended 2603.16936 PDF
#90 — Innovative (61)

Persistent homology-based segmentation tool for membrane images

cell.com · karypis - new results
Why this rank: PomSeg fills a genuine methodological gap by extending persistent homology from the easier nuclei segmentation setting to the substantially harder membrane segmentation problem, with parameters that carry direct biological meaning (cell size, overlap degree). The white-box architecture enabling parameter changes without recomputing persistent homology and the modular composability with deep learning preprocessors represent meaningful design contributions. However, the lack of voxel-level quantitative benchmarks against standard DL baselines and the fundamental limitation when cells share spherical interfaces temper the impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Cell membrane segmentation in 3D biological microscopy images — particularly embryo membrane images in developmental biology — is a critical but difficult task. While deep learning tools (e.g., Cellpose, PlantSeg) have achieved strong automation, they are fundamentally data-centered: parameters lack clear biological meaning, making iterative tuning opaque and frustrating for users. Prior persistent homology-based segmentation tools addressed this interpretability gap, but were restricted to cell nuclei images that are relatively easy to binarize. No persistent homology tool existed for membrane images, which present harder challenges: varying signal intensities, overlapping cell boundaries, and anisotropic z-resolution. This paper fills that specific gap. Methodology: PomSeg (Persistent Homology-based Membrane Segmentation) is a hierarchical, two-module pipeline. The 2D module applies a ridge filter (Sato filter) to enhance membrane boundaries, then runs sublevel-set filtration 0th persistent homology on the grayscale image. Points in the resulting persistence diagram are selected via a user-set persistence parameter (minimum intensity contrast between cell interior and membrane) and size thresholds; inverse analysis maps these back to connected-component binary masks. The 3D module stacks 2D binary masks into an isotropic 3D binary volume (z-resolution is compensated by slice replication with ratio r=3), applies a signed Manhattan distance transform — assigning negative values to cell interiors and zero near surfaces — and runs 3D sublevel-set persistent homology on this distance image. Birth and persistence parameters in the 3D persistence diagram correspond directly to cell size and degree of overlap with neighbors, respectively. Birth positions serve as markers for a watershed algorithm to finalize instance segmentation, followed by Gaussian-smoothed distance-transform refinement. Cell tracking across time points uses minimum-weight maximum matching on a bipartite graph of barycenter distances. The entire pipeline is white-box: parameter changes do not require recomputing persistent homology, enabling fast iterative tuning. Code and a GUI standalone application are publicly available. Key Results: On 8-cell stage mouse pre-implantation embryo time series (34 time points, 254×254×88 voxels each), PomSeg successfully segmented all 8 cells with a single fixed parameter set across all frames. The 8th and 9th largest persistence values maintained a consistent separation gap (lower bound of P8 = 6, upper bound of P9 = 5) across time, demonstrating robustness to per-frame noise variation. Volume correlation with PlantSeg-derived ground truth (manually refined) reached CC = 0.91, with coefficient of variation 0.033 for PomSeg vs. 0.032 for ground truth — indicating comparable intra-method consistency. Under aggressive z-resolution downsampling (intervals 2–6, forcing cells to touch), PomSeg successfully separated all touching cells. For multicellular stage embryos (~25–175 cells), relative cell count error versus PlantSeg ground truth averaged 0.04 (4%). Applied to diverse 2D datasets (fluorescence microscopy, plant cells, epithelial cells), PomSeg detected membranes correctly on clean images, with minor internal noise artifacts on difficult epithelial images. Compared to watershed on the same Sato-filtered input, watershed consistently produced mixed over- and under-segmentation with no satisfactory parameter setting, while PomSeg succeeded. Replacing the Sato filter with a 3D U-Net boundary predictor as preprocessing produced accurate segmentation, demonstrating modular extensibility. Strengths: - Parameter interpretability is genuine and non-trivial: birth maps directly to cell size, persistence to overlap amount, enabling biologists to reason about parameters without black-box tuning. Parameter changes skip expensive persistent homology recomputation. - Robustness to noise and z-resolution degradation is grounded in the stability theorem for persistent homology (Cohen-Steiner et al., 2007), not just empirically observed, and validated under controlled downsampling experiments. - Modular white-box architecture enables meaningful extensibility: METIS graph partitioning was incorporated for multicellular stages, and a 3D U-Net was drop-in substituted for preprocessing, showing genuine composability with ML tools. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The 3D module relies solely on shape (distance transform from binary mask) and fails when two touching cells have a perfectly spherical interface lacking surface indentations — a common failure mode when cells are strongly flattened or when low z-resolution misses narrow contact slices. No mechanism to exploit intensity information directly in the 3D step. - Quantitative comparison against deep learning baselines (Cellpose, PlantSeg without manual correction) is absent; ground truth itself was PlantSeg output with manual refinement, and the paper demonstrates complementarity rather than superiority. CC = 0.91 on cell volumes is solid but does not constitute a voxel-level accuracy benchmark (IoU, Dice) against held-out annotated data.
Search Results
#91 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Explaining CLIP Zero-shot Predictions Through Concepts

Onat Ozdemir, Anders Christensen, Stephan Alaniz, Zeynep Akata, Emre Akbas · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Scores 58 because the linear projection approach is technically clean and the faithfulness-by-construction property is appealing, but learning a single linear mapping in CLIP's concept space is a relatively straightforward idea. The causal validation and spatial localization analyses are thorough, though the ~5% accuracy drop on ImageNet-1k and dependence on externally generated concept sets limit the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large vision-language models like CLIP achieve strong zero-shot image recognition but produce opaque predictions that users cannot interpret. Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) offer interpretable intermediate representations through human-defined concepts, but require dense concept supervision and operate under a closed-world assumption that prevents generalization to unseen classes. The gap between CLIP's generalization ability and CBMs' interpretability is the central problem this paper addresses. Methodology: EZPC (Explaining CLIP Zero-shot Predictions through Concepts) learns a single linear projection matrix A ∈ R^(d×m) that maps CLIP's d-dimensional image and text embeddings into a shared m-dimensional concept space. Each column of A corresponds to a human-interpretable concept direction (e.g., "has feathers," "made of metal"). The matrix is initialized from CLIP text embeddings of concept phrases drawn from GPT-3-generated concept sets (from LF-CBM), and trained with two objectives: (1) a matching loss (MSE) anchoring A to the original concept embeddings to preserve interpretability, and (2) a reconstruction loss (KL divergence) ensuring the concept-space similarity distribution matches CLIP's original prediction distribution. At inference, zero-shot classification is performed by dot product in concept space, and per-class concept contribution scores are computed via element-wise product of image and class concept activations—providing faithful, by-construction explanations since concept scores directly compose the classification logit. Only A is trained; all CLIP backbone parameters remain frozen. Key Results: Evaluated on CIFAR-100, CUB-200-2011, ImageNet-100, ImageNet-1k, and Places365 using CLIP RN50 with an 80/20 seen/unseen class split. On generalized zero-shot harmonic mean: EZPC achieves 0.403 vs. CLIP's 0.408 on CIFAR-100, 0.682 vs. 0.693 on ImageNet-100, 0.465 vs. 0.474 on CUB, 0.481 vs. 0.530 on ImageNet-1k (largest gap, ~5%), and 0.352 vs. 0.362 on Places365—outperforming both Z-CBM and SpLiCE by 10–15% harmonic mean across most datasets. Inference overhead is negligible: 5.90 ms/image vs. 5.77 ms for CLIP, compared to 94× and 59× slowdowns for Z-CBM and SpLiCE respectively. Spatial localization on CUB using ground-truth segmentation achieves 96.7% pointing accuracy for positive concepts vs. 1.7% for negative concepts. Causal validation shows that removing top-10 concepts causes 16.9% prediction flips vs. 1.4% for random removal. Cross-dataset transfer from ImageNet-100 to CIFAR-100 yields a 3% higher harmonic mean than CLIP in the generalized setting. The method scales consistently across ViT-B/32, ViT-L/14, and SigLIP backbones with gaps of only 0.2–0.4 harmonic mean points. Strengths: - The explanation mechanism is faithful by construction: concept scores directly and additively compose the prediction logit, with no post-hoc approximation or surrogate model. - Inference cost is essentially identical to CLIP (single matrix multiplication), making EZPC practical for large-scale deployment unlike per-image optimization methods. - Causal validation is thorough—the paper explicitly distinguishes causal contribution from correlation via top-n vs. random ablation, and quantifies spatial localization quality against ground-truth segmentation masks. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The projection is constrained to be linear, which limits the ability to capture nonlinear or compositional semantic relationships that CLIP's high-dimensional space may encode. - Concept quality is inherited from GPT-3-generated sets via LF-CBM; the paper does not evaluate sensitivity to concept set quality or test with automatically discovered concepts, leaving a dependency on an external, potentially biased vocabulary. - The ~5% harmonic mean drop on ImageNet-1k suggests meaningful accuracy loss at scale, and the paper provides limited analysis of which class types are most harmed by the concept bottleneck constraint.

#92 — Moderately Innovative (58)

OPERA: Online Data Pruning for Efficient Retrieval Model Adaptation

Haoyang Fang, Shuai Zhang, Yifei Ma, Hengyi Wang, Cuixiong Hu, Katrin Kirchhoff, Bernie Wang, George Karypis · arxiv.org · New articles in my profile
Why this rank: OPERA makes a clean conceptual contribution by identifying the quality-coverage tradeoff intrinsic to hierarchical retrieval training and proposing dynamic pruning that operates at both query and document granularity with adaptive schedules. The theoretical analysis motivating the SP+DP pipeline is a nice touch. However, the absolute gains are modest (+1.9% NDCG, +0.7% Recall), no statistical significance is reported, and the approach introduces many hyperparameters whose sensitivity remains unexplored.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Domain-specific finetuning of dense retrievers is essential for peak performance, yet existing data pruning methods were designed for classification or generation tasks with i.i.d. samples and do not account for the hierarchical two-stage sampling structure of retrieval training (query sampling followed by positive/negative document selection). No prior work had studied data pruning specifically for dense retriever finetuning. The paper addresses this gap by asking whether selective use of training data can simultaneously improve both ranking quality and retrieval coverage, while reducing the computational cost of finetuning. Methodology: OPERA introduces two complementary pruning strategies built on cosine similarity between query and document embeddings from the pretrained model. Static Pruning (SP) retains only the top-k% highest-similarity query-document pairs before training, revealing a quality-coverage tradeoff: filtering improves NDCG (ranking) but degrades Recall because queries with fewer high-quality documents are disproportionately removed. Dynamic Pruning (DP) resolves this tradeoff by replacing hard exclusions with soft sampling modulation at two hierarchical levels simultaneously. At the query level, a fraction of top-scoring queries (ranked by mean contrastive loss) are oversampled, while a random selection of lower-scoring queries maintains diversity. At the document level, positive pairs above a similarity threshold receive a weight of β while others receive weight 1, ensuring all documents remain accessible with non-zero probability. Both α (query sampling strength) and β (document sampling strength) follow cosine decay schedules that increase selectivity over training as model representations improve, driven by continuous per-iteration score updates. An update interval Iu amortizes the cost of score recomputation. The paper also provides a theoretical analysis (Theorem 1) showing that any scoring function identifying true positives at a rate exceeding the noise rate will improve learned query representations, and that SP outperforms DP under equal selection quality but DP is more robust when initial signal quality is low. This motivates a two-stage SP+DP pipeline for noisy settings. Key Results: Evaluated on 8 datasets across 6 domains using bge-large-en-v1.5 (335M parameters), DP achieves the best average rank on both NDCG@10 (1.38) and Recall@20 (1.63) across all methods, compared to SP (2.63 / 3.88), InfoBatch (2.88 / 2.38), and standard FT (3.38 / 2.75). Concretely, DP improves over FT by +1.9% NDCG@10 (0.535→0.545) and +0.7% Recall@20 (0.530→0.534) on average. SP improves NDCG@10 (+0.5%) but at the cost of Recall@20 (drops from 0.530 to 0.512). On convergence speed, DP reaches peak FT-equivalent performance in under 50% of the training iterations (e.g., fewer than 8,000 vs. 16,000 on FEVER), while adding only 1.64–4.49% per-iteration overhead depending on update interval. Results generalize to Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B (decoder-based, 0.6B parameters), with SP leading on NDCG@10 (0.507) and DP on Recall@20 (0.375) under the same quality-coverage pattern. In noisy settings (ANTIQUE with level-2 documents injected as false positives), the two-stage SP.5+DP pipeline achieves the best Recall@20 (0.433) and SP.75+DP outperforms all baselines on both metrics. Strengths: - Identifies and formalizes a retrieval-specific quality-coverage tradeoff that is intrinsic to two-stage contrastive sampling, a conceptually clean and practically important finding. - Dynamic pruning is genuinely novel in operating hierarchically at both query and document granularities with cosine-scheduled, adaptive thresholds — attributes absent from all prior adaptive training methods (curriculum learning, SPL, InfoBatch). - Broad empirical validation: 8 datasets, 6 domains, 2 architecturally distinct retrievers (encoder-only BGE and decoder-based Qwen3), seen and unseen domains, with detailed convergence curves and ablations of each hierarchical component. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The LLM-based experiments use only Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B with a heavily constrained budget (2,000 iterations, high learning rate) that the authors themselves acknowledge is unfavorable to DP; results at 4B/8B scales are absent, limiting the generalizability claim for larger LLM retrievers. - DP introduces several hyperparameters (αs, αe, βs, βe, vs, ve, Iu) that were not tuned beyond the FT baseline defaults, leaving substantial potential performance on the table and making it unclear how sensitive results are to these choices in new settings. - No variance across random seeds is reported due to computational cost, so statistical significance of the modest absolute gains (e.g., +0.7% Recall@20 on average) cannot be formally assessed.
