#93 — Moderately Innovative (57)

When Should a Robot Think? Resource-Aware Reasoning via Reinforcement Learning for Embodied Robotic Decision-Making

Jun Liu, Pu Zhao, Zhenglun Kong, Xuan Shen, Peiyan Dong, Fan Yang, Lin Cui, Hao Tang, Geng Yuan, Wei Niu, Wenbin Zhang, Xue Lin, Gaowen Liu, Yanzhi Wang, Dong Huang · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The problem formulation — decoupling orchestration (when to think) from control (how to act) — is clean and practically relevant for LLM-integrated robotics, and the joint decision over reasoning role and budget allocation is a sensible design. The 60% inference time reduction with near-parity task success is a meaningful efficiency result. However, baselines are weak (fixed interval, simple heuristic), the abstract symbolic training environments are unrealistically thin, and the lack of comparison against recent adaptive LLM reasoning methods limits confidence in the claimed gains.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Embodied robotic systems increasingly use LLM-based agents for high-level planning, but invoking LLMs incurs substantial latency and compute cost. Invoking reasoning too frequently delays action execution; invoking it too rarely causes task failures. Existing systems use static or hand-crafted heuristics to decide when to call LLMs, with no principled mechanism for adapting to task complexity, execution history, or remaining computational budget. This paper asks: can a robot learn when to think versus when to act? Methodology: The authors propose RARRL (Resource-Aware Reasoning via Reinforcement Learning), an orchestration framework formulated as an MDP. At each decision step, a lightweight PPO-trained policy observes the task state, execution history, and remaining computational budget, and outputs three joint decisions: (1) whether to ACT directly or THINK (invoke an LLM), (2) which reasoning role to use (Planner or Verifier), and (3) how much budget to allocate (c ∈ {0,1,2}, mapping to no LLM call, planner-only with 256 max tokens, or planner+verifier with 512 tokens each). The orchestration policy is a 3-layer MLP trained via PPO with GAE. Crucially, it operates entirely at the decision-making layer—low-level control is unchanged, and LLM reasoning modules are treated as frozen black boxes. Training uses abstract symbolic task environments (not physical simulators), and results are evaluated on real LLM inference (GPT-4o-mini) in the ALFRED/AI2-THOR benchmark. The reward penalizes both action failures and execution latency: r_t = task_reward − λ·δ_t − μ·failure. Key Results: On ALFRED with real LLM inference (Table I), RARRL reduces LLM inference time by >60% vs. full reasoning (12.3s vs 31.5s on navigation) while maintaining near-parity task success (82.7% vs 84.0% on navigation; 69.5% vs 71.2% on delivery). It substantially outperforms heuristic (72.4%) and constrained PPO (79.3%) baselines at 82.7% TSR on navigation with statistically significant differences (p<0.05). Token consumption drops to 980 vs 4100 per episode for full reasoning. On abstract tasks (Table II), RARRL achieves 82.3–84.6% TSR with only 7.4% reasoning frequency and 620 tokens/episode, versus full reasoning at 85.4–88.3% TSR but 50% frequency and 4200 tokens. Under budget shock (abrupt reduction of compute budget), RARRL maintains 74.9% TSR versus heuristic's 61.8% (Table III). Ablations confirm that both budget state and execution history are essential components. Strengths: - Clean problem formulation: decoupling orchestration (when/how to think) from control (how to act) is a principled and practically relevant decomposition for LLM-integrated robotics. - End-to-end evaluation on a real benchmark (ALFRED/AI2-THOR) with actual GPT-4o-mini inference latency, not just simulated costs, gives the efficiency claims credibility. - Thorough ablation study isolating contributions of budget state, execution history, reasoning role selection, and adaptive budget allocation—all show meaningful individual impact. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The abstract training environments are very thin: symbolic task generators with hand-coded stochastic failure probabilities bear little resemblance to real perceptual environments. The 3% transfer gap claim is on the ALFRED simulator, not physical robots, and the generalization argument is undervalidated. - The action space is coarse (Navigate, Inspect, Pick, Deliver) and the budget discretization (c ∈ {0,1,2}) is arbitrary; it is unclear how the approach scales to more complex tasks with richer action spaces or continuous resource constraints. - No comparison to more recent adaptive LLM reasoning baselines (e.g., chain-of-thought gating, token budgeting methods for LLMs); the baselines are weak (fixed interval, simple heuristic threshold), making the gains appear larger than they may be against stronger adaptive methods.
Search Results 2603.16673 PDF
#94 — Moderately Innovative (57)

OntoDup: Governance-Aware Entity Matching for Scholarly Knowledge Graph Deduplication

mdpi.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: OntoDup's reframing of KG deduplication as a governed decision process with reified assertions, lifecycle states, and policy-driven materialization is a genuinely novel conceptual contribution that exposes operational trade-offs invisible to pairwise F1. However, the framework is primarily an ontological governance wrapper around existing matchers (Ditto, LLMs) rather than a new matching methodology, and the evaluation on two clean bibliographic benchmarks leaves production-scale feasibility unvalidated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Scholarly knowledge graphs (KGs) aggregate bibliographic records from heterogeneous sources (DBLP, ACM, Google Scholar), making duplicate record detection (DRD) essential for trustworthy downstream analytics. Near-duplicate publication records distort bibliometric indicators, inflate collaboration networks, and bias analyses. Existing state-of-the-art entity matching methods—rule-based, probabilistic, deep learning, and LLM-based—focus on pairwise prediction accuracy but provide no principled support for the operational lifecycle of matching decisions: who asserted what, based on what evidence, under what policy, and how decisions evolve. The gap is the absence of a governance layer connecting matching outputs to the controlled, auditable materialization of identity links in a KG. Methodology: OntoDup is an ontology-driven governance framework that reframes KG deduplication as a governed decision process rather than a one-shot classification task. Its core contribution is a two-layer semantic architecture. The governance layer records each pairwise matching outcome as a reified `ontodup:MatchAssertion` (an `rdf:Statement` subtype) carrying an explicit lifecycle status drawn from a SKOS concept scheme: Proposed, AutoAccepted, HumanValidated, HumanRejected, or Conflict. Each assertion also carries evidence, provenance (agent, method, thresholds), and optional LLM cost metadata (token counts, latency). The operational layer is a derived view: PIE inference rules promote only assertions in enabling states (AutoAccepted or HumanValidated) to consumable `owl:sameAs` links, apply symmetry/transitivity closure, propagate `notSameAs` constraints through equivalences, and flag contradictory assertions with `conflictWith` without destructive retraction. Corrections are recorded as status overrides in a separate correction graph and take effect at the next materialization cycle. The pipeline is modular: blocking (DeepBlocker or benchmark candidate lists), pairwise matching (Ditto neural matcher or zero-shot LLM prompting with GPT-4o/4.1 family), triage policy (dual thresholds τ_proposed and τ_autoaccept tuned on validation splits under precision and review-budget constraints), and SPARQL-based audit and interrogation. Evaluation uses two regimes on DBLP-ACM and DBLP-Scholar: Experiment A (pre-blocked, fixed candidate set) and Experiment B (end-to-end with DeepBlocker generating candidates from the graph). Key Results: In Experiment A (pre-blocked), Ditto achieves F1 = 0.9449 on DBLP-ACM (precision 0.9853, recall 0.9077) and F1 = 0.9387 on DBLP-Scholar (precision 0.9701, recall 0.9093) on materialized links. LLM matchers range from F1 = 0.9226 (gpt-4.1-nano) to 0.9801 (gpt-4o) on DBLP-ACM and 0.8081 (gpt-4o-mini, recall 0.71) to 0.9222 (gpt-4o) on DBLP-Scholar. The governance layer reveals materially different audit workloads: on DBLP-Scholar, Ditto leaves 48 proposals and loses 67 positives to negative decisions, while gpt-4o generates 524 proposals retaining 91 positives for review—different operational trade-offs invisible to pairwise F1 alone. LLM inference averages ~330 tokens/pair and 0.86–1.21 s/pair across models. In Experiment B (end-to-end with DeepBlocker, k=5 for DBLP-ACM achieving 99.6% blocking recall and k=150 for DBLP-Scholar achieving 98.1%), Ditto with conservative materialization achieves precision 0.9745 / recall 0.9306 / F1 0.9521 on DBLP-ACM but only precision 0.9860 / recall 0.5412 / F1 0.6989 on DBLP-Scholar. The recall drop on DBLP-Scholar is fully explained by governance decomposition: 1,766 positives remain in Proposed (auditable queue) and 687 fall below the triage threshold under the selective materialization policy, summing exactly to FN = 2,453. False positives remain small (54 DBLP-ACM, 41 DBLP-Scholar), enabling feasible manual correction. Post-correction, deduplicated entity counts shift from 4,910 → 2,690 on DBLP-ACM and 66,879 → 61,604 on DBLP-Scholar. Strengths: - The two-layer governance semantics cleanly separate decision provenance from operational consumption, enabling policy-driven re-projection of operational links without pipeline re-execution—a genuinely useful property for continuously evolving scholarly KGs. - The governance metrics (AutoAccepted/Proposed/Rejected distributions, non-materialized positives, per-assertion LLM cost) expose operational trade-offs—review workload, positive loss, inference cost—that are invisible to pairwise F1, providing a richer evaluation vocabulary for practitioners. - The framework is matcher-agnostic and integrates with existing components (DeepBlocker, Ditto, LLMs) without requiring a new standalone model, making it practically deployable as a wrapper around existing EM pipelines. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is restricted to two relatively clean, static, publication-level bibliographic benchmarks (DBLP-ACM and DBLP-Scholar). No experiments at million-scale, with OCR-heavy noise, continuously evolving collections, or heterogeneous entity types (authors, institutions) are reported, leaving production-scale feasibility unvalidated. - The end-to-end recall on DBLP-Scholar collapses to 0.54 under the conservative materialization policy chosen, and the paper acknowledges the π* = 0.99 precision target is infeasible under those conditions. The framework makes this trade-off visible but does not resolve it—there is no mechanism for automatically calibrating review budgets or adapting thresholds under distribution shift. - LLM cost analysis quantifies tokens and latency but does not explore cost-reduction strategies (caching, cascading, selective invocation), and the review simulation uses benchmark labels as a perfect oracle, making workload estimates optimistic relative to real curator variability with disagreement and errors.
Search Results
#95 — Moderately Innovative (57)

ViGoR-Bench: How Far Are Visual Generative Models From Zero-Shot Visual Reasoners? (36 ▲ )

Haonan Han, Jiancheng Huang, Xiaopeng Sun, Junyan He, Rui Yang, Jie Hu, Xiaojiang Peng, Lin Ma, Xiaoming Wei, Xiu Li · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 2 Apr 2026
Why this rank: ViGoR-Bench fills a clear gap by unifying I2I, sequential I2I, and I2V evaluation under a single reasoning-focused framework, and the dual-track process+result evaluation yields genuinely useful diagnostics like the 'Illusion of Reasoning' finding. The RL post-training result on maze navigation is striking but limited to one subtask. The benchmark itself is small and the core insight that visual fidelity does not imply reasoning correctness, while well-quantified, is not deeply surprising.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern AI image and video generation models achieve impressive visual fidelity but fail systematically at tasks requiring physical, causal, or symbolic reasoning—a gap the authors call a "logical desert." Traditional evaluation metrics (FID, CLIP-Score) measure statistical similarity or semantic alignment but are blind to logical correctness; a generated image can score well while violating basic physical laws. Existing reasoning-focused benchmarks are fragmented: they cover either image editing or video generation but not both, evaluate only final outputs and not intermediate reasoning, and lack rigorous automated evaluation aligned with human judgment. ViGoR-Bench is proposed to address all of these gaps in a single unified framework. Methodology: ViGoR-Bench is a 918-sample benchmark spanning three reasoning domains—Physical Reasoning (sorting, spatial reasoning, measurement, situational decision-making), Knowledge Reasoning (biology, physics, chemistry, geography, history, sports, common sense), and Symbolic Reasoning (Sudoku, maze navigation, jigsaw puzzles, algebraic equations, function plotting, LEGO block assembly, Klotski puzzles)—organized into 20 sub-tasks. Data is constructed through three pipelines: generative synthesis (LLM+image model generated scenes for physical tasks), real-world acquisition (web-curated educational content for knowledge tasks), and algorithmic construction (rule-based engines for symbolic tasks), all with human-in-the-loop verification. The benchmark covers Image-to-Image (I2I), Sequential I2I (I2Is), and Image-to-Video (I2V) tasks. Evaluation uses a dual-track protocol: a Process Metric evaluating intermediate reasoning steps (Background Consistency, Rule Obey, Visual Quality, Reasoning Accuracy on a 0–100 continuous scale), and a Result Metric evaluating the final output (same dimensions on a binary 0/1 scale). Gemini-2.5-Pro serves as the VLM-as-a-Judge, conditioned on ground-truth references. Human alignment is validated on 1,080 output instances and 540 process sequences across three expert annotators. A post-training study using Qwen-Image-Edit with SFT and GRPO-based RL on synthetic maze navigation data investigates benchmark utility as a training signal. Key Results: Experiments on 20+ models reveal substantial reasoning deficits across the board. The best model, Nano Banana Pro (proprietary), achieves only 68.4% average on result metrics without CoT and 61.2% with external CoT decomposition—well below any standard of competence for reasoning tasks. Open-source models lag severely: FLUX.1-Kontext-dev scores 38.9% average, Bagel 23.1%, and several models (HiDream-E1.1 at 1.7%) essentially fail. Explicit CoT prompting improves process interpretability scores (e.g., Nano Banana Pro CoT process avg: 72.0%) but does not reliably improve final result accuracy (result avg drops from 68.4% to 61.2% for Nano Banana Pro with external CoT), likely due to error accumulation across steps. Video generation models exhibit an "Illusion of Reasoning": Sora 2 Pro achieves 85.5% process Visual Quality but only 10.1% Reasoning Success, and Kling 1.6 scores 77.0% process VQ but 1.6% Reasoning Success. Performance degrades monotonically with maze/jigsaw grid size. For Sudoku, an inverted-U is observed, attributed to training data distribution bias. Post-training results are dramatic: Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 trained with RL on 8×8 maze data achieves 97.0% Reasoning Success and 99.0% average, surpassing all proprietary models, while SFT plateaus far earlier. Training on OOD harder data (8×8) generalizes better to easier in-distribution test cases than training on in-domain sizes. Gemini-2.5-Pro with GT references achieves 73.3% accuracy (process) and 78.6% (result) alignment with human experts, with lower variance than inter-annotator human variance. Strengths: - First benchmark to unify I2I, sequential I2I, and I2V evaluation under a single framework with 20 cognitively diverse sub-tasks, filling a clear gap in the fragmented evaluation landscape. - Dual-track process+result evaluation design is principled: it distinguishes models that "think correctly but draw poorly" from those that produce correct final outputs, yielding richer diagnostic insight. - Rigorous reliability analysis (1,080 outputs, 3 annotators, 3 judge runs) demonstrates that evidence-grounded VLM judging with GT references is competitive with human consensus, lending credibility to the automated evaluation pipeline. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is small (918 samples) and the per-subtask counts are sometimes very low (some knowledge sub-tasks appear to have fewer than 30 samples), raising concerns about statistical reliability of fine-grained comparisons. - The "Illusion of Reasoning" finding for video models—that high visual quality does not imply logical success—is presented as novel, but this conflation is largely a consequence of the benchmark's design framing process VQ and result RS as orthogonal metrics; the interpretation requires care to avoid circular reasoning. - Post-training experiments are limited to a single sub-task (maze navigation) and a single model family (Qwen-Image-Edit), making it difficult to generalize the RL > SFT claim across the full benchmark's diverse reasoning demands.

#96 — Moderately Innovative (57)

Detecting Miscitation on the Scholarly Web through LLM-Augmented Text-Rich Graph Learning

Huidong Wu, Haojia Xiang, Jingtong Gao, Xiangyu Zhao, Dengsheng Wu, Jianping Li · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The structural correspondence between LLM multi-hop reasoning and GNN message-passing layers is an elegant design insight, and the targeted distillation strategy that filters by confidence and correctness is a practical safeguard against hallucination propagation. Results are strong across three benchmarks. However, the approach builds on well-established knowledge distillation and GNN techniques, the S2ORC evaluation uses synthetic miscitations rather than real ones, and the requirement for LLM internal embeddings limits practical deployment.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Miscitation — where a cited source fails to support or contradicts the claim it is cited for — affects an estimated 25% of citations in the scientific literature. It propagates misinformation, distorts academic search engines, and undermines scientific integrity. Existing detection methods fall into two inadequate camps: network topology approaches that identify structural anomalies but ignore semantic content, and text-similarity approaches that operate on surface-level lexical features without deep semantic reasoning. LLMs offer stronger semantic understanding but suffer from hallucinations when reasoning without global graph context and are computationally intractable at web scale (billions of citation edges). The paper addresses the gap between these extremes: combining LLM semantic depth with GNN structural efficiency. Methodology: LAGMiD (LLM-Augmented Graph Learning-based Miscitation Detector) integrates three tightly coupled components over a text-rich citation graph G = (P, E, X^P, X^E). First, an evidence-chain reasoning mechanism uses chain-of-thought prompting to perform multi-hop verification: for a citation edge e_ij, a K-hop subgraph is extracted rooted at the cited paper, top-m semantically relevant nodes are selected via cosine similarity at each hop, and the LLM progressively accumulates verification states R_h across hops, finally outputting a miscitation level O ∈ [0,1], confidence C, and natural-language explanation U. Second, a layer-wise knowledge distillation transfers the LLM's intermediate reasoning states T_h (token embeddings from the final transformer layer at hop h) to the GNN's edge representations e^(h)_{h-1,h} via InfoNCE contrastive loss, creating a structural correspondence between GNN layers and LLM reasoning hops. Third, an iterative collaborative learning strategy selects the top w% most uncertain edges (by prediction entropy) for targeted LLM refinement, filtering distillation samples to those where LLM confidence C > τ_conf and LLM prediction matches ground truth. The total training objective combines task-specific binary cross-entropy with the targeted distillation loss. Implementation uses Qwen3-8B as the LLM backbone, a 2-layer GCN with hidden dim 1024, SciBERT for text encoding, K=2 hops, m=10 nodes/hop, w=20%, τ_conf=0.7. Key Results: LAGMiD achieves state-of-the-art on three benchmarks — RED, SciFact, and S2ORC — across AUC, F1, and Precision, with statistically significant margins (p < 0.05) over all baselines in every case. On RED: AUC 0.9615 vs. second-best GuARD at 0.9100 (+5.1%), F1 0.9167 vs. 0.8571 (+7.0%). On SciFact: AUC 0.8208 vs. AnomalyLLM at 0.8024 (+2.3%), F1 0.8205 vs. 0.7958 (+3.1%). On S2ORC: AUC 0.8100 vs. GLAD at 0.7883 (+2.8%), F1 0.8256 vs. GuARD at 0.7687 (+7.4%). Efficiency gains are substantial: LAGMiD delivers 10× inference speedup over LLM (Directed) and 100× over LLM (EC) on S2ORC. Ablation confirms all components are necessary: removing evidence-chain reasoning causes the largest performance drop (AUC −3.2% on RED, −6.2% on S2ORC), while removing knowledge distillation, layer-wise distillation, and targeted distillation each degrade results progressively. K=2 hops is the sweet spot — K=1 is insufficient for multi-hop dependencies and K>2 introduces noise. Strengths: - The structural correspondence between LLM reasoning hops and GNN message-passing layers is a principled and elegant design choice that enables meaningful knowledge transfer rather than generic feature alignment. - The targeted distillation strategy (routing only high-uncertainty, high-confidence samples) is computationally practical and avoids noise propagation from LLM hallucinations — an important safeguard given the task. - Comprehensive evaluation across diverse benchmarks (biomedical RED, scientific claim verification SciFact, large-scale CS S2ORC) with 8 baselines spanning GNN, PLM, LLM, and text-rich graph methods provides strong empirical grounding. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The S2ORC evaluation relies on synthetically injected miscitations (3 heuristic types: self-promotional, collaborator, cross-domain) rather than human-annotated ones, raising questions about whether the model learns to detect real-world subtle semantic distortions or just engineered statistical artifacts. - Evaluation datasets are quite small by graph standards (max 3,561 edges for classification), and the citation graphs themselves are sparse (413–5,844 nodes). Scalability claims of 10–100× speedup and suitability for 'billions of edges' are argued theoretically but not demonstrated empirically at scale. - The distillation requires access to LLM internal token embeddings (final transformer layer), which is not possible with black-box API-only models — limiting the approach to open-weight LLMs and constraining practical deployment options as frontier models increasingly move toward API-only access.
Recommended 2603.12290 PDF
#97 — Moderately Innovative (56)

Structure-Enhanced Adapter for Self-Supervised Heterogeneous Graph Learning

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The plug-and-play dual-path design is a clean engineering contribution that decouples structure learning from model architecture, enabling reuse across contrastive, generative, and semi-supervised HGNNs. However, both the homophily-boosting neighborhood expansion and the edge gating mechanisms are relatively standard adaptations of existing homogeneous graph structure learning ideas to the heterogeneous setting, and the reliance on predefined meta-paths inherits a well-known limitation without advancing beyond it.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Heterogeneous information networks (HINs) model real-world data with multiple node and edge types, yet existing heterogeneous graph neural networks (HGNNs) — both semi-supervised and self-supervised — assume fixed graph topologies and ignore structural noise inherent in raw graphs. The paper identifies two specific failure modes in HINs: (1) edge deficiency, where latent beneficial connections are absent (e.g., cold-start users with sparse interactions), and (2) semantic redundancy, where multi-relational edges dilute discriminative signal (e.g., purchases of popular items obscuring user preferences). Prior structure learning methods address only homogeneous (single-relation) graphs or, for heterogeneous graphs, remain tightly coupled to specific downstream tasks and models, preventing reuse. Methodology: The paper proposes HSADP (Heterogeneous graph Structure ADaPter), a plug-and-play dual-path module that can be inserted into any heterogeneous graph pre-training framework without architectural changes. The first path, the Homogeneous Subgraph Enhancer (HoSE), decomposes the heterogeneous graph into semantic homogeneous subgraphs via predefined meta-paths and then applies two complementary homophily-boosting mechanisms: (a) self-feature similarity neighborhood expansion — connecting same-type node pairs whose cosine similarity exceeds threshold η, and (b) multi-relational propagation — inferring new target-node edges by identifying target nodes that share similar non-target neighbors with sufficient co-occurrence frequency (threshold γ). The two expanded structures are fused with the original via learned attention weights and top-K sparsification. The second path, the Heterogeneous Edge Discriminator (HeED), processes each existing edge by concatenating the embeddings of its two endpoint nodes with a learnable relation embedding, passing them through a relation-specific MLP to produce an edge necessity probability, which dynamically gates the original edges. Both modules are trained end-to-end jointly with the pre-training objective of the host model. Feature spaces are aligned across node types via per-type MLPs before either path. The iterative update allows refined heterogeneous edge sets to feed back into the homogeneous subgraph construction. Key Results: HSADP is evaluated on five heterogeneous graph benchmarks (ACM, DBLP, Freebase, Yelp, AMiner) under 10-shot and 20-shot node classification. When plugged into four self-supervised baselines (HeCo, HGCML, HGMAE, HERO), it yields consistent improvements across nearly all dataset–metric combinations. Representative gains: on ACM 20-shot, HERO improves from 87.68 Ma-F1 to 91.23 (+3.55); on DBLP 20-shot, HERO improves from 91.35 to 93.48 (+2.13 Ma-F1) and Mi-F1 from 92.38 to 94.38; on Freebase 10-shot, HGMAE improves Ma-F1 from 47.33 to 52.64 (+5.31). Improvements are larger in the 10-shot regime, indicating the adapter is especially helpful under label scarcity. The adapter also improves semi-supervised HAN and HGT baselines (Table 3), demonstrating generality beyond self-supervised settings. HSADP consistently outperforms the specialized heterogeneous structure learning baseline HGSL, which requires label supervision. Ablations confirm both HoSE and HeED contribute independently, with their combination yielding the best performance. Strengths: - Clean plug-and-play design that decouples structural learning from model architecture, enabling application across contrastive, generative, and semi-supervised HGNNs without model-specific redesign. - Simultaneously addresses both structural pathologies (edge deficiency and semantic redundancy) with complementary mechanisms, whereas all prior heterogeneous GSL methods handle at most one. - Empirical validation is thorough: five datasets spanning different domains, 10- and 20-shot regimes, three metrics (Ma-F1, Mi-F1, AUC), four self-supervised backbones, and ablations for each component plus sensitivity analysis for three key hyperparameters. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method requires pre-specified meta-paths, which depend on domain knowledge and may be non-trivial to define for unfamiliar HINs; meta-path quality likely has significant impact on performance but is not systematically analyzed. - Hyperparameter sensitivity is non-trivial: similarity thresholds η and γ require careful tuning and the paper shows performance degrades noticeably for suboptimal values, raising questions about robustness across new datasets without extensive search. - Scalability is not analyzed — the pairwise cosine similarity computation and multi-relational propagation are potentially O(|V|²) in the worst case, and no runtime or memory measurements are reported for the large Freebase graph (180K nodes, 1M+ edges, 36 relation types).
Recommended
#98 — Moderately Innovative (56)

KMM-CP: Practical Conformal Prediction under Covariate Shift via Selective Kernel Mean Matching

Siddhartha Laghuvarapu, Rohan Deb, Jimeng Sun · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: KMM-CP provides a clean theoretical bias-variance decomposition connecting MMD and ESS to coverage error, and the selective KMM mechanism with KKT-justified sparsity is well-motivated. However, the approach is a principled but relatively standard application of kernel mean matching to a known problem (covariate shift in conformal prediction), and evaluation is limited to binary molecular classification under a single shift type.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Conformal prediction (CP) provides finite-sample coverage guarantees but requires exchangeability between calibration and test data — an assumption routinely violated under covariate shift, where the input distribution changes while the conditional label distribution stays fixed. Standard fixes reweight calibration samples using density ratios, but density-ratio estimation is brittle: classifier-based estimators produce heavy-tailed weights when the test distribution assigns mass to regions poorly covered by calibration data, collapsing the effective sample size (ESS) and degrading coverage. This is especially acute in molecular property prediction, where structural diversity between historical and novel compounds creates severe support mismatch in high-dimensional fingerprint spaces. Methodology: KMM-CP replaces explicit density-ratio estimation with Kernel Mean Matching (KMM), which directly minimizes Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) between the reweighted calibration distribution and the test distribution in an RKHS, subject to box constraints (0 ≤ w_i ≤ B) and a mass constraint. The box constraints directly bound weight variance, preserving ESS. The paper establishes a formal bias–variance decomposition (Theorem 3) showing that conformal coverage error is controlled by MMD (bias) plus 1/√ESS (variance) — precisely the two quantities KMM optimizes. An asymptotic validity corollary follows under mild conditions. The key innovation is Selective KMM (SKMM): a joint optimization over calibration weights and per-test-point selection variables α_j ∈ [0,1], with a yield constraint (mean α_j ≥ τ). KKT analysis (Theorem 4, Corollary 2) shows solutions concentrate near {0,1}, inducing sparse binary-like selection. The two-stage implementation first solves the joint problem, thresholds α_j at 0.2, then reruns standard KMM on the retained subset. This restricts conformal correction to regions of shared support, reducing both MMD (by ignoring unmatched target regions) and 1/√ESS (by avoiding extreme weights). The framework also extends to Mondrian (class-conditional) conformal prediction by normalizing weights within each class separately. Experiments use AttentiveFP GNNs on five TDC molecular benchmarks (Tox21, AMES, hERG, BBB-Martins, HIV) under fingerprint-based covariate shift, with 64-dimensional penultimate-layer embeddings as features for KMM. Key Results: Under fingerprint-based shift, uniform CP degrades severely: MAD from nominal coverage reaches 0.458 on BBB-Martins and 0.437 on Tox21. Classifier-based weighting partially helps on Tox21 (MAD 0.073) but collapses on HIV (0.297) and BBB-Martins (0.396). KDE baselines fail at 64 dimensions (MAD > 0.30 on multiple datasets) and the reduced-dimension CoDrugKDE variant (8D) remains above 0.30 on BBB-Martins. KMM consistently outperforms all density-ratio baselines (global mean rank 2.2 vs. 3.4–5.4 for others). SKMM achieves the best rank of 1.0 globally and 1.2 under Mondrian calibration, reducing MAD to 0.015 on Tox21, 0.009 on hERG, and 0.013 on HIV. On average, SKMM achieves a 55% reduction in MAD relative to CoDrugKDE. The empirical bias–variance proxy (MMD + √(1/ESS)) tracks calibration quality closely across methods, validating the theoretical bound. Selection weights αj concentrate near 0 and 1, with 58–77% of test instances retained across datasets. Strengths: - Tight theoretical grounding: the bias–variance decomposition connecting MMD and ESS to coverage error is clean and directly motivates the method's design choices, unlike prior KMM-for-CP work (Almeida et al., 2025) that lacks this analysis. - SKMM's selective mechanism is principled (KKT-justified sparsity) and practically effective, reducing coverage gap by >50% over strong baselines across five diverse molecular benchmarks. - The method avoids explicit density estimation entirely, sidestepping the curse of dimensionality that cripples KDE in 64-dimensional embedding spaces, and is fully reproducible with public code. Weaknesses / Limitations: - SKMM discards up to 40% of test instances by design (τ = 0.5 fixed), which is a hard constraint in applications requiring predictions for all inputs; the τ hyperparameter is not adapted to data and its sensitivity is underexplored. - KMM optimization via cvxopt is an O(n²) quadratic program in the number of calibration points, taking up to 50 minutes for the largest dataset (HIV, ~6k calibration points) on CPU — likely prohibitive at scale. - Evaluation is restricted to binary molecular classification under a single type of covariate shift (fingerprint distance from centroid); generalization to regression, multi-label tasks, image or text domains, or more complex shift types (label shift, concept drift) is not demonstrated.
Search Results 2603.26415 PDF
#99 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Embarrassingly Simple Self-Distillation Improves Code Generation (10 ▲ )

Ruixiang Zhang, Richard He Bai, Huangjie Zheng, Navdeep Jaitly, Ronan Collobert, Yizhe Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 2 Apr 2026
Why this rank: AP2O-Coder provides a reasonable engineering contribution by introducing error-type-aware curriculum learning into DPO-based code post-training, but the core idea — that training should focus on specific error categories rather than treating all failures uniformly — is intuitive rather than surprising. The absolute gains are modest (~3% pass@1), and the added complexity of sliding windows, periodic quizzes, and error-type bookkeeping raises questions about practical adoption.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Can an LLM improve its code generation capability using only its own raw outputs, without any verifier, teacher model, execution environment, or reinforcement learning? The supply of high-quality supervised signal for code is a binding constraint: human-written solutions are expensive, synthetic pipelines require stronger teachers or execution-based verification, and RL-based post-training is operationally complex and unstable. The paper asks whether a model can self-improve with nothing beyond a set of unlabeled problem prompts and its own decoding machinery. Methodology: Simple Self-Distillation (SSD) is a three-step recipe: (1) sample N solutions per problem from the frozen base model using a training-time temperature T_train and truncation (top-k, top-p) that differs from T=1; (2) fine-tune on these raw, unverified samples with standard cross-entropy SFT; (3) deploy the fine-tuned model at evaluation temperature T_eval. No filtering by correctness is applied—only minimal syntactic filtering (removing empty outputs and single-line stubs). Training uses ~10K competitive programming prompts from the rSTARcoder dataset, sampled once per prompt. The authors explain the mechanism through the "precision-exploration conflict": code generation interleaves "lock" positions (sharply peaked distributions where a distractor tail causes errors) and "fork" positions (genuinely multimodal distributions where diversity is needed). A single fixed decoding temperature cannot simultaneously suppress lock distractors and preserve fork diversity. SSD resolves this by training on temperature-shifted, truncated samples, which induces a three-term objective decomposition: support compression (suppressing distractor tails via truncation), within-support reshaping (flattening fork heads via T_train > 1), and KL alignment to the base model. This acts asymmetrically: locks become spikes, forks become plateaus. The effective temperature T_eff = T_train × T_eval governs performance in the no-truncation regime, while truncation raises the achievable ceiling independently. Key Results: SSD improves Qwen3-30B-Instruct from 42.4% to 55.3% pass@1 on LiveCodeBench v6 (+12.9pp, +30.4% relative), with gains of +6.5pp on easy, +14.2pp on medium, and +15.3pp on hard problems. Hard-problem pass@5 rises from 31.1% to 54.1%. Gains are consistent across five models: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (+3.5pp), Qwen3-4B-Instruct (+7.5pp), Qwen3-4B-Thinking (+3.3pp), Qwen3-30B-Thinking (+2.1pp). Pass@5 gains consistently exceed pass@1 gains (e.g., +18.1pp vs +12.9pp for Qwen3-30B-Instruct), indicating preserved diversity. Temperature sweeps of the frozen base model yield only 1.5–3.0pp variation in pass@1, confirming SSD's gains cannot be recovered by decoding alone. A stress test with T_train=2.0 and no truncation—where ~62% of training samples contain no extractable code—still improves Qwen3-30B-Instruct to 48.1% pass@1 (+5.7pp), demonstrating that gains derive from distributional reshaping rather than training on good programs. Out-of-domain transfer on 30B models is broadly stable (within ±2pp on AIME, HumanEval, CruxEval, MMLU); smaller models show more uneven benchmark-specific tradeoffs. Strengths: - The core idea is genuinely simple and requires no infrastructure beyond vLLM inference and standard SFT, yet produces substantial gains that no decoding hyperparameter sweep can match—a clean and surprising empirical finding. - The mechanistic explanation (precision-exploration conflict, lock/fork asymmetry, three-term objective decomposition via Rényi entropy) is theoretically rigorous and well-validated through controlled toy simulations, real-model distributional analysis, and formal propositions proving that decode-only policies cannot replicate the effect. - The "bad data, good results" stress test is a principled ablation that rules out the most obvious alternative explanation (training on reasonable-quality code) and isolates distributional reshaping as the operative mechanism. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method requires 8×B200 GPUs and thousands of training iterations; while conceptually simple, the practical cost is nontrivial and the paper does not discuss accessibility for practitioners without large-scale compute. - The evaluation is restricted to competitive programming (LiveCodeBench) as the primary benchmark. Smaller models (4B, 8B) show meaningful capability regressions on some non-competitive-programming benchmarks (e.g., Llama-8B loses most of its AIME accuracy, Qwen3-4B-Instruct regresses on HumanEval Shell), suggesting domain overfitting that may limit practical deployment. - The gains for thinking/reasoning models are noticeably smaller (+2.1–3.3pp) than for instruct models (+3.5–12.9pp), and the paper does not provide a mechanistic account of why SSD interacts differently with thinking-style decoding, leaving an important architectural question open.
Recommended 2604.01193 PDF
#100 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Trust-Aware Routing for Distributed Generative AI Inference at the Edge

Chanh Nguyen, Erik Elmroth · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The trust-floor pruning reduction from NP-hard RBSP to polynomial-time Dijkstra is a clean theoretical contribution with a formal sufficiency proof, and the physical testbed using real heterogeneous devices adds credibility. However, the evaluation is limited to GPT-2 Large (774M parameters), far below modern edge-relevant model sizes, and the 336 virtual peer replicas over 12 physical machines do not convincingly demonstrate real-world edge deployment challenges. The work addresses only crash failures, not Byzantine behavior, despite motivating the system with security concerns.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Running large generative AI models across decentralized edge devices (phones, IoT gateways, edge servers) is attractive for latency, privacy, and data sovereignty, but edge nodes are heterogeneous, resource-constrained, and unreliable. Pipeline parallelism distributes model layers across multiple peers, but the sequential dependency of autoregressive decoding means any single peer failure breaks the entire inference chain. Existing P2P frameworks (e.g., Petals) treat routing as a best-effort problem, selecting peers greedily on latency with no notion of reliability or trust history. This leaves inference pipelines vulnerable to "honey-pot" peers: fast nodes with high failure rates that attract latency-minimizing routers but collapse completion rates. The paper argues that routing for distributed GenAI inference should be treated as a first-class control problem with explicit risk bounds. Methodology: The paper presents G-TRAC (Generative Trust-Aware Routing and Adaptive Chaining), built around three mechanisms. First, a Hybrid Trust Architecture maintains a global trust-and-reputation ledger at a stable Anchor node; resource-constrained Seekers cache this state locally via periodic background synchronization, decoupling control-plane latency from the inference critical path. Each peer p carries a dynamic trust score r_p(t) ∈ [0,1] and a smoothed latency estimate via EWMA. Second, the routing objective is formulated as a Risk-Bounded Shortest Path (RBSP): minimize total effective latency (estimated latency plus failure-penalty term) subject to the end-to-end chain reliability product exceeding a user-defined threshold 1-ε. The RBSP is NP-hard in general (equivalent to Restricted Shortest Path), but G-TRAC reduces it to polynomial time by introducing a trust-floor τ = (1-ε)^(1/Kmax): all peers below τ are pruned before path selection, reducing the graph to a trusted subgraph on which standard Dijkstra finds the optimal chain. The paper provides a formal proof that any path selected from the pruned graph satisfies the global reliability constraint. Third, a Feedback-Driven Execution phase implements a bounded one-shot repair policy (a single retry with a replacement peer on failure) and performs asymmetric trust updates: all peers on a successful chain receive a small reward Δr+, while only the failing peer is penalized by Δr−. Key Results: Evaluation uses a heterogeneous physical testbed of 12 machines (servers, desktop, laptop, 2 Android phones) with 336 virtual peer replicas running GPT-2 Large (774M params, 36 layers) over a WireGuard mesh. Three peer profiles stress-test routing: Honey Pots (20-35% failure rate, ~1ms added latency), Turtles (0.1% failure, 150-300ms latency), and Golden Peers (0% failure, 20-40ms latency). Against four baselines (Naive DFS, Shortest Path, Max-Reliability, LARAC), G-TRAC achieves near-100% Service Success Rate (SSR) for sequences up to 20 tokens with only marginal degradation at 50 tokens, matching the Max-Reliability baseline statistically while delivering lower latency. Shortest Path collapses to <20% SSR due to the honey-pot effect. LARAC degrades to <40% SSR at 50 tokens. On per-token latency, G-TRAC achieves 2.4-2.6s average (P99 ~4.6s at 50 tokens), substantially better than Max-Reliability (4.4s average) and comparable to LARAC—while maintaining far higher SSR at longer sequences. Routing selection overhead stays below 10ms even at 1,000 nodes (sub-millisecond at practical scale), outperforming LARAC (210ms at 1,000 nodes) and Naive (>2s timeout). Appendix experiments show that distributed 4-hop inference (2.2s/token) matches monolithic execution (2.3s/token) while reducing per-device CPU time from 22s to 1.8-4.4s and memory footprint from 3.88GB to <1.85GB per device. Strengths: - The trust-floor pruning reduction from NP-hard RBSP to polynomial-time Dijkstra is mathematically clean, with a formal sufficiency proof and an explicit design formula for τ; this is the paper's strongest intellectual contribution. - The physical testbed uses real tensor transmission over a heterogeneous campus network (Ethernet + Wi-Fi, WireGuard overlay, Android phones via Termux), grounding results in realistic edge conditions rather than simulation. - G-TRAC occupies a genuine Pareto frontier in the trust-latency trade-off space, achieving near-MR reliability with near-LARAC latency, and the Wilson CI analysis makes the reliability comparisons statistically rigorous. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation uses only GPT-2 Large (774M parameters); modern edge-relevant models are 7B-70B parameters, and the paper does not address whether the memory, bandwidth, and pipeline-depth characteristics of these models change the qualitative conclusions about routing. - The trust model assumes conditional independence of peer failures given trust scores, which is explicitly acknowledged as a simplification. In practice, peers sharing physical infrastructure or network segments fail correlated, and the product-reliability formula overestimates actual chain reliability under such conditions. - The 336 "peers" are virtual replicas multiplexed over 12 physical machines with software-defined failure injection; this does not fully capture the diversity of real edge deployments and may understate the difficulty of trust convergence under genuine churn. - The paper claims robustness against "misbehavior" in the abstract but addresses only crash/stall failures, not Byzantine peers that return plausible but incorrect activations—a critical gap for the security-motivated edge scenarios described.
Search Results 2603.28622 PDF
#101 — Moderately Innovative (55)

OmniVLN: Omnidirectional 3D Perception and Token-Efficient LLM Reasoning for Visual-Language Navigation across Air and Ground Platforms

Zhongyuang Liu, Min He, Shaonan Yu, Xinhang Xu, Muqing Cao, Jianping Li, Jianfei Yang, Lihua Xie · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: OmniVLN assembles an ambitious multi-component system combining persistent-homology room partitioning, multi-resolution octant prompting, and cross-platform deployment. The token efficiency gains are practically significant and the persistent homology approach to room segmentation is a creative choice. However, the system is largely a sophisticated integration of existing components (Grounded SAM, DROID-SLAM, Qwen2.5-VL) without fundamental algorithmic novelty, and the extremely narrow evaluation (single 3-room lab, synthetic grids, no standard VLN benchmarks) makes it difficult to assess generalization.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Language-guided indoor navigation (VLN) in real multi-room environments suffers from two compounding problems: (1) narrow field-of-view sensors force repeated rotations, delay target discovery, and produce fragmented topological understanding; and (2) expanding perception to richer omnidirectional 3D maps causes a token explosion when naively verbalized for LLM-based planners, degrading reasoning stability and increasing latency. Existing systems are also hardware-specific, hindering transfer across aerial and ground robots. OmniVLN addresses all three deficiencies in a unified zero-shot framework. Methodology: OmniVLN integrates three tightly coupled subsystems. First, an omnidirectional multimodal perception front-end fuses a rotating LiDAR (motion-compensated via LiDAR-Inertial Odometry) with a 640×1920 panoramic camera stream. Open-vocabulary segmentation (Grounded SAM + SAM2) assigns per-point semantic labels via equirectangular projection, with majority voting over T frames for noise suppression. Second, an online five-layer Dynamic Scene Graph (DSG) is built incrementally: Mesh → Objects → Places (GVD-based topological nodes) → Rooms → Building. Room boundaries are determined threshold-free via Persistent Homology on the place graph, identifying stable plateaus in the 0th Betti number curve. Edges are refined via two-stage pruning: geometric occlusion checking plus short-range VLM verification with Qwen2.5-VL. The DSG is serialized as a persistent digital twin enabling offline reasoning. Third, a hierarchical egocentric reasoning module transforms the global DSG into an agent-centric 3D octant representation (8 volumetric octants from axis signs of local coordinates), with multi-resolution spatial attention: nearby objects receive full instance-level captions, peripheral objects receive abbreviated descriptions, and distant rooms are summarized as topological nodes. An H-CoT prompting chain guides the LLM through Room Filtering → Orientation → Functional Group Inference → Object Localization. An Actor-Critic loop validates each generated navigation primitive against DSG consistency and traversability before execution. Key Results: On a real 3-room IoT laboratory with 44 test objects, hierarchical DSG prompting improves spatial referring expression generation from 77.27% to 93.18% overall accuracy (+15.91%), with a particularly large gain in view-dependent relational reasoning (+22.73%), attributed to eliminating cross-room spatial hallucinations. Token efficiency experiments on 9 synthetic datasets (4 interconnected 5m×5m rooms, object densities N=5–50) show cumulative prompt token reductions of up to 69.98% in the global memory tier at high density (1067 vs. 3556 tokens), with 50.50% reduction in the peripheral tier. Navigation success rate in the dense global-memory scenario (D9) improves from 16% (flat-list baseline) to 80% (OmniVLN). Per-decision inference latency drops from 12.4s to 3.8s (~3× speedup) in high-density scenarios. Rotating LiDAR instantiates 85 object nodes vs. 61 for a fixed LiDAR under identical trajectories (+39% coverage). The system is validated on both a PX4-based aerial drone and a Unitree quadruped in real indoor environments. Strengths: - The combination of persistent-homology room partitioning with VLM-based edge verification is principled and directly addresses known failure modes (brittle heuristic boundaries, physically invalid relations) in prior DSG pipelines. - The multi-resolution octant prompting is a clean, computationally cheap mechanism (geometry pre-computed in <10ms) that demonstrably achieves near-logarithmic prompt growth, directly solving the token explosion bottleneck for long-horizon tasks. - Cross-platform validation on heterogeneous aerial and ground robots with a single shared reasoning stack is a genuine practical contribution, and the planned open-source dataset release supports reproducibility. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation scale is narrow: the real-world experiments involve a single 3-room lab environment with 44 objects, and the token/navigation experiments use synthetic 4-room grids with simplistic furniture categories — no comparison against established VLN benchmarks (R2R, MP3D-based tasks) makes it difficult to contextualize performance against the broader literature. - The system relies on offboard LLM inference over WiFi, which introduces latency, connectivity dependence, and potential synchronization issues in dynamic or large-scale deployments; real-time feasibility under degraded network conditions is not addressed. - The persistent-homology room partitioning and the VLM edge verification are evaluated only indirectly; there is no ablation isolating the contribution of each DSG construction component, leaving it unclear which parts of the pipeline drive the accuracy gains.
Search Results 2603.17351 PDF
#102 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Tiered Super-Moore's Law: Price Evolution, Production Frontiers, and Market Competition in Large Language Model Inference Services

Mingdeng Du · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Applying rigorous econometric tools (DEA/Malmquist decomposition, Chow structural break tests, tiered exponential decay) to LLM token pricing is a worthwhile and underexplored empirical contribution, and the finding that software/architectural innovation dominates hardware in driving cost reduction reorients a common narrative. However, the methodology is standard applied economics transplanted to a new domain rather than a methodological advance, and several key results rest on statistically fragile foundations (n=18 regression, borderline significance, proxy-based market share).
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The LLM inference market has undergone one of the fastest price collapses in commercial history—roughly 600-fold in under six years—yet rigorous economic analysis of token pricing is almost entirely absent. Prior AI economics literature focuses on labor displacement, aggregate productivity, and training costs, leaving unanswered how token prices evolve, what drives cost reduction, and what market structure is emerging. This gap matters because token prices determine who can access AI, how surplus is distributed, and how regulators should respond. Methodology: The paper assembles a three-source dataset: real-time cross-sectional pricing for 318 models from the OpenRouter API (March 2026), panel records for 3,237 models from Epoch AI's Notable AI Models database (2020–2026), and 62 manually cross-validated milestone pricing observations spanning GPT-3 through early 2026 models. Models are classified into three price tiers: flagship (>$5/M), mid ($0.50–$5/M), and economy (<$0.50/M). The analytical toolkit combines (1) tiered nonlinear least-squares exponential decay fitting with half-life comparison to Moore's Law's two-year benchmark; (2) Chow structural break tests on the pooled log-price time series; (3) Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA, CCR model) using blended price as input and a composite quality score as output, with Malmquist Productivity Index decomposition into efficiency catch-up (EC) and technological frontier shift (TC); and (4) log-log OLS regression of inference price on training cost, with HC3 robust standard errors and vendor fixed effects, plus a Welch t-test comparing $/FLOP between U.S. and Chinese firms. Key Results: Economy-tier tokens exhibit a price half-life of 1.10 years (82% faster than Moore's Law, R²=0.192) and mid-tier tokens 1.55 years (29% faster, R²=0.307), supporting the "Tiered Super-Moore" hypothesis. Flagship models defy exponential decay entirely (R²=0.031, half-life 3.59 years) due to reasoning premiums averaging 31.5× non-reasoning prices—ranging from 83× in 2024Q3 to 0.51× when DeepSeek-R1 entered at $0.55/M in 2025Q1, demonstrating the premium reflects market power rather than cost structure. A Chow test identifies May 1, 2024 as a statistically significant structural break (F=5.74, p=0.005), marking transition from technology-driven to competition-driven price decline, catalyzed by GPT-4o, DeepSeek-V2, and GPT-4o-mini. Cost decomposition finds TFP residuals account for ~103.7% of cost reduction; GPU hardware contributes −0.9%, confirming software/architectural innovation (MoE, Flash Attention, speculative decoding) as the dominant driver. The Malmquist Productivity Index peaked at 4.11 during 2024Q1–Q4, with technological frontier shift (TC=4.13) dominating efficiency catch-up (EC=0.994). Training cost–inference price elasticity is β=0.432 (p=0.050, R²=0.219, n=18), sub-unity, implying cost spreading over a large user base. The 63-fold U.S.–China training cost gap (GPT-4.5 at $340M vs. DeepSeek-V3 at $5.4M) is statistically attributable to architectural innovation—$/FLOP difference is insignificant (p=0.228). HHI declined from 4,558 to 2,086 over three years, crossing the DOJ/FTC threshold from highly concentrated to moderately concentrated; OpenAI's share fell from 65% to 34% while DeepSeek rose to 13% in 18 months. Strengths: - Novel multi-source dataset construction integrating cross-sectional, panel, and time-series data is commendable, and the tiered framing of price dynamics is empirically well-supported and conceptually clean. - The structural break identification is sharp and substantively meaningful—the May 2024 date aligns precisely with three independently verifiable market events, lending credibility beyond a purely statistical artifact. - The DEA/Malmquist decomposition cleanly separates frontier shift from efficiency catch-up and produces the striking result that hardware cost contributes negatively, reorienting the conventional "hardware drives AI costs" narrative toward architectural innovation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The training cost–inference pricing regression (n=18, R²=0.219, β significant only at p=0.050 OLS and p=0.071 HC3) is severely underpowered; the near-borderline significance and the complete loss of significance with vendor fixed effects (β=0.129, unreported p) undermine the causal interpretation of the elasticity estimate. - The quality score used in DEA is a heuristic composite (0–100) based on benchmark performance whose construction is not rigorously described; the resulting efficiency rankings—particularly the striking comparison of Anthropic (θ=0.04) vs. Liquid (θ=0.778)—are sensitive to this opaque measurement choice, and using a single input/output DEA model with only price and quality collapses the multi-dimensional nature of model capability. - Market share and HHI estimates rely on proxy-based inference in the absence of transaction volume data, making the concentration analysis indicative rather than definitive; the paper acknowledges this but does not adequately bound the uncertainty it introduces.
Search Results 2603.28576 PDF
#103 — Moderately Innovative (55)

A near CXL memory processing architecture for distributed graph neural network inference and training

tandfonline.com · karypis - new results
Why this rank: OptGNN presents a well-engineered CXL-based near-memory architecture for distributed GNN training with a principled separation of memory-bound and compute-bound operations. However, the core ideas—near-memory processing for graph workloads, computation-communication overlap, redundancy elimination—are established techniques recombined in a CXL context, and the 1.29× improvement over the closest baseline (CLAY-4) is modest. The simulation-only evaluation without real CXL hardware leaves significant uncertainty about real-world performance.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Distributed GNN training on large graphs faces two compounding bottlenecks: (1) irregular, fine-grained memory accesses during neighbor aggregation that underutilize DRAM bandwidth, and (2) high cross-device communication overhead when transmitting node features and gradients across partitions. Existing CPU-GPU hybrid systems are ill-suited because CPUs have limited memory bandwidth for random accesses and GPUs have capped on-chip memory (80 GB for H100), while industrial graphs can require terabytes. Prior near-memory approaches like GNNear lack workload balancing and CLAY has fixed scheduling with static VPU placement and no support for edge-update GNN models. The paper seeks a scalable near-memory architecture that addresses both intra-device bandwidth and cross-device communication simultaneously. Methodology: The authors propose OptGNN, a heterogeneous distributed architecture built around a CXL-based memory pool with embedded Near-Memory Processing Units (NMPUs) and a central CXL switch containing Switch Logic. The architecture separates memory-bound aggregation from compute-bound combination: NMPUs (placed per DRAM rank, 128 Aggregation PEs each) handle intra-device node feature aggregation and partial-sum computation for cross-device nodes; Matrix Multiplication Processing Engines (MMPEs, two homogeneous 32×128 systolic arrays) handle combination and edge-update operations. Two MMPEs allow simultaneous execution — one for edge update, one for combination — or both in parallel for models without edge updates. A Switch Logic module maintains an Inter-Index of cross-partition edges, generates node-centric data requirement lists, and hosts 128 additional Aggregation PEs for merging partial sums from different memory devices. Three key optimizations are layered on top: (1) computation-communication overlap — once a node completes local aggregation its result is immediately packed and dispatched to the MMPE without waiting for other nodes, overlapping data-intensive and compute-intensive phases; (2) redundancy elimination — NMPUs return pre-aggregated intermediate results for shared neighbor sets rather than raw features, reducing redundant memory accesses; (3) data packing — feature vectors are packed before transmission across the CXL switch to improve bus utilization. The system is implemented in Verilog, synthesized with Synopsys DC on TSMC 7 nm at 400 MHz, and evaluated against simulation via Ramulator for DRAM timing and energy. Key Results: OptGNN is evaluated on Reddit (233K nodes, 114M edges), Ogbn-products (2.4M nodes, 62M edges), and Ogbn-papers (111M nodes, 1.6B edges) with GCN and GraphSage models. Against DGL it achieves 14.33× speedup; against P3 14.94×; against GNNear 4.11×; against CLAY-1 2.46×; against CLAY-4 (the 4-device SOTA baseline) 1.29×. Energy consumption is 0.74× of GNNear and 0.88× of CLAY, measured under identical process technology and clock frequency. The ablation study isolates the contributions: the Vanilla base (CXL pool + NMP + MMPE without scheduling optimizations) performs similarly to CLAY-1; adding computation-communication overlap (Opt-CC) gives modest gains limited by remaining bandwidth bottlenecks; adding redundancy elimination (Opt-RE) on top achieves up to 1.84× over Vanilla on Reddit (high neighbor sharing) and 1.65× on Ogbn-products, with somewhat lower gains on Ogbn-papers (lower structural redundancy). Scalability analysis shows the acceleration advantage does not linearly degrade with graph size, unlike conventional distributed methods. Strengths: - Principled separation of memory-bound (aggregation via NMP) and compute-bound (combination/edge-update via MMPE) operations, with flexible dual-MMPE scheduling that eliminates hardware idle time for both edge-update and non-edge-update GNN models — a limitation CLAY does not address. - The three-level optimization stack (computation-communication overlap, redundancy elimination, data packing) each has a clear mechanistic justification tied to GNN data-dependency structure, and the ablation study credibly isolates each contribution. - Evaluation uses large, heterogeneous benchmark graphs (up to 111M nodes / 1.6B edges) covering a spectrum of structural redundancy levels, giving a more complete picture of where the architecture wins and where it does not. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is entirely simulation-based (Verilog synthesis + Ramulator); no real CXL hardware prototype exists, so CXL protocol overhead, coherence traffic, and PCIe 5.0 contention under multi-tenant workloads are modeled rather than measured — a significant gap given that real CXL latency characteristics can differ substantially from simulator assumptions. - The 1.29× improvement over CLAY-4 is modest and depends heavily on structural redundancy in the graph; on low-redundancy datasets the advantage narrows, and the paper does not evaluate on graphs where redundancy is near zero (e.g., tree-structured or synthetic random graphs), leaving the worst-case behavior uncharacterized. - The comparison against CLAY for GraphSage required modifying CLAY's VPU scheduling to support edge updates, introducing an experimental variable — the modified CLAY is not the published artifact, making the fairness of that specific comparison hard to assess independently.
Search Results
#104 — Moderately Innovative (55)

MuSEAgent: A Multimodal Reasoning Agent with Stateful Experiences (16 ▲ )

Shijian Wang, Jiarui Jin, Runhao Fu, Zexuan Yan, Xingjian Wang, Mengkang Hu, Eric Wang, Xiaoxi Li, Kangning Zhang, Li Yao, Wenxiang Jiao, Xuelian Cheng, Yuan Lu, Zongyuan Ge · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 31 Mar 2026
Why this rank: The shift from trajectory-level to atomic state-level experience retrieval is well-motivated and the MDP formalization is clean. The multi-viewpoint compositional indexing is a thoughtful design choice, and the OOD generalization result beating in-domain trajectory baselines is encouraging. However, the conceptual advance is relatively incremental — decomposing trajectories into state-action pairs and filtering by quality is a natural extension — and the heavy dependence on GPT-4o for hindsight evaluation limits practical applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal agents that leverage historical experiences for decision-making typically operate at the trajectory level — retrieving entire past interaction sequences as context. This introduces two fundamental problems: (1) visual inputs carry lower information density than text, so whole-trajectory retrieval injects substantial redundant or irrelevant context that amplifies noise under constrained context windows; and (2) trajectory-level retrieval fails to provide fine-grained guidance at intermediate reasoning bottlenecks, where state-specific tactical knowledge is far more beneficial than coarse task-level analogies. The paper addresses the gap between the granularity at which agents need guidance (individual decision steps) and the granularity at which prior work stores and retrieves experience (complete trajectories). Methodology: MuSEAgent formalizes multimodal agentic reasoning as a stateful Markov Decision Process (MDP) where the decision state at step t is a tuple of the user instruction, current visual observation, optional task descriptor, and accumulated execution history. The framework has two phases. In the offline Experience Abstraction phase, historical trajectories are decomposed into atomic state-action transitions (s_t, a_t, s_{t+1}); a hindsight reasoning model (GPT-4o by default) evaluates each transition, producing a scalar quality score q_t ∈ [0,10] and a textual guidance summary g_t. Only transitions with q_t ≥ δ (default 5.0) are retained in the experience bank B. Each retained experience is then indexed under M semantic viewpoints — compositions of state components such as (query, image), (query, image, task), or (query, task) — using a multimodal embedding model (Qwen3-VL-8B-Embedding), enabling retrieval through multiple complementary lenses. At inference time, the Experience Exploitation phase employs a Deep-and-Wide Search: Wide Search retrieves the Top-K experiences under a single selected viewpoint via cosine similarity, while Deep Search iterates over L rounds, each selecting a different viewpoint and retrieving Top-1, progressively aligning the query along different semantic axes (perceptual intent, tool history, task context). The final unified experience set E_{deep-wide} aggregates all retrievals and is injected into the agent's context before each action. The agent is equipped with a 13-tool bank covering OCR, object localization, image zoom-in/crop, region depth estimation, CLIP-based image-text/image-image similarity, web search, and arithmetic. Key Results: Experiments across four VQA benchmarks — V Bench, MME-RealWorld-Lite, ZoomBench, and HR-Bench — with three base models (Qwen3-VL-32B, Qwen3-VL-235B, Qwen3.5-397B) show consistent gains. On Qwen3-VL-32B, MuSEAgent achieves 65.30% average accuracy, outperforming the strongest trajectory-level baseline (Reflexion at 57.33%, Expel at 56.96%) by nearly 8 percentage points. On V Bench Relative Position with Qwen3-VL-235B, MuSEAgent improves 18.43% over Expel. Absolute performance scales with model size (69.76% on Qwen3.5-397B) but relative gains over baselines are larger for smaller models (+7.97% on 32B vs. +3.25–3.81% on larger models). The Deep Search saturates at L=3 iterations (71% → 85% on V Bench) and Wide Search at K=3 per round; beyond these values performance slightly declines due to context window dilution. In OOD generalization experiments where the experience bank is built entirely from the other three datasets, MuSEAgent (OOD) still achieves 59.64% average accuracy, exceeding Reflexion (57.33%) and Expel (56.96%) despite zero in-domain exposure, except on MME-RealWorld-Lite where it falls slightly below baselines. Ablations show that using both correct and incorrect trajectories (85.42% on V Bench) substantially outperforms using only correct (71.88%) or only incorrect (76.04%) trajectories, and that stronger hindsight models (GPT-4o) yield higher-quality experience banks than smaller self-reflection models. Strengths: - The shift from trajectory-level to atomic state-level experience granularity is well-motivated and cleanly formalized as an MDP; the quality-filtering mechanism elegantly separates useful signal from noise within trajectories. - Multi-viewpoint compositional state representation allows a single experience to be indexed and retrieved through different semantic angles, addressing the heterogeneous nature of multimodal states without requiring separate architectures per modality. - The OOD generalization result — beating in-domain trajectory baselines with zero target-domain data — is a strong empirical demonstration that stateful experiences capture transferable reasoning skills rather than task-specific memorization. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The offline experience abstraction phase depends on a powerful external model (GPT-4o) for hindsight evaluation, introducing a costly and proprietary dependency; the quality of the experience bank is therefore directly bounded by the hindsight model's capability and cost. - The evaluation is entirely on multiple-choice VQA benchmarks, which may favor pattern-matching retrieval over genuine compositional reasoning; performance on open-ended generation tasks or long-horizon embodied tasks where action sequences are less stereotyped is unknown. - The framework requires a separate exploration phase to populate the experience bank before evaluation, which assumes data availability and incurs non-trivial compute; the paper does not analyze the sample efficiency of experience construction nor what happens as the bank grows very large.
Recommended 2603.27813 PDF
#105 — Moderately Innovative (54)

Cross-Lingual LLM-Judge Transfer via Evaluation Decomposition

Ivaxi Sheth, Zeno Jonke, Amin Mantrach, Saab Mansour · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The decomposition into language-agnostic Universal Criteria Sets is a sensible and practical design for cross-lingual evaluation transfer, achieving good results with minimal English-only supervision. However, the core approach — decompose a complex judgment into structured sub-criteria and train a lightweight predictor on the resulting features — is a relatively straightforward application of existing ideas, and evaluation is restricted to binary faithfulness classification.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Automated evaluation of LLM outputs has been overwhelmingly English-centric, despite only ~15% of the global population speaking English. When AI systems are deployed to new languages, evaluation becomes a bottleneck: human annotations are scarce and expensive for most languages, language-specific fine-tuning is costly, and English-trained judges fail to transfer reliably to typologically distant languages. Existing multilingual judge approaches either require large-scale pretraining, language-specific fine-tuning, or synthetic data construction—none offer a lightweight, annotation-free path to cross-lingual evaluation. Methodology: The paper introduces a three-stage decomposition-based framework centered on a Universal Criteria Set (UCS). Stage 1 (Criteria Generation): given a task description, an LLM generates a set of high-level evaluation concepts (e.g., factual accuracy, certainty calibration, contradiction detection), then expands each into targeted 1–10 Likert-scale evaluation questions, all in English. Stage 2 (Criteria Evaluation): the LLM scores each input sample against every criterion question, producing a structured criteria-response vector z(x) as an intermediate representation. Concept-level aggregation averages per-concept scores to yield a lower-dimensional representation s(x). Stage 3 (Cross-Lingual Transfer): a lightweight predictor (shallow neural network with one 32-unit hidden layer) is trained on English-labeled concept-level representations and applied zero-shot to other languages, exploiting the hypothesis that evaluation semantics are language-agnostic even when surface text is not. No target-language labels, translations, or synthetic data are used. The key architectural choice—concept-level rather than criterion-level transfer—reduces sample complexity and overfitting risk. Key Results: Evaluated on MEMERAG (EN/FR/DE/HI/ES, RAG faithfulness) and mFACE (9 languages including Amharic, Burmese, Swahili, Thai, Arabic, Hindi, Spanish, French, and English, summarization faithfulness) with four LLM backbones (Qwen3-32B, Qwen3-235B, OSS-20B, OSS-120B). On MEMERAG with Qwen3-235B, UCS(EN) achieves average balanced accuracy (BA) of 81.6 vs. 78.1 for the strongest baseline (RocketEval), with per-language gains of 3.1–4.1 BA points across Germanic, Romance, and Indo-Aryan languages. On mFACE, UCS(EN) averages 74.9 BA vs. 72.0 for RocketEval, with particularly large gains in French (+7.0) and Spanish (+7.4). The framework is robust across all four LLM backbones. The transfer module stabilizes with only 20–30% of available English training data. One notable failure: Burmese is the single language where UCS underperforms zero-shot (67.7 vs. 70.2), attributed to limited LLM pretraining coverage and script complexity that the shared criteria space does not capture. Criteria importance analysis via Spearman correlation reveals that languages with lower alignment to the English importance profile tend to see larger performance drops when criteria are pruned. Strengths: - The decomposition into language-agnostic criteria is a principled and interpretable approach that avoids the brittleness of single monolithic prompts, providing a structured intermediate representation that doubles as a diagnostic tool for understanding evaluation behavior across languages. - Extremely sample-efficient: the transfer module trains on only a small fraction of English labeled data (~20–30% suffices for stable performance), making the method practical for real deployment scenarios where annotation budgets are constrained. - Strong empirical breadth: results hold across four LLM backbones, two datasets, and up to nine typologically diverse languages (including low-resource scripts like Amharic and Burmese), with low variance across random seeds. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is limited to binary faithfulness classification tasks; it remains unclear how well the framework generalizes to open-ended quality dimensions (coherence, fluency, relevance) or to non-binary judgment scales, which are common in real evaluation settings. - The Burmese exception (where zero-shot beats UCS) is noted but not explained mechanistically—the assumption of language-agnostic criteria does not hold uniformly, and the paper offers no criterion selection or adaptation strategy to handle such cases. - The approach introduces significant additional inference cost: per-concept criteria generation and per-concept scoring replace a single LLM call with multiple sequential calls, and the paper's ablation on decomposition granularity (Table 4) confirms the best results require the most calls—a non-trivial cost in production settings that is acknowledged but not fully quantified.
Recommended 2603.18557 PDF
#106 — Moderately Innovative (54)

First-Mover Bias in Gradient Boosting Explanations: Mechanism, Detection, and Resolution

Drake Caraker, Bryan Arnold, David Rhoads · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Scores 54 for precisely characterizing the first-mover bias mechanism in gradient boosting and providing falsifiable causal evidence via the LSM vs. DASH controlled comparison. The honest acknowledgment that simple seed-averaging achieves equivalent stability is commendable. However, the scope is narrow—limited to XGBoost and TreeSHAP—and the core insight that independent model training eliminates path-dependent bias, while useful, is not deeply surprising given random forests exhibit this property by construction.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: SHAP-based feature importance rankings from gradient-boosted models are widely used in high-stakes decisions—biomedical research, production ML pipelines, regulatory audits—yet they can be highly unstable when features are correlated. The standard practitioner response of training a larger, more powerful model actively worsens this problem. This paper investigates a specific, previously under-characterized mechanism driving this instability: first-mover bias, a path-dependent concentration of SHAP importance arising from sequential residual fitting in gradient boosting. When two correlated features compete for the first split, whichever wins gains a self-reinforcing advantage as subsequent trees inherit modified residuals that continue to favor the incumbent, concentrating SHAP mass on an arbitrary feature rather than distributing it equitably. Multicollinearity is pervasive (clinical, materials science, economic datasets), making this a practically significant and broadly relevant problem. Methodology: The paper proposes DASH (Diversified Aggregation of SHAP), a five-stage pipeline: (1) train a population of M=200 XGBoost models with randomly sampled hyperparameters, critically using low colsample_bytree (0.1–0.5) to force feature diversity across models; (2) filter for predictive quality, retaining models within ε of the best validation score; (3) select a diverse subset K≤30 via greedy max-min dissimilarity on gain-based feature utilization vectors; (4) compute interventional TreeSHAP for each selected model on a held-out explain set and average element-wise; (5) produce diagnostic tools—the Feature Stability Index (FSI = ratio of cross-model SHAP variance to mean importance per feature) and the Importance-Stability (IS) Plot (4-quadrant scatter of importance vs. FSI)—that detect first-mover bias without ground truth. The central mechanistic claim is tested by comparing DASH against a Large Single Model (LSM) that uses the same total tree count (~15,000) and the same low colsample_bytree, differing only in that LSM's trees are trained sequentially rather than independently. Experiments use synthetic data (N=5000, P=50, 10 correlated groups, 20 repetitions across ρ∈{0,0.5,0.7,0.9,0.95}) with both linear and nonlinear DGPs, and three real-world datasets (Breast Cancer Wisconsin, Superconductor, California Housing).
