#120 — Moderately Innovative (48)

Optimizing Distributed LLM Inference for Heterogeneous Workers through Dynamic Graph Partitioning

repositum.tuwien.at · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The zero-setup browser-based distributed inference concept is genuinely appealing, and the reduction to the Ordered Partition Problem with exact DP is algorithmically clean. However, the FP32-only evaluation inflates memory requirements 4–8× and prevents fair comparison with practical systems, the star topology doubles network latency versus peer-to-peer alternatives, and the work is largely an engineering integration of known techniques (DP partitioning, simulated annealing, WebGPU) rather than a methodological advance.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Frontier large language models require hundreds of gigabytes of memory, restricting inference to well-resourced institutions. While distributed inference across consumer devices offers a path forward, existing systems (Petals, EdgeShard) require manual worker configuration and multi-gigabyte downloads, limiting accessibility to expert users. No existing system adapts model partitioning at runtime to heterogeneous workers in a web environment, where browsers restrict GPU access to WebGPU rather than CUDA and prohibit RDMA. This thesis addresses the gap: enabling zero-setup, browser-based distributed LLM inference where users contribute compute simply by opening a webpage. Methodology: The system reduces LLM graph partitioning to the Ordered Partition Problem by exploiting the linear chain structure of transformer layers, coarsening the ONNX computational DAG to the layer level. A dynamic programming algorithm solves layer-to-worker assignment in O(n²m) for n layers and m workers, jointly minimizing execution cost (compute time, network latency, bandwidth) and initialization cost (weight download time accounting for cached layers). Worker ordering—which is NP-hard to optimize globally—is addressed via exhaustive search for m<8, simulated annealing for moderate sizes (n²m<50,000), and time-bounded random search otherwise. The cost model uses runtime-collected per-worker metrics: available memory, session overhead, computational speed (calibrated via timed benchmark pairs), network latency (WebSocket pings), and bandwidth (HTTP download tests). A time-decay sigmoid function gradually reduces initialization penalties over time, pushing the system toward inference-optimal assignments as workers stabilize. The server is implemented in 5,500+ lines of Rust, supports both Python/CUDA and browser/WebGPU workers, uses Protocol Buffers over WebSockets, and maintains distributed stateful KV caches on each worker. Dynamic repartitioning is triggered on worker connect/disconnect and periodic checks, with hot-swap capability using active/inactive assignment slots. Key Results: The cost model achieves a MAPE of 12.6% using initial metrics and 8.4% overall after runtime metric updates; for large models (Qwen3-8B), running estimates reach 4.4% MAPE with MAE below 7ms regardless of model size. Dynamic partitioning consistently outperforms static equal-layer splitting: 46% higher TPS with 3 workers and 41.3% higher TPS with 5 workers on Qwen3-4B. Ablation confirms all three metric types (computational speed, latency, bandwidth) contribute meaningfully—disabling bandwidth caused the largest throughput drop as the algorithm selected a 128 KB/s worker. The system successfully distributes Qwen3-14B (59 GB full-precision ONNX) across three heterogeneous devices achieving 3.17 tok/s, and a case study demonstrates recovery from unexpected worker disconnects within 7 seconds. Web-based inference (WebGPU) achieves competitive throughput with native execution and scales better with increasing sequence length, though is limited to models fitting in WebAssembly's ~4 GB memory space. Strengths: - Elegant reduction of graph partitioning to the Ordered Partition Problem exploiting LLM layer linearity, enabling exact DP solutions with tractable complexity and clear algorithmic guarantees - Zero-setup browser participation is genuinely novel—no existing system combines distributed inference, heterogeneous workers, and web-based deployment simultaneously - Thorough empirical evaluation across multiple model sizes (0.6B–14B), worker configurations, network conditions, and a metric ablation study; cost model validation is quantitatively rigorous Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation restricted to full-precision (FP32) Qwen3 models due to toolchain issues with quantization; this inflates memory requirements 4–8× over quantized alternatives and prevents direct comparison with practical deployments using Ollama or vLLM - The star topology routes all intermediate tensors through the central server, doubling network latency compared to peer-to-peer architectures; this is a fundamental architectural bottleneck especially in slow-network scenarios where the paper's own measurements show TPOT growing from 16ms to 100+ms with multiple workers - Simulated annealing hyperparameters (temperature schedule, neighborhood function, iteration budget) are not tuned or validated against ground truth for problem sizes beyond 5 workers, leaving the quality of approximate solutions for larger deployments uncharacterized

#121 — Moderately Innovative (48)

PerceptionComp: A Video Benchmark for Complex Perception-Centric Reasoning (14 ▲ )

Shaoxuan Li, Zhixuan Zhao, Hanze Deng, Zirun Ma, Shulin Tian, Zuyan Liu, Yushi Hu, Haoning Wu, Yuhao Dong, Benlin Liu, Ziwei Liu, Ranjay Krishna · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 2 Apr 2026
Why this rank: PerceptionComp is a carefully constructed benchmark with strong construct validity — the 66-point single-view human accuracy collapse is a compelling empirical demonstration that the benchmark genuinely isolates iterative perception. The conjunctive/sequential compositional logic with formal subset verification is rigorous. However, it remains a benchmark contribution without methodological novelty, and the small scale limits statistical power.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing video understanding benchmarks fail to adequately measure deep, long-horizon video reasoning that requires repeatedly revisiting a video to gather temporally distributed evidence. Perception-centric benchmarks (e.g., VideoMME, Perception Test) are often solvable after a single viewing, while logic-heavy benchmarks (geometry, maze solving) derive difficulty from symbolic structure rather than real-world visual complexity. No benchmark simultaneously demands long-horizon reasoning, high scene complexity, and forced repeated evidence gathering across multiple temporally separated segments. This gap matters because multimodal test-time thinking for real-world AI systems—robots, AI glasses—must compose multiple perceptual skills over extended video horizons. Methodology: PerceptionComp constructs difficulty along two orthogonal axes: (1) video scene complexity and (2) compositional question complexity. Videos (279 clips, 2–10 min) are selected using automatic proxies—SAM2 instance counts for object density and RAFT optical-flow magnitude for motion intensity—spanning seven categories (city walks, malls, sports, home tours, variety shows, movies, game livestreams). Questions are fully manually annotated (10–20 min per question) and composed of multiple perceptual subconditions targeting six skills: semantic understanding, spatial understanding, temporal understanding, visual correspondence, visual world knowledge, and near-future prediction. Two compositional logics are used: conjunctive (all subconditions must be satisfied simultaneously, verified that no proper subset suffices) and sequential (subconditions must be resolved in order, with later steps depending on entities established earlier). Each question is a five-choice MCQ with distractors drawn from the same answer category to minimize language prior exploitation. Three difficulty levels (L1/L2/L3) are assigned by expert annotators based on subcondition count and intrinsic difficulty. A two-stage annotation pipeline with cross-annotator verification achieves 89% inter-annotator agreement on a 100-question sample. Key Results: Human participants with unrestricted rewatching reach 100% accuracy (expert) and 85.10% (ordinary), but single-view human accuracy collapses to 18.97%—near the 20% chance baseline for five-choice questions—confirming the benchmark's dependence on repeated perception rather than memory or language priors. Human response times are more than 10× longer than VideoMME, 5× longer than Video-Holmes and LongVideoBench, and 2× longer than VideoMMMU, despite videos being under 10 minutes. State-of-the-art MLLMs fall far short: the best model, Gemini-3-Flash, reaches only 45.96%; frontier models cluster in the mid-40s (GPT-o3: 43.54%, Gemini-2.5-Pro: 44.34%, Gemini-3-Pro: 44.43%), and open-source models remain below 40% (Qwen3-VL-235B: 34.02%, GLM-4.5V-106B: 36.69%). Scaling model size does not consistently help—Qwen3-VL-235B (34.02%) underperforms Qwen3-VL-8B (34.06%). Test-time reasoning provides modest gains: GPT-o3 exceeds GPT-4o by 11.04%, Gemini-2.5-Pro exceeds Gemini-2.5-Flash by 6.19%. Thinking variants of Qwen3-VL sometimes underperform their instruct counterparts, suggesting that language-side reasoning amplifies rather than corrects perceptual errors. More input frames monotonically improve performance: GPT-o3 rises from 34.0% (16 frames) to 43.54% (50 frames); Qwen3-VL-8B gains 7.8 points from 16 to 64 frames. Larger thinking-token budgets for Gemini-2.5-Flash improve accuracy from 37.2% (1,024 tokens) to 40.4% (8,192 tokens). Error analysis of Gemini-3-Pro shows failures peak at reasoning steps 2–3 (29.6% and 31.5%), with spatial understanding as the dominant error cause (31.5%), followed by static feature perception (29.6%). A counter-intuitive finding: Gemini-3-Flash outperforms Gemini-3-Pro, attributed to a "streamlining effect" where the Flash model's more focused inference avoids detail fixation and spatial hallucinations induced by over-analysis. Strengths: - Rigorously designed to isolate perception as the primary bottleneck: the single-view human drop of 66+ percentage points provides a clean empirical validation that the benchmark genuinely requires iterative evidence gathering, not memory or language priors. - Compositional question design with two logics (conjunctive/sequential) and formal verification that no proper subset of subconditions suffices ensures questions cannot be shortcut, giving the benchmark strong construct validity. - Comprehensive evaluation covering proprietary frontier models, open-source instruct models, and open-source thinking models, with controlled ablations over both perception budget (frames) and reasoning budget (tokens) that cleanly disentangle two distinct failure modes. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is small (1,114 questions, 279 videos), which limits statistical power for fine-grained comparisons, especially across the 7 video categories and 3 difficulty levels; category-level results for sparse domains (Movie: 25 questions) are noisy. - All videos are from daily-life recordings (city walks, home tours, game livestreams), which may introduce selection bias toward particular visual styles, camera motion patterns, and culturally specific content, potentially limiting generalization claims about broad video understanding. - The use of Gemini-2.5-Flash as an automated judge for evaluating complex reasoning outputs introduces a potential circularity in the evaluation pipeline, as the judge model is in the same family as some evaluated models, and its accuracy for judging multi-step perceptual chains is not independently validated.

#122 — Moderately Innovative (48)

PLACID: Privacy-preserving Large language models for Acronym Clinical Inference and Disambiguation

Manjushree B. Aithal, Ph. D., Alexander Kotz, James Mitchell, Ph. D · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: PLACID addresses a genuine and practical deployment barrier — HIPAA/GDPR compliance for clinical acronym disambiguation — that prior work has ignored. The cascaded architecture exploiting the capability dissociation between general-purpose detection and domain-specific expansion is a clean empirical insight. However, the methodology is a straightforward composition of existing off-the-shelf models with zero-shot prompting, and the evaluation on PubMed abstracts rather than actual EHR notes undermines the clinical validity claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Clinical narratives are dense with ambiguous acronyms whose misinterpretation can cause life-threatening errors — a well-documented patient safety hazard. While large cloud-based LLMs achieve near-perfect zero-shot acronym disambiguation, transmitting unredacted clinical notes to external APIs violates HIPAA/GDPR. Pre-emptive de-identification is brittle and often strips the precise local context needed for disambiguation. No prior work had systematically evaluated whether small (2B–10B parameter) on-device models can bridge this gap without privacy compromise. Methodology: The paper introduces PLACID, a two-mode local inference pipeline evaluated entirely on-device (Apple M4-Max via MLX framework). The single-pass mode uses one model for simultaneous detection and expansion. The cascaded mode separates tasks: a general-purpose small model (Gemma2:2B, Llama3.2:3B, Mistral7B, Apple Foundation) performs acronym detection, then routes the detected acronym plus original text to a domain-specific biomedical fine-tuned model (MedGemma1.5:4B, BioMistral:7B) for contextual expansion. All inference is zero-shot with minimal structured prompts requesting JSON output. Evaluation uses the GLADIS biomedical benchmark (~6,299 instances from MedMentions/PubMed abstracts with UMLS-grounded expansions), measuring detection via exact string match and expansion quality via BLEU, METEOR, and ROUGE-L averaged over 5 inference iterations. An "overshadowed ratio" metric (6–10% in the biomedical sets) ensures the benchmark requires genuine contextual reasoning rather than frequency memorization. Key Results: General-purpose models achieve high acronym detection accuracy on biomedical text (Apple Foundation: 0.988 ± 0.072; Gemma2:2B: 0.930; Llama3.2:3B: 0.945) but expansion collapses dramatically — best single-pass ROUGE-L is only 0.655 (Mistral:7B). In the cascaded mode, domain-specific models close this gap substantially: BioMistral reaches ROUGE-L of 0.81 and MedGemma reaches 0.79 (clean), versus 0.62–0.75 for general models in the same cascaded setup. The clean preprocessing pipeline provides negligible gain (Δ ≤ 0.002), confirming surface formatting is not the bottleneck. Models exhibit consistent bimodal behavior — inputs are reliably correct or incorrect across iterations — with high standard deviations (0.229–0.484) indicating instance-level difficulty is the key bottleneck rather than stochastic model variance. Strengths: - Directly addresses a practical deployment barrier (HIPAA/GDPR compliance) that has been universally ignored in prior LLM-based disambiguation work, making the contribution clinically relevant. - The cascaded architecture cleanly exploits a demonstrated capability dissociation: general models are strong detectors, domain models are strong expanders — and the paper provides clean empirical evidence separating these two skills. - Rigorous experimental design: 5-iteration averaging, dual preprocessing pipelines, three complementary metrics, and the overshadowed-ratio difficulty control all reduce the risk of misleading results. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is PubMed abstracts (MedMentions), not actual EHR clinical notes — the claimed clinical validity is overstated, as PubMed prose differs substantially from ICU nursing notes or discharge summaries where the problem is most acute. - The cascaded mode evaluation assumes perfect detection (the expansion model is handed the correct acronym), so the reported 0.81 ROUGE-L is an upper bound that does not reflect true end-to-end pipeline performance when detection errors propagate. - Zero-shot only evaluation, while motivated, leaves open whether even modest few-shot prompting or RAG would enable a single general model to match the cascaded approach — this comparison is needed to justify the added complexity of two-model inference.
Search Results 2603.23678 PDF
#123 — Moderately Innovative (48)

Distributionally robust optimization for recommendation selection

Tomoya Yanagi, Shunnosuke Ikeda, Ken Kobayashi, Yuichi Takano · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The DRO formulation provides a theoretically cleaner treatment of estimation uncertainty than prior robust mean-variance approaches, with proper PSD guarantees and convergence proofs for the PADM solver. However, this is fundamentally an application of established DRO machinery (Delage & Ye 2010) to an existing portfolio-theoretic recommendation framework, and the evaluation excludes modern neural baselines.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Recommender systems must balance accuracy with diversity — purely accuracy-driven top-N selection tends to concentrate recommendations on popular items, degrading user satisfaction and sales diversity. Prior work applying financial mean-variance portfolio theory to this trade-off suffers from a fundamental flaw: the mean and covariance of ratings must be estimated from sparse data, and these estimates carry substantial errors that destabilize the optimization. A previous robust optimization (RMV) approach addressed estimation error via cardinality-based uncertainty sets, but it lacks theoretical guarantees on positive semidefiniteness of covariance matrices and ignores the statistical dependence between mean and covariance uncertainty. No prior work had applied distributionally robust optimization (DRO) — which optimizes against the worst-case probability distribution within an ambiguity set — to recommendation selection. Methodology: The paper formulates a cardinality-constrained DRO model for top-N recommendation selection. Missing ratings are treated as random variables; the ambiguity set is moment-based (Delage & Ye 2010), defined by two ellipsoidal constraints: the expected rating vector must lie in an ellipsoid of size κ₁ around the estimated mean, and the covariance matrix is upper-bounded (in PSD order) by κ₂ times the estimated covariance. This naturally couples mean and covariance uncertainty and guarantees valid covariance matrices by construction. The maximin DRO objective is dualized into a single-level mixed-integer semidefinite optimization (MISDO) problem. To solve it efficiently, the authors design a Penalty Alternating Direction Method (PADM): the problem is split into a continuous SDP subproblem (solved exactly in polynomial time via MOSEK) and a binary knapsack subproblem (solved in closed form by sorting). A penalty on the L₁ distance between continuous and binary copies drives them to agreement. Convergence to a stationary point is proved under two regularization variants (trace-bounded and Frobenius-norm-regularized formulations) that guarantee compactness of the feasible set. Key Results: Experiments on MovieLens-100K, Yahoo! R3, and BookCrossing compare DRO against mean-variance (MV) and robust mean-variance (RMV) baselines across N ∈ {3,5} recommendations. DRO consistently achieves higher recommendation diversity (1−Gini) at the same F1 accuracy level across all three datasets and both values of N. The diversity advantage is most pronounced at lower accuracy settings on MovieLens. On BookCrossing, DRO simultaneously achieves higher accuracy and higher diversity than all baselines at the high-diversity end of the Pareto curve — a particularly striking result suggesting the DRO ambiguity set helps avoid overfitting to popular items. Computation time for DRO is 0.5–3.8 s per user on an Apple M3, compared to <0.01 s for MV and <0.35 s for RMV; the authors argue this is acceptable for offline or batch recommendation pipelines. Strengths: - The DRO formulation is theoretically principled: it addresses both mean and covariance uncertainty jointly through moment-based ambiguity sets, which guarantees PSD covariance matrices — a concrete fix over the prior RMV approach's acknowledged drawback. - Convergence proofs for PADM are provided under two distinct regularization regimes, giving the algorithm a credible theoretical foundation absent from many heuristic recommendation methods. - Empirical evaluation spans three diverse datasets with different rating scales and sparsity profiles; results are consistent and the Pareto accuracy-diversity plots are informative visualizations of the trade-off. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation metrics are limited: only F1 (binary hit rate) and 1−Gini (aggregate diversity across users) are used. No user-level novelty, serendipity, coverage, or beyond-accuracy metrics are reported. The Gini coefficient measures aggregate distributional evenness over items, not intra-list diversity per user, so DRO's advantage may reflect catalog-level effects rather than improved individual recommendation lists. - The computational cost of DRO (up to ~4 s per user) is 100–400× slower than MV and substantially slower than RMV, which is a meaningful practical gap at scale despite the authors' claim it is acceptable. No experiments on larger datasets (e.g., MovieLens-1M, Amazon) are provided to test scalability. - The experimental comparison excludes modern neural collaborative filtering baselines (e.g., NCF, LightGCN) and portfolio methods built on learned embeddings. The paper uses simple SVD for rating prediction, so it remains unclear whether DRO adds value over the prediction quality gains obtainable from better embedding models.
Search Results 2603.22090 PDF
#124 — Moderately Innovative (48)

Communication-Aware Diffusion Load Balancing for Persistently Interacting Objects

Maya Taylor, Kavitha Chandrasekar, Laxmikant V. Kale · arxiv.org · 7 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The communication-aware neighbor selection from runtime communication graphs is a sensible and previously unexplored combination for diffusion-based load balancing, and the 2x speedup over GreedyRefine at scale is practically relevant. However, diffusion load balancing and communication-aware partitioning are both well-established ideas, and this work largely composes them within the existing Charm++ infrastructure rather than introducing fundamentally new techniques.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Irregular parallel applications (particle simulations, adaptive mesh refinement, graph analytics) suffer from time-varying load imbalance and communication patterns. Existing dynamic load balancing methods either ignore communication structure entirely (e.g., GreedyRefine, which disrupts communication locality) or require prohibitively many migrations (e.g., METIS). Diffusion-based methods are well-studied for independent task workloads, but not for the important class of long-lived, persistently communicating objects in over-decomposed runtime systems like Charm++. The key gap is a distributed, communication-aware diffusion strategy that simultaneously balances load and preserves communication locality—critical at strong-scaling limits where inter-node communication costs dominate. Methodology: The authors propose a three-stage distributed diffusion algorithm targeting Charm++'s over-decomposed object model. Stage 1 (Neighbor Selection): Each node constructs a neighbor graph based on actual inter-node communication volume rather than network topology; an iterative request/accept/confirm protocol produces K confirmed neighbors per node, where K is a user-tunable parameter. Stage 2 (Virtual Load Balancing): An iterative first-order fixed-point algorithm exchanges only load magnitudes between neighbors until neighborhood load variance drops below a threshold, computing ideal load transfers without yet selecting objects. Stage 3 (Object Selection): Objects are migrated preferentially to neighbors with which they already communicate (sorted by bytes communicated), preserving locality; when an object migrates, all of its communication partners update their neighbor tracking accordingly. A hierarchical extension refines migrations within each process across threads. A coordinate-based variant is also proposed for applications lacking explicit communication graphs: processors compute centroids of their objects and use spatial proximity as a proxy for communication affinity during neighbor selection and object migration. Key Results: In simulation across synthetic 3D stencil benchmarks (8–128 PEs), communication-aware diffusion (Diff-Comm) achieves max/avg load ratios of 1.06, 1.02, and 1.09 at 8, 32, and 128 PEs respectively—worse than GreedyRefine (1.00 always) and METIS (1.00 always), but comparable to ParMETIS (1.01, 1.04, 1.20). Critically, Diff-Comm's external/internal communication ratio (0.58, 0.173, 0.625) is far better than GreedyRefine (0.887, 0.422, 0.999) and competitive with or better than ParMETIS (0.637, 0.194, 0.602), while requiring far fewer migrations than METIS (18.9% vs. 87–99%). On the PIC PRK benchmark on up to 8 nodes of Perlmutter (NERSC), diffusion outperforms GreedyRefine at all node counts, with the gap widening at scale: at 8 nodes, diffusion achieves a 2x overall speedup over GreedyRefine and 7x over no load balancing. Diffusion achieves 2x lower maximum communication time per PE and 2.5x lower maximum computation time compared to GreedyRefine, attributed to superior communication locality preservation and more consistent load balance across iterations. Strengths: - Principled communication-aware neighbor selection directly from runtime communication graphs is novel for diffusion-based load balancing, and the design elegantly couples locality preservation with load redistribution in a distributed, scalable manner. - Practical applicability: the algorithm operates within Charm++'s existing infrastructure, requires no global synchronization, and the coordinate-based fallback variant extends it to applications without explicit communication graphs. - Convincing empirical advantage at scale: the 2x communication time speedup and 2x overall speedup over GreedyRefine at 8 nodes, with widening gap, suggests the approach becomes more valuable precisely where communication dominates—the strong-scaling regime most relevant to modern HPC workloads. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation scale is very limited: experiments reach only 8 nodes on a real machine and 128 PEs in simulation. Claims about scalability and superiority over alternatives remain speculative at the hundreds-to-thousands of nodes typical of production HPC runs. - The coordinate-based variant has a fundamental O(N log N) scalability bottleneck (each node sorts all processors by centroid distance), which the authors acknowledge but do not resolve, limiting its practical utility at scale without future work on spatial indexing. - Load balancing quality lags GreedyRefine and METIS significantly (e.g., 1.09 vs. 1.00 at 128 PEs), and the paper lacks analysis of when this residual imbalance materially limits application performance relative to the communication savings—making it unclear how to choose among strategies in practice.
Citations 2603.23329 PDF
#125 — Moderately Innovative (48)

The Workload-Router-Pool Architecture for LLM Inference Optimization : A Vision Paper from the vLLM Semantic Router Project

Huamin Chen, Xunzhuo Liu, Bowei He, Fuyuan Lyu, Yankai Chen, Xue Liu, Yuhan Liu, Junchen Jiang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The WRP framework provides a useful organizational taxonomy for a fragmented field and the workload identity signals are practically actionable, but as a vision paper synthesizing the authors' own 21 prior publications, it offers no new empirical validation of the cross-dimensional co-optimization thesis. Several quantitative claims are projections rather than measured deployment results, limiting the strength of the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Production LLM inference is shaped by three variables—the workload characteristics, the routing strategy, and the GPU pool architecture—yet these are studied by largely separate research communities that seldom interact. A routing policy tuned for one workload on one pool topology can be severely suboptimal for a different combination. As organizations shift from simple chat workloads toward agentic and multimodal applications, the cross-dimensional interactions become increasingly consequential: pool routing that reduces GPU instances by 17–39% depends on the workload's prompt-length distribution, and the same GPU fleet can vary 40× in energy efficiency depending on the context window served. No prior framework formalizes these interdependencies or derives research directions from them systematically. Methodology: The paper is explicitly a vision/position paper, not an empirical contribution. It synthesizes 21 prior publications from the vLLM Semantic Router (vLLM-SR) project into a unifying three-dimensional framework called Workload–Router–Pool (WRP). Workload is characterized along four axes: chat vs. agent, warm vs. cold, prefill-heavy vs. decode-heavy, and prompt-length CDF archetype (concentrated-below, dispersed, concentrated-above). The Router layer covers three adaptation regimes: static semantic rules (signal-driven dispatch, embedding similarity, safety classifiers), online bandit adaptation (feedback-driven re-routing from user satisfaction signals), and RL-based selection (for agents with sparse delayed rewards). The Pool dimension covers homogeneous vs. heterogeneous GPU fleets, disaggregated prefill/decode architectures, and KV-cache topology. The authors construct a 3×3 WRP interaction matrix, map their 21 prior papers onto it, identify sparse cells (particularly Router×Pool and Pool×Router), and propose 21 concrete research opportunities at the intersections, tiered by maturity: 6 engineering-ready (building blocks exist) and 15 research-tier (open questions remain). The paper also introduces workload identity signals—API-level metadata (tool definitions, session chaining fields, system-prompt fingerprints, bearer tokens) that enable zero-latency workload classification without content inspection. Key Results: As a vision paper, results are inherited from cited prior work rather than newly reported. Key quantitative claims from the vLLM-SR body of work include: token-budget pool routing reduces GPU instances by 17–39% on production traces; FleetOpt co-designing router compression with pool sizes yields 3.1–6.4% lower cost than retrofitting; the 1/W law shows two-pool routing gives ~2.5× better tokens/watt versus homogeneous, and these gains compose multiplicatively to ~4.25× when combined with GPU generation improvements; FastRouter achieves 98× router latency reduction with sub-800 MB GPU footprint; Adaptive VLM Routing (AVR) projects up to 78% cost reduction for computer-use agents within 2pp of an all-large-model baseline; the OATS tool-selection approach improves NDCG@5 from 0.869 to 0.940; and the Visual Confused Deputy security guardrail achieves F1=0.915. The 21 proposed opportunities span offline RL for session-length minimization, hallucination-driven model reputation routing, cumulative multi-turn risk scoring, failure-class-aware routing, runtime token-budget enforcement, joint tool–model optimization, KV-cache retention directives, pool-aware cascading, and a MAPE-K closed-loop meta-controller across all WRP knobs. Strengths: - The WRP matrix is a genuinely useful organizational contribution: it exposes which cross-dimensional interactions have been studied and which remain open, providing a structured research agenda for a fragmented field. - The workload identity signals section (Section 4.4) is one of the most practically actionable parts—bearer tokens, tool definition arrays, session chaining fields, and system-prompt fingerprints offer zero-latency workload classification that prior routing work has ignored entirely. - The 21 opportunities are concretely specified with feasibility analysis, primary barriers, and evaluation baselines, making them actionable rather than hand-wavy; the distinction between engineering-tier and research-tier is honest and useful. Weaknesses / Limitations: - As a vision paper synthesizing the authors' own prior work, it is inherently self-referential and lacks independent empirical validation of the WRP framework itself; the claim that co-optimization across dimensions yields the largest gains is asserted rather than demonstrated end-to-end. - Several of the 21 opportunities rest on projected or estimated benefits (e.g., 78% cost reduction for AVR is a projection, not a measured deployment result), and the paper does not always distinguish clearly between validated numbers and forward-looking estimates. - The framework is implicitly designed around the vLLM-SR architecture's specific capabilities (HaluGate, OATS, mmBERT classifiers, FleetOpt), which may limit generalizability to other inference stacks or multi-provider deployments where the router does not sit on the full request/response path.
Search Results 2603.21354 PDF
#126 — Moderately Innovative (47)

Unsupervised Anomaly Detection on Attributed Multiplex Heterogeneous Networks

link.springer.com · karypis - new results
Why this rank: AMG addresses a genuinely underserved setting by unifying heterogeneity, node attributes, and edge multiplicity in a single anomaly detector, and the PageRank-guided augmentation provides a reasonable inductive bias for structural anomaly detection. However, the core methodology—multi-channel GCN with contrastive learning and reconstruction losses—is a fairly standard combination of existing techniques, and the reliance on synthetic anomaly injection limits confidence in real-world generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Most graph anomaly detection methods assume homogeneous networks, but real-world systems (e-commerce, social networks, citation graphs) are better modeled as Attributed Multiplex Heterogeneous Networks (AMHENs): graphs with multiple node types, diverse node attributes, and edge multiplicity (multiple distinct edge types between the same node pair). The two core challenges are (i) jointly integrating all three AMHEN characteristics—heterogeneity, node attributes, and edge multiplicity—into a single detector, and (ii) comprehensively scoring diverse anomaly types: attribute anomalies (inconsistent node features), global structural anomalies (nodes forming the same clique across all sub-networks), and hierarchical structural anomalies (nodes forming cliques only within specific sub-networks). The closest prior work, AnomMAN, handles AMHENs but uses only plain reconstruction loss, missing multi-view signals needed for the full anomaly spectrum. Methodology: The proposed AMG (Augmentation-based Multi-channel Graph convolutional network) has three components. First, network decomposition splits the AMHEN into k sub-networks, one per edge type, sharing the same node set. Second, a hierarchical network augmentation module perturbs each sub-network by removing edges incident to high-importance nodes (ranked by PageRank or degree), producing augmented views that provide additional training signal and force the model to focus on node-intrinsic features rather than neighborhood co-occurrence. Third, a multi-channel GCN (R-GCN-style) processes each sub-network independently and aggregates embeddings via learned per-channel weights (Z = Σ αᵢ Hᵢ), capturing both local structure and cross-channel interactions. The integrated optimization combines four losses: structural reconstruction (L₂ between ZZᵀ and the summed adjacency), attribute reconstruction (L₂ between ZW_ar and X), augmented structural reconstruction (same as structural but on augmented views with shared weights), and an InfoNCE contrastive loss aligning original and augmented node embeddings. The final node anomaly score aggregates all four signals with a balance parameter γ. Notably, structural reconstruction omits sigmoid to naturally accommodate edge multiplicity values greater than 1. Key Results: AMG is evaluated on 10 benchmark configurations across 6 datasets (Yelp, Amazon, Alibaba, ACM, IMDB, SLAP), with global and hierarchical structural anomaly variants on the four injected datasets. Using AUC as the metric, AMG_PR (PageRank augmentation) achieves: Amazon 0.620, Yelp 0.612, Alibaba_G 0.923, ACM_G 0.895, IMDB_G 0.851, SLAP_G 0.960. The best prior method (AnomMAN) reaches 0.529/0.430/0.840/0.722/0.756/0.857 on those same datasets, meaning improvements of up to 23% over the best competitor. The abstract cites 17.4% and 20.6% improvements on Amazon and Yelp specifically. Ablation confirms every component contributes: removing the attribute anomaly score degrades average AUC most severely (from 0.897 to 0.738 on global structural datasets); removing the augmentation reconstruction loss particularly hurts balance between attribute and structural anomaly detection (average gap rises from 0.075 to 0.240). Per-epoch runtime (4.16s on Amazon) is moderate—slower than Dominant (1.01s) but faster than CoLA (9.91s) and Alarm (11.59s). The model converges stably within 120–160 epochs and is insensitive to γ (balanced near 0.5) but requires a high edge-removal ratio β ≈ 0.9 for effective contrastive separation. Strengths: - Addresses a practically important and underserved setting: AMHENs unify heterogeneity, node attributes, and edge multiplicity that prior detectors handle only partially. - The hierarchical augmentation design is well-motivated: PageRank-guided edge removal preferentially isolates hub nodes, which is precisely where structural anomalies (cliques) are injected, providing a natural inductive bias without supervision. - Comprehensive evaluation: 10 dataset configurations, 11 baselines spanning outlier methods, shallow networks, homogeneous GNNs, and one AMHEN-specific baseline, plus thorough ablations and hyperparameter sensitivity analysis. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The structural reconstruction Â = ZZᵀ has O(n²) complexity, which will be prohibitive for large-scale graphs; the authors acknowledge this but provide no mitigation beyond noting it as future work. - The anomaly injection protocol (clique insertion, attribute replacement by farthest neighbor) is standard but synthetic, and the two real-world labeled datasets (Yelp, Amazon) are spam-review networks—representativeness for other AMHEN domains is untested. - The model treats heterogeneity only through edge-type decomposition; it applies the same GCN formulation per sub-network without distinct node-type-aware transformations, which may underutilize node type information in networks with semantically very different node types.
Search Results
#127 — Moderately Innovative (47)

Template-Theorems Graph Construction to Enhance Mathematical Reasoning Capabilities of LLM

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The template-theorems knowledge graph provides a reasonable engineering solution to the theorem retrieval gap in mathematical reasoning, but the core idea of abstracting problems to templates for retrieval is an incremental refinement of RAG rather than a methodological departure. Absolute gains on strong models are modest (e.g., +1.0 on MATH500 for DeepSeek-R1), and the multi-LLM construction pipeline adds substantial uncharacterized cost.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs have achieved strong performance on elementary mathematical reasoning but continue to struggle with complex, advanced problems. The core issue is a retrieval-application gap: models acquire extensive mathematical theorem knowledge during pre-training but fail to retrieve and apply the right theorems when solving novel problems. Standard retrieval-augmented approaches using surface-level semantic similarity (cosine similarity, BM25) exacerbate this by matching superficially similar problems rather than structurally equivalent ones — a problem humans solve naturally by recalling analogous experiences and associated theorems from memory. Methodology: The paper proposes a two-component framework: (1) a template-theorems knowledge graph construction pipeline and (2) a graph retrieval-augmented generation mechanism. The knowledge graph stores abstract problem templates (generalized representations stripping away specific numerical/surface details) linked to relevant mathematical theorems. Construction proceeds in two stages: a Base Generation stage uses DeepSeek-R1 with few-shot prompts to extract templates and theorems from a seed dataset (MATH training set), and an Advanced Generation stage uses GPT-4.1 to synthesize new templates across difficulty levels independent of specific problems, boosting diversity and reducing single-model bias. Quality is enforced via answer validation (filtering hallucinated solutions against ground truth), consistency validation (comparing theorems generated from actual vs. template questions via embedding similarity threshold), step quality control (difficulty-conditioned step count filtering), and theorem matching against an authoritative library. At inference time, a query problem is first distilled into abstract templates offline, then the graph is queried via top-k similarity retrieval with a minimum threshold. Retrieved theorems are aggregated and refined into a theorem table, and the most compatible template is re-selected against that table before both are injected as context for the answering LLM. Key Results: Evaluated on five benchmarks spanning in-domain (MATH500) and out-of-domain (MMLU-Pro-Math, AMC10, AMC12, AIME24) settings with GPT-4.1, GPT-4o, and DeepSeek-R1. Against zero-shot and 8-shot CoT baselines: GPT-4.1 improves from 89.2/73.27/85.50/43.2 to 91.4/77.88/86.23/48.4 on MATH/AMC10/AMC12/AIME24. DeepSeek-R1 improves from 96.4/85.25/94.2/70.0 to 97.4/85.71/96.38/76.2. GPT-4o improves from 73.4/55.80/50.72/13.20 to 76.4/58.52/54.35/16.15. Gains are consistent across all models and notably present on OOD datasets. Ablations confirm each pipeline stage contributes meaningfully, with advanced generation being most critical for OOD generalization. A separate experiment shows that combining both template and theorem knowledge outperforms either alone (e.g., AMC10: +Tem=75.58, +The=76.50, +Both=77.88 vs. original 73.27). Efficiency analysis shows reduced inference cost time versus few-shot CoT, attributed to shorter, more targeted context. Strengths: - The problem-template abstraction layer as an intermediate retrieval step is well-motivated: it bridges the structural-similarity gap that pure semantic retrieval misses, and the ablation clearly validates its necessity. - Multi-stage quality control (answer validation, consistency validation, step count filtering, theorem matching) directly addresses LLM hallucination in the KB construction phase — a real and often ignored vulnerability in graph-from-LLM approaches. - Evaluation breadth is commendable: five datasets across difficulty levels, three distinct model families (GPT-4o, GPT-4.1, DeepSeek-R1), both in-domain and OOD settings, with consistent gains throughout. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The knowledge graph is seeded entirely from the MATH training set, and the claimed OOD generalization is still to datasets within the same mathematical reasoning genre (AMC, AIME, MMLU-Pro-Math) — genuine out-of-distribution transfer to different domains (physics, programming) is asserted but not demonstrated. - Absolute gains are modest on already-strong models: e.g., DeepSeek-R1 improves by +1.0 on MATH500 (96.4→97.4) and GPT-4.1 by +2.2. On AIME24 where reasoning is hardest and gains could matter most, the improvement is +5.2 for GPT-4.1 but only +1.5 for GPT-4o, suggesting the method's benefit is uneven and potentially correlated with the backbone's pre-existing theorem recall ability. - The pipeline depends on multiple LLM calls (DeepSeek-R1 for base generation, GPT-4.1 for advanced generation, GPT-4o-mini for evaluation), introducing cascading cost and API dependency that is not fully characterized; the reported efficiency improvement over few-shot reflects inference-time context length reduction but ignores KB construction overhead.
Recommended
#128 — Moderately Innovative (46)

MSR-Rec: Multi-Step Reasoning-Enhanced LLM for Sequential Recommendation

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: MSR-Rec's combination of reasoning distillation from a 671B model and bidirectional perplexity-based fusion is a practical engineering contribution, but each component draws heavily from established techniques (CoT distillation, LoRA fine-tuning, knowledge distillation). The narrow evaluation protocol (1,500 users, 6-item candidate sets) and circular reasoning quality evaluation substantially weaken the empirical claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Sequential recommendation systems based on LLMs typically formulate prediction as a single-step instruction-following task, relying on implicit pattern matching over user interaction histories. This approach fails to model the complex cognitive logic behind user decision-making, leading to cognitive biases and limited interpretability. Two critical gaps motivate this work: (1) recommendation datasets contain only implicit interaction records with no annotated multi-step reasoning chains to supervise LLM reasoning, and (2) the tension between reasoning generation (which prioritizes logical coherence) and recommendation prediction (which prioritizes item accuracy) creates optimization conflicts that prior work has not resolved. Methodology: MSR-Rec introduces a three-stage pipeline to activate and leverage multi-step reasoning in a fine-tuned small LLM (Llama3-8B). First, a task-decomposed reasoning chain is designed with six steps — user profile understanding, item feature analysis, matching logic reasoning, comparative differentiation, recommendation decision, and concise summary — to simulate the human decision-making process. This chain is used as a CoT prompt to elicit high-quality reasoning paths from DeepSeek-R1 (671B), followed by data pruning that filters paths for both correctness (predicted item matches ground truth) and step completeness. Second, the pruned reasoning data and behavior data are jointly used to fine-tune Llama3-8B via LoRA, with a weighted loss combining a reasoning loss (α=0.7) and a recommendation loss (β=0.3). Special delimiter tokens separate reasoning and recommendation outputs. Third, at inference, a bidirectional reasoning strategy generates recommendations independently from user-side prompts (preference-candidate matching) and item-side prompts (feature-user alignment), then adaptively fuses outputs using perplexity: when predictions agree, the lower-perplexity output is chosen; when they conflict, the higher-perplexity output (signaling more distinctive reasoning) is selected. Key Results: On three Amazon Review datasets (Electronics, Home & Kitchen, Health & Personal Care, each with 1,500 users), MSR-Rec achieves HitRatio@1 of 0.4453, 0.3453, and 0.4513 respectively, outperforming the strongest baselines (LLaRA and TALLRec) by 30.97%, 25.11%, and 6.94%. Gains are largest on the sparser Electronics and Home datasets, suggesting reasoning is especially beneficial when interaction signals are weak. On reasoning quality metrics (METEOR, BERTScore, GPTScore), MSR-Rec outperforms TALLRec and LLaRA substantially, but scores slightly below RecSAVER (which uses the full 671B DeepSeek-R1 at inference), confirming that the quality gap between a fine-tuned 8B model and a 671B model in reasoning generation persists. Ablation confirms that removing reasoning supervision drops HitRatio@1 by ~2–5 points and GPTScore by ~10–20 points, while using the full training-time reasoning chain at inference (MSR-Rec full) improves reasoning metrics but hurts recommendation accuracy — validating the design choice to use simplified bidirectional prompts at inference. Strengths: - The use of a large frontier model (DeepSeek-R1 671B) purely for offline reasoning data synthesis, then distilling into a small 8B model, is a practical and cost-effective design that avoids inference-time reliance on expensive models. - The bidirectional reasoning with perplexity-based adaptive fusion is a lightweight and principled way to reconcile conflicting user-side and item-side predictions without additional learned components. - The ablation study is thorough, isolating the contributions of reasoning supervision during fine-tuning vs. reasoning prompts during inference, and showing both are necessary. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation setup is narrow: only 1,500 users per dataset with a 6-item candidate set (1 ground truth + 5 negatives), which does not reflect realistic full-catalog ranking and inflates HitRatio@1 compared to standard evaluation protocols used in most RecSys work. - The reasoning quality comparison uses DeepSeek-R1's own outputs as the reference ground truth, which introduces circular evaluation bias — MSR-Rec was trained on these same paths, so the metrics partially reward memorization rather than genuine reasoning quality. - There is no analysis of computational cost or latency at inference time: bidirectional reasoning requires two separate LLM forward passes plus perplexity computation, which may be prohibitive for real-time recommendation compared to single-pass baselines.
Recommended
#129 — Moderately Innovative (46)

Co-design threading model and circuit cutting for static and adaptive quantum circuits: W. Cambiucci et al.

link.springer.com · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The HyPAQ framework fills a genuine gap by addressing adaptive quantum circuit partitioning alongside static circuits. However, the reduced FM partitioner is a straightforward variant of a classical algorithm, the adaptive circuit solution relies on manual developer annotations rather than automatic partitioning, and experiments are limited to simulation-based bipartitioning with simplified communication models—leaving the most challenging aspects of the problem unresolved.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Current NISQ quantum processors are severely limited by qubit counts, high error rates, and short coherence times. Distributed quantum computing (DQC)—executing a single algorithm across multiple QPUs—is a promising path to scalability, but requires partitioning large quantum circuits into subcircuits and coordinating their execution with minimal inter-QPU communication. Existing circuit cutting methods (wire cuts, gate cuts) and graph/hypergraph partitioning heuristics have been developed primarily for static quantum circuits with fixed, predetermined gate sequences. Adaptive (dynamic) quantum circuits—which modify their gate sequences at runtime based on intermediate measurements and classical control flow (IF/ELSE, WHILE, FOR)—present a fundamentally harder partitioning problem: the circuit topology evolves during execution, communication requirements vary in real time, and compile-time partitioning cannot capture all runtime branching. This gap motivates a unified framework addressing both circuit types. Methodology: The paper proposes three interconnected contributions packaged in the HyPAQ framework. For static circuits, a reduced variant of the Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM) hypergraph partitioning heuristic is introduced. Circuits are first translated into primal hypergraphs (qubits as vertices, multi-qubit gates as hyperedges) via one of two normalization algorithms: a standard QASM parser (Algorithm 1) or a pattern-aware expanded-hypergraph constructor that captures gate groupings and adaptive control structures (Algorithm 4). An incidence matrix is then constructed (Algorithm 2), and Algorithm 3—the core reduced FM partitioner—minimizes cut size (proxying inter-QPU communication cost) under explicit hard capacity constraints [Min_Size, Max_Size]. Key distinctions from classical FM include: restricting candidate moves to cut-frontier vertices, enforcing balanced or intentionally unbalanced partitions, circuit-aware hyperedge weights reflecting two-qubit interaction costs, and early stopping when cumulative gain is non-positive. For adaptive circuits, a co-design threading model is proposed: developers annotate OpenQASM 3 programs with `#pragma qpu_begin/end` directives that explicitly demarcate segments (threads) suitable for independent execution on separate QPUs. This eliminates the need for fully automatic partitioning of programs whose topology is runtime-dependent. Finally, a layered Quantum Resource Manager (QRM) architecture is proposed, spanning programming, compilation, ISA, resource management, thread execution, hardware abstraction, and hardware layers, that orchestrates partition assignment, dependency tracking, communication coordination, and QPU allocation across all circuit types. Key Results: For static circuits, experiments compared HyPAQ (reduced FM), classical FM, optimized KL (Kernighan-Lin), and Mid-Cut on VQE, QPE, and random benchmark circuits of 4–120 qubits generated via MQT Bench. HyPAQ and classical FM achieved the lowest EBIT costs across all circuit families; KL incurred higher EBIT costs especially for QPE and random circuits; Mid-Cut degraded with scale. On runtime, HyPAQ and FM scaled smoothly (consistent with O(E) linear complexity), while KL was substantially slower with spikes at larger sizes, and Mid-Cut traded quality for speed. For unbalanced bipartitions (75–25 split), reduced FM maintained lower partitioning time than KL across all tested sizes. For adaptive circuits, simulated experiments on 16–48 qubit random adaptive circuits (2–16 QPUs) showed execution time decreasing sharply from 2 to 8 QPUs with diminishing returns beyond that point. Fidelity improved almost linearly with QPU count under the tested conditions, as the per-QPU workload reduction outweighed cross-QPU communication penalties; however, the authors acknowledge a circuit-dependent 'sweet spot' would exist for larger, denser circuits. Strengths: - Addresses both static and adaptive circuit types in a unified framework, filling a genuine gap where prior hypergraph partitioning work assumed fixed circuit topologies - The reduced FM variant provides O(E) linear-time complexity versus KL's O(n³), with competitive or superior partition quality on all tested benchmarks - The expanded hypergraph construction (Algorithm 4) captures semantic gate groupings and adaptive control structures beyond raw pairwise qubit interactions - The QRM architecture is well-specified across full-stack layers and clarifies where each algorithm executes, making it actionable for implementors Weaknesses / Limitations: - All adaptive circuit experiments are simulation-based using a simplified uniform communication penalty model; no validation on real multi-QPU hardware is presented, making fidelity and timing results model-dependent rather than empirically grounded - The co-design threading model requires manual developer annotations, which is burdensome for complex adaptive programs and does not scale automatically; the paper acknowledges no direct comparison against automatic or random partitioning strategies is included - Experiments are limited to bipartitioning (two-way splits), with multi-way partitioning for k>2 QPUs addressed only abstractly in the QRM design and not benchmarked - The hyperedge weight model uses simple gate counts or aggregated costs; hardware-aware weights (noise profiles, coupling fidelities) are mentioned as future work but not evaluated
Search Results
#130 — Moderately Innovative (45)

The Evolution of Tool Use in LLM Agents: From Single-Tool Call to Multi-Tool Orchestration

Haoyuan Xu, Chang Li, Xinyan Ma, Xianhao Ou, Zihan Zhang, Tao He, Xiangyu Liu, Zixiang Wang, Jiafeng Liang, Zheng Chu, Runxuan Liu, Rongchuan Mu, Dandan Tu, Ming Liu, Bing Qin · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: A comprehensive and well-organized survey with a useful six-dimensional taxonomy and the most complete benchmark compilation (46+ entries) for multi-tool LLM orchestration. The formal cost-aware MDP formulation provides cleaner conceptual grounding than prior surveys. As a pure survey with no empirical validation or meta-analysis, novelty is inherently limited; safety and efficiency sections are notably thinner than inference/training coverage.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs are constrained by static parametric knowledge, hallucination risks, and inability to interact with external environments. While early tool-augmented LLM research (TALM, MRKL, Toolformer, ReAct) established that models can select and invoke individual APIs, this framing is no longer adequate. Real-world tasks require coordinating multiple tools over long trajectories with intermediate state, execution feedback, failure recovery, and constraints on safety, cost, and latency. The conceptual gap—between single-call correctness and multi-tool orchestration reliability—is the central motivation for this survey. A secondary gap is terminological: 'tool use,' 'tool calling,' 'workflow execution,' and 'orchestration' are used interchangeably in prior literature despite referring to distinct capability levels. Methodology: This is a 35-page literature survey, not a novel system. The authors provide a formal problem formulation (trajectory-based MDP with tool inventory, cost-aware objective) and then organize approximately 234 references into six analytical dimensions: (1) inference-time planning and execution (topological planning via DAGs/AND-OR graphs, state-space search, hierarchical planning, agent self-improvement via Reflexion and capability evolution); (2) training and trajectory construction (training-free retrieval methods, synthetic data synthesis following a Synthesis-Validation-Expansion framework, SFT for schema alignment and dependency planning, RL with specialized reward engineering for sparse-signal multi-step settings); (3) safety and control (parallel execution risks—race conditions, partial commits, state contamination—and sequential chain risks—context drift, memory poisoning, butterfly effects propagated through parameter reuse); (4) efficiency (latency reduction via parallel execution, asynchronous decoupling, speculative reasoning; cost reduction via dynamic tool retrieval, adaptive model routing, intelligent caching); (5) capability completeness in open environments (boundary perception, autonomous tool creation, open-environment adaptation via skill libraries); and (6) benchmark design and evaluation (taxonomized across 46+ benchmarks along four axes: topological complexity, temporal scale, dynamic environments, state persistence/self-correction). Key Results: The survey does not report experimental results but synthesizes empirical findings across surveyed works. Key observations include: (a) linear ReAct-style reasoning fails qualitatively for nested, parallel, or long-horizon tasks—graph-structured execution substantially outperforms flat token-sequence approaches; (b) benchmarks reveal steep performance degradation as horizon length increases, with AgentLongBench showing model failures in dynamic synthesis at 4M-token rollouts and UltraHorizon (400+ tool calls, 200K+ tokens) confirming severe context saturation; (c) τ²-bench shows >40% performance degradation in dual-control human-agent settings versus single-agent; (d) SFT alone (behavioral cloning) is insufficient for long-horizon generalization—RL with process reward design (PORTool, ToolRL, DeepAgent's ToolPO) is required for robust multi-step policy; (e) synthetic data quality now prioritizes systematic coverage of long-tail compositional patterns over raw scale; (f) safety risks in multi-tool systems are qualitatively different—cross-tool state contamination and cascading failures from MINJA-style memory injection or 'butterfly effects' cannot be addressed by single-step filters alone. Strengths: - The six-dimensional taxonomy is coherent and well-motivated; it connects inference, training, safety, efficiency, completeness, and evaluation in a unified framework rather than treating them as isolated sub-fields. - The formal problem formulation (cost-aware MDP with trajectory notation) provides precise conceptual grounding that prior surveys lack, enabling cleaner distinctions between capability levels. - The benchmark taxonomy (Table 2, 46 entries with 2023–2026 coverage, categorized by topology/temporal scale/environment type/state persistence) is the most comprehensive compilation of its kind and immediately useful as a reference. Weaknesses / Limitations: - As a survey, it lacks any empirical validation or meta-analysis of the claims it synthesizes; assertions about what 'works better' rely entirely on reported results from individual papers that may not be directly comparable. - The coverage skews heavily toward inference-time and training-time methods, while safety and efficiency sections are thinner and more descriptive than analytical—concrete quantitative comparisons across defense strategies are absent. - The paper acknowledges but does not resolve the open problem of how to jointly optimize the six dimensions; the interactions between, e.g., RL training and safety alignment, or efficiency optimizations and state consistency, remain largely uncharacterized.
Citations 2603.22862 PDF
#131 — Moderately Innovative (45)

Bridging" Nature" and" Spirit": The CRMhs Ontology for the Integration of Heritage Science and Cultural Heritage Data

preprints.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: CRMhs fills a genuine gap by providing Heritage Science with its own formal ontology that bridges natural-science analysis and humanistic interpretation within the CIDOC CRM ecosystem. The integration of CRMinf for argumentation modeling and empirical grounding in real laboratory workflows distinguishes it from purely theoretical proposals, though the lack of quantitative evaluation limits confidence in its practical superiority.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Heritage Science generates large volumes of heterogeneous data from diverse professional communities — conservators, chemists, physicists, archaeologists — using incompatible terminologies, formats, and storage conventions. The absence of a shared semantic framework causes fragmented knowledge, loss of provenance, and compromised reproducibility. Critically, no prior ontology addressed Heritage Science as an autonomous domain that uniquely combines rigorous natural-science analysis with humanistic interpretation. Existing models such as CRMsci handled scientific observations generically but lacked the domain-specific semantics needed to capture the full workflow from physical sampling through instrumental analysis to cultural interpretation. The paper also identifies a persistent bottleneck: scientific context is routinely lost during the transition from the laboratory to final publication. Methodology: The paper introduces CRMhs, a formal OWL ontology that extends CIDOC CRM (ISO 21127:2023) and reuses four existing CRM family extensions: CRMsci (scientific observations), CRMdig (provenance metadata), CRMinf (argumentation and inference), and CRMtex (ancient texts). The core design principle is a hybrid structure that introduces domain-specific classes for Heritage Science while directly reusing CIDOC CRM classes for time, place, and actors without redefinition. Key new classes include HS1 Scientific Activity (any heritage science operation), HS3 Analysis (measurement/observation subclass), HS6 Study Object (physical entities under investigation), HS7/HS8 Study Object Portion/Area (localized regions of interest with spatial coordinates), HS9 Study Object Sample (extracted discrete portions), HS11/HS12 Scientific Device/Component, HS13 Scientific Dataset, and HS14 Analysis Settings. Properties encode the full provenance chain: HSP4 analysed, HSP5 prepared, HSP16 produced dataset, HSP17 was taken from, etc. The ontology aligns with external vocabularies (Getty AAT, PeriodO, Pleiades, IMA Mineral List) to support terminological interoperability. CRMinf integration enables modeling of competing hypotheses, beliefs, and the evidential transition from raw data to interpretation. The model was developed empirically through laboratory datasets from Italian CNR, INFN, and Opificio delle Pietre Dure, and deployed in the ARTEMIS and 4CH European projects. Two archaeological case studies demonstrate the model: (1) lead isotope analysis (thermal ionization mass spectrometry) of a 4th-century BCE lead statuette to determine provenance, and (2) synchrotron X-ray fluorescence mapping of abraded Roman marble inscriptions to recover illegible text. Key Results: The paper is primarily a system/ontology contribution rather than an empirical benchmarking study; quantitative comparison against alternative ontologies is not performed. The key demonstrated outcomes are qualitative: (1) In the lead statuette case, CRMhs successfully encodes the complete analytical chain — physical sampling (HS9), chemical preparation (HS10), TIMS analysis (HS3), calibration with NIST SRM 981, production of an isotopic dataset (HS13), and argumentation via CRMinf that concludes provenance from Keos or Laurion rather than Corinth — thereby formalizing the tension between unverified antiquarian tradition and scientific evidence. (2) In the epigraphic case, CRMhs combined with CRMtex models the complete pipeline from synchrotron XRF area mapping (HS8 Study Object Area with HSP21 surface coordinates) through image generation (HS13 → E36 Visual Item) to text recognition (TX5) and grapheme-sequence reconstruction (TX12), recovering the reading "PopillIVS A(uli) L(ibertus)" from line 6 of CIL VI 12139. The ARTEMIS project adopted CRMhs as its reference ontology for Heritage Digital Twins, and the THESPIAN suite used it operationally for INFN-LABEC XRF and radiocarbon datasets. The ontology is publicly available via Zenodo (doi:10.5281/zenodo.18956182) and GitHub. Strengths: - The hybrid design — reusing CIDOC CRM entities without duplication while adding domain-specific classes — is principled and avoids ontological proliferation; this ensures native interoperability with the entire CRM ecosystem and major European infrastructure (E-RIHS, ARIADNE). - Integration of CRMinf for argumentation modeling is a notable differentiator: it allows explicit representation of competing beliefs, unverified provenance claims, and the evidential basis for interpretive transitions, which is rare in heritage ontologies. - Empirical grounding in actual laboratory workflows from CNR, INFN, and Opificio delle Pietre Dure, plus adoption in operational projects (ARTEMIS, 4CH, THESPIAN), provides stronger validation than purely theoretical ontology proposals. Weaknesses / Limitations: - No quantitative evaluation is presented: the paper does not measure query expressivity, data coverage, or interoperability against competing approaches (e.g., CHAMEO, OBOE) on any common benchmark dataset. Claims of superiority over prior models are entirely qualitative and self-referential. - The two case studies are illustrative rather than evaluative — both involve selective modeling of published third-party datasets rather than end-to-end deployment in a live repository, leaving scalability and completeness of coverage for the full Heritage Science spectrum (environmental monitoring, biological analysis, architectural survey) undemonstrated. - The paper is a preprint and not peer-reviewed, and the ontology itself is still described as an evolving construct requiring "a sustained period of further experimentation," which limits confidence in the stability of class definitions and property semantics for production use.
Search Results
#132 — Moderately Innovative (45)

Generative World Renderer (74 ▲ )

Zheng-Hui Huang, Zhixiang Wang, Jiaming Tan, Ruihan Yu, Yidan Zhang, Bo Zheng, Yu-Lun Liu, Yung-Yu Chuang, Kaipeng Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 3 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Generative World Renderer demonstrates that dataset quality alone can yield substantial performance gains without architectural changes — a valid and practically useful finding. The capture pipeline has clever engineering (dual-monitor mosaicing, metallic/roughness decoupling). However, the contribution is fundamentally a dataset paper restricted to two specific games, and the partially self-constructed evaluation benchmark risks optimistic bias.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Scaling generative bidirectional rendering (both forward: scene attributes → image, and inverse: image → scene attributes) to real-world scenarios is bottlenecked by the scarcity of large-scale, temporally continuous video data synchronized with high-fidelity G-buffers (depth, normals, albedo, metallic, roughness). Existing synthetic datasets suffer from limited scene complexity, static camera trajectories, simplified material models, and absence of adverse weather conditions. This causes a persistent domain gap: state-of-the-art models like DiffusionRenderer fail on complex reflections, dynamic objects, fine-grained details, and long video sequences. The paper addresses this data bottleneck directly rather than proposing a new model architecture. Methodology: The core contribution is a large-scale dataset of 4M frames (720p/30fps) curated from two AAA games — Cyberpunk 2077 (urban, metal-rich) and Black Myth: Wukong (natural, high-roughness) — with six synchronized channels (RGB + depth, normals, albedo, metallic, roughness). Data capture uses a non-intrusive three-stage pipeline: (1) G-buffer interception via ReShade hooks at the graphics API level, using RenderDoc for offline frame analysis to identify valid render targets through stable metadata invariants; (2) a dual-screen mosaic compositing strategy with OBS at near-lossless bitrate across two stitched 2K monitors to achieve 720p effective resolution per channel; (3) post-processing including camera-space normal reconstruction from depth via inverse projection, decoupled metallic/roughness rendering into spatially distinct screen regions to avoid inter-channel compression bleeding, VLM-based (Qwen3-VL-235B) annotation of scene/weather/motion/texture attributes, and synthesis of motion-blur variants via 8× RIFE frame interpolation averaged in linear space. The dataset is used to fine-tune DiffusionRenderer (Cosmos-based checkpoint) on 57-frame clips at 1280×720/24fps. For evaluation without ground truth, the authors propose a VLM-based assessment protocol using Gemini 3 Pro as judge, rating temporal consistency, spatial quality, and semantic plausibility via pairwise ranking. A forward rendering application fine-tunes Wan 2.1-T2V-1.3B with G-buffer conditioning for text-driven game style editing. Key Results: On the authors' own Black Myth: Wukong benchmark (39 held-out clips), fine-tuning on the proposed dataset substantially outperforms DiffusionRenderer across all modalities: depth AbsRel improves from 1.118 to 0.697, RMSE log from 0.723 to 0.430, δ<1.25 from 0.267 to 0.609; metallic MAE improves dramatically from 0.134 to 0.024; roughness MAE from 0.237 to 0.218. On the MPI-Sintel cross-dataset benchmark, depth RMSE improves from 0.268 to 0.220, depth δ<1.25 from 0.331 to 0.478, and albedo PSNR from 14.87 to 15.40 dB. VLM evaluation on 40 real-world videos shows consistent improvements in semantic, appearance, and temporal metrics. User study with 25 CG experts confirms VLM rankings, with 85%/75% expert agreement when VLM prefers the proposed method (metallic/roughness), and 70%/61% agreement when VLM prefers the baseline. Motion-blur augmentation consistently improves performance; a 113-frame fine-tuned variant substantially improves long-video temporal coherence. Strengths: - The data-centric framing is well-motivated and practically impactful: the paper demonstrates convincingly that a dataset contribution alone — without architectural changes — yields large, consistent performance gains across multiple benchmarks and evaluation paradigms. - The capture pipeline is technically clever and non-trivial: handling game-specific G-buffer packing formats, decoupling metallic/roughness channels to prevent compression artifacts, reconstructing camera-space normals from depth, and using dual-monitor mosaicing to achieve synchronized multi-channel capture without engine modification. - The VLM-based evaluation protocol for real-world videos fills a genuine gap — ground truth is unavailable for in-the-wild inverse rendering, and the strong correlation with expert judgment (70–85% agreement) provides meaningful validation of the approach. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The dataset is restricted to two specific AAA games under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, limiting commercial use and raising questions about generalization to domains not well-covered (e.g., interior scenes, non-urban natural environments, human-centric scenes); both games are cinematically lit and do not represent the full diversity of real-world capture conditions. - The evaluation benchmarks are partially self-constructed (Black Myth: Wukong test set created by the authors from their own capture), which risks optimistic bias; the only independent synthetic benchmark is MPI-Sintel, which is small and object-centric. - Normal quality on the held-out benchmark actually degrades slightly (mean angular error 45.01° → 42.57° is improvement, but Acc@11.25° worsens from 0.110 to 0.150 — worse precision), and the camera-space normal reconstruction from depth via finite differences is a known source of noise that the paper does not fully address.
Recommended 2604.02329 PDF
#133 — Moderately Innovative (45)

Do diacritics matter? evaluating the impact of arabic diacritics on tokenization and llm benchmarks

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Thorough and well-executed empirical study that fills a genuine gap in Arabic NLP by systematically connecting diacritization conditions to both tokenization behavior and benchmark performance across nine models. The finding that retaining wild diacritics often helps is practically useful. However, this is fundamentally a benchmarking study with no new methods or architectures proposed, and all benchmarks are zero-shot multiple-choice on MSA only.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Arabic diacritics are orthographic marks that convey phonological, lexical, and morphosyntactic information, but their prevalence varies widely across domains — dense in children's books, poetry, and religious texts, but nearly absent in news and user-generated content. While many Arabic NLP pipelines strip diacritics during preprocessing to sidestep orthographic variation, this practice has unknown consequences for modern LLMs. The paper addresses a critical gap: no prior work had systematically studied how varying degrees of diacritization affect subword tokenization and downstream benchmark performance across a broad set of Arabic LLMs. Methodology: The authors evaluate nine LLMs — five Arabic-centric (AceGPT-v2-8B, ALLaM-7B, Fanar-9B, Jais-13B, Jais-2-8B), three multilingual (Aya-Expanse-8B, Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen3-8B), and GPT-4o — under three controlled diacritization conditions: (i) all diacritics removed (undiac), (ii) naturally occurring diacritics preserved (wild), and (iii) fully diacritized text (full*) produced by a BERT-based morphological disambiguation system with 88.9% word-level accuracy. Evaluation uses three Arabic benchmarks: ArabicMMLU (14,455 samples), ArabCulture (3,482 samples), and AraTrust (522 samples), all run zero-shot. Tokenization analysis measures fertility (subwords per word), token density (characters per subword), tokenization consistency (% equal subword counts, Jaccard similarity), and layer-wise cosine similarities of word representations across diacritization conditions using the Wild2MaxDiacs dataset. Key Results: Fertility increases monotonically from undiac to wild to full across all tokenizers. The Jais tokenizers are worst-affected under full diacritization, reaching fertilities of 9.3–10.1 subwords/word vs. 1.9 undiacritized, approaching character-level tokenization because individual diacritic Unicode characters are absent from their vocabularies. ALLaM (57.5% Arabic vocabulary) and Fanar show the best tokenization robustness to wild diacritics (24.6% and 22.3% equal subword counts undiac-wild). Jaccard similarities confirm near-zero overlap under undiac-full for all models. Layer-wise cosine similarity analysis shows representations converge across layers due to anisotropy, but undiac-full divergence persists at lower layers. On benchmarks, removing wild diacritics has minimal and mostly statistically insignificant impact on performance. Full diacritization, however, consistently and significantly degrades performance across models: macro-average drops from 62.8 (wild) to 56.2 (full*) across benchmarks. Jais Chat (13B) suffers the most — 14.4 points on ArabicMMLU, 9.7 on ArabCulture, 26.2 on AraTrust under full vs. wild. GPT-4o is the most robust, with only a 0.7-point macro drop. Counterintuitively, retaining wild diacritics often yields equal or slightly better performance than stripping them, challenging the conventional preprocessing practice. Strengths: - First systematic, multi-model study connecting tokenization behavior to benchmark performance under controlled diacritization conditions, filling a documented gap in Arabic LLM evaluation. - Rigorous statistical testing (McNemar's test) to distinguish meaningful performance differences from noise, avoiding overclaiming. - Analysis spans both surface-level tokenization metrics and internal representation similarity, providing mechanistic grounding for observed performance degradation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is strictly zero-shot multiple-choice; it is unknown whether findings generalize to generation tasks, few-shot settings, or fine-tuned models where the model has seen diacritized text in context. - The full* condition relies on an automatic diacritizer with 88.9% word-level accuracy, introducing noise that conflates true diacritization effects with diacritization errors, potentially inflating the measured performance drop. - All three benchmarks are Modern Standard Arabic; the impact of diacritics on dialectal Arabic — where diacritization tools are far less mature and diacritic conventions differ — is entirely unexplored.
Recommended
#134 — Moderately Innovative (45)

DataFlex: A Unified Framework for Data-Centric Dynamic Training of Large Language Models (139 ▲ )

Hao Liang, Zhengyang Zhao, Meiyi Qiang, Mingrui Chen, Lu Ma, Rongyi Yu, Hengyi Feng, Shixuan Sun, Zimo Meng, Xiaochen Ma, Xuanlin Yang, Qifeng Cai, Ruichuan An, Bohan Zeng, Zhen Hao Wong, Chengyu Shen, Runming He, Zhaoyang Han, Yaowei Zheng, Fangcheng Fu, Conghui He, Bin Cui, Zhiyu Li, Weinan E, Wentao Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 3 Apr 2026
