#162 — Incremental (33)

Project Imaging-X: A Survey of 1000+ Open-Access Medical Imaging Datasets for Foundation Model Development (36 ▲ )

Zhongying Deng, Cheng Tang, Ziyan Huang, Jiashi Lin, Ying Chen, Junzhi Ning, Chenglong Ma, Jiyao Liu, Wei Li, Yinghao Zhu, Shujian Gao, Yanyan Huang, Sibo Ju, Yanzhou Su, Pengcheng Chen, Wenhao Tang, Tianbin Li, Haoyu Wang, Yuanfeng Ji, Hui Sun, Shaobo Min, Liang Peng, Feilong Tang, Haochen Xue, Rulin Zhou, Chaoyang Zhang, Wenjie Li, Shaohao Rui, Weijie Ma, Xingyue Zhao, Yibin Wang, Kun Yuan, Zhaohui Lu, Shujun Wang, Jinjie Wei, Lihao Liu, Dingkang Yang, Lin Wang, Yulong Li, Haolin Yang, Yiqing Shen, Lequan Yu, Xiaowei Hu, Yun Gu, Yicheng Wu, Benyou Wang, Minghui Zhang, Angelica I. Aviles-Rivero, Qi Gao, Hongming Shan, Xiaoyu Ren, Fang Yan, Hongyu Zhou, Haodong Duan, Maosong Cao, Shanshan Wang, Bin Fu, Xiaomeng Li, Zhi Hou, Chunfeng Song, Lei Bai, Yuan Cheng, Yuandong Pu, Xiang Li, Wenhai Wang, Hao Chen, Jiaxin Zhuang, Songyang Zhang, Huiguang He, Mengzhang Li, Bohan Zhuang, Zhian Bai, Rongshan Yu, Liansheng Wang, Yukun Zhou, Xiaosong Wang, Xin Guo, Guanbin Li, Xiangru Lin, Dakai Jin, Mianxin Liu, Wenlong Zhang, Qi Qin, Conghui He, Yuqiang Li, Ye Luo, Nanqing Dong, Jie Xu, Wenqi Shao, Bo Zhang, Qiujuan Yan, Yihao Liu, Jun Ma, Zhi Lu, Yuewen Cao, Zongwei Zhou, Jianming Liang, Shixiang Tang, Qi Duan, Dongzhan Zhou, Chen Jiang, Yuyin Zhou, Yanwu Xu, Jiancheng Yang, Shaoting Zhang, Xiaohong Liu, Siqi Luo, Yi Xin, Chaoyu Liu, Haochen Wen, Xin Chen, Alejandro Lozano, Min Woo Sun, Yuhui Zhang, Yue Yao, Xiaoxiao Sun, Serena Yeung-Levy, Xia Li, Jing Ke, Chunhui Zhang, Zongyuan Ge, Ming Hu, Jin Ye, Zhifeng Li, Yirong Chen, Yu Qiao, Junjun He · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 1 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Project Imaging-X is a valuable resource contribution cataloguing 1,000+ datasets with standardized metadata and a concrete fusion framework, but it is fundamentally a survey and indexing effort with no empirical validation — no model is trained, no hypothesis is tested. The MDFP framework, while useful, is descriptive taxonomy rather than methodological innovation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Medical imaging foundation models are severely constrained by data scarcity and fragmentation. While leading natural-image foundation models train on billions of samples (e.g., SA-1B: 1B images, LAION-5B: 5B images), even the largest public medical imaging datasets contain only hundreds of thousands of items. The landscape is highly fragmented across narrow, task-specific collections that overwhelmingly target a few modalities (CT, MRI) and anatomical regions (brain, chest, abdomen), leaving many clinically important areas underserved. Prior surveys of medical imaging datasets lacked image-level statistics, omitted recent large-scale datasets, and provided no systematic framework connecting dataset characteristics to foundation model requirements. This work addresses that gap with the most comprehensive census to date. Methodology: The authors catalogued 1,000+ open-access medical imaging datasets released from 2000–2025, drawing from TCIA, Grand Challenge, OpenNeuro, Kaggle, NITRC, Synapse, CodaLab, and GitHub. Datasets are organized by a three-tier taxonomy: imaging dimensionality (2D/3D/video) → modality → task and anatomical region. Each dataset entry is enriched with two JSONL files: data-meta.jsonl (16 standardized metadata fields including modality, anatomy, data volume, valid image count, label presence, license, and task type) and per-sample annotation files structured into four logical blocks (record, context, media_geometry, tasks). Volumetric data is standardized to NIfTI, 2D modalities to lossless 16-bit formats, and video to H.264/H.265 with companion JSON metadata. Building on this catalog, the authors propose the Metadata-Driven Fusion Paradigm (MDFP), a four-phase framework: Phase 1 (Metadata Harmonization) standardizes modality labels, dimensions, and anatomical hierarchies using UMLS/MeSH ontologies via semi-automated API + LLM alignment; Phase 2 (Semantic Alignment) maps ML task types (segmentation, classification, detection, regression) to their clinical correlates (lesion delineation, diagnosis, screening, biomarker quantification); Phase 3 (Fusion Blueprints) clusters datasets by modality-task-anatomy combinations and evaluates integration potential quantitatively by data volume, valid image counts, storage, and anatomical/task diversity; Phase 4 (Indexing) produces structured public metadata indices. All phases are implemented in a lightweight client-side Interactive Discovery Portal (static app on GitHub Pages) supporting rule-based JSON filtering and faceted search. Key Results: The survey documents clear growth inflections after 2012 (deep learning demand) and 2023 (foundation model adoption). 2D images dominate in scale; 3D volumes and video remain scarce due to acquisition cost, storage, and annotation complexity. By modality, pathology images are most numerous (WSI patch extraction multiplies counts), followed by CT, X-ray, and MRI (~10.4% of total images). Segmentation is the dominant task across all modalities. Brain, chest, and abdomen are the most represented anatomical regions; modalities such as PET, mammography, and endoscopy are substantially underrepresented. The largest individual sources are AbdomenAtlas (1.5M 2D CT images, 5,195 3D CT volumes) and CT-RATE (25,692 3D chest CT scans from 21,304 patients). A case study of goal-conditioned fusion via MDFP targeting a 2D CT/MR/Fundus foundation model assembled 57 datasets: CT (10 datasets, 1,173,965 images, labeled_ratio=1.0), MR (5 datasets, 681,025 images, labeled_ratio=1.0), Fundus (42 datasets, 280,311 images, labeled_ratio=0.952). The analysis identifies pronounced data gaps in multimodal paired imaging, 3D volumes, video, and rare-disease settings as priority areas for future collection. Strengths: - Unprecedented scale: the most comprehensive census of public medical imaging datasets to date (1,000+), covering 25 years of releases across all major modalities, tasks, and anatomies, with standardized metadata enabling systematic gap analysis. - Actionable integration framework: MDFP provides a concrete, reproducible pipeline (not just a taxonomy) for assembling goal-conditioned training corpora, with all phases operationalized in an open interactive portal. - Rigorous metadata schema: the 16-field data-meta standard, grounded in UMLS/MeSH ontologies with LLM-assisted harmonization, is more principled than prior ad-hoc catalogues and captures clinically meaningful distinctions (license, annotation granularity, hierarchical anatomy). Weaknesses / Limitations: - No empirical validation: the survey does not train or evaluate any foundation model on the assembled corpora, so the practical benefits of MDFP-fused datasets over existing baselines remain undemonstrated; the case-study statistics are descriptive, not experimental. - Task-annotation mismatch is acknowledged but unresolved: most catalogued datasets target classical CV tasks (segmentation masks, bounding boxes) that are misaligned with the clinical objectives (diagnosis, treatment planning) needed for truly useful foundation models, yet no re-annotation or transformation methodology is proposed. - Coverage gaps and recency bias: the survey relies on publicly accessible repositories and manual verification, so private institutional datasets, datasets behind paywalls, and very recent releases may be missed; rapidly evolving modalities (e.g., whole-slide imaging at full resolution, fluorescence microscopy, multi-parametric MRI) are likely underrepresented.
Recommended 2603.27460 PDF
#163 — Incremental (32)

Ontology -driven energy management in smart buildings: A comprehensive review of methodologies, tools, and challenges

doras.dcu.ie · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: A thorough PRISMA-style systematic review synthesizing 81 studies across building energy ontologies, but the contribution is primarily taxonomic. The comparative framework covering Brick, SAREF, SSN, and OEO is useful for practitioners, yet the review offers no quantitative meta-analysis or benchmarking to substantiate the value proposition of ontology-based approaches.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Building energy systems (BES) increasingly integrate IoT sensors, AI-driven control, demand response (DR), and digital twins, yet semantic interoperability remains a critical barrier to realizing their full potential. Heterogeneous data formats, incompatible communication protocols (e.g., Modbus/BACnet vs. MQTT/CoAP), siloed datasets, and the absence of shared vocabularies prevent seamless coordination across devices, buildings, and grids. Prior reviews have addressed narrow subfields — occupant behaviour, IoT sensors, or DR — without synthesizing the full landscape of ontology-based approaches across diverse BES contexts. This paper fills that gap with a holistic, cross-domain review. Methodology: The authors conducted a PRISMA-style systematic literature review across four databases (Scopus, IEEE Xplore, ScienceDirect, SpringerLink) covering 2011–2024. Using the search string (ontology OR ontologies) AND ("energy system" OR "energy storage" OR "energy management") AND building, they identified 997 articles, reduced to 663 after deduplication, and selected 81 primary studies through rigorous relevance screening. The review is organized around: (1) metadata trends (geographic distribution, scope, energy systems covered, tooling); (2) existing ontology landscape (Brick, SAREF/SAREF4BLDG, SSN/SOSA, OEO); (3) application domains (BEMS, occupant behaviour, fault detection, DR/energy flexibility, urban/district heating); (4) ontology languages and tools (OWL/RDF dominant at ~65% combined, Protégé used in ~53% of studies); and (5) advanced integrations (multi-agent systems, digital twins, AI/ML). The paper also proposes conceptual framework diagrams illustrating how heterogeneous data flows through ontology layers into intelligent applications. Key Results: Among the 81 primary studies, ~60% focus on building-level issues (HVAC, BEMS, occupant comfort), ~20% take multi-scale perspectives, ~16% address device-level control, and only ~4% cover grid-level problems. OWL is the dominant ontology language (37%), followed by combined OWL+RDF (28%). Protégé is the primary development tool (53%). European countries — particularly Germany, Austria, and Portugal — account for ~67% of publications. A comparative analysis of the four leading ontologies reveals clear complementary gaps: Brick excels at building operations but lacks IFC alignment; SAREF/SAREF4BLDG covers IoT devices but has limited spatial/control-logic modeling; SSN/SOSA is W3C-standard and extensible but too generic without domain extensions; OEO offers broad energy domain coverage but is weak on building-level device semantics. No existing ontology comprehensively covers the full variable set needed for real-time, predictive, occupant-responsive BES. Key application findings include: ontology-backed OWL fault detection (Li et al.) using Z-score semantic rules achieves automated anomaly interpretation; EFOnt (Li & Hong) enables SPARQL-queryable representations of energy flexibility KPIs; MAS frameworks (Anvari-Moghaddam et al.) use shared ontologies to coordinate DR across heterogeneous agents; and digital twin integrations (Akroyd et al., Lee et al.) demonstrate scalable, BIM-supported ontology-calibrated virtual building models. The review identifies recurring unresolved challenges: static occupant behaviour models, absence of consensus on flexibility metrics, limited energy storage coverage, and fragmented data governance. Strengths: - Broad, rigorous systematic methodology screening nearly 1,000 articles down to 81 primary studies with explicit inclusion/exclusion criteria, providing credible coverage of a fragmented field. - Integrative comparative framework covering all major ontologies (Brick, SAREF, SSN, OEO), tools, and languages with a structured table of limitations — more actionable than prior narrow reviews. - Clear articulation of three concrete future research directions: (1) holistic open ontologies capturing occupant behaviour + device constraints + grid signals; (2) extending schemas to multi-energy carriers and advanced storage; (3) aligning semantic models with AI-enabled predictive control. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The review is purely descriptive/taxonomic with no quantitative meta-analysis; no comparative benchmarking of ontology-based vs. non-ontology-based approaches on energy savings, fault detection accuracy, or DR performance metrics is attempted, leaving the actual value proposition empirically unsubstantiated. - The 81-paper corpus is relatively small for a 14-year window and four major databases, raising questions about selection bias; the paper does not report inter-rater reliability for article screening, weakening reproducibility of the inclusion decisions. - The proposed conceptual framework diagrams (Figs. 7–13) are illustrative rather than validated against real deployments, limiting their prescriptive utility for practitioners looking to implement ontology-driven BEMS.
Search Results
#164 — Incremental (31)

The AgriTrust Framework: Federated Semantic Governance for Trusted and Interoperable Agricultural Data Sharing

mdpi.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: AgriTrust competently integrates existing semantic web technologies (OWL, SHACL, ODRL, SPARQL) with blockchain and governance concepts for agricultural data sharing. The :ownedBy sovereignty property and CrossChainToken are reasonable design choices but not technically novel. Validation on 627 hand-crafted triples leaves scalability and real-world viability entirely unaddressed.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Agricultural data (AgData) is increasingly critical for global trade — regulations like the EU Deforestation-Free Regulation (EUDR) now require verifiable, traceable supply chain data as a prerequisite for market access. Yet a pervasive "AgData Paradox" prevents this potential from being realized: stakeholders recognize data sharing's value but are blocked by three interrelated barriers — a trust deficit (producers fear loss of data sovereignty and unfair value capture), technical fragmentation (heterogeneous formats and siloed platforms), and absent governance (no standardized rules for ownership, consent, or benefit sharing). Existing solutions address at most one barrier: isolated blockchain platforms ensure immutability but create new silos; centralized platforms offer governance within their walls but concentrate control; semantic ontologies provide shared vocabularies but lack executable enforcement. No prior framework integrates governance, semantics, and distributed infrastructure into a cohesive operational whole. Methodology: AgriTrust is a federated semantic governance framework built around four components. (1) A multi-stakeholder governance model with a consortium-based authority, explicit roles (data producer, consumer, platform provider, certifier), and four pillars: data sovereignty, machine-readable data contracts, equitable value sharing, and regulatory compliance. (2) The AgriTrust Ontology — a formal OWL 2 ontology with 25 core classes and 30 properties organized into four blocks: assets/tokenization, agents/governance, process/provenance, and observations/metrics. Key design choices include the :ownedBy property as a first-class sovereignty constraint, :DataContract linked to ODRL policies for machine-executable governance, :Certificate as a subclass of W3C Verifiable Credentials, and :CrossChainToken enabling blockchain-agnostic multi-ledger references. The ontology reuses PROV-O, SOSA/SSN, and GeoSPARQL, and is complemented by SHACL shapes for structural validation. (3) A blockchain-agnostic federated architecture where TaaS platform nodes synchronize against a central ontology registry, communicate via standardized SPARQL endpoints, and enforce data contracts via smart contracts or API gateways. (4) Validation via a Python pipeline executing OWL 2 RL reasoning, SHACL validation, and a battery of SPARQL queries against a hand-crafted proof-of-concept knowledge graph spanning coffee (EUDR compliance), soybean (mass balance with CrossChainTokens), agrochemical tracking (organic certification), and beef (individual animal traceability). Key Results: The validation pipeline expanded a base of 627 explicit triples to 2,010 triples after OWL 2 RL reasoning (+220%), demonstrating inferential richness. All 7/7 SPARQL query modules executed successfully, returning unified federated views across coffee, soy, and cattle assets. Assets were successfully distributed and queried across three simulated blockchain providers (Hyperledger Fabric, Ethereum, Polygon), confirming blockchain-agnostic operation. All 4/4 governance artifacts (2 certificates, 2 data contracts) passed SHACL validation with zero violations; a deliberately malformed contract correctly triggered two SHACL violations, demonstrating enforcement at ingestion time. OWL reasoning completed in 0.34 s and SHACL validation in 0.12 s on the 627-triple dataset (Intel i7, 16GB RAM), though the authors explicitly caution these figures are not performance baselines. The framework successfully modeled EUDR provenance chains, mass balance via CrossChainToken referential linking, agrochemical prohibition checking via SPARQL, and individual vs. collective asset duality in beef — all within a unified ontological model. Strengths: - The integration of data sovereignty as a first-class, machine-enforceable ontological property (:ownedBy) is technically novel and addresses the core trust gap that governance-only or blockchain-only approaches leave open. - The blockchain-agnostic design via :CrossChainToken and :BlockchainProvider decouples semantic identity from ledger implementation, enabling multi-jurisdictional asset registration without requiring atomic cross-chain execution — a practically sound scope decision. - Direct embedding of W3C ODRL and Verifiable Credentials into the domain ontology (rather than as external layers) makes governance artifacts queryable via SPARQL and validatable via SHACL, moving governance from policy text to auditable graph structure. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The validation dataset is a hand-crafted proof of concept with only 627 triples and 4 physical assets; scalability of OWL reasoning, SHACL throughput, and federated SPARQL latency at production scale (millions of assets, concurrent users) remains entirely untested and is deferred to future work. - Smart contract enforcement is not implemented — ODRL policies are validated structurally by SHACL but are never compiled to or executed on any actual blockchain (Hyperledger Fabric chaincode, Ethereum, etc.), leaving the "executable governance" claim unvalidated beyond semantic well-formedness. - The framework's viability depends on constituting a legitimate, well-funded multi-stakeholder governance authority with fair representation — a politically and economically complex requirement that is acknowledged but left entirely to future institutional work, making adoption dynamics highly uncertain.

#165 — Incremental (28)

Semantic Web Knowledge Graphs for Patient-Centred Healthcare: A Systematic Review and Design Framework

scitepress.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: A competent PRISMA-compliant systematic review that introduces a useful interpretability-scalability framework for organizing KG paradigms in healthcare. However, as a purely qualitative survey with no empirical contribution and an unvalidated design framework, the novelty is limited to taxonomic organization of existing work.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Healthcare data is vast, heterogeneous, and predominantly unstructured (>80%), spanning EHRs, clinical notes, lab results, imaging, and genomics. Existing analytical paradigms — statistical methods, ML, relational databases, and NLP — each address partial aspects of this complexity but fail individually: statistical models assume linearity and structured inputs; ML models are opaque black boxes; relational databases cannot natively integrate unstructured content; NLP is annotation-intensive and domain-fragile. No prior review had synthesized knowledge graph (KG) methodologies specifically through the lens of patient-centred applications, creating a gap in understanding how different KG paradigms trade off interpretability against scalability for clinical use. Methodology: Following PRISMA 2020 guidelines, the authors searched five bibliographic databases (PubMed, IEEE Xplore, Web of Science, Scopus, arXiv) through October 2025. Starting from 812 records, they applied strict inclusion criteria — requiring original empirical research that constructs or operationally uses a KG on patient-level data with a described evaluation protocol — arriving at 72 included studies. A representative subset of 28 studies spanning four application domains (patient similarity/classification, diagnostic support, personalized treatment/drug repurposing, operational insights such as infection tracking) was selected for detailed comparative illustration. The synthesis framework is two-dimensional, positioning KG approaches along interpretability and scalability axes, distinguishing three paradigms: (1) ontology/symbolic KGs (high interpretability, low scalability); (2) embedding-based KGs (low interpretability, high scalability); and (3) hybrid KGs (moderate on both). The paper is qualitative — no meta-analytic pooling is performed, citing metric heterogeneity across studies. Key Results: Across the 72 included studies, ontology-based KGs demonstrated strength in semantic traceability and guideline alignment, supporting chronic disease monitoring and infection tracking (e.g., Neo4j-based hospital infection surveillance). Embedding-based approaches scaled to high-dimensional biomedical networks and enabled drug repurposing discoveries (e.g., COVID-19 network medicine framework). NLP-driven pipelines successfully extracted structured knowledge from clinical text via entity recognition, relation extraction, ontology mapping, and graph database ingestion. Hybrid systems showed the most balanced trade-off. No aggregate quantitative synthesis (e.g., pooled AUC or F1) is reported due to metric and task heterogeneity. The paper produces a five-priority implementation roadmap covering standardized evaluation, scalability/real-time updates, semantic consistency, explainability/provenance, and prospective/external validation. Strengths: - Rigorous PRISMA 2020 methodology with dual-reviewer screening provides a reproducible, bias-minimized study corpus of 72 papers. - The interpretability-scalability synthesis framework is a useful organizing lens that clarifies the structural trade-offs across KG paradigms rather than treating them as interchangeable. - The implementation roadmap (Table 3) is practically grounded, mapping each priority to a minimal pilot scope, concrete evaluation metrics (AUC/F1, calibration, throughput, drift), and specific deliverables — more actionable than typical survey conclusions. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The synthesis is entirely qualitative; the stated rationale of metric heterogeneity is real but limits scientific rigor — no attempt is made to identify subsets with comparable metrics for even partial quantitative synthesis. - As a short-paper venue contribution (10 pages), the detailed comparative analysis covers only 28 of the 72 included studies, and the selection criteria for this subset are not fully transparent, introducing potential cherry-picking of illustrative examples. - The paper lacks original empirical contribution: it proposes a Neo4j-based patient KG system as future work but presents no experimental results, leaving the design framework unvalidated on actual clinical data.
Search Results
#166 — Incremental (28)

… Inference of 1.58-bit State Space Models: ARM NEON vs x86 AVX-512 vs GPU CUD: A Comparative Benchmark of BitMamba-2 with Optimized SIMD Kernels on …

engrxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Scores 28 as a straightforward systems benchmarking study that measures known ternary-quantized SSM inference across CPU platforms. The AVX-512 negative result on Cascade Lake is an honest and useful finding, but the work introduces no new algorithms or models. The titular GPU CUDA comparison was never actually performed, and conclusions rest on partial data from broken builds.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying large neural network models on non-GPU (CPU/ARM) hardware is increasingly important for edge, mobile, and cost-sensitive applications. Prior work (a companion paper by the same author) demonstrated that State Space Models (SSMs) with 1.58-bit ternary quantization achieve viable inference on ARM (Apple M1 NEON), but left two questions open: (1) how does x86 AVX2's wider SIMD (256-bit vs. 128-bit) compare to ARM NEON for ternary matmul throughput, and (2) how close is CPU SSM inference to GPU inference speeds? This paper provides controlled cross-platform benchmarks to answer these questions. Methodology: The paper benchmarks BitMamba-2 (255M and 1B parameter models, 1.58-bit ternary quantized) across six environments: ARM NEON on Apple M1 (from companion paper), x86 AVX2 under WSL2 (GCC 9.4, Intel i9-10980HK), x86 AVX2 Windows native (MSVC, same hardware), published native Linux AVX2 reference (Intel i3-12100F), native Linux scalar on pre-AVX2 hardware (Intel i5-3230M, Ivy Bridge, SSE4.2 only), and a first-party native Linux AVX-512 server benchmark (Intel Xeon Silver 4210R, Cascade Lake). For the Xeon, both the existing AVX2 kernel path and newly implemented native AVX-512 kernels (512-bit intrinsics across rms_norm, activation quantization, and ternary matmul) are benchmarked. The ternary matmul kernel exploits the AVX2 `_mm256_sign_epi8` single-instruction trick (32 int8 lanes); the AVX-512 port requires a 4-instruction mask-based replacement since `_mm512_sign_epi8` does not exist. Benchmarks use a fixed prompt, 50 and 200 generation tokens, and measure tokens/second, latency, and prefill time. O(1) memory is validated by comparing throughput across sequence lengths. Key Results: Native x86 AVX2 (i3-12100F reference) achieves ~146 tok/s (255M) and ~53 tok/s (1B), giving a 1.77–1.92× advantage over ARM NEON (82.5/27.6 tok/s), close to the theoretical 2× SIMD width ratio. The Xeon 4210R running the AVX2 code path reaches 113.1/47.6 tok/s. Critically, native AVX-512 kernels on the same Xeon are 18–31% slower than the AVX2 path (92.4/32.6 tok/s) due to Cascade Lake frequency throttling (~20–30% clock reduction on 512-bit instruction dispatch), absence of `_mm512_sign_epi8`, and port contention. WSL2 introduces a 3.1× overhead versus Windows native MSVC, which itself is 4.1× slower than native Linux GCC—decomposing the total 12.6× WSL2-to-native-Linux gap. The scalar fallback (no AVX2) on the i5-3230M yields only 2.42/0.58 tok/s, giving an AVX2-to-scalar ratio of 60–91×, far exceeding the theoretical 8× lane-width ratio due to combined effects of specialized instructions, hardware generation, and memory bandwidth. O(1) memory is confirmed across all platforms except the 1B model under WSL2 (−27.6% degradation attributed to virtualization memory pressure). Normalized throughput (tok/s per billion parameters) is 27–561 on CPU vs. 3–8 for cloud GPU APIs (Claude 3.5 Haiku, GPT-4o Mini), though the latter serve models 10–80× larger. Strengths: - Rigorous decomposition of the performance stack (virtualization, compiler/OS, ISA, SIMD width, hardware generation) using controlled same-hardware comparisons, yielding actionable engineering insight. - The AVX-512 negative result is a genuine controlled experiment on identical hardware—a rare, honest finding that wider SIMD is not universally beneficial and provides concrete guidance (keep AVX2 as default on Cascade Lake). - The scalar baseline on Ivy Bridge (first such measurement) quantifies the ~60–91× SIMD contribution to ternary SSM inference, establishing SIMD as the dominant deployment prerequisite. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The GPU CUDA baseline was never actually measured—blocked by environment incompatibilities (Python 3.8, CUDA 11.7, WSL2)—so the paper's title claim of "vs GPU CUDA" is unfulfilled; GPU comparisons rely entirely on third-party API throughput for models 10–80× larger, making the CPU-vs-GPU conclusion qualitative at best. - The Windows native MSVC benchmark produced valid results for only one of four configurations (1B/50-token); the other three terminated prematurely, so the 3.1× WSL2 penalty estimate rests on a single data point from a partially broken build. - Cross-hardware comparisons (e.g., i5-3230M scalar vs. i3-12100F AVX2) conflate hardware generation (~2013 vs. 2022) with SIMD effects, making it impossible to cleanly attribute the 60–91× ratio to SIMD alone; the paper acknowledges but cannot fully disentangle these factors. - The paper evaluates only one model family (BitMamba-2) with one quantization scheme (1.58-bit ternary) on models up to 1B parameters; generalizability to larger SSMs, other quantization formats, or Transformer-based models is not established.

#167 — Incremental (28)

Graph Pattern Mining for Anomalous Business Partnerships

scitepress.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The reframing of graph anomalies as business opportunities rather than threats is a mildly interesting perspective shift, but the technical approach combines standard tools (Leiden clustering, LOF) with hand-tuned domain-specific weights. Validation on a single tiny dataset (355 nodes, 421 edges) with no baseline comparisons and unvalidated capability scores makes the contribution difficult to assess.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Traditional methods for identifying valuable business partnerships rely on industry or geographic similarity, systematically overlooking non-obvious but high-value cross-sector collaborations. This paper addresses the gap between general-purpose graph anomaly detection (designed for fraud/intrusion) and the business intelligence need to surface unexpected yet strategically valuable partnerships. The reframing is notable: anomalies here represent opportunities, not threats. Methodology: The paper proposes three complementary graph mining algorithms applied to a business network G=(V,E) where nodes are companies and edges are partnerships. Algorithm 1 (Industry Ecosystem Anomaly Detection) runs Leiden community detection at five resolution levels (γ ∈ {0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0}), then scores each cross-community edge via a weighted combination of four domain-specific measures: competitive intensity dcomp (Porter's Five Forces, discretized to 0.9/0.6/0.2), capability complementarity dcap (resource-based view, five dimensions normalized to [0,1]), strategic similarity dstrat, and market overlap dmarket (Jaccard), combined as S = 0.4dcomp + 0.3dcap + 0.2dstrat + 0.1dmarket. Algorithm 2 (Strategic Complementarity Surprise) computes a surprise score = log((comp+ε)/(exp+ε)), where exp is estimated from historical partnership frequencies between industry-region pairs, highlighting partnerships with high capability complementarity relative to market expectations. Algorithm 3 (Competitive Context LOF) adapts classic LOF to four business-specific neighborhood types (competitive, geographic, strategic, ecosystem) with aggregation weights 0.35/0.25/0.25/0.15, flagging partnerships with LOF > 1.5 as outliers. The key design choice throughout is operationalizing established management theory (Porter, Barney's RBV, Teece's dynamic capabilities) directly as algorithmic distance measures rather than applying theory post-hoc. Key Results: The framework was validated on the Norwegian Boards Dataset: 355 public companies, 421 board interlock partnerships, network density 0.67%, 8 sectors. Algorithm 1 analyzed 36 cross-industry partnerships; mean anomaly score 0.477 (SD 0.125); top partnership (Aker Seafoods–Polaris Media, Food×Media) scored 0.801. Capability complementarity showed negative correlation with anomaly scores (−0.533), consistent with the hypothesis that complementary partnerships are rarer. Algorithm 2 yielded mean strategic value 0.413 (SD 0.062); Food×Media ranked highest (value 0.582, surprise 0.800). Algorithm 3 produced mean LOF 0.919 (SD 0.358); only one partnership exceeded the 1.5 threshold (DNB ShippingInvest–Norwegian Property, LOF 1.619). Cross-algorithm consistency—Aker Seafoods–Polaris Media appearing in top results of all three algorithms—is cited as evidence of robustness. No comparison to alternative anomaly detection baselines is provided. Strengths: - Principled integration of strategic management theory (Porter, RBV, dynamic capabilities) into the scoring functions rather than treating domain knowledge as a post-processing filter, yielding interpretable, theory-grounded anomaly scores. - Three complementary algorithms addressing distinct facets of the discovery problem (structural rarity, capability fit, competitive context), with cross-algorithm agreement as a validation signal. - Multi-resolution Leiden clustering (5 γ values) captures both broad ecosystem partnerships and niche collaborations in a single pass. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Tiny, single-country dataset (355 companies, 421 edges, 0.67% density) with no comparison to any baseline anomaly detection method; it is impossible to assess whether the domain-specific scoring actually outperforms generic graph anomaly detection or simple industry-dissimilarity heuristics. - Key inputs—capability scores across five dimensions—are approximated from public data with significant noise, missing-value imputation via industry medians, and no validation of the scores themselves; the entire scoring pipeline rests on this unvalidated foundation. - Scoring weights (0.4/0.3/0.2/0.1 in Algorithm 1; 0.35/0.25/0.25/0.15 in Algorithm 3) are set by theoretical intuition rather than learned or empirically optimized, and sensitivity analysis is absent. - The LOF results are weak: mean score 0.919 with only one partnership exceeding the 1.5 outlier threshold, suggesting Algorithm 3 adds limited discriminative power on this dataset.

#168 — Incremental (27)

Research on the Diagnosis of Abnormal Sound Defects in Automobile Engines Based on Fusion of Multi-Modal Images and Audio

mdpi.com · genai manufacturing - new results
Why this rank: This is a competent but standard combination of known feature extraction methods (WPD, MFCC, pruned ResNet18) with XGBoost classification. The PPE-CMA attention mechanism's theoretical grounding is weak — the 'energy conservation' justification for β = 1 − α is a conceptual stretch unsupported by ablation against unconstrained weights. Lab-only validation with synthetic noise injection and editorial issues further limit the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Automotive engine abnormal sound diagnosis is critical for predictive maintenance, yet single-modal methods (audio-only or image-only) suffer from low accuracy and poor noise robustness. Existing multi-modal fusion approaches rely on simple feature concatenation or deep end-to-end networks that fail to exploit the physical coupling between acoustic and visual fault signatures, introduce redundant features, and are too computationally heavy for on-board edge deployment. The paper also targets the small-sample problem endemic to industrial fault datasets and the lack of cross-engine generalization validation in prior work. Methodology: The proposed framework combines three components. First, audio features are extracted by fusing 5-level wavelet packet decomposition (WPD, 32-dim energy features) with 39-dim MFCC (13 static + 13 delta + 13 delta-delta), yielding a 71-dim vector. Image features (thermal imaging, vibration visualization, valve state) are extracted by three channel-pruned ResNet18 instances (30% L1-norm pruning, selected via ablation over 10–50% levels), each producing 512-dim vectors that are concatenated and PCA-reduced to 512 dims. Second, the Physical Prior-Embedded Cross-Modal Attention (PPE-CMA) module computes cosine similarity S between aligned audio vector A and image vector I, setting attention weights α = S and β = 1 − α, then fuses as F = α·A + β·I. The constraint β = 1 − α is motivated by an "energy conservation of fault information" argument: the two modalities are complementary, so higher audio relevance implies lower image weight and vice versa. Third, an improved lightweight XGBoost classifier (mutual information feature selection, L1 regularization, 0.8 column sampling) performs final classification. A hybrid data augmentation strategy (audio: time-domain noise addition at SNR 5–15 dB + frequency-domain stretching ±10%; image: random cropping on non-fault regions + same-class mixup) addresses small-sample conditions. The dataset consists of 18,000 samples across 6 fault states (including two composite faults) collected from a 1.5T gasoline engine at 3 operating speeds. Key Results: On the self-constructed standard dataset, the proposed method achieves 98.7% accuracy and 98.2% F1-score (AUC 0.991), outperforming single-modal MFCC-XGBoost (89.2%) by 9.5% and single-modal ResNet18-XGBoost (85.6%) by 13.1%. Against multi-modal baselines: +6.4% over feature-splicing fusion and +3.6% over CNN-LSTM fusion. It marginally exceeds the end-to-end MA-Net (98.5% acc, 32.4M params, 5.2 ms) while using only 8.7M parameters and 0.8 ms inference on NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX—13× faster than MA-Net. Under 90 dB noise the proposed method retains 96.5% accuracy versus 72.3% for audio-only. Under small-sample conditions (200 samples/class), hybrid augmentation yields 91.3% average accuracy vs. 78.3% without augmentation. Cross-engine validation (2.0T diesel, 1.2T range extender) and cross-domain validation (SEMFault-2024) yield average accuracies of 96.8%, 96.5%, and 97.4% respectively. Fuel efficiency improves ~5.6% and CO₂ emissions decrease ~1.1 kg/100 km after fault-guided maintenance. Strengths: - The PPE-CMA attention weight formulation is physically motivated and produces interpretable, fault-type-consistent weight allocations (e.g., α=0.62 for valve faults with strong acoustic signatures, β=0.65 for bearing faults with dominant vibration signatures), providing a principled alternative to unconstrained attention. - The hybrid design (hand-crafted audio features + pruned CNN image features + XGBoost) achieves accuracy on par with a 4× larger end-to-end network while running at 0.8 ms on edge hardware, making it genuinely deployable for on-board real-time diagnosis. - Thorough experimental coverage: 6 fault classes including composite faults, 3 noise levels, small-sample regime, ablation over 5 pruning levels, and cross-domain generalization on a public dataset—collectively providing a more complete evaluation than most prior work in this application area. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The core theoretical claim—that cosine similarity between learned feature vectors quantifies "physical coupling strength" between acoustic and visual fault manifestations—is asserted rather than rigorously derived. The β = 1 − α constraint conflates directional alignment in embedding space with energy conservation of physical information, which is a significant conceptual stretch that the paper does not adequately justify with ablation (e.g., no comparison against learned/unconstrained α and β). - The dataset is entirely self-constructed in controlled laboratory conditions with precisely calibrated fault parameters (e.g., valve clearance set to exactly 0.4 mm) and synthetic noise injected from a speaker; there is no validation under real in-vehicle operating conditions with naturally occurring faults, leaving the gap between lab performance (~98.7%) and real-world deployment uncharacterized. - The paper discloses use of MidJourney to generate the schematic diagrams and ChatGPT for literature analysis and English polishing, raising questions about the rigor and originality of the presented figures and literature positioning, and the fuel efficiency improvement claims (540–590% formatted as improvement rates in Table 5 appear to be data entry errors for ~5.4–5.9%) suggest inadequate editorial review.
Search Results
#169 — Incremental (25)

Efficient Inference of Large Vision Language Models

Surendra Pathak · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: A taxonomic survey organizing LVLM inference optimizations into four categories. While the taxonomy is reasonably well-structured and the open-problems section identifies genuine challenges, the paper contributes no original experiments, no quantitative comparisons, and no meta-analysis of trends, significantly limiting its actionable value beyond a literature map.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) face severe computational and memory bottlenecks that impede real-world deployment. High-resolution images and video inputs generate massive numbers of visual tokens, and the quadratic complexity of transformer self-attention makes processing these tokens prohibitively expensive. The decode phase compounds the problem: autoregressive generation repeatedly reads a linearly-growing KV cache from GPU high-bandwidth memory (HBM), creating memory-bandwidth bottlenecks and out-of-memory (OOM) failures. No prior work had systematically organized the fragmented literature on LVLM-specific inference acceleration across algorithmic, system, architectural, and decoding dimensions. Methodology: This paper is a survey that organizes LVLM inference optimization into a four-category taxonomy: (1) Visual token compression — image token pruning (FastV, SparseVLM, DivPrune, PyramidDrop, PruMerge) and merging (ToMe, VisionZip, PuMer), and video-specific spatiotemporal merging (ChatUniVi, LLaMA-VID) and dynamic multi-granular compression (HoliTom, FastVID, DyCoke, LongVU, FlexSelect); (2) KV cache and memory management — algorithmic KV cache selection (SnapKV, H2O, StreamingLLM), budget allocation (PyramidKV, DynamicKV, CAKE), merging (D2O, CHAI), block-wise memory paging (vLLM/PagedAttention), prefix-aware caching (SGLang/RadixAttention, ChunkAttention), and tiered heterogeneous storage (FlexGen, InfLLM); (3) Efficient architectural design — cross-modal projectors/resamplers (Flamingo, NVILA), sparse MoE (MoE-LLaVA, DeepSeek-VL2), and hardware-aware attention (FlashAttention 1/2/3); (4) Advanced decoding — multimodal speculative decoding (ViSpec, LANTERN, SpecVLM, DREAM) and early exit/layer skipping (AdaInfer). The paper also identifies open problems including the importance-diversity dilemma in token selection, streaming video constraints, attention-sink artifacts from rotary positional embeddings, MoE load imbalance, and the fundamental O(N²) complexity wall. Key Results: As a survey, the paper presents no original experimental results. It synthesizes findings reported across ~82 cited works. Qualitatively, it establishes that token compression methods (e.g., FastV) can deliver substantial FLOPs reductions with modest accuracy degradation on VQA benchmarks; PagedAttention nearly eliminates KV cache memory fragmentation; speculative decoding frameworks (e.g., ViSpec) achieve significant throughput gains on video workloads; and FlashAttention reduces attention memory complexity from O(N²) to O(N) in HBM usage. The survey notes that no unified benchmark enables direct cross-method comparison, which is itself flagged as a gap. Strengths: - Comprehensive and well-structured taxonomy covering both algorithmic and system-level optimizations, providing a useful reference map for researchers entering the field. - Correctly identifies the distinction between prefill-phase (compute-bound) and decode-phase (memory-bound) bottlenecks, and organizes techniques accordingly — a non-trivial organizational insight. - The open-problems section is substantive rather than perfunctory: the importance-diversity dilemma, streaming video causal constraints, and disaggregation overhead for large visual caches are genuine unsolved challenges. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper contains no original experiments, no quantitative comparison tables, and no unified evaluation — making it difficult to understand the relative effectiveness of covered methods or under which conditions each technique is preferred. - Coverage depth is uneven: seminal systems works (vLLM, FlashAttention, SGLang) receive thorough treatment while more recent methods (e.g., DREAM, ASAP — the latter being the author's own prior work, cited as [43]) are described superficially, raising concerns about objectivity and completeness. - As a single-author survey from a student with limited prior publication record, the work lacks the breadth of perspective that multi-author or community surveys bring, and the absence of any ablative analysis or meta-analysis of trends (e.g., accuracy-efficiency trade-off curves) significantly limits its actionable value.
Search Results 2603.27960 PDF
#170 — Incremental (25)

Leveraging Ontology -based Systems through Continuous Ontology Engineering

sol.sbc.org.br · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The six COE principles are a reasonable transposition of CSE/DevOps concepts to ontology engineering, and the BASF case study provides rare industrial evidence. However, the principles largely repackage established software engineering practices without rigorous analysis of where the analogy breaks down, and the single-organization qualitative study with author-employee bias limits generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Traditional ontology engineering (OE) methods — both prescriptive (SABiO, NeOn, METHONTOLOGY) and agile-iterative (eXtreme Design, SAMOD, AMOD) — fail to meet the demands of industrial settings where ontologies are built as components of continuously evolving information systems. In such contexts, requirements change frequently, time constraints are tight, and the ontology development process must stay synchronized with an agile/DevOps-driven system development pipeline. The disconnect between OE and system development leads to misalignment, wasted effort, and late deliveries. Prior work on Lean Ontology Development (Cummings and Stacey, 2018) and the COntE framework (Salamon and Barcellos, 2022) proposed process-level approaches but lacked foundational principles to characterize what Continuous Ontology Engineering (COE) actually requires. Methodology: The paper advances COE in two ways. First, it defines six foundational principles characterizing COE, drawn by analogy from Continuous Software Engineering (CSE): (1) Iterative Development — short time-boxed sprints with cross-functional teams delivering modular ontology increments; (2) Alignment between Development and Operations — applying DevOps/CI-CD to automate ontology build, test, and deploy (using tools such as ROBOT, OnToology, ODK, VoCol); (3) Alignment between Development and Business — involving domain stakeholders via use-case-driven backlogs and BizDev practices to avoid waste and overproduction; (4) Continuous Feedback and Experimentation — Build-Measure-Learn cycles to evaluate ontology representations and elicit ongoing user input; (5) End-to-End Flow — treating OE as a holistic process from business need conception through production deployment, not just development activities; and (6) Integrated Tool Support — requiring a coherent toolchain where outputs of one activity automatically feed the next. Second, the authors conduct a participative case study at BASF (the world's largest chemical company, >111,000 employees), where two of the authors are embedded practitioners. Nine ontology engineers at BASF were surveyed and interviewed about their existing OE process, which was then analyzed against the six COE principles. The BASF process encompasses four macro-steps: Requirements Specification (use cases + competency questions in a git-backed PM tool), Implementation (reference ontology → operational ontology via Draw.io/Chowlk, validated by domain experts with SPARQL/Hermit tests), Publication (automated documentation via WIDOCO, quality gates via ROBOT, deployment as ontology-as-a-service), and Maintenance (bug/issue tracking in git). Key Results: The analysis maps BASF's practices to COE principles and identifies gaps. BASF satisfies iterative development (sprint-based modular builds with expert-prioritized CQ backlogs), development-business alignment (use-case driven development with domain expert and system engineer participation), and development-operations alignment (CI/CD pipelines with automated verification, validation, and deployment). However, BASF does not yet implement continuous/automated feedback and experimentation — feedback is captured only at maintenance time and reactively. End-to-end flow is partial: automation gaps remain, strategy-budget alignment is not explicitly tracked, and ontology/system development plans are not formally coupled. Integrated tool support is incomplete — the pipeline covers implementation, testing, and publication well, but requirements management and conceptual modeling tools (project management tools, Draw.io) operate outside the integrated infrastructure, blocking full automation. Lessons learned include: comprehensive integrated tool support is necessary but hard to achieve due to the heterogeneity of artifact types (text, models, code); use-case definitions are essential for business alignment; planning must be rechecked each iteration; the reference-to-operational ontology transformation should be automated (e.g., via Chowlk/MDD) to enable continuous built-in quality; COE requires cultural change and top-level commitment, not just technical changes; and communication strategies must be deliberately designed for multi-stakeholder OE. Strengths: - The six COE principles provide a principled, coherent theoretical framework grounded in well-established CSE/DevOps/Lean literature, filling a genuine gap left by prior process-only frameworks. - The BASF case study offers concrete, practitioner-validated evidence from a large industrial deployment — rare in the ontology engineering literature — with specific tools, workflows, and artifacts described in sufficient detail to be actionable. - The paper honestly identifies where BASF falls short of COE ideals (continuous feedback, full end-to-end automation, integrated requirements tooling), making the lessons learned credible rather than self-promotional. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is entirely qualitative and descriptive — there are no quantitative metrics comparing pre/post COE adoption (e.g., delivery lead time, defect rates, stakeholder satisfaction scores), making it impossible to assess the magnitude of benefit. - Generalizability is severely limited by the single-organization case study, compounded by the inherent bias of two authors being BASF employees evaluating their own practices; the authors acknowledge this but offer no mitigation beyond planning future studies. - The COE principles are largely a direct transposition of CSE/DevOps concepts to the ontology domain without a rigorous analysis of where the analogy breaks down — the paper notes that continuous feedback is 'not usual in OE' but does not propose concrete mechanisms to address this fundamental structural difference between software and ontology artifacts.

#171 — Incremental (25)

Extending hypernetwork-based recommender systems for the cold-start problem using side information

dione.lib.unipi.gr · karypis - new results
Why this rank: A straightforward extension of an existing hypernetwork-based recommender with plot embeddings and demographic features, yielding marginal (+0.51%) or negative results. The honest reporting of null findings has some value, but the architectural modifications are shallow and the single-dataset evaluation limits the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The cold-start problem — generating accurate recommendations for users with little or no interaction history — remains a fundamental challenge in recommender systems. While side information (user demographics, item content) has been widely used in traditional systems, its effectiveness within meta-learning hypernetwork-based frameworks is understudied. Specifically, HyperRS (Lu et al., 2023), a recently proposed hypernetwork-based meta-learning recommender, was deliberately designed without user demographic features, leaving open whether such auxiliary signals can improve its cold-start adaptability. Methodology: This thesis extends HyperRS — which uses a hypernetwork to generate user-specific parameters for a base recommender network conditioned on a small support set of user-item interactions — with three variants. P-HyperRS adds SBERT-derived movie plot embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, 384-dim) as an extra item feature branch via an adapter layer, projector, and L2 normalization, injecting the plot signal into both the attention mechanism and the final item embedding. UD-HyperRS adds user demographic attributes (gender, age, occupation, zip-code) through a learned embedding lookup and a two-layer MLP, combining the resulting demographic embedding with the ID-based user embedding via element-wise addition. PUD-HyperRS combines both extensions. All models are evaluated on MovieLens-1M enriched with OMDB movie plots (98.87% retrieval success), under a cold-user/warm-item split (60/20/20, no user overlap), using binary NDCG@N as the metric. Two cold-start severities are tested: moderate (|S|=10, |Q|=10) and strict (|S|=5, |Q|=15). Key Results: Under moderate cold-start (|S|=10), P-HyperRS achieves NDCG@10=0.1922 (+0.51% vs. vanilla HyperRS at 0.1871), while UD-HyperRS (0.1842, −0.29%) and PUD-HyperRS (0.1793, −0.78%) both underperform the baseline. Under strict cold-start (|S|=5), all extensions degrade: vanilla HyperRS leads at NDCG@10=0.1620, UD-HyperRS is second (0.1510, −1.10%), and P-HyperRS is worst (0.1350, −2.70%). The combined PUD-HyperRS falls in between (0.1398, −2.22%). All HyperRS variants outperform ColdGAN (NDCG@5=0.1124) by 0.46–1.25%. AdaML (NDCG@5=0.8491) uses an incomparable evaluation protocol with much larger support sets and is not a fair comparison. Strengths: - Clean ablation design: three model variants isolate the contributions of item-side (plot), user-side (demographics), and combined information, enabling controlled causal inference about each signal type. - Practically motivated data pipeline: movie plots retrieved via OMDB/IMDb cross-referencing with 98.87% coverage; token analysis confirms no truncation by the 256-token SBERT limit, avoiding a silent data quality issue. - Honest, null-leaning results are clearly reported and interpreted — the finding that interaction-based signals dominate over auxiliary side information in hypernetwork frameworks is a substantive empirical contribution. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Single-domain evaluation: all experiments use MovieLens-1M exclusively, making generalization to other domains (e-commerce, music) entirely untested and uncertain. - Marginal and inconsistent gains: the best improvement is only +0.51% NDCG@10 and reverses under stricter cold-start conditions, raising doubts about whether the architectural additions provide any reliable benefit beyond noise. - Shallow user demographics: only static categorical attributes (gender, age, occupation, zip-code) are used; richer behavioral signals (click history, session data) that are more predictive of preference are not explored, likely underestimating the potential of user-side information. - Hyperparameter search is non-exhaustive and all variants share identical embedding sizes (64), potentially handicapping the extensions relative to the baseline by not allowing them to exploit the higher-dimensional input they receive. - Only two cold-start severities tested (|S|=5 and |S|=10); real-world cold-start often means |S|=1–3, leaving model behavior in the most practically relevant regime unexplored.

#172 — Incremental (24)

AI-Driven Cloud Security: AIOps for Threat Detection and Compliance

cisse.info · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: This is a literature survey with no original experiments; all quantitative claims are drawn from secondary sources of varying quality. The four-layer taxonomy provides modest organizational value, but the formal methodology framework is entirely unvalidated, and the poor reference quality (mismatched citations, gray literature) undermines its credibility as a systematic review.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern cloud and hybrid IT environments generate terabytes of telemetry daily across microservices, containers, and serverless functions, overwhelming legacy rule-based SIEM tools that produce excessive false positives and miss zero-day threats. In 2024, over 2,800 cloud-related security incidents were reported globally at an average cost of $4.5M per incident. Traditional periodic compliance audits are also increasingly impractical as infrastructures span multiple providers and regulatory regimes (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI DSS). This paper consolidates academic and industry research (2022–2025) on Artificial Intelligence for IT Operations (AIOps) applied to cloud security, aiming to provide a unified reference for researchers and practitioners. Methodology: The paper is a literature review combined with a proposed formal methodology framework. It traces the evolution of security operations paradigms from SIEM → SOAR → AIOps → LLMOps and proposes a four-layer taxonomy: threat detection, compliance, response, and governance. The technical contribution is a mathematical formulation of an end-to-end AIOps stack covering: (1) transformer-based temporal encoding of heterogeneous telemetry; (2) anomaly scoring via autoregressive likelihood, autoencoder reconstruction error, and contrastive prototype comparison, combined via a calibrated ensemble; (3) drift and adversarial poisoning detection using EWMA, KL divergence, and influence functions; (4) compliance scoring as weighted soft predicates over configuration state with cryptographic hash-chained evidence; (5) RL-based automated remediation modeled as an MDP/POMDP with reward balancing security loss, operational cost, and compliance penalty; and (6) differential privacy (DP-SGD) for training on sensitive telemetry. Explainability is addressed via integrated gradients for sequence models and GNNExplainer for graph models. Key Results: The paper cites empirical evidence rather than reporting original experiments. Key figures: AIOps reduces MTTD by ~45% (from 24 hours to 13 hours); audit preparation time is cut 40–60%; transformer-based log anomaly detectors achieve F1 > 0.90 on benchmarks including HDFS, BGL, Thunderbird, and OpenStack. Sector-specific claims: financial AI anomaly detection achieved 92% accuracy vs. 68% for rule-based systems on insider trading detection; healthcare automated compliance auditing reduced HIPAA violations by 55%; retail self-healing automation reduced downtime 35% year-over-year. Enterprise AIOps adoption reached 65% by end of 2025 with projections above 70% by 2026. AIOps adoption is currently ~30% of large enterprises with rapid growth projected. Strengths: - The four-layer taxonomy (detection, compliance, response, governance) provides a clear organizing framework for a fragmented literature and is the paper's most useful conceptual contribution. - The formal methodology section is unusually detailed for a survey, providing concrete mathematical formulations (MDP/POMDP, DP-SGD, influence functions) that bridge abstract concepts to implementable designs. - The comparative SIEM/SOAR/AIOps/LLMOps table with adoption statistics and capability trade-offs is practically useful for practitioners evaluating tool roadmaps. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper lacks original experiments; all quantitative claims are drawn from secondary sources, and a disproportionate share of key statistics (45% MTTD reduction, 40–60% audit savings, sector accuracy figures) trace back to a single IBM Cost of Data Breach Report [40] or are unverifiable aggregations, raising concerns about evidentiary rigor. - Reference quality is poor: many citations are preprints, gray literature, SSRN working papers, and book chapters with marginal relevance; several in-text citations are mismatched to their reference list entries, undermining the survey's credibility as a systematic review. - The proposed formal methodology is presented without any experimental validation, ablation, or comparison to baselines — it is an untested design proposal, not a validated system, yet is framed alongside empirical claims in a way that could mislead readers.

#173 — Incremental (24)

A comprehensive approach to building an intelligent system for proactive personnel risk assessment in critical infrastructure

bijournal.hse.ru · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The paper's identification of ontology-specific cyber threats (knowledge base poisoning, semantic sabotage) is a modestly novel contribution. However, the work is entirely conceptual with no prototype, no ontology schema, and no empirical validation — it describes requirements for a system rather than building one, and its cited accuracy figures lack methodological scrutiny.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Organizations in critical infrastructure sectors (energy, transport, finance, IT) face severe insider threat risks that traditional personnel screening methods cannot adequately address. Fragmented checks — resume review, interviews, reference calls — fail to detect complex, multi-stage threats because they lack semantic integration across heterogeneous data sources (biographical, behavioral, financial, digital). According to Russian statistics cited, 45% of companies encounter incidents caused by unreliable personnel, with 22% resulting in catastrophic consequences. The paper argues that the solution is not another isolated screening tool but a holistic methodology for designing proactive, AI-driven risk assessment systems — one that simultaneously satisfies technological, legal, and cybersecurity requirements. Methodology: The paper proposes a multi-layer assessment framework built around ontological modeling as the semantic integration backbone. The methodology synthesizes three components typically treated in isolation: (1) ethical-legal analysis — addressing fairness, privacy, the right to explanation under GDPR and Russian Federal Law 152-FZ, with requirements that ontologies exclude irrelevant personal attributes and that hiring decisions remain human-supervised; (2) cybersecurity architecture — classifying unique threat vectors against the ontology itself (knowledge base poisoning, semantic sabotage by insiders, exfiltration of the relational graph), and prescribing Security by Design countermeasures including blockchain audit logs, RBAC modeled within the ontology, encrypted storage with tokenization, and modular partitioning of sensitive vs. public data; (3) comparative analysis of existing assessment technologies — document verification (60–68% accuracy, $8–15/candidate), psychometric testing including MMPI-2 and Big Five (75–80%, $18–50), AI analysis via NLP/computer vision (85–90%, $50–100), and polygraph (81–91%, $70–110). The paper argues that none of these methods is used in integrated form currently, and that an ontology-based semantic layer is required to combine them into a predictive rather than reactive system. Key Results: The paper's contributions are conceptual rather than empirical — no system is implemented or benchmarked. The main outputs are: (1) a taxonomy of ethical-legal constraints on ontology design for candidate screening; (2) a classification of novel cyber threat vectors specific to centralized ontological knowledge bases (ontology compromise, contextual data leakage, insider abuse of modification rights); (3) a comparative table of existing assessment methods with accuracy and cost figures drawn from literature and market data; (4) a set of architectural requirements (Security by Design, Privacy by Design, XAI explainability, cross-expert validation for ontology updates). Cited figures include: IBM Watson reducing resume contradiction errors by 40% (93% date inconsistency detection); HireVue predicting candidate reliability at 78%; MMPI-2 identifying 80% of candidates with criminal tendencies in one MIT study; US CLEAR program reducing government hiring risk by 40%. Strengths: - Genuine synthesis across three domains (technology, ethics/law, cybersecurity) that are rarely addressed together in the insider-threat screening literature - The identification of ontology-specific cyber threats — knowledge base poisoning, semantic sabotage, contextual leakage — is a novel and practically important contribution absent from most prior work - Actionable architectural guidance (blockchain audit logs, RBAC-via-ontology, modular partitioning, tokenization) is concrete enough to inform system design Weaknesses / Limitations: - Entirely conceptual: no prototype, no ontology schema, no empirical validation of the integrated framework — the paper describes requirements for a system rather than building or evaluating one - Cited accuracy and cost figures are drawn from disparate third-party sources (IBM, MIT, HireVue, Checkster, Deloitte) without methodological scrutiny, making quantitative comparisons unreliable; the provenance and replicability of several statistics are questionable - The ontological modeling approach is declared central to the methodology but its internal structure is explicitly deferred to future work, leaving the key technical contribution undefined

#174 — Incremental (23)

The well-being-enhanced READINESS model: a human-centric extension for risk and crisis communication in generative AI contexts

emerald.com · trust and safety genAI - new results
Why this rank: This purely conceptual paper extends an existing crisis communication framework by adding well-being as a transversal dimension, an integration that is well-established in occupational health and organizational psychology. The absence of any empirical validation, testable propositions, or formal model specification, combined with potential construct redundancy with the base model's existing emotional intelligence components, limits the novelty to a domain-specific reframing rather than a new theoretical mechanism.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Crisis communication frameworks for navigating generative AI (genAI) risks have focused on operational preparedness and technical resilience while systematically neglecting the psychosocial toll on communication professionals. The existing READINESS model (Jin et al., 2024, 2025) — built around mindset, multilevel efficacy, and dynamic process — addresses cognitive and emotional adaptability but leaves well-being implicit rather than structurally embedded. Meanwhile, genAI adoption in high-stakes communicative functions (PR, crisis response, corporate messaging) introduces compounding stressors: cognitive overload, deskilling anxiety, ethical dissonance, surveillance fatigue, role ambiguity, and moral injury. These are not peripheral conditions — they directly shape whether professionals can maintain ethical clarity, adaptive leadership, and coordinated response. The gap is thus theoretical (no crisis communication model treats well-being as a constitutive input) and practical (organizations lack frameworks for embedding psychosocial support into READINESS infrastructure). Methodology: This is a purely conceptual paper. The authors synthesize three bodies of literature — (1) genAI risk perception research (notably Wang et al., 2025, an empirical study of European communication professionals), (2) perceived organizational support (POS) and well-being scholarship (Anton, 2025), and (3) crisis communication theory including the Communication Theory of Resilience (CTR) and situational crisis communication theory — to propose a revised framework. The extension introduces well-being as a fourth, transversal dimension that cuts across each of the original three READINESS pillars: mindset receives emotional sustainability (stress regulation, burnout resistance, metacognitive awareness); multilevel efficacy receives psychological safety (relational trust, POS, voice-enabling environments); and dynamic process receives ethical reflexivity (ongoing moral sensemaking, communicative deliberation). These three well-being sub-dimensions are theorized as sequential mediators converging on ethical resilience — the sustained capacity to engage morally complex crises without disengagement or ethical fatigue — which in turn enables holistic READINESS. The framework is visualized in a single conceptual diagram (Figure 1) and accompanied by a research agenda table (Table 1) with 12 empirical questions organized into four clusters. Key Results: There are no empirical findings; the paper's contribution is entirely conceptual. The key outputs are: (1) a revised model architecture in which well-being is upstream rather than downstream of crisis performance; (2) a clear distinction between ethical READINESS (anticipatory capacity to engage ethical complexity) and ethical resilience (sustained maintenance of that capacity under prolonged pressure), clarifying a conceptual ambiguity in the original framework; (3) a policy argument that professional associations (IPRA, CIPR, PRSA, etc.) and senior leadership should be accountable for the emotional and ethical strain borne by communicators, not only for reputational risk; and (4) a differentiated account of well-being needs across employment contexts (freelancers vs. organizationally-embedded professionals, different career stages, gender). Strengths: - Addresses a genuine and undertheorized gap: the original READINESS model's silence on well-being is a real structural weakness, and the extension is logically coherent — each well-being sub-dimension maps cleanly onto its corresponding pillar. - The distinction between ethical READINESS (anticipatory orientation) and ethical resilience (sustained capacity) is conceptually precise and useful for future operationalization. - The research agenda (Table 1) is specific and actionable, covering measurement, governance, education, and role-level effects — providing a credible scaffold for empirical follow-on work. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is entirely non-empirical and offers no testable propositions, hypotheses, or formal model specification — it is a framework proposal without validation, making it difficult to evaluate whether the theorized mediating chain (emotional sustainability → psychological safety → ethical reflexivity → ethical resilience → READINESS) holds causally or whether the constructs are genuinely distinct from existing efficacy and mindset constructs already in the base model. - The conceptual contribution is modest in novelty: integrating well-being into organizational readiness frameworks is well-established in occupational health and organizational psychology; the paper's claim of originality rests primarily on the specific application to genAI crisis communication contexts rather than on any fundamentally new theoretical mechanism. - The framework does not address measurement or boundary conditions — it is unclear how emotional sustainability differs operationally from the emotional intelligence already embedded in the original READINESS mindset pillar, nor does the paper grapple with potential redundancy or collinearity among the three well-being sub-dimensions.
Search Results
#175 — Incremental (22)

Semantic Mapping and Cross-Model Data Integration in BIM: A Lightweight and Scalable Schedule-Level Workflow

mdpi.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Addresses a genuine practical gap in BIM schedule-level integration, but the core technique is keyword matching and string normalization — decades-old methods. The contribution is methodological formalization of existing practice rather than technical innovation, validated only on a single clean toy project.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: BIM schedule data exported from discipline-specific models (structural, architectural, HVAC/MEP) uses inconsistent naming conventions, parameter structures, and semantic hierarchies, creating persistent information silos that block automated querying, cross-model aggregation, and schedule-level analytics. Existing approaches — rigid rule-based templates, ontology frameworks (IFC, OWL), and learning-based element classifiers — either lack scalability, require large labeled training sets that are rarely available, impose prohibitive setup overhead, or focus on element-level geometry rather than the tabular schedule abstraction that practitioners actually operate on. The schedule-level integration problem (working on exported CSV tables rather than native BIM geometry) has received little systematic methodological treatment despite being pervasive in day-to-day engineering coordination, RFI resolution, and quantity takeoff workflows. Methodology: The paper proposes a five-stage, deterministic pipeline operating entirely on CSV-format schedule exports, making it independent of any specific BIM authoring tool. The core innovation is the introduction of a customizable "Category–Subcategory–Family" three-level semantic hierarchy, where a user-defined subcategory layer bridges standardized top-level categories (e.g., Revit's official Category) and project-specific Family labels. Stage 1 performs lossless schedule export and cleaning (deduplication, blank removal, field standardization with selective merging of synonymous fields like "Structural Material/Material"). Stage 2 applies hierarchical classification via keyword matching and string normalization (lowercasing, delimiter removal, abbreviation expansion) configured through a versioned CSV/JSON mapping table — no trained ML model is used at any stage; AI/NLP tools are mentioned only as optional aids for initial mapping-table configuration. Stage 3 runs automated consistency checking to detect many-to-many Family–Subcategory conflicts, flagging them for human review. Stage 4 generates batch statistics and visualizations via Python/pandas/matplotlib. Stage 5 performs cross-model aggregation (e.g., merging opening and wall schedules to compute per-floor opening-to-wall ratios). The workflow is validated on the publicly available "Snowdon Towers" Revit sample model, spanning structural (1,191 elements), architectural (1,367 elements), and HVAC (1,090 elements) disciplines chosen to represent high-, medium-, and low-heterogeneity conditions respectively. Key Results: Sample-based validation (200 structural, 150 architectural, 50 HVAC records, stratified across element categories and building levels, reviewed by two independent domain experts) showed mapping agreement rates of >95%, >96%, and ≈100% respectively against human-validated reference classifications. Automated processing times were 8, 7, and 2 minutes versus manual times of 90, 60, and 20 minutes — yielding effort reductions of ~91%, ~88%, and ~90%. Cross-model aggregation successfully produced per-floor opening counts, opening areas, wall areas, and opening-to-wall ratios without bespoke integration scripting, demonstrating that once semantics are normalized, downstream metrics can be recomputed automatically as project data evolves. The HVAC case confirmed that the pipeline can auto-detect when normalization is unnecessary, avoiding redundant processing for already-standardized domains. Strengths: - Addresses a real, underserved gap: schedule-level BIM integration is ubiquitous in practice but almost entirely ad hoc; formalizing it as a reusable, versioned pipeline with explicit boundary conditions is practically valuable. - Lightweight and accessible: entirely deterministic, no training data or GPU infrastructure required, operates on commodity CSV exports from any BIM platform, and is accessible to non-programmers via Excel-editable mapping tables. - The customizable subcategory layer is a concrete, transferable design pattern — it can be independently versioned, updated as standards evolve, and reused across project runs without modifying native BIM models. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The algorithmic contribution is minimal: the core technique is keyword matching and string normalization — a decades-old approach. The paper's value is methodological formalization, not technical novelty, yet the framing sometimes overstates the contribution as if it were a new system. - Validation is limited to a single, clean, publicly available toy project (Snowdon Towers); no testing on real-world projects with messier, more heterogeneous naming or larger scale is provided. The 85%+ time-savings claim lacks a rigorous controlled baseline — the "manual time" figures are averages from a small sample and may not generalize. - The mapping-table configuration step still requires substantial upfront manual effort per project, and the paper largely glosses over how labor-intensive defining the initial subcategory taxonomy is for a new project type or naming convention.
Search Results
#176 — Routine (17)

Ontology -driven personalized information retrieval for XML documents

Ounnaci Iddir, Ahmed-ouamer Rachid, Tai Dinh · arxiv.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: A straightforward combination of well-established techniques — TF-IDF weighting, ontology-based concept hierarchies, and cosine similarity — applied to XML document retrieval. The evaluation on a self-constructed dataset designed to match the system's own ontology creates strong circularity, and the absence of any modern IR baseline comparison makes the results uninterpretable in context.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Traditional information retrieval systems return identical results to all users issuing the same query, ignoring differences in knowledge, goals, and preferences. Existing personalized IR work either exploits XML structure without deep semantic user modeling, or builds user profiles without leveraging ontology-based concept weighting and the hierarchical granularity of XML documents. Additionally, most prior systems focus on the profiling phase without mechanisms for continuous profile adaptation as user queries evolve. This paper targets the intersection of these gaps: fine-grained, personalized retrieval over semi-structured XML documents using a domain ontology. Methodology: The system represents XML document nodes, user queries, and user profiles uniformly as weighted concept vectors derived from a domain ontology. The ontology (based on the OBIREX e-learning ontology for computer science) organizes knowledge into seven hierarchical levels: Domain, Path, Field, Element, Script, Concept, and Granule. Concept weights are assigned by depth: deeper (more specific) concepts receive higher weights via a linear margin formula WR(Ck) = W_AVG + Δ × (Coef(Ck) − Coef_AVG).
