Document indexing uses a TF×IEF×WR scheme where the ontology weight WR modulates standard TF-IDF-style term weighting. Index construction propagates concept weights bottom-up through the XML tree, weighting contributions by inverse structural distance from leaf text nodes to ancestor element nodes, multiplied by the user interest vector. Relevance scoring uses cosine similarity for text nodes and a pertinence function for element nodes that boosts nodes with many relevant descendant text nodes. User profiles are initialized with uniform weights over ontology concepts and updated after each query via an exponential reinforcement rule: wCI_j(t+1) = (e^{w_{tj}} − 1) + wCI_j(t). The system is implemented as a Java/Servlet web application using OWL and the Jena API. Key Results: Experiments used a custom dataset of 400 XML documents and 300 queries in the computer science domain (4 subdomains). Against a non-weighted baseline (no ontology weights, no user profile), the proposed system improves substantially: averaged over 5 queries for the same user, precision rises from 0.426 to 0.710 and recall from 0.756 to 0.978. For the multi-user experiment across 4 users and 8 query instants, the proposed method achieves mean R = 0.864, P = 0.716, F1 = 0.781. Each user profile correctly aligns with stated domain interests — for example, User1 (databases) achieves F1 = 0.889 on database queries, User4 (networks) achieves F1 = 0.947 on network queries. Performance is stable across instants and the profile update mechanism demonstrably reinforces domain-specific weights over repeated queries. Strengths: - Coherent integration of ontology concept weighting, XML structural indexing, and dynamic user profiling into a single vector-space framework with clearly defined formulas throughout. - The depth-based concept weighting scheme (favoring specific leaf concepts) is well-motivated and mechanically consistent — the total ontology weight is guaranteed to sum to 1. - Evaluation tracks per-user, per-instant precision/recall over time, making the personalization effect explicitly visible rather than collapsing everything into a single aggregate. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is conducted on a self-constructed, small-scale dataset (400 documents, 300 queries) that the authors built specifically to match their ontology. This creates a strong circularity: the documents were organized according to the same ontology used for indexing, which trivially advantages the ontology-based system and makes the results hard to generalize. - There is no comparison against any competitive IR baseline beyond the authors' own earlier (2014) unweighted system. Modern neural or BM25-based XML retrieval systems are entirely absent, making it impossible to assess where this approach stands relative to the current state of the art. - The profile update rule (exponential of query concept weights) lacks theoretical justification or sensitivity analysis — it is unclear how results would change with different update functions or what happens when user interests shift abruptly rather than accumulating steadily.
Search Results 2603.21139 PDF
#177 — Routine (17)

Targeting JAK/STAT Signaling with Cold Atmospheric Plasma: A Potential Therapy for Systemic Lupus Erythematosus

cambridge.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This is a narrative review proposing a speculative therapeutic hypothesis (CAP for SLE via JAK/STAT) without any original experimental data or SLE-specific evidence. The fundamental physical mismatch between localized surface plasma and systemic autoimmune disease is unresolved, and the heavy self-citation pattern suggests promotional framing rather than balanced evidence synthesis.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a chronic autoimmune disease affecting ~3.4 million people worldwide, predominantly women of childbearing age (9:1 female-to-male ratio), with highly heterogeneous multi-organ manifestations. Current therapeutics — antimalarials, corticosteroids, immunosuppressants, biologics, and CAR-T therapies — carry significant toxicities (retinopathy, infections, organ toxicity), elicit heterogeneous responses, and can paradoxically induce drug-induced lupus. The pathogenesis involves a complex crosstalk among many signaling pathways (IFN, TLR, NOD, PI3K/AKT, MAPK/ERK, mTOR, JAK/STAT), obscuring a clear therapeutic target. This review argues that the JAK/STAT axis is uniquely positioned as a master regulator across all SLE pathogenic stages and proposes cold atmospheric plasma (CAP) as an innovative modality to target it. Methodology: This is a narrative review, not an original experimental study. The authors propose a three-stage conceptual framework for SLE pathogenesis — (I) immune hyperactivation, (II) cytokine imbalance, and (III) impaired debris clearance — and map 21 known SLE-associated signaling pathways (derived from a published GWAS/sequencing study of 958 patients and 1026 controls) onto this framework. They argue that JAK/STAT is the sole pathway spanning all three stages, with specific JAK/STAT member assignments: STAT1/STAT2/STAT5 relay type I IFN signals (Stage I); STAT4/STAT6 modulate Th1/Th2 cytokine balance (Stage II); STAT3 participates in complement-mediated debris deposition (Stage III). The authors then survey clinical trials of JAK inhibitors (upadacitinib, filgotinib, baricitinib, ruxolitinib, tofacitinib, deucravacitinib) and STAT inhibitors (fludarabine, artesunate) for SLE. Finally, they propose CAP — ionized gas generating reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (RONS) including OH•, O2•, H2O2, NO, and ONOO⁻ — as a candidate therapy based on its known dose-dependent modulation of the JAK/STAT axis and immune cells, citing indirect evidence from cancer and other autoimmune disease contexts. Key Results: This review presents no new experimental data. Key findings from cited literature include: upadacitinib (JAK1 inhibitor) reduced flares in a phase II trial (NCT03978520) over 48 weeks; filgotinib achieved ≥5-point CLASI-A improvement in 68.8% of moderate-to-severe cutaneous lupus patients; baricitinib (JAK1/2) failed to meet primary endpoints in phase III trials (SLE-BRAVE-I and II) and did not receive FDA approval for SLE; deucravacitinib (TYK2 inhibitor) demonstrated disease severity reduction in a phase II trial without major safety signals; tofacitinib showed phase I efficacy and tolerability. For CAP specifically: CAP suppressed JAK/STAT3 signaling in epithelial cells; activated JAK2/STAT1 in macrophages in melanoma; inhibited Th17 differentiation in vitro; reduced NF-κB and IL-6 in rheumatoid arthritis fibroblasts; and treated vitiligo and psoriasis. A critical dose-dependency is noted — 2-minute argon CAP induced pro-oxidant state in rat plasma while 1-minute exposure stimulated antioxidant capacity. No SLE-specific CAP experiments are reported. Strengths: - The three-stage SLE pathogenesis framework is a useful organizational contribution that systematically maps disparate signaling pathways and explains why JAK/STAT is uniquely cross-cutting. - The clinical trial summary table (Table 1) is comprehensive, cataloguing 20+ trials across 8 JAK/STAT-targeting agents with outcomes, providing a current reference snapshot. - The identification of dose-dependent, context-sensitive CAP effects as a theoretical basis for immunosuppressive (vs. immunostimulatory) action is scientifically grounded and highlights a meaningful mechanistic distinction. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The core proposal — that CAP can treat SLE via JAK/STAT modulation — rests entirely on indirect, heterogeneous evidence from cancer models and unrelated autoimmune diseases; no SLE-specific CAP data exist, making the hypothesis speculative. - CAP is inherently a localized surface therapy, while SLE is a systemic disease affecting kidneys, heart, and CNS; the proposed workaround (plasma-activated infusions, extracorporeal circuits) is entirely hypothetical and lacks any feasibility or safety data in vivo. - The paper has a notable self-citation pattern, with a substantial fraction of references (Refs. 32–41, 99, 122, 131–134, etc.) being the corresponding author's own prior CAP work, raising concerns about selective evidence curation and potential promotional framing of the technology.
Citations
#178 — Routine (17)

Detection Method of Abnormal Text Information in Social Networks Integrating GUIDE and NOTICE

journals.riverpublishers.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: This paper presents a straightforward combination of a graph autoencoder and BERT-based classifier with a simple linear fusion mechanism, despite framing it as a novel 'dual-attention' approach. The proprietary dataset, unrelated padded references, unnamed evaluation platforms, and unsubstantiated deployment claims collectively indicate minimal scientific rigor. The actual technical contribution — sigmoid-gated score averaging — is routine.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Social networks generate vast quantities of abnormal text — fake news, spam advertising, hate speech, and online violence — that challenge automated detection. Existing approaches fall into two one-dimensional camps: semantic methods (BERT-based) that miss propagation-topology anomalies, and graph neural network methods that lack deep textual understanding. The paper argues that adversarial content increasingly exploits this gap, using semantic camouflage to evade content filters while spreading through structurally anomalous patterns. The proposed framework aims to fuse both dimensions for more robust detection. Methodology: The framework combines two co-designed modules. The GUIDE (Graph-based User Interaction and Diffusion Evaluation) module performs structural anomaly detection using a graph autoencoder architecture: node features are encoded via normalized GCN layers (spectral graph convolution), reconstructed, and anomaly scores assigned based on reconstruction error. Network motif analysis (Z-score significance testing) identifies statistically over-represented subgraph patterns in abnormal vs. normal conditions. The NOTICE (Natural Language Ontology-driven Textual Anomaly Classification Engine) module maps text to a predefined ontology of anomaly categories using BERT-based semantic projection (similarity-weighted sum over ontology concept vectors), multilingual BERT averaging for cross-lingual unification, and ontology rule validation. Fusion occurs via a dual-attention mechanism: GUIDE produces a motif-weighted anomaly score (Eq. 8), NOTICE produces a semantic score, and these are combined with a sigmoid-gated cross-attention (Eq. 9), with final probability computed as a linear combination Pabn(v) = α·AbnScore'(v) + (1-α)·Ssemantic(v). The heterogeneous network models three node types (users, content, publishers) with three motif types. Key Results: Evaluated on a 2.35M-post dataset from "ZN Lab" (Twitter + Weibo) with SMOTE-balanced classes and 70/15/15 splits, GUIDE-NOTICE achieves F1 of 91.2% (fake news), 89.7% (junk advertising), and 88.3% (hate speech), outperforming TextGCN (85.7%, 83.4%, 82.6%), BERT-CNN (82.3%, 76.5%, 79.1%), and GAT (78.6%, 81.2%, 73.4%) by 5.5–17.8 percentage points. AUC is 0.886 vs. 0.842/0.786/0.723. The model has 12.5M parameters, 3.2 GFLOPs per inference, 76 ms latency, and ~645 MB peak memory — substantially better than GAT (25.4M params, 155 ms, 2100 MB). Ablation confirms synergy: removing NOTICE drops Macro F1 from 89.7 to 82.0 (hate speech drops 15.9 pp); removing GUIDE drops it to 84.6 (junk advertising drops 13.9 pp). Deployment testing claims 89.4% accuracy processing 230M items/day, reducing manual review by 43%. Strengths: - Addresses a real and practically important problem (multi-type abnormal content detection at scale) with a plausible dual-module architecture - Ablation study is included and demonstrates complementary module contributions, with task-specific patterns (GUIDE better for spam, NOTICE better for fake news, fusion best for hate speech) - Efficiency metrics (645 MB memory, 76 ms latency) are competitive, and the model is parameter-efficient relative to GAT Weaknesses / Limitations: - The "ZN Lab" dataset is not publicly available, not properly cited, and appears to be proprietary or fabricated — reproducibility is impossible, and the extreme class imbalance (only 4.3% normal content) is atypical and inflates anomaly detection F1 scores - The fusion mechanism (Eq. 14) is a simple linear combination — the very "superficial score averaging" the paper claims to transcend; the "dual-attention" framing in Eq. 9 reduces to a sigmoid scalar gate, not a genuine cross-modal attention mechanism - Several references are completely unrelated to the paper's topic (e.g., [1] chatbot conversation design, [2] text-to-image diffusion, [14] traffic ramp control, [15] IoT cluster head selection), suggesting reference padding or fabrication to cite the journal itself - The cross-platform generalization tests use unnamed "Platform D" and "Platform E" with no characterization, making it impossible to assess the scope of generalization claims - The 230M items/day deployment claim is presented without any experimental methodology — it appears to be extrapolation rather than actual system benchmarking - Authors' primary expertise is e-commerce and information management, and the paper lacks the depth of implementation detail (hyperparameters, GNN layer counts, training procedure) expected of credible ML systems research
Search Results
#179 — Routine (15)

ONTOLOGICAL DIMENSIONS OF FOLKLORISM IN THE POETRY OF MATNAZAR ABDULHAKIM

claritasconference.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: An essayistic literary analysis that applies established Western theoretical frameworks (Lotman, Jung, Frye) to Uzbek poetry without citing specific poems, quoting textual evidence, or engaging Central Asian literary scholarship. The hermeneutic elasticity concept is suggestive but undeveloped. Claims are unverifiable without a defined corpus.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: This paper addresses how folklorism operates in the poetry of Uzbek poet Matnazar Abdulhakim — specifically, whether folkloric elements function as superficial thematic borrowing or as a deeper structural and semantic framework. The motivation is to move beyond descriptive accounts of folklore influence in literature and toward an ontological, semiotic, and philosophical analysis of how traditional material is transformed into modern poetic language. The gap addressed is the lack of rigorous theoretical treatment of Uzbek literary folklorism within broader frameworks of mythopoetics and cultural memory studies. Methodology: The paper employs a multi-framework qualitative literary analysis drawing on cultural semiotics (Lotman), archetypal criticism (Jung, Frye), hermeneutics, and cultural memory theory (Assmann, Erll). The author examines recurring symbols (soil, fire, wind, ancestral land), temporal structures (cyclical vs. linear time), landscape imagery, and heroic archetypes across Abdulhakim's poetry. No primary corpus is formally defined — the analysis is essayistic and illustrative rather than systematic. The approach situates Abdulhakim within global modernist strategies of mythic method while arguing for the distinctively Uzbek cultural grounding of his work. Key Results: The paper argues that folklorism in Abdulhakim's poetry functions across four simultaneous dimensions: ontological (shaping perception of reality), semiotic (encoding cultural memory through recurrent sign networks), ecological (landscape as ontological partner rather than backdrop), and ethical (heroism recast as internal moral struggle). The cyclical temporality of folklore coexists with modern linear fragmentation, producing philosophical tension rather than nostalgia. The hero archetype is internalized from epic collective figure to lyrical subject of spiritual perseverance. Symbolic minimalism — few images carrying high semantic density — is identified as a distinctive aesthetic feature paralleling folkloric narrative economy. No quantitative results are presented; all findings are interpretive. Strengths: - Applies a coherent multi-framework theoretical apparatus (semiotics, hermeneutics, archetype theory, cultural memory) rather than treating folklorism descriptively - The concept of "hermeneutic elasticity" — symbols stable enough to preserve tradition yet open enough for new readings — is a genuinely useful analytical contribution - Situates a Central Asian poet within broader comparative and global theoretical debates, arguing for universal resonance without erasing cultural specificity Weaknesses / Limitations: - No formal corpus is defined: specific poems are never cited, quoted, or analyzed in detail, making all claims unverifiable and the analysis essentially assertion-based - The paper reads more as a theoretical essay than an empirical literary study; the abstract promises "analysis" and "findings" but delivers only generalizations unsupported by textual evidence - Heavy reliance on secondary Western theoretical sources (Jung, Frye, Lotman) with no engagement with Uzbek or Central Asian literary scholarship, creating a mismatch between the object of study and the analytical lens
Search Results
#180 — Routine (14)

Adapted Ontology Development Process: A Co-Simulation Scenario Ontology for Energy Systems

tib-op.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: A brief extended abstract introducing a handful of top-level co-simulation concepts into the Open Energy Ontology. The contribution is extremely narrow, with no quantitative evaluation, no competency question testing, and critical domain elements deferred to future work.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Co-simulation is a key methodology for analyzing complex multi-domain energy systems, yet there is no shared semantic framework for describing, sharing, comparing, and reproducing simulation scenarios across heterogeneous frameworks. Without such a framework, interoperability between co-simulation tools (e.g., VILLASframework, DaceDS, mosaik) is limited, and scenarios cannot be easily exchanged or reused within research data infrastructure. The paper addresses this gap by developing a co-simulation scenario ontology integrated into the Open Energy Ontology (OEO) within the NFDI4Energy national research data initiative. Methodology: The authors follow the Linked Open Terms (LOT) methodology, adapting it to the context of extending an existing ontology rather than building from scratch. The process comprises: (1) collaborative requirements engineering using Conceptboard (a flexible visual whiteboard tool, chosen over LOT's prescribed strict notation to lower the barrier for non-ontology experts); (2) clustering of collected terms into concept groups and mapping them onto the OEO/Basic Formal Ontology (BFO) class hierarchy; (3) GitHub-based community discussion with the OEO developer team; (4) OWL encoding and merging into the OEO model module; and (5) evaluation by annotating real scenarios from the three reference frameworks. Key new concepts introduced include `simulation scenario` (subclass of `plan specification` from IAO, not of OEO's existing `scenario` which emphasizes future narratives), `simulation configuration` (technical setup: components, connections, data), `output creation objective`, and `output data creation objective`. Key Results: The paper is a short extended abstract (6 pages) and presents no quantitative experimental results. The primary output is the integration of the first co-simulation scenario concepts into the OEO's model module, publicly available via GitHub. The ontology is validated qualitatively by demonstrating that the three reference frameworks' scenarios can be annotated using the new terms. The class hierarchy anchors new terms under `owl:Thing → entity → continuant → generically dependent continuant → information content entity → directive information entity`, with `simulation scenario` and `simulation configuration` under `plan specification`. Further concepts (simulation components, connections, parameters) are mentioned as under development. Strengths: - Pragmatic adaptation of the LOT methodology to the extension-of-existing-ontology context, avoiding unnecessary process overhead while maintaining structured development. - Integration into the established OEO/BFO hierarchy ensures formal rigor and reusability across the broader energy ontology ecosystem. - Community-driven development via GitHub issues and OEO team review promotes transparency and sustainability beyond the immediate project. Weaknesses / Limitations: - This is a 6-page extended abstract with no quantitative evaluation; there is no formal competency question testing, no coverage metrics, and no comparison against alternative ontological choices — making it impossible to assess actual semantic adequacy. - The ontology coverage described is extremely narrow (a handful of top-level concepts); critical domain-specific elements like grid topology, time series data, and solver configurations are explicitly deferred to future work, leaving the ontology incomplete for practical use.
Search Results
#181 — Routine (12)

Open Source LLM ile Tableau Entegreli Veri Analiz Asistanı

orclever.com · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This is a straightforward system integration of existing components (Mistral, LangChain, Tableau VDS API) with no algorithmic novelty. The evaluation on 50 queries against a single toy dataset with no baselines, no error analysis, and opaque ground-truth construction falls well below research standards. It reads as a deployment report rather than a contribution to the field.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Corporate data analysis workflows typically require technical expertise (SQL, BI tool proficiency) that mid- and senior-level managers lack, creating bottlenecks in data-driven decision-making. This paper proposes a natural language interface that allows non-technical users to query and analyze enterprise data in Turkish or English without writing queries. The system targets on-premises data security requirements by using open-source rather than cloud-hosted LLMs. Methodology: The system chains a Mistral 24B open-source LLM with Tableau's VDS (Virtual Data Source) API via a LangChain agent. User queries (in TR/EN) are submitted through a Tableau dashboard extension, processed by a LangChain PromptTemplate + Agent that maps the natural language input to a VDS API request, retrieves the relevant data slice from a published Tableau data source, and feeds it to a text summarizer that returns a natural-language response. The pipeline is deployed on 2× NVIDIA L40S GPUs running Python 3.12 with pandas, numpy, transformers, and langchain. Experiments used the standard Tableau Superstore sample dataset (9,994 rows, fields: Order ID, Date, Region, Category, Sub-Category, Product Name, Sales, Quantity, Profit). Key Results: Evaluation used 50 manually crafted natural language queries. The system achieved 92% answer accuracy (measured as match rate against results obtained via conventional Tableau Desktop queries). Average response latency was 1.5 seconds. Performance was reported to be consistent across both Turkish and English queries. No baseline NL-to-BI system is compared against; the only comparison point is manual Tableau querying, for which the paper claims "100% access and efficiency gain" — a poorly defined metric. Strengths: - Practical on-premises deployment using open-source LLM avoids data leaving the enterprise perimeter, which is a real corporate concern. - Bilingual (TR/EN) support is a concrete, under-addressed need for non-English-language enterprises. - LangChain + VDS API integration provides a clean architectural separation between NL understanding and data retrieval. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Extremely thin evaluation: 50 queries on a single toy dataset (Tableau's own Superstore sample) with no train/test split, no error analysis, and no comparison to any existing NL-to-BI baseline (e.g., Tableau's built-in Ask Data, Microsoft Copilot for Power BI, or academic NL-to-SQL systems). - The 92% accuracy figure is self-reported with an opaque ground-truth construction process; the paper does not describe who created the 50 queries, how ground truth was established, or how partial/ambiguous matches were handled. - The paper presents no novel technical contribution — it is a system integration of existing components (Mistral, LangChain, Tableau VDS API) with no new algorithms, training, or architectural innovations. It reads as a practitioner deployment report rather than a research contribution.
Citations

Thematic Comparisons

Multimodal Model Integration & Generation — avg: 70 (Innovative)

These papers advance unified multimodal systems handling understanding, generation, and long-form content. LatentUM (Latent-Space Unified Model) resolves the disjoint representation problem where semantic features for understanding and reconstruction features for generation diverge. LongCat-Next lexicalizes modalities as discrete tokens, enabling language-centric unified processing. GEMS (Agent-Native Multimodal Generation) addresses complex multi-faceted instructions through memory and skill management. ShotStream enables multi-shot video generation with narrative coherence, essential for AI-driven storytelling. Out of Sight Video World Models tackle the challenging problem of agents re-entering scenes after exiting, requiring memory of hidden state. These papers shift from treating modalities as separate to true integration through unified latent spaces and persistent state.
  • LatentUM: Unleashing the Potential of Interleaved Cross-Modal Reasoning via a Latent-Space Unified Model [#9, Highly Innovative]
  • LongCat-Next: Lexicalizing Modalities as Discrete Tokens [#15, Highly Innovative]
  • Out of Sight but Not Out of Mind: Hybrid Memory for Dynamic Video World Models [#47, Innovative]
  • ShotStream: Streaming Multi-Shot Video Generation for Interactive Storytelling [#70, Innovative]
  • GEMS: Agent-Native Multimodal Generation with Memory and Skills [#114, Moderately Innovative]

LLM Reasoning via Reinforcement Learning — avg: 70 (Innovative)

These papers address core challenges in training LLMs to perform multi-step reasoning via reinforcement learning. LoopRPT extends RL to looped architectures where reasoning unfolds implicitly across latent steps rather than explicit tokens, while REAL tackles the fundamental mismatch where standard RL treats all errors equally despite magnitude differences in regression tasks. PADRE proposes pseudo-likelihood training as an alternative to PGRL's token-level efficiency bottleneck, and FIPO introduces future-KL-influenced policy optimization to assign context-aware advantages beyond outcome-based rewards. TARo presents adaptive routing that selectively applies expensive post-training only where needed, reducing computational burden. MemReward enables reward modeling from limited labels by leveraging experience graphs. Collectively, these papers chart an evolution from outcome-based to structure-aware training signals.
  • PADRE: Pseudo-Likelihood Training for Reasoning Diffusion Language Models [#1, Highly Innovative]
  • FIPO: Eliciting Deep Reasoning with Future-KL Influenced Policy Optimization [#12, Highly Innovative]
  • LoopRPT: Reinforcement Pre-Training for Looped Language Models [#30, Innovative]
  • REAL: Regression-Aware Reinforcement Learning for LLM-as-a-Judge [#53, Innovative]
  • TARo: Token-level Adaptive Routing for LLM Test-time Alignment [#86, Innovative]
  • MemReward: Graph-Based Experience Memory for LLM Reward Prediction with Limited Labels [#109, Moderately Innovative]

Reasoning Enhancement and Analysis — avg: 70 (Innovative)

These papers collectively address the problem of reasoning quality in large language models, but from complementary angles. VRE (2603.26348) proposes a self-iterative training framework to improve visual grounding in multimodal reasoning by inserting structured reflection blocks that re-attend to visual evidence—treating reasoning as an iterative deepening process within a single model. Unify-Agent (2603.29620) tackles reasoning in a different way by augmenting a unified multimodal model with an agentic architecture that explicitly grounds reasoning in retrieved world knowledge, decomposing tasks into cognitive gap detection, evidence acquisition, and recaptioning stages. By contrast, Reasoning Shift (2604.01161) takes a diagnostic approach, revealing that the presence of irrelevant context systematically shortens reasoning traces and reduces post-answer self-verification without improving speed—identifying a behavioral vulnerability that neither training nor retrieval can currently fix. Together, the papers reveal a field in transition: VRE and Unify-Agent show that reasoning quality can be enhanced through deliberate architectural and training interventions, while Reasoning Shift exposes fundamental limitations in how context influences the model's willingness to verify and self-check, suggesting that context robustness remains an open challenge despite advances in reasoning frameworks.
  • Reasoning Shift: How Context Silently Shortens LLM Reasoning [#34, Innovative]
  • Reflect to Inform: Boosting Multimodal Reasoning via Information-Gain-Driven Verification [#51, Innovative]
  • Unify-Agent: A Unified Multimodal Agent for World-Grounded Image Synthesis [#77, Innovative]

LLM Safety & Alignment — avg: 66 (Innovative)

These papers address fundamental safety challenges in LLM deployment across different dimensions. Safeguarding via Self-Destruct proposes trapdoor mechanisms as defenses against jailbreaks, providing an alternative to expensive RLHF. Safe-Unsafe Concept Separation reveals that safety properties emerge in a single direction of activation space, enabling efficient safety interventions. Foundations of Trustworthy AI challenges assumptions about determinism in neural inference across hardware platforms. Therefore I am examines whether visible reasoning actually drives decision-making or merely post-hoc justifies predetermined outcomes. These papers collectively probe whether current alignment approaches address root causes versus symptoms.
  • Safeguarding Language Models via Self-Destruct Trapdoor [#2, Highly Innovative]
  • Therefore I am. I Think. [#42, Innovative]
  • On the Foundations of Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence [#67, Innovative]
  • Safe-Unsafe Concept Separation Emerges from a Single Direction in Language Models Activation Space [#135, Moderately Innovative]

Image & Video Generation from Diffusion Models — avg: 66 (Innovative)

These papers advance visual content generation toward quality, consistency, and editability. Generative World Renderer tackles bidirectional rendering (scene→image and image→scene) using sparse multimodal data. GaussianGPT enables autoregressive 3D scene generation for incremental editing. VGGRPO applies reinforcement learning with 4D latent rewards to maintain 3D geometric consistency in video generation. CutClaw achieves music-synchronized video editing at hours-long scale via agentic orchestration. Gen-Searcher arms diffusion models with search capabilities to escape frozen parametric knowledge. On-the-fly Repulsion combats typicality bias in diffusion by enforcing diversity in the latent space. VOID tackles object removal when objects have physical interactions, not just appearance. These collectively show generation quality requires moving beyond static appearance toward consistency, editability, and grounding in external knowledge.
  • VOID: Video Object and Interaction Deletion [#7, Highly Innovative]
  • VGGRPO: Towards World-Consistent Video Generation with 4D Latent Reward [#23, Highly Innovative]
  • On-the-fly Repulsion in the Contextual Space for Rich Diversity in Diffusion Transformers [#29, Highly Innovative]
  • GaussianGPT: Towards Autoregressive 3D Gaussian Scene Generation [#60, Innovative]
  • Gen-Searcher: Reinforcing Agentic Search for Image Generation [#85, Innovative]
  • CutClaw: Agentic Hours-Long Video Editing via Music Synchronization [#112, Moderately Innovative]
  • Generative World Renderer [#132, Moderately Innovative]

Novel Architectures & Representation Learning — avg: 66 (Innovative)

These papers propose new architectural approaches to longstanding problems. TDMM-LM bridges facial understanding and animation through language models, addressing temporal sparsity from frame-as-image-tokens. Expert Streaming accelerates MoE inference on edge devices through dynamic expert trajectory scheduling. Agent Harnesses (Natural-Language Agent Harnesses) manages the growing complexity of control stacks for multi-step reasoning, tool use, and memory. MuSEAgent (Multimodal Reasoning with Stateful Experiences) enables agents to leverage historical experiences at trajectory level for better grounding. Trace2Skill distills behavioral trajectories into reusable skills. Together these papers show emerging agent systems need new abstractions beyond bare language models: skill libraries, experience memory, and explicit harness design.
  • Expert Streaming: Accelerating Low-Batch MoE Inference via Multi-chiplet Architecture and Dynamic Expert Trajectory Scheduling [#18, Highly Innovative]
  • Natural-Language Agent Harnesses [#63, Innovative]
  • TDMM-LM: Bridging Facial Understanding and Animation via Language Models [#89, Innovative]
  • MuSEAgent: A Multimodal Reasoning Agent with Stateful Experiences [#104, Moderately Innovative]

Code Generation & LLM Self-Improvement — avg: 64 (Innovative)

These works tackle improving code generation quality through different mechanisms. AP2O-Coder uses adaptive preference optimization that progressively targets different error types (compilation vs. runtime) rather than treating all failures uniformly. Self-Distillation shows that LLMs can improve their own code generation using only raw outputs without external verifiers or teacher models. Think Anywhere in Code Generation distributes reasoning throughout generation rather than upfront, enabling more flexible problem-solving. Self-Improvement of LLMs surveys automated improvement techniques where models generate their own training signal through critiques and refinement. Together these represent a paradigm shift toward autonomous LLM capability enhancement without human supervision.
  • Think Anywhere in Code Generation [#5, Highly Innovative]
  • Self-Improvement of Large Language Models: A Technical Overview and Future Outlook [#19, Highly Innovative]
  • Embarrassingly Simple Self-Distillation Improves Code Generation [#99, Moderately Innovative]
  • AP2O-Coder: Adaptively Progressive Preference Optimization for Reducing Compilation and Runtime Errors in LLM-Generated Code [#139, Moderately Innovative]

Spatial, Mathematical & Reasoning Benchmarks — avg: 64 (Innovative)

These papers tackle LLM capabilities on structured reasoning and perception tasks. Template-Theorems Graph Construction shows mathematical reasoning improves through graph-based theorem management. KMM-CP (Conformal Prediction under Covariate Shift) handles distribution shift in safety-critical applications through selective confidence prediction. Steerable Visual Representations enable redirecting attention in vision transformers away from dominant objects toward less-prominent concepts. NearID (Identity Representation via Near-Identity Distractors) shows vision encoders conflate identity with background context, requiring careful perturbation analysis. Together these reveal that visual and mathematical reasoning require explicit structure—templates, graphs, and steering mechanisms—rather than emergent abilities from pretraining alone.
  • Steerable Visual Representations [#17, Highly Innovative]
  • NearID: Identity Representation Learning via Near-identity Distractors [#39, Innovative]
  • KMM-CP: Practical Conformal Prediction under Covariate Shift via Selective Kernel Mean Matching [#98, Moderately Innovative]
  • Template-Theorems Graph Construction to Enhance Mathematical Reasoning Capabilities of LLM [#127, Moderately Innovative]

Vision-Language Model Reasoning & Hallucination — avg: 63 (Innovative)

These papers investigate VLM reasoning failures and interpretability. Explaining CLIP Zero-shot Predictions aims to make opaque recognition decisions interpretable through concept activation vectors. Navigating the Mirage reveals VLMs' extreme vulnerability to misleading charts that exploit cognitive biases, requiring a dual-path agentic framework for robustness. Hallucinations in Multimodal CoT documents how explicit reasoning traces paradoxically amplify hallucinations where visual and textual outputs contradict each other. Make Geometry Matter shows that geometric tokens from 3D foundation models improve spatial reasoning in VLMs. RVLM (Recursive VLM) for medical imaging adds recursion and multiple passes to enable auditable reasoning trails required by clinical standards. These works shift VLM evaluation from accuracy metrics to reasoning quality and hallucination control.
  • Understanding and Mitigating Hallucinations in Multimodal Chain-of-Thought Models [#22, Highly Innovative]
  • Navigating the Mirage: A Dual-Path Agentic Framework for Robust Misleading Chart Question Answering [#43, Innovative]
  • Make Geometry Matter for Spatial Reasoning [#68, Innovative]
  • Explaining CLIP Zero-shot Predictions Through Concepts [#91, Moderately Innovative]
  • RVLM: Recursive Vision-Language Models with Adaptive Depth [#140, Moderately Innovative]

LLM Benchmarking & Evaluation Methodologies — avg: 63 (Innovative)

These papers challenge and improve LLM evaluation practices. Magic Tower introduces a novel benchmark for strategic game playing, revealing LLM weaknesses on complex, long-horizon decision-making under uncertainty. Shodh-Memory benchmarks edge-native AI agent memory systems, showing production systems have 10-100× latency overhead. Measuring Unmeasurable Capabilities reveals existing benchmarks measure only final outcomes at coarse granularity, missing intermediate reasoning steps. MiroEval benchmarks deep research agents on both process (multi-step web investigation) and outcome (report quality), not just end-to-end success. Brevity Constraints show large models lose to small ones when response length is constrained—contradicting monotonic scaling assumptions. HippoCamp evaluates contextual agents on personal computers (local file navigation), the ultimate test of real utility. These papers show synthetic benchmarks increasingly diverge from real-world utility, requiring new evaluation paradigms.
  • Brevity Constraints Reverse Performance Hierarchies in Language Models [#4, Highly Innovative]
  • Measuring the Unmeasurable: Unveiling Latent Cognitive Capabilities of LLM [#26, Highly Innovative]
  • MiroEval: Benchmarking Multimodal Deep Research Agents in Process and Outcome [#48, Innovative]
  • HippoCamp: Benchmarking Contextual Agents on Personal Computers [#71, Innovative]
  • Magic Tower: A Novel Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Models in Strategic Game Playing [#138, Moderately Innovative]
  • Shodh-Memory: A Cognitive Memory System for Edge-Native AI Agents [#152, Incremental]

Clinical AI & Medical Imaging Applications — avg: 62 (Innovative)

These papers address clinical deployment challenges where reasoning transparency and grounding are non-negotiable. PLACID (Privacy-preserving LLMs for Acronym Clinical Inference) tackles the high-stakes problem of ambiguous medical acronyms where interpretation errors cause patient harm. Agentic Hybrid LLM-RAG Framework combines reasoning with factual grounding through knowledge graphs, replacing fluency with clinically reliable outputs. MedOpenClaw evaluates VLMs on realistic radiology tasks using full studies rather than pre-selected diagnostic images, exposing real deployment gaps. Medical AI Scientist extends AI discovery frameworks specifically to clinical medicine. These papers reveal that clinical AI requires fundamentally different evaluation and architecture choices prioritizing interpretability and factual grounding over capabilities alone.
  • MedOpenClaw: Auditable Medical Imaging Agents Reasoning over Uncurated Full Studies [#8, Highly Innovative]
  • Towards a Medical AI Scientist [#27, Highly Innovative]
  • PLACID: Privacy-preserving Large language models for Acronym Clinical Inference and Disambiguation [#122, Moderately Innovative]
  • An Agentic Hybrid LLM–RAG Framework for Explainable Clinical Decision Support [#146, Moderately Innovative]

Data-Centric Learning & Model Training — avg: 62 (Innovative)

These papers address data quality, selection, and model optimization. Data-Efficient Pre-training via Synthetic Megadocs tackles the fundamental constraint that unique web text is fixed while compute grows 4-5× yearly, showing synthetic data can overcome this ceiling. Trace2Skill distills trajectory-local lessons into transferable agent skills, enabling skill knowledge reuse. First-Mover Bias in SHAP Explanations reveals SHAP feature importance from gradient boosters becomes unstable under correlation, requiring careful interpretation in high-stakes decisions. From Data to Laws (Conservation Laws Discovery) addresses scientific machine learning's core challenge—discovering conservation laws without false positives across parameter variations. These papers show training increasingly depends on data engineering (synthesis, filtering, curriculum design) rather than pure scale.
  • Data-efficient pre-training by scaling synthetic megadocs [#62, Innovative]
  • Trace2Skill: Distill Trajectory-Local Lessons into Transferable Agent Skills [#82, Innovative]
  • From Data to Laws: Neural Discovery of Conservation Laws Without False Positives [#88, Innovative]
  • First-Mover Bias in Gradient Boosting Explanations: Mechanism, Detection, and Resolution [#106, Moderately Innovative]

Distributed LLM Inference Systems — avg: 61 (Innovative)

These papers address the architecture and coordination challenges of running large models across multiple devices. Switch-Centric In-Network addresses the critical path bottleneck of all-reduce operations in tensor parallelism via in-network acceleration. Heddle provides orchestration for agentic RL's multi-step rollout generation across distributed workers. Trust-Aware Routing schedules inference on heterogeneous, unreliable edge nodes by learning node reliability patterns. Optimizing Distributed LLM Inference uses dynamic graph partitioning to handle heterogeneous worker capabilities without manual configuration. Web-Based Distributed LLM removes installation barriers via browser-based inference. Together they address the full stack: compute efficiency, scheduling, heterogeneity handling, and accessibility.
  • A Switch-Centric In-Network Architecture for Accelerating LLM Inference in Shared-Memory Network [#3, Highly Innovative]
  • Heddle: A Distributed Orchestration System for Agentic RL Rollout [#21, Highly Innovative]
  • Trust-Aware Routing for Distributed Generative AI Inference at the Edge [#100, Moderately Innovative]
  • Optimizing Distributed LLM Inference for Heterogeneous Workers through Dynamic Graph Partitioning [#120, Moderately Innovative]
  • An Architecture for Web-Based Distributed LLM Inference [#143, Moderately Innovative]

Time Series Forecasting & Temporal Models — avg: 61 (Innovative)

These papers address modeling temporal dependencies across different problem classes. Foundation Model for Instruction-Conditioned Time Series extends in-context learning paradigm to tabular temporal data, enabling zero-shot forecasting without retraining. QUITOBENCH addresses the canonical benchmark gap—while NLP has GLUE and vision has ImageNet, time series lacks large-scale standard evaluation. Cross-Platform Benchmark of State Space Models compares ARM/x86/GPU inference for 1.58-bit quantized Mamba, showing efficiency varies dramatically across hardware. Speculative Decoding for Hybrid State Space Models optimizes the memory bandwidth bottleneck of autoregressive inference. Together these show temporal modeling requires both strong inductive biases (state space structure) and careful inference optimization.
  • A Foundation Model for Instruction-Conditioned In-Context Time Series Tasks [#14, Highly Innovative]
  • Improving Inference Efficiency of Hybrid State Space Models through Speculative Decoding [#46, Innovative]
  • QUITOBENCH: A High-Quality Open Time Series Forecasting Benchmark [#66, Innovative]
  • Cross-Platform Benchmark of State Space Model Inference: ARM NEON vs x86 AVX2 vs GPU CUDA with 1.58-bit Quantized Mamba [#167, Incremental]

Research Infrastructure & Deep Reasoning Evaluation — avg: 61 (Innovative)

These papers address infrastructure and evaluation for research and knowledge systems. Language Models Don't Know What You Want evaluates deep research systems that synthesize literature, revealing they fail to adapt to individual researcher needs despite being positioned as personalization tools. Detecting Miscitation finds that 25% of scientific citations fail to support or contradict their claims, enabling detection through graph-based learning. Composer 2 benchmarks software engineering agents, revealing public benchmarks increasingly misalign with real-world utility due to domain mismatch and over-specification. GEditBench v2 evaluates general image editing with human-aligned benchmarks rather than proprietary LLM judges. These papers shift research infrastructure evaluation from static accuracy metrics toward utility, fidelity, and human-aligned assessment.
  • Language Models Don't Know What You Want: Evaluating Personalization in Deep Research Needs Real Users [#41, Innovative]
  • Composer 2 Technical Report [#76, Innovative]
  • Detecting Miscitation on the Scholarly Web through LLM-Augmented Text-Rich Graph Learning [#96, Moderately Innovative]
  • GEditBench v2: A Human-Aligned Benchmark for General Image Editing [#119, Moderately Innovative]

Graph Neural Networks & Heterogeneous Networks — avg: 61 (Innovative)

These papers advance GNN methods for complex real-world graph structures. Structure-Enhanced Adapter for heterogeneous graphs handles multiple node/edge types while adapting to structural noise that breaks topology assumptions. Unsupervised Anomaly Detection on Attributed Multiplex Heterogeneous Networks addresses the reality that real systems have multiple relationship types and diverse node attributes. StreamTGN optimizes temporal GNNs for serving rather than just training, critical for real-time link prediction and fraud detection. Incremental GNN Embedding enables continuous node embedding updates as dynamic graphs evolve. Simulating Judicial Reasoning uses graph neural networks to model legal precedent relationships and predict case outcomes, showing GNNs' capacity for structured reasoning. Collectively these extend GNNs beyond homogeneous graphs to handle the multiplicity and evolution of real-world networks.
  • StreamTGN: A GPU-Efficient Serving System for Streaming Temporal Graph Neural Networks [#6, Highly Innovative]
  • Incremental GNN Embedding Computation on Streaming Graphs [#25, Highly Innovative]
  • Structure-Enhanced Adapter for Self-Supervised Heterogeneous Graph Learning [#97, Moderately Innovative]
  • Unsupervised Anomaly Detection on Attributed Multiplex Heterogeneous Networks [#126, Moderately Innovative]
  • Simulating Judicial Reasoning: A Plea-Guided Explainable Graph Neural Network for Civil Judgment Prediction [#145, Moderately Innovative]

3D Scene & Content Synthesis — avg: 60 (Innovative)

These papers address 3D asset generation and content creation workflows. Know3D uses vision-language models to guide 3D generation, addressing the inherent ill-posedness of single-image 3D reconstruction by grounding hallucinations in parametric knowledge. Extend3D tackles town-scale 3D scene generation from single images for game/film production. PerceptionComp benchmarks video understanding on perception-centric reasoning requiring repeated evidence gathering. RealChart2Code advances chart-to-code generation with real data and multi-task evaluation, showing VLMs still lag on complex visualization replication. GenMask directly generates segmentation masks from diffusion models rather than treating them as implicit feature backbones. These demonstrate that high-quality 3D content requires explicit grounding, multi-task coherence, and direct optimization rather than black-box end-to-end generation.
  • GenMask: Adapting DiT for Segmentation via Direct Mask Generation [#13, Highly Innovative]
  • Extend3D: Town-Scale 3D Generation [#37, Innovative]
  • Know3D: Prompting 3D Generation with Knowledge from Vision-Language Models [#69, Innovative]
  • PerceptionComp: A Video Benchmark for Complex Perception-Centric Reasoning [#121, Moderately Innovative]
  • RealChart2Code: Advancing Chart-to-Code Generation with Real Data and Multi-Task Evaluation [#157, Incremental]

Language Properties & Cross-Lingual Understanding — avg: 60 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers investigate linguistic phenomena in LLMs across languages and phenomena. Arabic Diacritics explores how orthographic marks (dense in formal texts, sparse in web content) affect tokenization and model performance. Vocabulary Shapes Cross-Lingual Word-Order shows vocabulary composition predicts word-order flexibility; subword tokenization disrupts this by destroying morphological structure. Cross-Lingual LLM-Judge Transfer shifts evaluation from English-centric to multilingual, challenging the assumption that English represents universal linguistic phenomena. Density Modeling questions whether LLMs capture natural language probability distributions, a core assumption in linguistic analyses. Together these reveal LLMs' linguistic properties are highly language-specific and emergent from vocabulary/tokenization choices rather than universal.
  • Vocabulary Shapes Cross-Lingual Variation of Word-Order Learnability in Language Models [#38, Innovative]
  • Are Language Models Any Good at Density Modeling? [#64, Innovative]
  • Cross-Lingual LLM-Judge Transfer via Evaluation Decomposition [#105, Moderately Innovative]
  • Do Diacritics Matter? Evaluating the Impact of Arabic Diacritics on Tokenization and LLM Benchmarks [#133, Moderately Innovative]

Embodied AI & Autonomous Vehicles — avg: 60 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers address the intersection of vision-language understanding and physical action in embodied systems. FASTER (Real-Time Flow VLAs) tackles the latency bottleneck in vision-language-action models for robot control, showing pretrained VLMs become performance cliffs in real-world dynamic environments. OmniVLN addresses egocentric navigation where narrow field-of-view forces repeated rotations and omni-directional perception with token-efficient reasoning. UniDriveVLA bridges spatial perception and semantic reasoning for autonomous driving without treating them as separate problems. EgoSim provides explicit 3D grounding for egocentric world models, preventing geometric drift during viewpoint changes. CARLA-AIR unifies air and ground agent simulation for coordinated multi-robot systems. Together these reveal that embodied AI requires tight integration of perception, reasoning, and action rather than sequential pipelines.
  • EgoSim: Egocentric World Simulator for Embodied Interaction Generation [#31, Innovative]
  • UniDriveVLA: Unifying Understanding, Perception, and Action Planning for Autonomous Driving [#59, Innovative]
  • FASTER: Rethinking Real-Time Flow VLAs [#79, Innovative]
  • OmniVLN: Omnidirectional 3D Perception and Token-Efficient LLM Reasoning for Visual-Language Navigation across Air and Ground Platforms [#101, Moderately Innovative]
  • CARLA-AIR: Fly Drones Inside a CARLA World — A Unified Infrastructure for Air-Ground Embodied Intelligence [#148, Incremental]

Audio & Speech Processing — avg: 60 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers address audio signal processing and multimodal speech systems. How Auditory Knowledge in LLM Backbones Shapes Audio Language Models questions how much auditory information LLMs encode through text-only pretraining and whether this predicts downstream LALM performance. Research on Abnormal Sound Defects in Automobiles combines audio and visual fusion for predictive maintenance, showing single-modality approaches fail on noisy real-world data. Sommelier (Scalable Open Multi-turn Audio Pre-processing) addresses full-duplex speech language models through high-quality multi-speaker conversational training data. These papers show audio AI requires dedicated inductive biases beyond treating audio as a modality to be plugged into LLM backbones.
  • How Auditory Knowledge in LLM Backbones Shapes Audio Language Models: A Holistic Evaluation [#74, Innovative]
  • Sommelier: Scalable Open Multi-turn Audio Pre-processing for Full-duplex Speech Language Models [#107, Moderately Innovative]

LLM Systems Infrastructure and Evaluation — avg: 60 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers address the growing infrastructure and evaluation needs of production LLM systems as workloads diversify from simple chat to agentic and multimodal tasks. Workload–Router–Pool (2603.21354) is a vision paper that synthesizes prior work into a unifying three-dimensional framework, revealing that routing decisions, workload characteristics, and GPU pool topology interact in complex ways—showing that gains of 17–39% GPU reduction or 4.25× energy efficiency require simultaneous co-optimization across all three dimensions, not piecemeal improvements. PRBench (2603.27646) tackles the complementary problem of *evaluation*, proposing the first end-to-end benchmark for agent reproducibility in physics research, where all current frontier models score 0% on the callback rate (simultaneous achievement across methodology, code, and numerical accuracy), revealing that isolated capability measurements mask severe gaps in coordinated execution. Together, the papers expose a foundational mismatch: the Workload–Router–Pool framework shows that production inference depends on fine-grained interaction between routing strategy and workload type, yet PRBench reveals that agents cannot reliably execute coordinated multi-step tasks end-to-end, suggesting that routing optimization is premature without first solving the fundamental problem of agent reliability and reproducibility.
  • PRBench: End-to-end Paper Reproduction in Physics Research [#52, Innovative]
  • The Workload–Router–Pool Architecture for LLM Inference Optimization [#125, Moderately Innovative]

Video Understanding & Visual Reasoning — avg: 59 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers target video understanding from reasoning to long-form generation and autonomous driving. Clue Matters leverages latent visual clues to empower VideoQA through explicit reasoning grounded in visual evidence rather than one-shot black-box inference. ViGoR-Bench measures whether visual generative models can perform physical, causal, or symbolic reasoning—revealing a systematic logic gap. ImagenWorld uses explainable human evaluation on open-ended tasks rather than pointwise metrics, better reflecting actual generative quality. PackForcing shows short-video training (4–16 frames) suffices for long-video generation through block-by-block KV caching. LongTail Driving Scenarios with reasoning traces provides benchmarks for autonomous driving on rare edge cases with interpretable decision traces. Collectively these show video evaluation requires moving beyond frame-level metrics toward reasoning quality, diversity, and real-world task performance.
  • PackForcing: Short Video Training Suffices for Long Video Sampling and Long Context Inference [#16, Highly Innovative]
  • Clue Matters: Leveraging Latent Visual Clues to Empower Video Reasoning [#81, Innovative]
  • ViGoR-Bench: How Far Are Visual Generative Models From Zero-Shot Visual Reasoners? [#95, Moderately Innovative]
  • ImagenWorld: Stress-Testing Image Generation Models with Explainable Human Evaluation on Open-Ended Real-World Tasks [#115, Moderately Innovative]
  • LongTail Driving Scenarios with Reasoning Traces: The KITScenes LongTail Dataset [#137, Moderately Innovative]

LLM Parameter & Compute Efficiency — avg: 57 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers target LLM efficiency through compression, quantization, and selective computation. RSR-core optimizes the dominant matrix-vector multiplication bottleneck using low-bit quantization. Batch-wise Adaptive Pruning addresses deployment reality by pruning reasoning models in batched inference settings where attention patterns vary per batch. Universal YOCO enables depth scaling through recursive computation without linear KV cache growth. Sparse Faster Lighter Transformers show feedforward layers (66% parameters, 80% FLOPs) can be pruned substantially. EcoThink avoids applying expensive CoT reasoning to all queries. MCW-KD uses Wasserstein distances for knowledge distillation beyond point-wise divergences. Token's Dilemma handles continual learning of new tasks in MoE LLMs via drift-aware token assignment. Collectively they demonstrate that efficiency gains require understanding inference compute patterns rather than uniform compression.
  • Universal YOCO for Efficient Depth Scaling [#20, Highly Innovative]
  • Sparser, Faster, Lighter Transformer Language Models [#35, Innovative]
  • On Token's Dilemma: Dynamic MoE with Drift-Aware Token Assignment for Continual Learning of Large Vision Language Models [#55, Innovative]
  • MCW-KD: Multi-Cost Wasserstein Knowledge Distillation for Large Language Models [#83, Innovative]
  • Batch-wise Adaptive Pruning: Periodic Neuron Activation-Aware Weight Pruning for Language Reasoning Model [#117, Moderately Innovative]
  • RSR-core: A High-Performance Engine for Low-Bit Matrix-Vector Multiplication [#150, Incremental]
  • EcoThink: A Green Adaptive Inference Framework for Sustainable and Accessible Agents [#161, Incremental]

Multimodal Model Serving & Inference Optimization — avg: 56 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers target the intersection of multimodal (vision + text) model efficiency. Parallel In-context Learning for LVLMs enables processing multiple in-context examples in parallel rather than sequentially, overcoming the quadratic attention complexity bottleneck. Rocks Pebbles Sand presents a modality-aware scheduling system where vision preprocessing and encoding stages are handled differently from language generation stages, addressing the asymmetric compute requirements of multimodal serving. Attention-aware Inference Optimizations reduce memory overhead during decoding via efficient KV cache management. Efficient Inference of Large VLMs addresses the fundamental problem that high-resolution images generate massive visual token sequences, combining techniques like compression and hierarchical processing. These works recognize that multimodal systems require fundamentally different serving strategies than text-only LLMs.
  • Attention-aware Inference Optimizations for Large Vision-Language Models with Memory-efficient Decoding [#44, Innovative]
  • Parallel In-context Learning for Large Vision Language Models [#56, Innovative]
  • Rocks, Pebbles and Sand: Modality-aware Scheduling for Multimodal Large Language Model Inference [#84, Innovative]
  • Efficient Inference of Large Vision Language Models [#170, Incremental]

Infrastructure, Systems & Hardware Optimization — avg: 56 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers target hardware and systems challenges at different scales. CXL Memory Processing for Distributed GNN Inference tackles irregular fine-grained memory accesses that underutilize DRAM bandwidth during neighbor aggregation. Co-design Threading & Circuit Cutting for Quantum Circuits addresses NISQ processor limitations through distributed quantum computing across multiple QPUs. SAMSEM (IC Metal Line Segmentation) uses physical inspection via SEM imaging for post-manufacturing hardware assurance. Semantic Graphs of Learning Activities from LLM Embeddings provides lightweight explainability for smart learning systems. These papers show system efficiency requires co-optimization across multiple abstraction levels from memory hierarchies through quantum parallelism.
  • SAMSEM – A Generic and Scalable Approach for IC Metal Line Segmentation [#65, Innovative]
  • A near CXL memory processing architecture for distributed graph neural network inference and training [#103, Moderately Innovative]
  • Co-design threading model and circuit cutting for static and adaptive quantum circuits [#129, Moderately Innovative]

LLM Agents & Web Navigation Planning — avg: 56 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers address challenges in deploying LLMs as autonomous decision-making agents. Why Do Web Agents Fail analyzes end-to-end task failures through hierarchical planning decomposition rather than black-box success metrics. When Should a Robot Think tackles the latency-accuracy tradeoff by using RL to learn when reasoning is worth the compute cost. Evolution of Tool Use traces how agent paradigms progressed from single-tool calls to multi-tool orchestration, though recent architectures show tool use alone is insufficient. LENS (Learning Architecture Navigator) optimizes multi-agent system architectures through an agentic supernet that learns which configurations suit which queries. OMNI-SIMPLEMEM addresses the foundational problem that agents need persistent, multimodal memory across long time horizons. Collectively these show agent reliability requires structured planning, selective reasoning, and long-term memory rather than pure end-to-end learning.
  • OMNI-SIMPLEMEM: Autoresearch-Guided Discovery of Lifelong Multimodal Agent Memory [#36, Innovative]
  • Why Do LLM-based Web Agents Fail? A Hierarchical Planning Perspective [#87, Innovative]
  • When Should a Robot Think? Resource-Aware Reasoning via Reinforcement Learning for Embodied Robotic Decision-Making [#93, Moderately Innovative]
  • The Evolution of Tool Use in LLM Agents: From Single-Tool Call to Multi-Tool Orchestration [#130, Moderately Innovative]
  • LENS: Learning Architecture Navigator for LLM Agentic Systems [#141, Moderately Innovative]

Knowledge Tracing & Adaptive Learning — avg: 53 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers advance student modeling from binary correctness to concept-level mastery and interpretability. MSR-Rec shows sequential recommendation benefits from multi-step reasoning over implicit pattern matching. Knowledge Tracing Circuit Complexity models how mastery propagates through concept hierarchies, proving limitations of shallow models. ConceptKT shifts from predicting answer correctness to identifying specific concept deficiencies. Extended LSTM improves learner performance prediction by capturing temporal patterns. Theory-Based Interpretability in Deep KT bridges the gap between opaque neural models (high accuracy) and interpretable probabilistic models (rigid structure) through grounded transformers. Collectively these show adaptive learning requires understanding not just what students know, but which prerequisite concepts block advancement.
  • Theory-Based Interpretability in Deep Knowledge Tracing via Grounded Transformers [#61, Innovative]
  • Circuit Complexity of Hierarchical Knowledge Tracing and Implications for Log-Precision Transformers [#80, Innovative]
  • ConceptKT: A Benchmark for Concept-Level Deficiency Prediction in Knowledge Tracing [#108, Moderately Innovative]
  • MSR-Rec: Multi-Step Reasoning-Enhanced LLM for Sequential Recommendation [#128, Moderately Innovative]
  • Extended LSTM to Enhance Learner Performance Prediction [#162, Incremental]

Distributed Computing & Resource Management — avg: 52 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers address parallel computation, load balancing, and fair resource allocation. Communication-Aware Diffusion Load Balancing solves the problem that irregular parallel applications (particle sims, AMR, graph analytics) have time-varying load and communication patterns. Rank-Aware Resource Scheduling for MPI shows CPU allocation proportional to mesh density eliminates waste in cloud deployments. Two-Level Nonlinear Schwarz Methods provide robust, scalable solvers for discretized nonlinear PDEs. Justitia (Fair Scheduling of Task-Parallel LLM Agents) addresses the novel scheduling problem of task-parallel agents where selective resource allocation improves aggregate throughput. Experience Replay for Continual Learning prevents catastrophic forgetting through experience memory. Together these show distributed computing challenges have evolved from homogeneous to heterogeneous workloads requiring adaptive, fairness-aware coordination.
  • Justitia: Fair and Efficient Scheduling of Task-parallel LLM Agents with Selective Pampering [#50, Innovative]
  • Two-Level Nonlinear Schwarz Methods - A Parallel Implementation with Application to Nonlinear Elasticity and Incompressible Flow Problems [#73, Innovative]
  • Communication-Aware Diffusion Load Balancing for Persistently Interacting Objects [#124, Moderately Innovative]
  • Rank-Aware Resource Scheduling for Tightly-Coupled MPI Workloads on Kubernetes [#144, Moderately Innovative]
  • Experience Replay for Continual Learning [#155, Incremental]

Scientific AI & Academic Discovery — avg: 52 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers apply AI to scientific discovery and academic communication. FlowPIE (Flow-Guided Literature Exploration) enables test-time scientific idea evolution through dynamic literature exploration, showing static retrieval limits idea generation. ToM4AI Workshop addresses the growing intersection of Theory of Mind with AI systems, enabling agents to reason about others' mental states. Well-being-Enhanced READINESS Model extends crisis communication frameworks to account for psychosocial toll on professionals in generative AI contexts. DataFlex (Unified Data-Centric Dynamic Training) unifies fragmented data-centric training methods into a coherent framework. SKILL0 (In-Context Agentic RL for Skill Internalization) enables agents to learn behavioral knowledge from context rather than requiring retraining. These papers show scientific progress increasingly depends on agents reasoning with external knowledge, managing discovery at scale, and accounting for human factors.
  • FlowPIE: Test-Time Scientific Idea Evolution with Flow-Guided Literature Exploration [#11, Highly Innovative]
  • SKILL0: In-Context Agentic Reinforcement Learning for Skill Internalization [#28, Highly Innovative]
  • DataFlex: A Unified Framework for Data-Centric Dynamic Training of Large Language Models [#134, Moderately Innovative]
  • ToM4AI 2026: Proceedings of the 2nd Workshop on Advancing AI Through Theory of Mind [#160, Incremental]
  • The well-being-enhanced READINESS model: a human-centric extension for risk and crisis communication in generative AI contexts [#175, Incremental]

Model Scaling & Architectural Theory — avg: 52 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers provide theoretical understanding of LLM scaling and architecture. Tiered Super-Moore's Law documents the 600-fold token price collapse over six years with rigorous economic analysis of token pricing dynamics. The Latent Space Foundation examines how computation fundamentally unfolds through continuous activations rather than token-level generation, suggesting models should be understood as latent-space operators. Scalable Prompt Routing addresses the hard problem of selecting best-fit LLMs from dozens with narrow performance gaps, showing simple content-based routing fails. Together these papers move from empirical scaling laws toward economic, theoretical, and pragmatic understanding of LLM deployment.
  • Scalable Prompt Routing via Fine-Grained Latent Task Discovery [#75, Innovative]
  • Tiered Super-Moore's Law: Price Evolution, Production Frontiers, and Market Competition in Large Language Model Inference Services [#102, Moderately Innovative]
  • The Latent Space: Foundation, Evolution, Mechanism, Ability, and Outlook [#153, Incremental]

Security, Privacy & Robustness — avg: 52 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers address LLM security from extraction through deployment to multiagent systems. AI-Driven Cloud Security uses AIOps for threat detection, addressing the telemetry explosion (terabytes daily) that overwhelms traditional SIEM tools. TAMI-MPC (Trusted Acceleration of MPC) enables privacy-preserving inference through secure multi-party computation. Near-Verbatim Extraction Risk measures memorization beyond greedy decoding, capturing both near-verbatim copying and hidden memorization in sampling-based decoding. Emergent Social Intelligence Risks focuses on failure modes emerging from multi-agent interactions, revealing that system-level failures differ fundamentally from individual agent failures. Abnormal Text Detection integrates GUIDE and NOTICE approaches for social media content moderation. These papers collectively show security must address infrastructure, deployment, and emergent system properties rather than individual model vulnerabilities.
  • Estimating near-verbatim extraction risk in language models with decoding-constrained beam search [#10, Highly Innovative]
  • TAMI-MPC: Trusted Acceleration of Minimal-Interaction MPC for Efficient Nonlinear Inference [#49, Innovative]
  • Emergent Social Intelligence Risks in Generative Multi-Agent Systems [#72, Innovative]
  • AI-Driven Cloud Security: AIOps for Threat Detection and Compliance [#173, Incremental]
  • Detection Method of Abnormal Text Information in Social Networks Integrating GUIDE and NOTICE [#179, Routine]

Recommendation Systems & Personalization — avg: 51 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers tackle personalization and ranking across different recommendation contexts. Distributionally Robust Optimization balances accuracy-driven selection with diversity, addressing real-world objectives beyond pure accuracy. LLM-as-Recommender Trustability reveals LLM selection systems are easily hacked by preference biases when users have narrow tastes. VEGAR Tourist Attraction Recommender leverages signed feedback and spatial-sentiment knowledge graphs for tourist experiences. Extending Hypernetwork-Based Recommenders addresses cold-start through side information exploitation. Subspace-Aware Multimodal Recommendation integrates heterogeneous signals (text, images, interactions) via graph construction and contrastive alignment. These show recommendation quality depends on handling distributional shifts, user preferences, and multimodal integration rather than accuracy metrics alone.
  • Is Your LLM-as-a-Recommender Agent Trustable? LLMs' Recommendation is Easily Hacked by Biases (Preferences) [#32, Innovative]
  • Subspace-Aware Graph Construction and Contrastive Alignment for Multimodal Recommendation with Large Language Models [#54, Innovative]
  • Distributionally robust optimization for recommendation selection [#123, Moderately Innovative]
  • VEGAR: A tourist attraction recommender system based on signed feedback and a signed spatial-sentiment knowledge graph [#149, Incremental]
  • Extending Hypernetwork-Based Recommender Systems for the Cold-Start Problem Using Side Information [#172, Incremental]

Information Retrieval & Efficient Search — avg: 49 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers address retrieval challenges from dense search to toponym resolution. OPERA (Online Data Pruning for Efficient Retrieval) shows that existing pruning methods designed for classification fail on retrieval's dynamic rank-dependent objectives. FEAT (Linear-Complexity Foundation Model for Structured Data) extends in-context learning to tabular data where traditional retrieval requires dataset-specific training. Open-Source LLM Data Analysis Assistant integrates LLMs with Tableau to democratize analytics for non-technical users. UniTopRank provides language-independent toponym resolution for geoparsing pipelines used in spatial humanities and web indexing. Together these show retrieval needs to move beyond traditional similarity search toward task-aware ranking and semantic disambiguation.
  • FEAT: A Linear-Complexity Foundation Model for Extremely Large Structured Data [#33, Innovative]
  • OPERA: Online Data Pruning for Efficient Retrieval Model Adaptation [#92, Moderately Innovative]
  • UniTopRank: a scalable and language-independent method for toponym resolution [#113, Moderately Innovative]
  • Open-Source LLM Integrated Data Analysis Assistant with Tableau [#182, Routine]

Biomedical AI & Drug Discovery — avg: 49 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers apply AI to biomedical challenges from drug discovery to genomic modeling. TRACE-DDI predicts drug-drug interactions using transformer encoders and pathway-anchored knowledge graphs, addressing the clinical screening bottleneck. PUPPET proposes neural-symbolic standardized patients for mental health training, scaling the gold-standard training methodology. Lingshu-Cell develops generative cellular models for transcriptome simulation, moving from descriptive genomic analysis to predictive cellular biology. Project Imaging-X catalogs 1000+ open-access medical imaging datasets for foundation model development, addressing the data scarcity limiting medical AI. JAK/STAT Signaling with Cold Atmospheric Plasma explores novel therapeutic mechanisms for autoimmune disease. These demonstrate AI's potential across the biomedical pipeline from molecular interactions through clinical applications.
  • Lingshu-Cell: A generative cellular world model for transcriptome modeling toward virtual cells [#24, Highly Innovative]
  • PUPPET: Neural-Symbolic Standardized Patients for Mental Health [#45, Innovative]
  • TRACE-DDI: A Hybrid Framework of Transformer–GAT Context Encoder and Pathway-Anchored Knowledge Graphs for DDI Prediction [#136, Moderately Innovative]
  • Project Imaging-X: A Survey of 1000+ Open-Access Medical Imaging Datasets for Foundation Model Development [#163, Incremental]
  • Targeting JAK/STAT Signaling with Cold Atmospheric Plasma: A Potential Therapy for Systemic Lupus Erythematosus [#178, Routine]

Knowledge Graphs & Biomedical Retrieval-Augmented Generation — avg: 46 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers demonstrate the power of structured knowledge graphs for LLM reasoning in specialized domains. CrossLinker uses knowledge graphs with link-aware cross-attention for drug-target interaction prediction, showing graphs capture interaction context better than static entity representations. Iterative Multi-Granular RAG constructs hierarchical graph structures to solve multi-hop questions by maintaining coherent reasoning chains. S-Path-RAG finds shortest paths through knowledge graphs to retrieve minimal sufficient evidence for complex queries. UniAI-GraphRAG applies ontology-guided extraction and multi-dimensional clustering for vertical domain applications. Lightweight KG Construction for Node-RED makes community knowledge actionable at design time through low-cost semantic integration. OntoDup uses governance-aware entity matching to deduplicate scholarly knowledge graphs. These papers show that unstructured RAG fragments evidence, while structured knowledge graphs enable reasoning across multiple hops with interpretable paths.
  • Iterative Multi-Granular RAG with Contextual Hierarchical Graph [#57, Innovative]
  • CrossLinker: Knowledge Graph-Enhanced Drug-Target Interaction Prediction via Link-Aware Cross-Attention and Contrastive Alignment [#78, Innovative]
  • OntoDup: Governance-Aware Entity Matching for Scholarly Knowledge Graph Deduplication [#94, Moderately Innovative]
  • S-Path-RAG: Semantic-Aware Shortest-Path Retrieval Augmented Generation for Multi-Hop Knowledge Graph Question Answering [#110, Moderately Innovative]
  • Lightweight Knowledge Graph Construction and Embedded Reasoning for Node-RED Applications [#159, Incremental]
  • UniAI-GraphRAG: Synergizing Ontology-Guided Extraction, Multi-Dimensional Clustering, and Dual-Channel Fusion for Robust Multi-Hop Reasoning

Domain-Specific Tools and Applied Methods — avg: 42 (Moderately Innovative)

These papers represent pragmatic applications of machine learning and signal processing to specialized scientific and industrial domains, each addressing interpretability and practical deployment as core concerns. The engine diagnosis tool (1406) combines hand-crafted multi-modal features (MFCC + wavelet audio, pruned CNN image features) with interpretable attention weights that map to physical fault signatures, achieving 98.7% accuracy while remaining deployable on edge hardware at 0.8ms per inference. The semantic graphs approach (1414) constructs a graph-based representation from frozen embeddings to enable classification and performance prediction with clear nearest-neighbor interpretability, avoiding expensive repeated LLM calls. PomSeg for membrane segmentation (2667-2375) grounds its method in persistent homology theory, enabling white-box parameter tuning where birth and persistence values directly correspond to cell size and overlap—explicitly rejecting black-box deep learning trade-offs. Across all three, the shared ethos is that domain-specific knowledge, interpretability, and computational efficiency matter as much as raw accuracy; each prioritizes users understanding *why* a decision was made and being able to tune parameters meaningfully, a design philosophy that contrasts sharply with end-to-end deep learning paradigms.
  • Persistent homology-based segmentation tool for membrane images [#90, Innovative]
  • Semantic Graphs of Learning Activities from LLM Embeddings: A Lightweight and Explainable Approach for Smart Learning Systems [#154, Incremental]
  • Research on the Diagnosis of Abnormal Sound Defects in Automobile Engines Based on Fusion of Multi-Modal Images and Audio [#169, Incremental]

Ontology Engineering & Knowledge Representation — avg: 39 (Incremental)

These papers represent the state of ontology development across diverse domains. Smart Buildings Ontology shows how semantic frameworks enable IoT integration and demand response. CRMhs Ontology bridges heritage science and cultural data with incompatible terminologies. Traffic Light Detection Ontology applies semantic specification to safeguard autonomous vehicles against physical attacks. BIM/LCA Semantics bridges architecture/engineering design models with life-cycle assessment tools. Continuous Ontology Engineering shifts from project-based to continuous evolution, matching industrial realities. Agentic NeSy (Neuro-Symbolic) proposes agentic, modular architectures replacing fixed pipelines. Co-Simulation Scenario Ontology provides shared semantics for complex multi-domain energy system simulations. These works collectively show ontologies are essential infrastructure for cross-domain integration, yet development methodologies struggle between prescriptive standards and evolving practical needs.
  • An Agentic Outlook on Neuro-Symbolic AI: Towards Modular, Adaptive, and Explainable AI Systems [#58, Innovative]
  • Ontology-Driven Detection of Traffic Light Manipulation in Intelligent Transportation Systems [#111, Moderately Innovative]
